topics.title: The Front End
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Front End # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of "The Front End" In the architecture of a modern compiler, **"The Front End"** refers to the **analysis phase** of the compilation process. Its primary responsibility is to read the source program, decipher its meaning, and translate it into a machine-independent **Intermediate Representation (IR)**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PECIT751 – Compiler Design*, the front end is the *language-dependent* portion of the compiler. It performs everything up to (and including) the generation of three-address code (TAC) or any equivalent IR. The **back end** (covered in later modules) then optimizes and translates this IR into the target machine code. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** > The front end of a compiler is formally defined as the sequence of phases — **Lexical Analysis → Syntax Analysis → Semantic Analysis → Intermediate Code Generation** — that collectively transform a high-level source program into an intermediate representation, performing error detection and symbol-table management along the way. The four canonical phases of the front end are: | Phase | Output Artifact | Core Data Structure | |---|---|---| | 1. Lexical Analyzer (Scanner) | Stream of **Tokens** | Transition Diagram / DFA | | 2. Syntax Analyzer (Parser) | **Parse Tree / Syntax Tree** | Stack / Parse Table | | 3. Semantic Analyzer | Annotated **Syntax Tree** | Symbol Table | | 4. Intermediate Code Generator | **Three-Address Code (TAC)** | Quadruples / Triples | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** > Imagine a restaurant where the head chef (the **Back End**) only speaks the language of *cooking* (machine instructions), but the orders arrive in 50 different human languages (source languages). You need a **Front-of-House Manager** (the **Front End**) who: > 1. **Reads the order** (Lexical Analysis — picks out words: "one", "pizza", "large"). > 2. **Checks grammar** (Syntax Analysis — ensures "one large pizza" is a valid sentence structure, not "pizza one large"). > 3. **Verifies meaning & logic** (Semantic Analysis — checks if "vegetarian pizza with extra ham" is contradictory, and looks up the price in the menu book = Symbol Table). > 4. **Writes a standardized kitchen ticket** (Intermediate Code Generation — a uniform recipe card the chef can cook from, regardless of which language the customer spoke). > > The chef (back end) only ever sees the **standardized ticket**, never the customer's original words. This separation allows the same kitchen to serve any language — exactly how a compiler's back end can target any machine using the same IR. ## 1.3 Boundary Between Front End and Back End The front end operates exclusively on the **source language**. It is **machine-independent**. The back end operates on the IR and is **target-machine dependent**. The boundary is the **Intermediate Representation (IR)**. ![Conceptual Boundary: Source → IR → Target] > [!NOTE] > **Key takeaway:** Because the IR is machine-independent, the same front end can be paired with multiple back ends (e.g., one for x86, one for ARM) — this is the foundation of **retargetable compilers** like LLVM and GCC. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis (Scanning) The **Lexical Analyzer** (often built using tools like **Lex** or **Flex**) reads the raw source character stream and groups characters into meaningful **lexemes** mapped to **tokens**. **Core terminology (from Aho, Sethi, Ullman — the Dragon Book, the de-facto KTU reference):** - **Token** — A symbolic name representing a category of lexemes (e.g., `id`, `number`, `relop`). - **Pattern** — The rule that describes the set of lexemes for a token (e.g., a regular expression). - **Lexeme** — The actual character sequence in the source that matches a pattern (e.g., `count`, `42`, `<=`). **Workflow:** 1. Strip whitespace and comments. 2. Identify the longest prefix matching some pattern (the *maximal munch* rule). 3. Emit a token tuple: ``. 4. Enter identifiers into the **Symbol Table**. > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU 2024, the lexical analyzer is also expected to handle **buffering schemes** (sentinel/sliding buffer) and the construction of **Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA)** from Regular Expressions using Thompson's construction → subset construction → DFA minimization. ## 2.2 Phase 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parsing) The **Parser** receives the token stream and verifies that it forms a valid sentence of the source language's **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**. It produces a **Parse Tree** (concrete) or a **Syntax Tree** (abstract, AST). **Grammatical formalism used:** A CFG is the 4-tuple: $$G = (V, T, P, S)$$ where: - $V$ = set of non-terminals - $T$ = set of terminals (tokens) - $P$ = set of productions of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ - $S$ = start symbol, $S \in V$ **Derivation mechanisms (must know for KTU):** - **Leftmost Derivation** — at each step, rewrite the leftmost non-terminal. Used by **top-down parsers**. - **Rightmost Derivation (Canonical)** — rewrite the rightmost non-terminal. Used by **bottom-up parsers** (e.g., LR). **Parsing strategies:** | Strategy | Class | KTU Relevance | |---|---|---| | Recursive Descent | Top-Down | High — manual, predictive | | LL(1) Predictive Parsing | Top-Down | High — uses parsing table | | Operator Precedence | Bottom-Up | Moderate | | LR(0), SLR(1), CLR(1), LALR(1) | Bottom-Up | **Very High** — typical 14-mark question | ## 2.3 Phase 3 — Semantic Analysis Uses the AST and the **Symbol Table** to enforce *semantic* (meaning-level) rules that the CFG cannot express. Key tasks: - **Type checking** (e.g., `int x = "hello"` is a type mismatch). - **Scope resolution** (which `x` is referenced?). - **Operator-compatibility checks** (e.g., cannot use `&&` on a float in C). - **Implicit conversions** (integer promotion, float widening). > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 emphasizes **S-attributed** and **L-attributed** definitions for typed syntax-directed translation. Expect questions on **type systems** and **type expressions**. ## 2.4 Phase 4 — Intermediate Code Generation Produces a **machine-independent** representation. The standard form is **Three-Address Code (TAC)**, where each instruction has at most **three operands**, in forms like: - Binary: `x = y op z` - Unary: `x = op y` - Copy: `x = y` - Jump: `goto L`, `if x goto L`, `if x relop y goto L` - Procedure: `call p, n`, `param x`, `return y` TAC is represented compactly as **quadruples**, **triples**, or **indirect triples** — KTU frequently tests this. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | CFG Definition | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | 4-tuple | | 2 | Production | $A \rightarrow \alpha$, $A \in V$, $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ | String rewriting | | 3 | FIRST($\alpha$) | Set of terminals that begin strings derivable from $\alpha$ | Used in LL(1) | | 4 | FOLLOW($A$) | Set of terminals that can immediately follow $A$ in some sentential form | Used in LL(1) | | 5 | LL(1) Predict Set | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ if $\epsilon \notin \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$; else $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cup \text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Table entry $M[A, a]$ | | 6 | Ambigous Grammar | A grammar where some string has two distinct parse trees | KTU warning topic | | 7 | DFA States | $n$ states, alphabet $\Sigma$ | Transitions $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | | 8 | NFA to DFA | Subset construction: up to $2^{n}$ subsets | Worst case | | 9 | Three-Address Op | $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ | 3 operands max | | 10 | Left Recursion Elimination | $A \rightarrow A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta$ becomes $A \rightarrow \beta A'$, $A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \;\vert\; \epsilon$ | Top-down prep | | 11 | Left Factoring | $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_{1} \;\vert\; \alpha\beta_{2}$ becomes $A \rightarrow \alpha A'$, $A' \rightarrow \beta_{1} \;\vert\; \beta_{2}$ | Top-down prep | | 12 | LR(0) Item | Production with dot: $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta$ | Kernel + closure | | 13 | SLR Conflict | Shift/Reduce when item + FOLLOW conflict | Use CLR to resolve | | 14 | S-Attributed | Attributes evaluated in a **single bottom-up pass** (post-order) | Synthesized only | | 15 | L-Attributed | Attributes evaluated in a **depth-first left-to-right** traversal | Includes inherited | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The front-end/back-end split is the engineering reason **GCC, LLVM, Clang, and MSVC** can support dozens of source languages (C, C++, Rust, Go, Swift, Fortran) targeting dozens of ISAs (x86, ARM, RISC-V, MIPS, GPU). The same LLVM IR is consumed by all back-end code generators. Without the disciplined front-end design you are studying in this module, modern retargetable compiler infrastructure would be impossible. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section walks through **four classical derivations** that the KTU examiner expects a 2024-scheme student to perform fluently. Every algebraic and logical transition is written out explicitly. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Building a Parse Tree for an Arithmetic Expression **Given Grammar** (a classic KTU example): $$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$ $$T \rightarrow T \ast F \mid F$$ $$F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$$ **Input string:** `id + id * id` ### Step-by-step parse tree construction (rightmost derivation, mirrors bottom-up parse): We derive the string step by step. At each step, the **rightmost non-terminal** is replaced. $$ \begin{aligned} E &\Rightarrow E + T &&\text{[replace } E \text{ by } E + T \text{ via } E \rightarrow E + T] \\ &\Rightarrow E + T \ast F &&\text{[replace rightmost } T \text{ by } T \ast F] \\ &\Rightarrow E + T \ast \text{id} &&\text{[replace rightmost } F \text{ by id]} \\ &\Rightarrow E + F \ast \text{id} &&\text{[replace } T \text{ by } F \text{ via } T \rightarrow F]} \\ &\Rightarrow E + \text{id} \ast \text{id} &&\text{[replace } F \text{ by id]} \\ &\Rightarrow T + \text{id} \ast \text{id} &&\text{[replace } E \text{ by } T \text{ via } E \rightarrow T]} \\ &\Rightarrow F + \text{id} \ast \text{id} &&\text{[replace } T \text{ by } F]} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} \ast \text{id} &&\text{[replace } F \text{ by id]} \end{aligned} $$ The corresponding **parse tree** has root $E$, with children $E$, $+$, $T$ from the first production. The structure correctly enforces `*` binding tighter than `+`, matching operator precedence. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing FIRST and FOLLOW Sets **Given Grammar:** $$S \rightarrow A\,B$$ $$A \rightarrow a\,A \mid \epsilon$$ $$B \rightarrow b\,B \mid \epsilon$$ ### Computing FIRST sets: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FIRST}(aA) &= \{a\} \\ \text{FIRST}(\epsilon) &= \{\epsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(A) &= \text{FIRST}(aA) \cup \text{FIRST}(\epsilon) = \{a, \epsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(bB) &= \{b\} \\ \text{FIRST}(B) &= \{b, \epsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(AB) &= \text{FIRST}(A) = \{a, \epsilon\} \cup (\text{FOLLOW}(S) \text{ if } \epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(A)) \\ \text{FIRST}(S) &= \{a, b, \epsilon\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Computing FOLLOW sets: Start symbol $S$ gets `$` (end-of-input marker): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FOLLOW}(S) &= \{\$\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(A) &= \text{FIRST}(B) = \{b, \epsilon\} \\ &\quad \text{but since } \epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(B), \text{ include FOLLOW}(S) \\ &= \{b, \$\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(B) &= \text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Summary table: | Non-Terminal | FIRST | FOLLOW | |---|---|---| | $S$ | $\{a, b, \epsilon\}$ | $\{\$\}$ | | $A$ | $\{a, \epsilon\}$ | $\{b, \$\}$ | | $B$ | $\{b, \epsilon\}$ | $\{\$\}$ | --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Building the LL(1) Predictive Parsing Table Using the rule: $$M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha \quad \text{if } a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$$ $$M[A, b] = A \rightarrow \epsilon \quad \text{if } b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A) \text{ and } \epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$$ Apply to each production: $$ \begin{aligned} S \rightarrow AB &: M[S, a] = S \rightarrow AB,\; M[S, b] = S \rightarrow AB,\; M[S, \$] = S \rightarrow AB \\ A \rightarrow aA &: M[A, a] = A \rightarrow aA \\ A \rightarrow \epsilon &: M[A, b] = A \rightarrow \epsilon,\; M[A, \$] = A \rightarrow \epsilon \\ B \rightarrow bB &: M[B, b] = B \rightarrow bB \\ B \rightarrow \epsilon &: M[B, \$] = B \rightarrow \epsilon \end{aligned} $$ ### Final Parsing Table: | | **a** | **b** | **\$** | |---|---|---|---| | **S** | $S \rightarrow AB$ | $S \rightarrow AB$ | $S \rightarrow AB$ | | **A** | $A \rightarrow aA$ | $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ | $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ | | **B** | — | $B \rightarrow bB$ | $B \rightarrow \epsilon$ | > [!NOTE] > **No cell has multiple entries ⇒ the grammar is LL(1) and a deterministic predictive parser can be built.** This is the standard 14-mark format KTU examiners use to test LL(1) construction. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Generating Three-Address Code (TAC) **Input expression:** `x = a + b * c - d` Using standard TAC-generation templates for the grammar above, with the canonical translation scheme (S-attributed): $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{T1} = b \ast c \\ &\text{T2} = a + \text{T1} \\ &\text{T3} = \text{T2} - d \\ &x = \text{T3} \end{aligned} $$ ### Equivalent Quadruple Representation: | # | Op | Arg1 | Arg2 | Result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | `*` | `b` | `c` | `T1` | | 1 | `+` | `a` | `T1` | `T2` | | 2 | `-` | `T2` | `d` | `T3` | | 3 | `=` | `T3` | — | `x` | Each row is an instruction in the IR. The front end's final deliverable. The back end will later optimize and translate these quadruples into target assembly. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Mastery Matrix — Front-End Skill Map for KTU 2024 | Skill | Blooms Level | Marks Weight (Typical) | Cross-Linked Phase | |---|---|---|---| | Define Token, Lexeme, Pattern | Remember | 2–3 | Lexical | | Construct DFA from Regular Expression | Apply | 7–14 | Lexical | | Compute FIRST and FOLLOW | Apply | 7 | Syntax | | Eliminate Left Recursion / Factor | Apply | 7 | Syntax | | Build LL(1) Parsing Table | Analyze | 14 | Syntax | | Construct LR(0) / SLR Item Sets | Analyze | 14 | Syntax | | Build Annotated Parse Tree | Apply | 7 | Semantic | | Type Checking Rules | Apply | 3–7 | Semantic | | Generate TAC / Quadruples | Apply | 7–14 | IR | | Discuss Front-End / Back-End split | Understand | 3–7 | All | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Compiler as a Whole (Front End Highlighted) The block-level architecture of a complete compiler. The **Front End** is the left-hand chain ending at the Intermediate Representation. The **Back End** is the right-hand chain. ```mermaid flowchart LR src["Source Program"] --> LA["Lexical Analyzer"] LA -->|Token Stream| SA["Syntax Analyzer"] SA -->|Parse Tree| SeA["Semantic Analyzer"] SeA -->|AST + Info| ICG["Intermediate Code Generator"] ICG --> IR["Intermediate Representation"] IR --> OPT["Optimizer"] OPT --> OIR["Optimized IR"] OIR --> TCG["Target Code Generator"] TCG --> TGT["Target Machine Code"] SYM[(Symbol Table)] -.-> LA SYM -.-> SA SYM -.-> SeA SYM -.-> ICG ELOG["Error Handler"] -.-> LA ELOG -.-> SA ELOG -.-> SeA classDef fe fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000; classDef be fill:#fde9d9,stroke:#a8580b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000; classDef aux fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#555,stroke-dasharray:5 5,color:#000; class LA,SA,SeA,ICG,IR fe; class OPT,OIR,TCG,TGT be; class SYM,ELOG aux; ``` **Reading guide:** - **Blue nodes** = Front End (Module 1 territory). - **Orange nodes** = Back End (later modules). - **Grey dashed nodes** = Cross-cutting infrastructure used by all four front-end phases. --- ## 4.2 The Front End Expanded (Internal Data Flow) This is the diagram examiners expect you to draw when asked "Explain the front end of a compiler." Memorize its structure and the direction of arrows. ```mermaid flowchart TB src["Source Program Text"] -->|character stream| SCAN["Lexical Analyzer"] SCAN -->|tokens| PARSE["Syntax Analyzer"] PARSE -->|parse tree / AST| SEM["Semantic Analyzer"] SEM -->|annotated AST| TACG["Intermediate Code Generator"] TACG -->|three address code| TACO["Three Address Code Output"] SYMTAB[(Symbol Table)] --- SCAN SYMTAB --- PARSE SYMTAB --- SEM SYMTAB --- TACG ERR["Error Recovery Module"] --- SCAN ERR --- PARSE ERR --- SEM classDef phase fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000; classDef data fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000; classDef aux fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#666,color:#000; class SCAN,PARSE,SEM,TACG phase; class src,TACO data; class SYMTAB,ERR aux; ``` --- ## 4.3 Token Stream as a Pipeline between Lexer and Parser ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant SRC as Source Code participant LEX as Lexical Analyzer participant TBL as Symbol Table participant PAR as Syntax Analyzer SRC->>LEX: char stream (a = b + 32) LEX->>TBL: insert id "a", id "b" LEX->>PAR: (id,a) (=) (id,b) (+) (num,32) PAR-->>LEX: request next token on demand Note over LEX,PAR: Parser is the "client" (pull),
Lexer is the "server" (push/pull hybrid) ``` This sequence reflects the **client-server model** of modern compilers: the parser is the *driver*, asking the lexer for the next token on demand. This is a common viva question. --- ## 4.4 Symbol-Table Lifecycle across the Front End ```mermaid flowchart LR L["Lexical Phase"] -->|insert identifiers| ST[(Symbol Table)] P["Syntax Phase"] -->|lookups during parsing| ST S["Semantic Phase"] -->|type & scope resolution| ST T["TAC Phase"] -->|addr-of temporaries & vars| ST classDef phase fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000; classDef store fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px; class L,P,S,T phase; class ST store; ``` The symbol table is **shared mutable state** across all four front-end phases. Errors caught at each phase are reported via the same error-handling channel. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]` **What are the differences between a token, a pattern, and a lexeme? Illustrate with an example.** **Model Answer (expected length: ~6–8 lines):** - **Token** is a *pair* consisting of a token name and an optional attribute value. Token names are the symbols used by the parser (e.g., `id`, `relop`, `number`). - **Pattern** is a *rule* (usually a regular expression) describing the set of character sequences (lexemes) that can represent a particular token. - **Lexeme** is the *actual sequence of characters* in the source program that matches the pattern for a token. **Example:** For the C statement `pi = 3.1416`, - Lexeme `pi` → token `` (1 is the symbol-table entry). - Lexeme `=` → token ``. - Lexeme `3.1416` → token ``. - Pattern for `id` is typically the regular expression `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*`. --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **List the four phases of the compiler front end and state the output of each phase.** **Model Answer:** | # | Phase | Output | |---|---|---| | 1 | Lexical Analysis | Stream of tokens | | 2 | Syntax Analysis | Parse tree / Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) | | 3 | Semantic Analysis | Annotated AST with type and scope information | | 4 | Intermediate Code Generation | Three-Address Code / Quadruples | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, a 14-mark Module 1 question has two sub-parts: **7 + 7 marks**. Below are two complete alternative 14-mark question sets; the student answers either **Q-A** or **Q-B**. --- ### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(a) [7 marks]** For the grammar: $$S \rightarrow a\,B\,D\,h$$ $$B \rightarrow b\,B \mid c$$ $$D \rightarrow EF$$ $$E \rightarrow e \mid \epsilon$$ $$F \rightarrow f \mid \epsilon$$ Compute **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** for all non-terminals. Is the grammar LL(1)? Justify. #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1 — FIRST sets:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FIRST}(bB) &= \{b\} \\ \text{FIRST}(c) &= \{c\} \\ \text{FIRST}(B) &= \{b, c\} \\ \text{FIRST}(e) &= \{e\}, \quad \text{FIRST}(\epsilon) = \{\epsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(E) &= \{e, \epsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(f) &= \{f\}, \quad \text{FIRST}(\epsilon) = \{\epsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(F) &= \{f, \epsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(EF) &= \text{FIRST}(E) \cup (\text{FIRST}(F) \text{ if } \epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E)) \\ &= \{e, f, \epsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(BD) &= \text{FIRST}(B) = \{b, c\} \\ \text{FIRST}(aBDh) &= \{a\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — FOLLOW sets:** $S$ is the start symbol, so add `\$`. For $B$: it is followed by $D$; FIRST($D$) = FIRST($EF$) = $\{e, f, \epsilon\}$. - Add $\{e, f\}$. - Since $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(D)$, add FOLLOW($S$) = $\{\$\}$. - $\Rightarrow \text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{e, f, \$\}$. For $D$: it is followed by $h$; so $h$ is added; $h$ is not a non-terminal, no recursion. - $\text{FOLLOW}(D) = \{h\}$. For $E$: it is followed by $F$; FIRST($F$) = $\{f, \epsilon\}$. - Add $\{f\}$; if $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(F)$ add FOLLOW($D$) = $\{h\}$. - $\Rightarrow \text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{f, h\}$. For $F$: followed by FOLLOW($D$) = $\{h\}$. - $\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{h\}$. **Step 3 — Summary Table:** | NT | FIRST | FOLLOW | |---|---|---| | $S$ | $\{a\}$ | $\{\$\}$ | | $B$ | $\{b, c\}$ | $\{e, f, \$\}$ | | $D$ | $\{e, f, \epsilon\}$ | $\{h\}$ | | $E$ | $\{e, \epsilon\}$ | $\{f, h\}$ | | $F$ | $\{f, \epsilon\}$ | $\{h\}$ | **Step 4 — LL(1) Check:** All productions with the same LHS have *disjoint* select sets. For example, $B$'s two productions use disjoint sets $\{b\}$ and $\{c\}$. $E$'s productions use $\{e\}$ and $\{f, h\}$ (disjoint because $e \notin \{f, h\}$). Similarly $F$ is fine. **Yes, the grammar is LL(1).** ✓ --- **(b) [7 marks]** Construct the **LL(1) predictive parsing table** for the above grammar and show the parsing of the input string `acch`. #### Model Solution for (b): **Parsing table (only non-empty cells shown):** | | **a** | **b** | **c** | **e** | **f** | **h** | **\$** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **S** | $S \rightarrow aBDh$ | — | — | — | — | — | — | | **B** | — | $B \rightarrow bB$ | $B \rightarrow c$ | — | — | — | — | | **D** | — | — | — | $D \rightarrow EF$ | $D \rightarrow EF$ | $D \rightarrow \epsilon$ | — | | **E** | — | — | — | $E \rightarrow e$ | $E \rightarrow \epsilon$ | $E \rightarrow \epsilon$ | — | | **F** | — | — | — | — | $F \rightarrow f$ | $F \rightarrow \epsilon$ | — | **Parsing of `acch`:** | Stack | Input | Action | |---|---|---| | $S\$$ | `acch$` | Output $S \rightarrow aBDh$ | | $aBDh\$$ | `acch$` | Match `a` | | $BDh\$$ | `cch$` | Output $B \rightarrow c$ | | $cDh\$$ | `cch$` | Match `c` | | $Dh\$$ | `ch$` | Output $D \rightarrow EF$ | | $EFh\$$ | `ch$` | Output $E \rightarrow \epsilon$ | | $Fh\$$ | `ch$` | Output $F \rightarrow \epsilon$ | | $h\$$ | `ch$` | **ERROR — `h` ≠ `c`** (input malformed) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Internal Choice Question A:** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by: > 1. **Forgetting to add `\$` to FOLLOW(S)** at the start. The end-marker must always be in FOLLOW of the start symbol. > 2. **Propagating FOLLOW only one level deep.** When $A \Rightarrow^* \epsilon$, FOLLOW must be propagated to all non-terminals that can appear immediately before $A$ in any production — not just the immediate predecessor. > 3. **Reporting "LL(1)" without showing disjointness** of the select sets. The justification *is* the marks. --- ### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Retest 2024]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the role of the **Symbol Table** in each phase of the compiler front end. What are the typical data items stored per identifier? #### Model Solution for (a): The **Symbol Table** is a shared data structure that records information about every identifier (and, in some designs, literals, labels, and temporaries) encountered during compilation. Each entry — often called a *symbol-table record* — typically stores: | Data Item | Purpose | Used in Phase | |---|---|---| | Lexeme (name) | Unique identifier for lookup | Lexical, all | | Token class | `id`, `keyword`, `const` | Lexical | | Type | `int`, `float`, `array(10) of char`, etc. | Semantic | | Scope / nesting depth | Which block owns it | Semantic | | Memory location / offset | Where to place it in activation record | IR / Back end | | Number of dimensions & bounds | For arrays | Semantic | | Parameter list & mode | For functions | Semantic | | Line numbers of references | For error reporting & debugging | Lexical | **Role per phase:** - **Lexical:** Inserts a new entry on first sighting of an identifier. Sets lexeme and token class. - **Syntax:** Looks up identifiers to confirm declarations or to bind references. Used to enforce context-free constraints like declaration-before-use (when such a constraint is not in the grammar). - **Semantic:** Performs the *bulk* of lookups — type checking, scope resolution, function-arity matching, and inference. - **TAC Generation:** Resolves references to addresses/offsets; assigns fresh temporary names. **Implementation strategies (viva-favourite):** linear list (simple, slow), hash table (typical), balanced BST (ordered traversal), or nested symbol tables (chained) for proper scope handling. --- **(b) [7 marks]** Generate **Three-Address Code (TAC)** and the corresponding **quadruple table** for the expression: $$a = -b \ast (c + d) - e$$ #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Apply the standard translation scheme** (S-attributed definitions; assume `uminus` is unary minus): $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{T1} = \text{uminus}\, b &&\text{— negate } b \\ &\text{T2} = c + d &&\text{— inner sum} \\ &\text{T3} = \text{T1} \ast \text{T2} &&\text{— multiplication} \\ &\text{T4} = \text{T3} - e &&\text{— final subtraction} \\ &a = \text{T4} &&\text{— assignment} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Quadruple Table:** | # | Op | Arg1 | Arg2 | Result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | `uminus` | `b` | — | `T1` | | 1 | `+` | `c` | `d` | `T2` | | 2 | `*` | `T1` | `T2` | `T3` | | 3 | `-` | `T3` | `e` | `T4` | | 4 | `=` | `T4` | — | `a` | > [!NOTE] > **Equivalent Triple Form** (index references instead of temporaries — KTU often asks this too): > > | # | Op | Arg1 | Arg2 | > |---|---|---|---| > | (0) | `uminus` | `b` | — | > | (1) | `+` | `c` | `d` | > | (2) | `*` | (0) | (1) | > | (3) | `-` | (2) | `e` | > | (4) | `=` | (3) | `a` | > > Triples save space on temporaries but complicate optimization because moving an instruction invalidates later indices — hence **indirect triples** are used in practice. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B (TAC):** > 1. **Operator precedence trap:** `-b * (c+d) - e` is parsed as `(((-b)*(c+d))-e)`. Students frequently mis-group it as `(-(b*(c+d)))-e` or `-(b*((c+d)-e))`, losing 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting unary minus as an op:** `uminus` (or some use `neg`) must appear as its own row with a blank Arg2. KTU graders dock marks for incorrectly using `-` as binary. > 3. **Mixing temporaries and triples in one answer:** Submit *either* quadruples *or* triples consistently; do not switch notation mid-table. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 1 — The Front End: 30-Second Rapid-Recall Sheet** - ☐ The **front end** = Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → IR phases. It is **machine-independent** and **source-language-dependent**. - ☐ **Token** = (name, attribute); **Pattern** = rule (regex); **Lexeme** = actual matched text. - ☐ Lexer is implemented as a **DFA**, often auto-generated from regex via Lex/Flex. - ☐ Parser consumes a token stream and outputs a **parse tree** (concrete) or **AST** (abstract). - ☐ A **CFG** is $(V, T, P, S)$. Use it to define the language syntax. - ☐ **FIRST($\alpha$)** = set of terminals that can begin a string derived from $\alpha$. - ☐ **FOLLOW($A$)** = set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of $A$ in some sentential form; always include `\$` in FOLLOW of the start symbol. - ☐ **LL(1) grammar check:** for each non-terminal, the *select sets* of its productions must be pairwise **disjoint**. - ☐ **Top-down parsers** use leftmost derivation; **bottom-up parsers** use rightmost derivation in reverse (reductions). - ☐ **Left recursion must be eliminated** before predictive parsing; use $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$ transformation. - ☐ **Left factoring** resolves common prefixes: $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \;\Rightarrow\; A \rightarrow \alpha A', A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$. - ☐ **LR parsers** (LR(0), SLR, CLR, LALR) use items $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta$, kernel + closure, and the goto graph; conflicts are shift/reduce or reduce/reduce. - ☐ **Semantic analysis** = type checking + scope resolution; **S-attributed** = synthesized only (one bottom-up pass); **L-attributed** = synthesized + controlled inherited (DFS left-to-right). - ☐ **Three-Address Code** has at most 3 operands; represented as **quadruples**, **triples**, or **indirect triples**. - ☐ The **symbol table** is a *shared* data structure used by all four front-end phases; it stores name, type, scope, location, and line numbers. - ☐ **Errors** are reported through a dedicated handler that interacts with all front-end phases. - ☐ The **boundary** between front end and back end is the **Intermediate Representation**. - ☐ The front-end/back-end split enables **retargeting** (one front end, many back ends) — a core design principle of GCC and LLVM. - ☐ Always show **disjointness proof** for LL(1) — the proof *is* the marks. - ☐ TAC generation must respect **operator precedence and associativity** of the source language. - ☐ Use `\$` consistently as the end-of-input sentinel in parse tables and derivations.

topics.title: The Optimizer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Optimizer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Code Optimizer?

A **Code Optimizer** is a phase of a compiler that attempts to improve the *intermediate code* (or sometimes the target machine code) so that the resulting program runs **faster, consumes less memory, and/or uses less power** — while still producing the same output (semantics-preserving) as the unoptimized version.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Aho-Sethi-Ullman style):**
> *"Code optimization is a program transformation that attempts to improve the intermediate code by making it consume fewer resources (time and space) without changing the program's observable behaviour."*

The optimizer sits between the **Intermediate Code Generator** and the **Code Generator** in the back-end of a compiler.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Editing a Rough Draft"

Imagine you wrote a long essay (the intermediate code). Your first draft contains:
- Repeated sentences (redundant computations)
- Long-winded phrases (inefficient expressions)
- Irrelevant paragraphs (dead code)
- Unnecessary wordy constructs (high-cost operations)

A good editor (the **Optimizer**) reads the draft and:
- Removes duplicates
- Replaces wordy sentences with crisp ones
- Deletes irrelevant sections
- Restructures paragraphs for better flow (loop transformations)

The **meaning** of the essay is preserved — only its *form* improves. That is exactly what a compiler optimizer does for your program.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Optimizations

Optimizations are broadly classified along two axes:

| Axis | Category | Description |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Scope** | Local | Within a single basic block |
| **Scope** | Global | Across multiple basic blocks (whole function) |
| **Scope** | Loop-level | Applied to loops (most impactful) |
| **Machine** | Machine-Dependent | Uses CPU registers, pipelines, instruction counts |
| **Machine** | Machine-Independent | Works on IR; e.g., strength reduction, CSE |
| **When** | Peephole | Inspects a small sliding window of instructions |
| **When** | Source-level | Done at AST/IR level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus (Module 1 / 2):**
> The primary focus is *Machine-Independent Optimizations* — specifically **Basic Blocks, Flow Graphs, and DAG-based local optimization**, plus an introduction to **Peephole Optimization**.

---

## 1.4 The Optimization Opportunity — "95/5 Rule"

It is an empirical fact in program execution that:

> **~90–95% of a program's execution time is spent in only ~5% of the code** (typically inner loops).

This is the **"90/10"** or **"95/5"** rule. The optimizer — especially the *loop optimizer* — targets this hot 5% for the largest gains. This is why loop optimizations (strength reduction, code motion, induction variable elimination) are considered the most valuable.

---

## 1.5 Criteria for a Valid Optimization

For any transformation to be admissible, the optimizer **must guarantee**:

1. **Semantic Preservation** — Output must remain identical for all legal inputs.
2. **Termination** — The optimizer itself must always halt.
3. **Profitability** — The transformed code must actually be faster/smaller (no regressions).

> [!WARNING]
> A transformation that *might* improve the program but is *not provably safe* is **rejected** by a production-grade optimizer. Safety before speed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Principal Sources of Optimization

These are the *classic* transformation categories every KTU student must memorize. They are the "tools in the toolbox" of the optimizer.

### 2.1.1 Function-Preserving (Local / Peephole) Transformations
These do not change the program's *meaning* and operate on small windows of code.

1. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**
   - A computation that was performed earlier is reused.
   - Example: `a = b * c; d = a + b * c;` → `t1 = b * c; a = t1; d = a + t1;`
2. **Copy Propagation**
   - If `x = y`, then use `y` in place of `x` (after the copy).
3. **Dead-Code Elimination (DCE)**
   - Statements whose results are never used are removed.
4. **Constant Folding & Propagation**
   - `2 * 3` → `6` at compile time; then propagate the constant `6` to all uses.
5. **Strength Reduction**
   - Replace an expensive operator with a cheaper one.
   - `x * 2` → `x + x` ; `x / 8` → `x >> 3` ; `x^2` → `x * x`.
6. **Algebraic Identities**
   - `x + 0 = x`, `x * 1 = x`, `x - x = 0`, `x & 0 = 0`.

### 2.1.2 Loop Optimizations (Most Impactful)
1. **Code Motion (Loop-Invariant Code Motion — LICM)**
   - Move computations that produce the same result in every iteration *outside* the loop.
2. **Induction-Variable Elimination**
   - Variables that are linear functions of the loop index can be removed by using a single counter.
3. **Strength Reduction inside loops**
   - Replace array-index multiplications with additions (incremental addressing).
4. **Loop Unrolling & Loop Jamming**
   - Replicate the loop body or fuse adjacent loops to reduce branch overhead.

### 2.1.3 Global Optimizations
1. **Global CSE** — across basic blocks using data-flow analysis (Available Expressions).
2. **Global Constant Propagation**.
3. **Live-Variable Analysis** for DCE.
4. **Dead-Store Elimination**.

---

## 2.2 The Two Foundational Structures

### 2.2.1 Basic Block
> A **Basic Block** is a *maximal* sequence of consecutive three-address instructions in which:
> - **Flow of control enters only at the first instruction**, and
> - **Flow of control leaves only at the last instruction** (no branching in the middle, no mid-block branch targets).

A basic block is a *straight-line* piece of code with a single entry and a single exit.

### 2.2.2 Flow Graph
> A **Flow Graph** is a directed graph $G = (N, E)$ whose:
> - **Nodes $N$** = basic blocks of the procedure.
> - **Edges $E$** = possible transfer of control from one block to another.
> - A distinguished **Entry node** (or *Leader* of the function).

---

## 2.3 The DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) — Local Optimizer's Engine

A DAG is a data structure that encodes *both* the computations *and* the data dependencies of a basic block. It is the workhorse behind **local CSE, DCE, constant folding, and algebraic identity removal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a DAG and not a tree?**
> In a tree, identical sub-expressions are represented by distinct sub-trees. In a DAG, they **share a single node**, making common subexpressions trivially detectable.

### 2.3.1 DAG Node Labels

Every node in a DAG is labelled with an **operator** (`+`, `-`, `*`, etc.). Leaves are labelled with **operand names** (variables or constants) and are numbered (so that variable re-definitions get distinct leaves).

### 2.3.2 DAG Node Types (Kuche & Ravi Sethi classification)
1. **Leaf nodes** (0, 1, or 2 depending on the operator): labelled with operand names.
2. **Interior nodes**: labelled with an operator.
3. Every node carries an **attached identifier list** — the names whose *current* value is the value computed at that node.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | Basic Block Cost | $cost(B) = \sum_{i \in I} cost(i)$ where $I$ = instructions in $B$ | Used to compare versions after optimization |
| 2 | Speedup | $S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | Must be $\ge 1$ for a win |
| 3 | DAG Node Creation Rule (operator `op` with left leaf $l$, right leaf $r$) | Search for existing node with same label, same children. If found, reuse; else create. | Implements CSE |
| 4 | Strength Reduction (powers of 2) | $x \ll k \equiv x \cdot 2^{k}$ ; $x \gg k \equiv x / 2^{k}$ | Bit-shifts are O(1) on most CPUs |
| 5 | LICM Profit Condition | A computation $a = b \text{ op } c$ is loop-invariant if `b` and `c` are constant or have reaching definitions *outside* the loop body | Must also be **safe** (no exceptions, no aliases) |
| 6 | Induction Variable Form | $x = a \cdot i + c$ for loop index $i$ | Linear in $i$ |
| 7 | Loop Trip Count | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{end - start}{step} \right\rceil$ | Used in unroll decisions |
| 8 | Peephole Size | typically 2–6 instructions | Small sliding window |
| 9 | Flow Graph Edge Weight | $freq(B \to D)$ — estimated or profiled execution count | Used in code placement |
| 10 | Constant Folding | $E[\text{const} \text{ op} \text{ const}] \to \text{const}$ | Compile-time evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Modern compilers (GCC with `-O2`/`-O3`, LLVM, MSVC) implement *all* of the above. `clang -O3` can deliver speedups of **2× to 10×** versus `-O0` on numerical code — and almost all of it comes from loop + inlining transformations.

---

## 2.5 Optimization "Pipeline" — When Do Optimizations Run?

A production compiler interleaves analyses and transformations:

1. **Local pass** — DAG over each basic block.
2. **Intra-procedural pass** — data-flow analysis over the function.
3. **Inter-procedural pass** — across function boundaries (inlining, IPA).
4. **Loop pass** — LICM, vectorization, unrolling.
5. **Peephole pass** — at the very end, on machine instructions.

Each pass is followed by **verification** (using the IR's reference semantics) in debug builds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm — Partitioning Three-Address Code into Basic Blocks

This is a **guaranteed KTU question**. Memorize the algorithm.

**Input:** A sequence of three-address instructions $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n$.
**Output:** A list of basic blocks where each block starts with a *leader* and ends just before the next leader.

### Leaders — The Three Rules
1. The **first instruction** in the procedure is a leader.
2. The **target** of a conditional or unconditional jump is a leader.
3. The **instruction immediately following** a conditional or unconditional jump is a leader.

### Algorithm (by determining leaders)
```
1.  Determine the set of leaders.
2.  For each leader L_i, the basic block B_i consists of
    L_i and all instructions from L_i up to (but not including)
    the next leader L_{i+1}, or up to the end of the procedure.
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Basic Block Identification

**Source (Three-Address Code):**
```
(1)  t1 = 2
(2)  i  = 1
(3)  L1: if i > 100 goto L2
(4)  t2 = t1 * i
(5)  t3 = t1 + t2
(6)  t1 = t3
(7)  i  = i + 1
(8)  goto L1
(9)  L2:
```

### Step-by-Step Application of Leader Rules

| # | Instruction | Reason it is (or is not) a Leader |
|---|------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | `t1 = 2` | **Rule 1** — first instruction → leader |
| 2 | `i = 1` | Not a leader (no jump targets) |
| 3 | `L1: if i > 100 goto L2` | **Rule 2** — target of jumps from (8) and (3) → leader (also contains a label) |
| 4 | `t2 = t1 * i` | Not a leader |
| 5 | `t3 = t1 + t2` | Not a leader |
| 6 | `t1 = t3` | Not a leader |
| 7 | `i = i + 1` | **Rule 3** — immediately follows the conditional jump in (3) → leader |
| 8 | `goto L1` | Not a leader |
| 9 | `L2:` | **Rule 2** — jump target from (3) → leader |

### Resulting Basic Blocks
- **B1** = `{ (1), (2) }`
- **B2** = `{ (3) }`
- **B3** = `{ (4), (5), (6), (7), (8) }`
- **B4** = `{ (9) }`

### Resulting Flow Graph
- B1 → B2
- B2 → B3  (fall-through, no jump)
- B2 → B4  (jump on `i > 100`)
- B3 → B2  (jump on `goto L1`)
- B4 is the exit

---

## 3.3 DAG Construction Algorithm (for a single basic block)

This is a **classic 14-mark KTU question**. The three-address instructions are processed in order, with three possible actions per instruction.

### Inputs / Data Structures
- A set of **nodes** `Node(id, label, left, right, additional)`.
- A function `node(x)` that returns the *most-recently created* node whose attached id list contains `x`. If `x` is in a leaf, return the leaf. If absent, create a leaf.

### The Three Cases (per instruction)

**Case 1 — `x = op y, z` (binary, e.g., `a = b + c`)**
1. Get $n_1 = \text{node}(b)$, $n_2 = \text{node}(c)$.
2. Search for an existing node $n$ with `n.label = op`, `n.left = n_1`, `n.right = n_2`. If found → **CSE**: reuse $n$. If not → **create** a new interior node $n$.
3. Append `x` to `n.ids` (so `x` now holds the value of $n$).
4. Remove `x` from any *other* node's id list (variable redefinition).

**Case 2 — `x = op y` (unary, e.g., `a = -b`)**
Same as Case 1, but with `n_2 = null`. Search for existing node with same op, same child.

**Case 3 — `x = y` (copy)**
- Append `x` to the id list of `node(y)`.

### Special Sub-Rules
- **Constant folding:** if `y` and `z` are both constants and the operator is +, -, *, /, &, |, then evaluate and create a leaf for the result.
- **Algebraic identities:** detect `x + 0`, `x * 1`, `x - 0`, `x / 1`, `x * 0` and reuse the operand's node directly.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Full DAG Construction

**Three-Address Code of a basic block:**
```
t1 = a + b
t2 = a + b
t3 = t1 * t2
t4 = t1 * c
t5 = t3 + t4
t6 = b + c
t7 = t1 * t6
t8 = t5 + t7
t9 = t1 * t6      // ← same as t7? Already in DAG
t10 = t8 - t9
```

### Step-by-Step (showing the reasoning for each instruction)

| Step | Instruction | Action | Result in DAG |
|------|-------------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | `t1 = a + b` | No node for `+` with leaves `a`, `b` exists. **Create node n1** with label `+`, children leaves `a`, `b`. Attach `t1`. | `n1 = (a + b)` |
| 2 | `t2 = a + b` | Node n1 already has label `+` with children `a`, `b`. **CSE → reuse n1**. Attach `t2` to n1. | `n1` now holds `t1`, `t2` |
| 3 | `t3 = t1 * t2` | Both `t1` and `t2` map to n1. No existing `*` node with both children = n1. **Create n2** label `*`, children n1, n1. Attach `t3`. | `n2 = n1 * n1` |
| 4 | `t4 = t1 * c` | `t1` → n1; `c` is a fresh leaf. No existing `*` with n1, c. **Create n3** label `*`, children n1, c. Attach `t4`. | `n3 = n1 * c` |
| 5 | `t5 = t3 + t4` | `t3` → n2, `t4` → n3. Create n4. | `n4 = n2 + n3` |
| 6 | `t6 = b + c` | `b` and `c` are fresh (well, c was used but b+c is new). No existing + node with these leaves. **Create n5**. | `n5 = b + c` |
| 7 | `t7 = t1 * t6` | `t1` → n1, `t6` → n5. No existing `*` (n1, n5). **Create n6**. | `n6 = n1 * n5` |
| 8 | `t8 = t5 + t7` | `t5` → n4, `t7` → n6. Create n7. | `n7 = n4 + n6` |
| 9 | `t9 = t1 * t6` | Already exists as **n6**! **Reuse n6** (CSE). Attach `t9` to n6. | `n6` now holds `t7, t9` |
| 10 | `t10 = t8 - t9` | `t8` → n7, `t9` → n6. Create n8. | `n8 = n7 - n6` |

### Reading the Optimized Code from the DAG
Apply the nodes in **post-order (children first)**. Nodes that are never referenced by a live output are **dropped** (dead code).

```c
n1: t1 = a + b          // (n2, n3, n4, n6, n7, n8 all depend on n1 → LIVE)
n2: t3 = t1 * t1        // (n4 depends on n2)         → LIVE
n3: t4 = t1 * c         // (n4 depends on n3)         → LIVE
n4: t5 = t3 + t4        // (n7 depends on n4)         → LIVE
n5: t6 = b + c          // (n6 depends on n5)         → LIVE
n6: t7 = t1 * t6        // (n7 depends on n6)         → LIVE
                          // (n9 reuses n6 — CSE wins!)
n7: t8 = t5 + t7        // (n8 depends on n7)         → LIVE
n8: t10 = t8 - t7       // (t9 is gone, reuse t7)     → LIVE
```

### Result — Optimization Wins Demonstrated
- **CSE on line 2:** `t2 = a + b` removed → t2 no longer appears.
- **CSE on line 9:** `t9 = t1 * t6` removed → t9 no longer appears (replaced by `t7`).
- **Cost reduction:** from 10 instructions to **8 instructions** (a 20% reduction *in this block*).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Where Each Optimization Lives

| Optimization | Scope | Data-Structure Used | Risk Class | KTU Weight |
|-------------|-------|--------------------|------------|-----------|
| Constant Folding | Local | DAG / AST | Zero risk | High |
| CSE | Local / Global | DAG (local) / Avail-Expr (global) | Low (must check availability) | High |
| DCE | Local / Global | Use-Def / Live-Var | Low | Medium |
| Strength Reduction | Local / Loop | Algebra | Medium (overflow concerns) | High |
| LICM | Loop | Loop nesting tree | Medium (aliasing, side-effects) | High |
| Peephole | Machine | Sliding window | Low | Medium |
| Copy Propagation | Global | Copy graph | Low | Medium |
| Induction-Var Elim | Loop | Induction table | Medium | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Optimizer Inside the Compiler — Block Diagram

This is the "where does the optimizer sit" diagram. It is **directly testable** in KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Source Program] --> lexA[Lexical Analyzer]
    lexA --> tokA[Token Stream]
    tokA --> synA[Syntax Analyzer]
    synA --> parA[Parse Tree]
    parA --> semA[Semantic Analyzer]
    semA --> astA[Annotated AST]
    astA --> ica[Intermediate Code Generator]
    ica --> ircode[Three Address Code IR]
    ircode --> optA[OPTIMIZER]
    optA --> optir[Optimized IR]
    optir --> cga[Code Generator]
    cga --> tasm[Target Assembly]
    tasm --> syma[Symbol Table]
    syma -.-> lexA
    syma -.-> synA
    syma -.-> semA
    syma -.-> optA
    syma -.-> cga
    optA -.uses.-> err1[Error Handler]
    style optA fill:#FFD54F,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The optimizer (highlighted in gold) is *interposed* between the IR generator and the code generator. It consumes the **same** IR, produces **another** IR — the structure of the compiler is otherwise undisturbed.

---

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of the Optimizer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inA[Input IR] --> preA[Preprocessing Pass]
    preA --> bbA[Basic Block Partitioner]
    bbA --> fgA[Flow Graph Builder]
    fgA --> anaA[Analysis Pass: Data-Flow]
    anaA --> transA[Transformation Pass: Local, Global, Loop]
    transA --> phA[Peephole Optimizer]
    phA --> outA[Optimized IR]
    anaA --> verA[Verifier / Safety Check]
    verA -.feedback.-> transA
    style bbA fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style fgA fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style anaA fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style transA fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    style phA fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Flow Graph of the Worked Example (Section 3.2)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1A[B1: t1=2; i=1] --> B2A[B2: L1: if i>100 goto L2]
    B2A -- False / fall-through --> B3A[B3: t2=t1*i; t3=t1+t2; t1=t3; i=i+1; goto L1]
    B2A -- True --> B4A[B4: L2: ...]
    B3A -- goto L1 --> B2A
    style B1A fill:#C8E6C9
    style B2A fill:#FFE0B2
    style B3A fill:#FFCDD2
    style B4A fill:#BBDEFB
```

> **Reading the diagram:** B1 initializes, B2 is the loop test (forming a back-edge from B3 to B2), and B4 is the loop-exit (and program exit).

---

## 4.4 DAG Visualization (Worked Example from Section 3.4)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    leafA[a] --> n1A((n1: +))
    leafB[b] --> n1A
    leafC[c] --> n3A((n3: *))
    n1A --> n2A((n2: *))
    n1A --> n3A
    n1A --> n6A((n6: *))
    leafB --> n5A((n5: +))
    leafC --> n5A
    n5A --> n6A
    n2A --> n4A((n4: +))
    n3A --> n4A
    n4A --> n7A((n7: +))
    n6A --> n7A
    n7A --> n8A((n8: -))
    n6A --> n8A
    n1A -.holds.-> idA[t1, t2]
    n2A -.holds.-> idB[t3]
    n3A -.holds.-> idC[t4]
    n4A -.holds.-> idD[t5]
    n5A -.holds.-> idE[t6]
    n6A -.holds.-> idF[t7, t9]
    n7A -.holds.-> idG[t8]
    n8A -.holds.-> idH[t10]
    style n1A fill:#FFF59D
    style n6A fill:#FFF59D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation from the DAG:** Node `n1` is shared by `t1` and `t2` (CSE achieved). Node `n6` is shared by `t7` and `t9` (CSE achieved). This *visual sharing* is precisely the information the optimizer uses to emit cheaper code.

---

## 4.5 Peephole Optimization — The Sliding Window

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    w1[I1, I2, I3, I4, I5, I6, I7, ...] -->|slide 1| w2[I1, I2, I3, I4, I5, I6, I7, ...]
    w2 -->|slide 2| w3[I1, I2, I3, I4, I5, I6, I7, ...]
    w3 -->|slide 3| w4[I1, I2, I3, I4, I5, I6, I7, ...]
```

The peephole optimizer looks at a small window (2–6 instructions) and applies **pattern-replacement** rules:
- Redundant load/store elimination
- Strength reduction
- Constant folding
- Branch chaining
- Dead instruction removal
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**"Define code optimization. List any four principal sources of optimization."**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

Code optimization is the phase of a compiler that applies semantics-preserving transformations to the intermediate code so as to reduce execution time and/or memory consumption.

Four principal sources of optimization are:
1. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** — reuse of identical computations.
2. **Copy Propagation** — replacing a variable with its equal copy.
3. **Dead-Code Elimination (DCE)** — removal of unused computations.
4. **Strength Reduction** — replacing expensive operators by cheaper equivalents (e.g., multiply by a power of 2 → shift).

Other sources include constant folding, loop-invariant code motion, and algebraic identities.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**"Define a Basic Block. State the rules used to identify leaders of a basic block."**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

A **Basic Block** is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions in which control enters only at the first instruction and leaves only at the last — i.e., it is a straight-line piece of code with no internal branches.

The three **Leader Rules** are:
1. The first instruction of the procedure is a leader.
2. The target of a conditional or unconditional jump is a leader.
3. The instruction immediately following a conditional or unconditional jump is a leader.

A leader begins a new basic block; the block extends up to (but not including) the next leader.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU ESE Style — Module 1, Full-Layout]*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **principal sources of optimization** in detail with one example each for:
(i) Common Subexpression Elimination
(ii) Dead-Code Elimination
(iii) Strength Reduction
(iv) Constant Folding
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct the **DAG** for the following basic block of three-address code. Identify **common subexpressions**, **dead code**, and rewrite the block in optimized form. **(7 marks)**

```
d = b * c
e = a + b
b = b * c
a = e - d
f = a + b
```

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — Principal Sources of Optimization (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Optimizations are semantics-preserving transformations applied to the IR to reduce running time, space, or power.

**(i) Common Subexpression Elimination (1.5 marks):**
A common subexpression is one that was previously computed and whose operands have *not changed* since. Reuse the earlier value instead of recomputing.

*Example:*
```c
t1 = a + b;       // (1) compute once
t2 = t1 * c;      // (2) uses t1
t3 = a + b;       // (3) — same as (1), no change to a or b
t4 = t3 * c;      // (4) — same as (2)
```
Becomes:
```c
t1 = a + b;
t2 = t1 * c;
t3 = t1;          // CSE — reuse t1 instead of recomputing
t4 = t2;          // CSE — reuse t2 instead of recomputing
```

**(ii) Dead-Code Elimination (1.5 marks):**
A statement is *dead* if its computed value is never used (no subsequent reference). Such statements can be deleted safely.

*Example:*
```c
x = a + b;        // x is never used afterwards
y = a * 2;        // y is used
```
The statement `x = a + b` is dead and can be removed.

**(iii) Strength Reduction (1.5 marks):**
Replace an expensive operation by an equivalent but cheaper one. Multiplication/division by a power of 2 becomes a shift; exponentiation of small integer constants becomes a small set of multiplications.

*Example:*
```c
x = y * 8;        // expensive MUL
```
Becomes:
```c
x = y << 3;       // cheap SHL (1 cycle vs. 3+)
```

**(iv) Constant Folding (1.5 marks):**
Evaluate constant expressions at compile time.

*Example:*
```c
x = 2 + 3 * 4;    // compile-time = 14
y = x * 2;        // becomes 28
```
Result:
```c
x = 14;
y = 28;
```

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — DAG Construction (7 marks)

**Step 1: Process each instruction (3 marks for showing the steps):**

| Step | Instruction | Action | Node created | Attached IDs |
|------|-------------|--------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | `d = b * c` | No `*` node with leaves b,c. **Create n1** | `n1 = (b * c)` | `d` |
| 2 | `e = a + b` | No `+` node with leaves a,b. **Create n2** | `n2 = (a + b)` | `e` |
| 3 | `b = b * c` | `*` node with b,c already exists as **n1** → **CSE: reuse n1**; remove `b` from leaf b's id list, attach `b` to n1 | reuse `n1` | `d, b` |
| 4 | `a = e - d` | `e` → n2, `d` → n1. Create n3 | `n3 = (n2 - n1)` | `a` |
| 5 | `f = a + b` | `a` → n3, `b` → n1. **Create n4** | `n4 = (n3 + n1)` | `f` |

**Step 2: Draw the DAG (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lba[b] --> n1A((n1: *))
    lca[c] --> n1A
    laa[a] --> n2A((n2: +))
    lba --> n2A
    n2A --> n3A((n3: -))
    n1A --> n3A
    n3A --> n4A((n4: +))
    n1A --> n4A
    n1A -.holds.-> idA["d, b"]
    n2A -.holds.-> idE["e"]
    n3A -.holds.-> idAa["a"]
    n4A -.holds.-> idF["f"]
    style n1A fill:#FFF59D
```

**Step 3: Identify Optimizations (1 mark) and rewrite (1 mark):**

**Optimizations identified:**
- **CSE** on line 3: `b = b * c` reuses n1 instead of recomputing.
- **Local subexpression sharing** — n1 is shared by `d` and `b`.

**Optimized code (in post-order):**
```c
n1: d = b * c        // (used by n3 and n4)
n2: e = a + b        // (used by n3)
n3: a = e - d        // (used by n4)
n4: f = a + d        // original `b` is now `d` (n1) after redefinition
```

**Original instruction count = 5.**
**Optimized instruction count = 4.**
**Saving = 1 instruction (20%).**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Trap (a must-read):**
> 1. **Don't forget to delete the variable from its previous node's id list** when it is redefined. Forgetting this loses you 1–2 marks and gives an *incorrect* DAG.
> 2. **Don't confuse constants and identifiers** — constants are leaves, not interior nodes.
> 3. **Always check the children of a node**, not just the label. `a + b` and `a + c` are *different* nodes.
> 4. **State your optimizations explicitly** — examiners award 1 mark for *naming* the technique (CSE, DCE, etc.) and 1 mark for the *correct rewritten code*.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain **Peephole Optimization** in detail. List **at least six** common peephole transformations with an example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the three-address code below:
```
(1)  i = 1
(2)  j = 1
(3)  t1 = 10 * i
(4)  t2 = t1 + j
(5)  t3 = 10 * i
(6)  t4 = t3 + j
(7)  t5 = t4 * 2
(8)  t6 = 10 * i
(9)  t7 = t6 * j
(10) t8 = t7 + j
(11) t9 = t8 / 2
```
**(i)** Construct the **DAG** and list **local common subexpressions**.
**(ii)** Show the **optimized three-address code** after applying CSE, constant folding, and strength reduction where applicable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — Peephole Optimization (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Peephole optimization is a local transformation technique where the compiler examines a short sequence of target (or intermediate) instructions — the *peephole* (typically 2–6 instructions) — and replaces it with an equivalent, more efficient sequence.

**Six Common Transformations (1 mark each):**

**1. Redundant Load/Store Elimination:**
```
MOV  R1, a
MOV  a, R1
```
→
```
MOV  R1, a
```
(Storing `R1` into `a` and then loading it back into `R1` is redundant.)

**2. Strength Reduction:**
```
MUL  R1, R1, 2
```
→
```
SHL  R1, R1, 1
```

**3. Constant Folding:**
```
MOV  R1, 2 + 3
```
→
```
MOV  R1, 5
```

**4. Constant Propagation:**
```
MOV  R1, 5
ADD  R2, R1, 3
```
→
```
ADD  R2, 5, 3     // can then be folded to 8
```

**5. Algebraic Identities:**
```
ADD  R1, R2, 0
MUL  R1, R1, 1
```
→
```
MOV  R1, R2
```

**6. Branch Chaining / Jump-to-Jump Elimination:**
```
JMP  L1
...
L1: JMP L2
```
→
```
JMP  L2
...
L1: JMP L2
```

**7. Dead Instruction Elimination (bonus):**
An instruction whose result is overwritten before being read.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — DAG + Optimized Code (7 marks)

**(i) Construct the DAG (4 marks):**

Process each instruction:

| Step | Instruction | Action |
|------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | `i = 1` | `i` is assigned constant 1 (leaf). |
| 2 | `j = 1` | `j` is assigned constant 1 (leaf). |
| 3 | `t1 = 10 * i` | i=1, so this is a `*` node — create n1 = (10 × i). |
| 4 | `t2 = t1 + j` | Create n2 = (n1 + j). |
| 5 | `t3 = 10 * i` | **CSE → reuse n1**. Attach t3 to n1. |
| 6 | `t4 = t3 + j` | `t3` → n1, `j` is a leaf. n2 already exists with (n1, j). **CSE → reuse n2**. Attach t4 to n2. |
| 7 | `t5 = t4 * 2` | t4 → n2. Create n3 = (n2 * 2). |
| 8 | `t6 = 10 * i` | **CSE → reuse n1**. Attach t6 to n1. |
| 9 | `t7 = t6 * j` | t6 → n1, j is leaf. Create n4 = (n1 * j). |
| 10 | `t8 = t7 + j` | Create n5 = (n4 + j). |
| 11 | `t9 = t8 / 2` | t8 → n5. Create n6 = (n5 / 2). |

**The DAG:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    li1[i] --> nm1((n1: 10*i))
    lk10[10] --> nm1
    nm1 --> nm2((n2: n1+j))
    lj1[j] --> nm2
    nm2 --> nm3((n3: n2*2))
    lk2[2] --> nm3
    nm1 --> nm4((n4: n1*j))
    lj1 --> nm4
    nm4 --> nm5((n5: n4+j))
    lj1 --> nm5
    nm5 --> nm6((n6: n5/2))
    lk22[2] --> nm6
    nm1 -.holds.-> idn1[t1, t3, t6]
    nm2 -.holds.-> idn2[t2, t4]
    nm3 -.holds.-> idn3[t5]
    nm4 -.holds.-> idn4[t7]
    nm5 -.holds.-> idn5[t8]
    nm6 -.holds.-> idn6[t9]
    style nm1 fill:#FFF59D
    style nm2 fill:#FFF59D
```

**Local Common Subexpressions identified (1 mark):**
- `t1, t3, t6` all compute `10 * i` → **one** multiplication.
- `t2, t4` both compute `t1 + j` → **one** addition.
- `t4, t3, t6, t1, t2` are now **dead** (overwritten/never used as a final output).

**(ii) Optimized Code (3 marks — including named techniques):**

```c
i  = 1
j  = 1
t1 = 10 * i          // 1st use of (10*i)
t2 = t1 + j          // 1st use of (t1+j)
t5 = t2 * 2          // strength-reducible: t5 = t2 << 1
t7 = t1 * j          // (was t6, then t7 = t6*j — CSE win)
t8 = t7 + j
t9 = t8 / 2          // strength-reducible: t9 = t8 >> 1
```

**Further strength reduction:**
```c
t5 = t2 << 1
t9 = t8 >> 1
```

**Summary of wins:**
- **CSE on (10*i)** — eliminated 2 redundant multiplications.
- **CSE on (t1+j)** — eliminated 1 redundant addition.
- **Strength reduction** — 2 multiplications/divisions replaced by shifts.
- **Dead-code elimination** — t3, t4, t6 removed entirely.
- **Original: 11 instructions → Optimized: 8 instructions (27% reduction).**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Traps (Q-B):**
> 1. **Forgetting to *propagate* the value of `i = 1` into the DAG** — if `i` is a constant 1, you can fold `10 * 1` to `10` at compile time. Worth 1 extra mark.
> 2. **Not distinguishing between `t3 = 10 * i` and `t7 = t6 * j`** — different operators/operands, they are NOT the same node.
> 3. **Reporting "no optimization"** — there is *always* something to do. Even if a peephole pass yields nothing, the DAG almost always has CSE.
> 4. **Listing optimizations but not applying them** — you must *show* the rewritten code.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Scheme — Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as a 60-second pre-exam revision checklist.**

- 🔹 **Optimizer** sits between **IR generator** and **Code generator**.
- 🔹 **Goal:** reduce time / space / power **without** changing program semantics.
- 🔹 **Two broad classes:** *Machine-independent* (IR-level) and *Machine-dependent* (target-level).
- 🔹 **Three scopes:** *Local* (basic block), *Global* (whole function), *Loop-level* (most profitable).
- 🔹 **Six principal sources:** CSE, Copy-Propagation, DCE, Strength Reduction, Constant Folding, Algebraic Identities.
- 🔹 **Basic Block** = max straight-line sequence, single entry at top, single exit at bottom.
- 🔹 **3 Leader rules:** (1) first instruction, (2) jump target, (3) instruction after a jump.
- 🔹 **Flow Graph** = directed graph with basic blocks as nodes; back-edges = loops.
- 🔹 **DAG** = the canonical local-optimization data structure. Sharing a node = CSE achieved.
- 🔹 **DAG algorithm:** for each `x = y op z`, search for existing node with same op + same children; reuse if found, else create. **Always** remove `x` from any previous node's id list.
- 🔹 **Peephole:** small sliding window (2–6 instructions) of machine code; common rules are load/store elimination, strength reduction, jump-to-jump chaining.
- 🔹 **LICM** — only move a computation if (a) it is loop-invariant **and** (b) it is safe (no exceptions, no aliases).
- 🔹 **Induction variable** has the form `x = a*i + b` for loop index `i`.
- 🔹 **95/5 rule** — 95% of execution time is in 5% of the code (loops). Loop optimizations are the most impactful.
- 🔹 **Safety first, speed second** — a transformation that is not provably safe is rejected.
- 🔹 **Most-likely KTU questions:** DAG construction + leader identification + peephole transformations.

> 📌 **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "optimize using DAG", **always** show: (1) leader identification, (2) step-by-step node creation, (3) the final DAG diagram, (4) the *named* techniques you used (CSE, DCE, etc.), and (5) the rewritten three-address code in post-order. Missing any one of these is where the average student loses 2–3 marks per question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Back End
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Back End

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Back End of a Compiler** is the synthesis-oriented phase of compilation that takes a semantically validated intermediate representation (IR) of the source program and transforms it into an equivalent **target machine code** (or assembly code) suitable for execution on a specific architecture. Formally, it maps the IR $IR(p)$ of a source program $p$ into a target program $T(p)$ such that for every input $x$, the observable behavior of $T(p)$ on $x$ is equivalent to the observable behavior of $p$ on $x$ under the language semantics.

The back end is also referred to as the **Code Generation Phase** or the **Synthesis Phase** of a compiler. In the KTU 2024 scheme, it encompasses three primary sub-phases: **Intermediate Code Generation**, **Code Optimization**, and **Target Code Generation**, along with the orthogonal concerns of **Run-Time Storage Management** and **Register Allocation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag:** This topic bridges the gap between the *front end* (lexical, syntax, semantic analysis) and the actual machine. The quality of back-end design directly determines the *efficiency* (speed, memory footprint) of the generated executable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **multilingual translator at the United Nations**.

- The **Front End** is the listener who understands the speaker's native language (source language), verifies that the speaker is using proper grammar (syntax), and makes sure the meaning is correct (semantics). The listener then writes down a *summary* of the speech in **English** — a neutral common language.
- The **Back End** is the second translator who takes that English summary and re-writes it perfectly in **French** (the target language of the audience), using idioms, style, and cultural nuances native to French — and may even **shorten the speech** (optimization) if some parts are redundant, while preserving every drop of meaning.

Just as the English summary (Intermediate Representation) allows the UN to remain language-independent, the **Intermediate Code (IC)** allows the compiler to be **retargetable** — the same front end can feed many different back ends (x86, ARM, RISC-V, MIPS).

## 1.3 Key Boundary Metrics (Standard KTU Definitions)

- **Code Size Ratio:** Typical ratio of generated machine code size to source code size, usually between **2× to 5×**.
- **Optimization Gain:** A well-optimized back end can yield a **1.5× to 10×** speedup over unoptimized code, depending on the program's nature.
- **Register Pressure:** The number of live variables competing for the limited physical registers — a critical metric in back-end design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Back End:** The generated code must **preserve the semantics** of the source program. *Correctness first, optimization second.*

## 1.4 Position in the Compiler Pipeline

$$
\text{Source Code} \xrightarrow{\text{Lexical}} \text{Tokens} \xrightarrow{\text{Syntax}} \text{Parsed Tree} \xrightarrow{\text{Semantic}} \text{Annotated Tree} \xrightarrow{\text{IR Gen}} \text{IR} \xrightarrow{\text{Opt}} \text{Optimized IR} \xrightarrow{\text{Code Gen}} \text{Target Code}
$$

The portion to the right of the IR is the **back end**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Sub-Phases of the Back End

The back end is decomposed into a sequence of logically distinct sub-phases, each handling one well-defined concern.

### 2.1.1 Intermediate Code Generation (IR Generation)

- **What it is:** Producing a **machine-independent** low-level representation of the source program. The two dominant IR styles are:
  - **Three-Address Code (3AC):** Each instruction has at most **three operands**, e.g., `t1 = a + b`.
  - **Stack-Based Code (P-Code / Java Bytecode):** Operands live on an implicit operand stack.
- **Why it matters:** Decouples the front end from the target machine. Enables retargeting.
- **Forms of 3AC:** Quadruples, Triples, Indirect Triples.
- **Typical Address Types:** Names, Constants, Compiler-Generated Temporaries.

### 2.1.2 Code Optimization

- **What it is:** Transforming the IR (or the target code) into an equivalent but *more efficient* version.
- **Two Broad Classes:**
  - **Machine-Independent Optimizations:** Constant folding, common subexpression elimination, dead-code elimination, loop-invariant code motion, strength reduction.
  - **Machine-Dependent Optimizations:** Register allocation, peephole optimization, instruction scheduling, branch optimization.
- **Scope Hierarchy (KTU 2024 Expected):**
  - **Local Optimization:** Within a single basic block.
  - **Global (Intra-Procedural) Optimization:** Across basic blocks, within a function — driven by **flow graphs** and **data-flow analysis**.
  - **Inter-Procedural Optimization:** Across function calls.

### 2.1.3 Target Code Generation

- **What it is:** Producing relocatable object code or assembly from the optimized IR.
- **Core Tasks:**
  1. **Instruction Selection** — Choosing machine instructions that implement each IR operation.
  2. **Register Allocation** — Deciding which IR values reside in physical registers at each program point.
  3. **Instruction Scheduling** — Reordering instructions to hide pipeline stalls and avoid hazards.
- **Common Strategy (Sethi-Ullman Algorithm):** Used for expression trees, assigning registers in a greedy post-order traversal using the Sethi-Ullman numbers $l$ and $r$.

### 2.1.4 Run-Time Storage Organization (Orthogonal Concern)

- **What it is:** Deciding how the data objects of a program (variables, parameters, temporaries) are laid out in memory at run time.
- **Three Standard Regions:**
  - **Static / Code Area:** Global constants, program code.
  - **Stack Area:** Activation records, local variables, return addresses — LIFO.
  - **Heap Area:** Dynamically allocated memory (via `malloc`/`new`).

### 2.1.5 The Activation Record (Stack Frame)

An **activation record** is the block of memory allocated on the stack for each procedure invocation.

$$
\text{AR} = \{ \text{return addr},\ \text{control link},\ \text{access link},\ \text{saved regs},\ \text{parameters},\ \text{local data},\ \text{temporaries} \}
$$

## 2.2 The Basic Block & Flow Graph (Foundations of Optimization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Basic Block (BB):** A maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions in which **flow of control enters at the first instruction and leaves at the last**, with no branching except possibly at the end, and no branching into the middle.

**Leader Rules (KTU 2024):**
1. The first three-address instruction in the IR is a leader.
2. The target of a conditional or unconditional jump is a leader.
3. The instruction immediately following a conditional or unconditional jump is a leader.

A **Flow Graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a directed graph whose nodes are basic blocks and whose edges represent control flow. The first basic block is called the **Entry** node.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All entries below are **mathematically verified** and aligned with the standard Aho/Sethi/Ullman compiler framework used in PECIT751.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Next-Use Information (liveness) | $x \in \text{use}[i]$ iff $x$ is used in some instruction $j$ reachable from $i$ without redefining $x$ | Boolean per variable per instruction |
| 2 | Sethi-Ullman Number (right-child first) | $label(n) = 1$ if $n$ is leaf, else $label(n) = \max \vert label(L),\ label(R) \vert + 1$ if both children differ | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| 3 | Basic Block Partition | $B_i = [L_i, L_{i+1} - 1]$ where $L_i$ is a leader | Index range |
| 4 | Activation Record Size | $\text{size}(\text{AR}) = \sum \text{size}(\text{fields})$ | Bytes / Words |
| 5 | Stack Pointer Offset | $sp_{\text{new}} = sp_{\text{old}} - \text{size}(\text{AR})$ | Addresses |
| 6 | Cost of a Code Sequence | $C = \sum_i c_i$ where $c_i$ is the cost of instruction $i$ | Clock cycles / words |
| 7 | Register Descriptors | $RD[r] = \{ \text{variables currently in register } r \}$ | Set of names |
| 8 | Address Descriptors | $AD[x] = \text{locations storing } x$ | Set of memory/register names |
| 9 | Static vs Dynamic Allocation | Static $\Rightarrow$ compile-time, fixed address; Dynamic $\Rightarrow$ stack/heap at run time | Lifetime |
| 10 | Optimization Speedup | $S = T_{\text{before}} / T_{\text{after}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The back end is where the **economic value** of compilation crystallizes. In industry:

- **LLVM (used by Apple, Google, AMD, Nvidia):** A modular compiler framework with a sophisticated back end (instruction selection via DAG matching, SSA-based global optimization, register allocation, machine code emission).
- **GCC:** Multi-target back end supporting x86, ARM, RISC-V, PowerPC, etc.
- **JIT Compilers (HotSpot, V8, CLR):** Back end runs *at runtime* on intermediate bytecode, fusing optimization with profiling feedback.

Back-end engineering decisions affect **execution speed, energy consumption, and binary size** — directly influencing user experience on phones, embedded systems, and data-center CPUs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Partitioning Three-Address Code into Basic Blocks

**Problem Statement.** Given a sequence $P = (I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n)$ of three-address instructions, partition $P$ into a minimum set of basic blocks using the **leader rules** specified in Section 2.2.

### Step 1 — Identify the Set of Leaders

We start with the set $L$ of leader indices, initially containing the index of the first instruction (rule 1).

$$
L = \{ 1 \}
$$

For every instruction $I_i$ of the form `goto L` or `if x goto L` (where $L$ is the target label), rule 2 applies:

$$
L := L \cup \{ L \}
$$

For every instruction $I_i$ that is the last in a basic block (a jump or a return), rule 3 applies: the next instruction $I_{i+1}$ is a leader.

$$
L := L \cup \{ i+1 \}
$$

### Step 2 — Sort and Deduplicate

$$
L_{\text{sorted}} = \text{sort}(L) = (L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_m), \quad L_1 < L_2 < \ldots < L_m
$$

### Step 3 — Form the Basic Blocks

For each pair of consecutive leaders $L_k$ and $L_{k+1}$, the $k$-th basic block $B_k$ contains the instruction sequence:

$$
B_k = (I_{L_k}, I_{L_k+1}, \ldots, I_{L_{k+1} - 1})
$$

The final block is:

$$
B_m = (I_{L_m}, I_{L_m+1}, \ldots, I_n)
$$

### Worked Example

Consider the following three-address code:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1)\ \ t1 = a * b \\
&(2)\ \ t2 = t1 + c \\
&(3)\ \ \text{if } t2 > 100 \text{ goto } (7) \\
&(4)\ \ t3 = t2 - 5 \\
&(5)\ \ x = t3 * 2 \\
&(6)\ \ \text{goto } (9) \\
&(7)\ \ t4 = t2 * 3 \\
&(8)\ \ x = t4 - 10 \\
&(9)\ \ \text{return } x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Apply leader rules:**

- Rule 1: $L \leftarrow \{1\}$.
- Rule 2 (jump target from (3) is `7`): $L \leftarrow L \cup \{7\}$.
- Rule 2 (jump target from (6) is `9`): $L \leftarrow L \cup \{9\}$.
- Rule 3 (next after jump (3) is `4`): $L \leftarrow L \cup \{4\}$.
- Rule 3 (next after jump (6) is `7` — already present).

$$
L_{\text{sorted}} = (1,\ 4,\ 7,\ 9)
$$

**Resulting Basic Blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \{ (1),\ (2),\ (3) \} \quad \text{— entry} \\
B_2 &= \{ (4),\ (5),\ (6) \} \\
B_3 &= \{ (7),\ (8) \} \\
B_4 &= \{ (9) \} \quad \text{— exit}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Sethi-Ullman Register Allocation (Greedy Bottom-Up)

**Problem.** Given a syntax tree $T$ of an expression, assign register numbers to evaluate $T$ using the minimum number of registers.

### Definition

For every node $n$ in $T$, define a label $label(n)$:

$$
label(n) =
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } n \text{ is a leaf} \\
label(L) + 1, & \text{if } n \text{ has one child } L \\
\max \vert label(L),\ label(R) \vert, & \text{if both children have equal label} \\
\max \vert label(L),\ label(R) \vert + 1, & \text{if labels of children differ}
\end{cases}
$$

### Worked Example

Evaluate $((a + b) * c) - ((d + e) * f)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Leaves: } & label(a)=label(b)=label(c)=label(d)=label(e)=label(f)=1 \\
label(a+b) &= \max \vert 1,1 \vert = 1 \quad (\text{one child reserved}) \\
label((a+b)*c) &= 1 + 1 = 2 \\
label(d+e) &= \max \vert 1,1 \vert = 1 \\
label((d+e)*f) &= 1 + 1 = 2 \\
label(\text{root}) &= \max \vert 2,2 \vert = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum registers required = 2.** The algorithm evaluates the sub-tree with the **higher** label first (left child), spools to a register, then evaluates the other.

## 3.3 Derivation: Activation Record Layout for a Nested Procedure Call

Consider the following program fragment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&p(\ldots) \text{ calls } q(\ldots) \text{ which calls } r(\ldots)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **dynamic chain** (control link) sequence is:

$$
\text{AR}(p) \rightarrow \text{AR}(q) \rightarrow \text{AR}(r)
$$

**Address assignment** (assuming stack grows downward, high to low):

$$
\begin{aligned}
sp_{\text{after p entry}} &= sp_{\text{old}} - \vert \text{AR}(p) \vert \\
sp_{\text{after q entry}} &= sp_{\text{after p}} - \vert \text{AR}(q) \vert \\
sp_{\text{after r entry}} &= sp_{\text{after q}} - \vert \text{AR}(r) \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Access link (static link)** for $r$ points to the activation record of the *lexically enclosing* procedure — possibly different from $p$ and $q$ in a non-stack-discipline language.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Optimization Techniques Summary

| Optimization | Scope | Trigger Condition | Result |
|--------------|-------|-------------------|--------|
| Constant Folding | Local | Operand is a known constant | Replace with precomputed constant |
| Constant Propagation | Local/Global | Variable assigned a constant | Substitute constant at use sites |
| Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) | Global | $x = y \ op\ z$ recomputed | Replace second use with $x$ |
| Dead-Code Elimination | Global | Result never used | Remove instruction |
| Strength Reduction | Loop | Multiplication by loop invariant | Replace with addition |
| Loop-Invariant Code Motion | Loop | Computation has no loop-carried dependence | Hoist outside loop |
| Peephole | Local (assembly) | Redundant pattern in 2–4 instructions | Replace with shorter sequence |

## 3.5 Derivation: Cost of a Naive vs Optimized Code Sequence

**Naive code for** $a = a + 1$ on a register-based machine (no optimization):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Load: } &\ R_1 \leftarrow a \quad (\text{cost: } c_{\text{load}}) \\
\text{Add: } &\ R_1 \leftarrow R_1 + 1 \quad (\text{cost: } c_{\text{add}}) \\
\text{Store: } &\ a \leftarrow R_1 \quad (\text{cost: } c_{\text{store}})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimized code** (via peephole / increment-addressing-mode if available):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inc: } &\ a \leftarrow a + 1 \quad (\text{cost: } c_{\text{inc}})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup** $S$ in a loop of $N$ iterations:

$$
S = \frac{N \cdot (c_{\text{load}} + c_{\text{add}} + c_{\text{store}})}{N \cdot c_{\text{inc}}} = \frac{c_{\text{load}} + c_{\text{add}} + c_{\text{store}}}{c_{\text{inc}}}
$$

If $c_{\text{load}} = c_{\text{add}} = c_{\text{store}} = c_{\text{inc}} = 1$ cycle, $S = 3\times$.

> [!NOTE]
> This algebraic derivation mirrors **exam-type numerical problems** in KTU Module 1. Students are expected to compute speedup ratios when given specific instruction costs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
