## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Back End

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end data flow** of the back end, from intermediate code ingestion to target code emission. It is engineered as a **functional architecture block diagram** rather than a physical drawing, in accordance with the KTU scheme for topics involving hardware-coupled software processes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FE["FRONT END (Context Boundary)"]
        IC["IR / 3-Address Code"]
    end

    subgraph BE["BACK END PIPELINE"]
        ICGEN["Intermediate Code Generator"]
        BB["Basic Block Partitioner"]
        DAG["DAG / Flow Graph Builder"]
        OPT["Optimizer (Local + Global)"]
        ISEL["Instruction Selector"]
        RALLOC["Register Allocator"]
        ISCHED["Instruction Scheduler"]
        ASMGEN["Assembly / Object Code Emitter"]
    end

    subgraph RTS["RUN-TIME SUPPORT"]
        AR["Activation Record Manager"]
        STKMGR["Stack / Heap Manager"]
        REGFILE["Physical Register File"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["FINAL OUTPUT"]
        OBJ[".obj / .o / .exe / .elf"]
    end

    IC --> ICGEN
    ICGEN --> BB
    BB --> DAG
    DAG --> OPT
    OPT --> ISEL
    ISEL --> RALLOC
    RALLOC --> ISCHED
    ISCHED --> ASMGEN
    ASMGEN --> OBJ

    RALLOC <--> REGFILE
    ASMGEN <--> AR
    AR <--> STKMGR
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Activation Record Layout (Stack Frame)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AR["Activation Record (Stack Frame)"]
        TOP["Return Address"]
        CL["Control Link (Dynamic Chain)"]
        AL["Access Link (Static Chain)"]
        SR["Saved Machine Registers"]
        PAR["Incoming Parameters"]
        OUTPAR["Outgoing Parameter Space"]
        LOC["Local Variables"]
        TMP["Compiler-Generated Temporaries"]
    end

    TOP --> CL --> AL --> SR --> PAR --> OUTPAR --> LOC --> TMP
```

**Memory Addressing Convention (Stack Grows Downward):**

| Offset from FP (Frame Pointer) | Field |
|--------------------------------|-------|
| $\text{FP} + 4$ | Return Address |
| $\text{FP}$ | Control Link |
| $\text{FP} - 4$ | Access Link |
| $\text{FP} - 8$ | Saved Register 1 |
| $\cdots$ | $\cdots$ |
| $\text{FP} - (4k)$ | Local $k$ |

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Flow Graph Example

A **flow graph** $G = (V, E)$ for the basic blocks derived in §3.1:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["B1: t1=a*b; t2=t1+c; if t2>100 goto 7"]
    B2["B2: t3=t2-5; x=t3*2; goto 9"]
    B3["B3: t4=t2*3; x=t4-10"]
    B4["B4: return x"]

    B1 -->|true| B3
    B1 -->|false| B2
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> B4
```

**Key Terms:**

- $B_1$ is the **Entry** node.
- $B_4$ is the **Exit** node.
- Edges are labelled with the **branch condition** that triggers the transition.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Instruction Selection Process (Tree Tiling)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["IR Tree / DAG"] --> B["Match against Machine Patterns"]
    B --> C{"Pattern Matches?"}
    C -->|Yes| D["Emit Machine Instruction(s)"]
    C -->|No| E["Decompose IR into Sub-Trees"]
    E --> B
    D --> F["Assemble Final Code Sequence"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Basic Block. Construct the basic blocks for the following 3AC sequence.

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2]
> Given the three-address code below, list the leaders and identify the basic blocks. Mention which block is the entry and which is the exit.
>
> ```text
> (1)  t1 = a + b
> (2)  t2 = t1 * c
> (3)  if t2 > 100 goto (7)
> (4)  t3 = a - b
> (5)  t4 = t3 * 2
> (6)  goto (9)
> (7)  t5 = t1 + d
> (8)  t4 = t5 * 3
> (9)  return t4
> ```

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Leaders (1 mark):**
- Rule 1: (1) is a leader.
- Rule 2: Jump targets (7) and (9) are leaders.
- Rule 3: Instructions (4) and (7) immediately follow jumps (3) and (6).
- Final leader set: $L = \{1, 4, 7, 9\}$.

**Step 2 — Form Basic Blocks (1 mark):**

| Block | Instructions |
|-------|--------------|
| $B_1$ (Entry) | (1), (2), (3) |
| $B_2$ | (4), (5), (6) |
| $B_3$ | (7), (8) |
| $B_4$ (Exit) | (9) |

**Step 3 — Identify Entry/Exit (1 mark):** $B_1$ is the entry block; $B_4$ is the exit block (contains `return`).

---

### Question 2: State and explain the Sethi-Ullman algorithm for register allocation.

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Supplementary]
> Compute the Sethi-Ullman numbers for the expression tree of $(a + b) \cdot (c - d) + e \cdot f$ and determine the minimum number of registers required.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Algorithm Definition (1 mark):**
The Sethi-Ullman algorithm is a bottom-up greedy scheme that assigns registers to an expression tree to minimize register usage. Each node is labelled with the number of registers needed to evaluate its sub-tree.

**Recursive Rule (1 mark):**
- Leaf: $label = 1$.
- Single child: $label(n) = label(\text{child}) + 1$.
- Two children with equal labels: $label(n) = label(\text{left})$.
- Two children with different labels: $label(n) = \max \vert label(\text{left}),\ label(\text{right}) \vert + 1$, evaluating the higher-labelled child first.

**Computation (1 mark):**
- $label(a+b) = 1$, $label(c-d) = 1$, $label((a+b)\cdot(c-d)) = 2$.
- $label(e\cdot f) = \max \vert 1,1 \vert = 1$.
- $label(\text{root}) = \max \vert 2,1 \vert + 1 = 3$.

**Minimum registers = 3.**

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - **Forgetting to mark the entry/exit block** costs 1 full mark in basic-block questions.
> - In Sethi-Ullman, students often forget the **+1 in the "different labels" case** and use $\max$ instead of $\max + 1$.
> - In code-generation cost problems, **do not forget to account for register loads/stores** that the IR forces; merely summing instruction costs is *not* enough.
> - For activation record layouts, students frequently **omit the control link** or misorder the fields. The KTU-recommended order is: return address → control link → access link → saved registers → parameters → locals.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q A.** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper based on 2024 Scheme]
> **(a)** Explain the **three-address code** representation. Generate the three-address code for the following snippet and then identify all the basic blocks. Comment on the optimizations possible.
>
> ```c
> for (i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
>     x = x + i;
>     y = y * 2;
> }
> ```
>
> **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Three-Address Code (3AC) Generation (3 marks):**
Three-address code is an intermediate representation in which each instruction has at most three operands (one operator and two sources, or one destination and one source). The 3AC for the given loop is:

```text
 1: i = 1
 2: if i > 100 goto 9
 3: t1 = x + i
 4: x = t1
 5: t2 = y * 2
 6: y = t2
 7: t3 = i + 1
 8: i = t3
 9: goto 2
10: // after loop
```

**Basic Block Identification (2 marks):**
Leaders: $\{1, 2, 3, 9, 10\}$.
- $B_1 = \{1\}$ (entry)
- $B_2 = \{2\}$ (loop condition)
- $B_3 = \{3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ (loop body)
- $B_4 = \{9\}$ (jump back to condition)
- $B_5 = \{10\}$ (exit)

**Optimizations Possible (2 marks):**
1. **Constant Folding:** `i = 1` could be combined with `i + 1` if $i$ is known at compile time — not applicable here as $i$ varies.
2. **Loop-Invariant Code Motion:** None of `x + i`, `y * 2` are loop-invariant (both depend on the loop counter or are themselves updated).
3. **Strength Reduction:** `i + 1` (instruction 7) and `y * 2` (instruction 5) are good candidates. Replace `i + 1` by increment addressing mode; replace `y * 2` by left-shift `y << 1`.
4. **Dead Code Elimination:** After strength reduction, the temporaries $t_1, t_2, t_3$ become redundant.

---

> **(b)** Discuss the **run-time storage organization** of a compiler. Draw the typical activation record layout and explain each field. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Run-Time Storage Organization (3 marks):**
A program's memory is partitioned at run time into:
- **Static / Code Area:** Holds executable code, string literals, and global variables with static storage duration. Allocated at compile time, fixed addresses.
- **Stack Area:** Holds activation records. Grows/shrinks in LIFO discipline. Used for local variables, parameters, return addresses.
- **Heap Area:** Holds dynamically allocated objects (e.g., via `malloc`/`new`). May grow during program execution; requires explicit deallocation or garbage collection.

**Activation Record Layout (2 marks):**

| High Address | Return Address |
|--------------|----------------|
|              | Control Link (saved FP) |
|              | Access Link (for closures/nested scopes) |
|              | Saved Machine Registers |
|              | Incoming Parameters |
|              | Outgoing Parameter Space |
|              | Local Variables |
|              | Temporaries |
| Low Address  | (Stack Grows Down) |

**Field Explanations (2 marks):**
1. **Return Address:** Where to resume after the procedure returns.
2. **Control Link (Dynamic Link):** Pointer to the caller's activation record; implements the dynamic call chain.
3. **Access Link (Static Link):** Pointer to the activation record of the lexically enclosing scope — essential for languages with nested procedures (Pascal, Ada, ML).
4. **Saved Registers:** Values of registers that the callee must preserve for the caller.
5. **Parameters:** Values passed into the procedure.
6. **Outgoing Parameter Space:** Slots the callee uses to build arguments for procedures it calls.
7. **Locals:** Storage for the procedure's local variables.
8. **Temporaries:** Compiler-generated slots for subexpression evaluation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q B.** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper based on 2024 Scheme]
> **(a)** Explain the **issues in the design of a code generator**. Discuss **instruction selection**, **register allocation**, and **evaluation order** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Design Issues (1 mark):**
The back-end code generator must address three principal concerns:
1. **Correctness** — generated code must preserve source semantics.
2. **Efficiency** — minimize execution time, code size, and energy.
3. **Retargetability** — the generator should adapt to multiple architectures.

**Instruction Selection (2 marks):**
The process of mapping each IR operation to one or more machine instructions. Approaches include:
- **Macro-expansion / Tree-pattern matching:** Match IR sub-trees against machine instruction templates.
- **Burg / BURG toolchain:** Bottom-up rewrite systems using dynamic programming.
- **Cost-driven selection:** Minimize a cost function (cycles, bytes) of the emitted sequence.
- Example: IR `t = a + b` may be selected as one 3-operand `ADD` instruction on a RISC machine, or as a `LOAD` + `ADD` + `STORE` sequence on an accumulator-based CISC.

**Register Allocation (3 marks):**
Decides which IR values occupy the limited physical registers at each program point.
- **Register descriptors ($RD$):** Track which variables are currently in each register.
- **Address descriptors ($AD$):** Track all locations (registers and memory) that hold a given variable.
- **Strategies:**
  - **Local allocation (within a basic block):** Next-use information drives a greedy scheme that keeps a variable in a register if it will be used again.
  - **Global allocation (graph coloring):** Build an interference graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = temporaries and $E$ connects two vertices if their lifetimes overlap. A $K$-coloring of $G$ corresponds to a $K$-register allocation. If the graph is not $K$-colorable, **spill** some temporaries to memory.
- **Spilling:** When registers are insufficient, some variable is stored in memory and reloaded on demand.

**Evaluation Order (1 mark):**
Determines the order in which sub-expressions of an expression tree are computed. Matters for register allocation (since temporaries generated earlier may still be live when later ones are computed). The Sethi-Ullman algorithm gives an optimal evaluation order minimizing register usage for trees.

---

> **(b)** Apply the **Sethi-Ullman algorithm** to the expression tree of $((a+b) * c) - ((d+e) / f)$ and determine the **minimum number of registers** required. Also write the evaluation order generated by the algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Tree Construction (1 mark):**

```
        (-)
       /   \
     (*)   (/)
     / \   / \
   (+)  c (+)  f
   / \     / \
  a   b   d   e
```

**Label Computation (3 marks):**

Using the rules from Section 3.2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
label(a) &= label(b) = label(c) = label(d) = label(e) = label(f) = 1 \\
label(a+b) &= \max \vert 1,1 \vert = 1 \quad (\text{equal labels}) \\
label((a+b) \cdot c) &= label(a+b) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
label(d+e) &= \max \vert 1,1 \vert = 1 \\
label((d+e) / f) &= label(d+e) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
label(\text{root}) &= \max \vert 2, 2 \vert = 2 \quad (\text{equal labels})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum Registers = 2.**

**Evaluation Order (2 marks):**
The algorithm evaluates the **left** sub-tree first (since labels are equal and tie-break by left):
1. Evaluate `a + b` → register $R_1$.
2. Multiply $R_1$ with $c$ → register $R_1$.
3. Spool $R_1$ to a safe temporary (e.g., $T_1$).
4. Evaluate `d + e` → register $R_1$.
5. Divide $R_1$ by $f$ → register $R_1$.
6. Subtract $T_1 - R_1$ → final result.

**Register Trace (1 mark):**

| Step | Action | $R_1$ | $R_2$ (Spool) |
|------|--------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | a + b | result | — |
| 2 | * c | result | — |
| 3 | spool | — | $R_1$ (spool) |
| 4 | d + e | result | spool preserved |
| 5 | / f | result | spool preserved |
| 6 | subtract | $R_1 - R_2$ | — |

Throughout, only 2 registers are live, confirming the computed minimum.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Recall Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Back End Definition:** The back end maps IR → target machine code while preserving semantics.
- **Three-Address Code Rule:** Maximum **three operands per instruction**; produces temporaries.
- **Basic Block:** Maximal straight-line sequence; **single entry at first instr, single exit at last instr**.
- **Leader Rules (3):** (1) First instruction, (2) Jump target, (3) Instruction after a jump.
- **Flow Graph:** Nodes = basic blocks, Edges = control flow. $B_1$ = entry, $B_n$ = exit.
- **DAG vs Control Flow Graph:** DAG eliminates common subexpressions *within* a basic block; CFG tracks inter-block control.
- **Sethi-Ullman Numbering:** Leaf = 1, equal-label children = $\max$, different labels = $\max + 1$.
- **Register Allocation:** Local → Next-use based; Global → Interference graph coloring; if not $K$-colorable → **spill** to memory.
- **Peephole Optimization:** Looks at a small sliding window of instructions (typically 2–4); replaces redundancies.
- **Activation Record Fields (Top-to-Bottom):** Return Address → Control Link → Access Link → Saved Regs → Parameters → Outgoing Args → Locals → Temporaries.
- **Three Storage Areas:** Static (compile-time fixed), Stack (LIFO activations), Heap (dynamic allocation).
- **Cost Metric:** Always check that your speedup calculation uses the **same units** in numerator and denominator.
- **Optimizations to Memorize:** Constant folding, common subexpression elimination (CSE), dead-code elimination, strength reduction, loop-invariant code motion, copy propagation.
- **Exam Mantra:** *Identify leaders → form blocks → draw flow graph → apply data-flow analysis → emit optimized code.*
- **KTU 2024 Trivia:** Module 1 often includes a sub-part asking for "activation record of a *nested* procedure" — remember the **access (static) link** points to the *lexical* parent, not the dynamic caller.
- **Watch Out:** In $a = a + 1$ style questions, do not ignore the load/store cost on accumulator machines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanners - Recognizing Words
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Scanners - Recognizing Words # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Scanner** (also called the **Lexical Analyzer** or **Lexer**) is the *first phase* of a compiler. Its primary job is to read the source program as a stream of characters and group them into meaningful sequences called **lexemes**, and for each lexeme it produces a **token**. Formally, it implements the regular-expression based specification of the language's vocabulary and is, in principle, equivalent to a **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)** > A lexical analyzer reads input characters of the source program, groups them into lexemes, and produces as output a sequence of tokens. Each token is a (token-name, attribute-value) pair used by the subsequent parser (syntax analyzer). ## 1.2 Key Terminology — The Three Pillars | Term | Formal Meaning | Example in `int a = 10;` | |------|----------------|--------------------------| | **Token** | A category/symbol (pair: name + optional attribute) | ``, ``, `<= >`, `` | | **Lexeme** | The actual sequence of characters matched by a token's pattern | `a`, `int`, `=`, `10` | | **Pattern** | A rule (usually a regular expression) describing all lexemes for a token | `letter (letter \| digit)*` for identifiers | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toll Booth Inspector" Imagine a long highway of cars (the source program characters). Before cars enter a smart city (the parser), they must pass through a **toll booth** (the scanner): - The toll booth inspector does **not** understand the whole journey's meaning (that's the parser's job). - The inspector simply **classifies** each car: - 🚌 Bus? → Bus token - 🚗 Sedan? → Car token - 🚛 Truck? → Truck token - 🚲 Bicycle? → Cycle token - The inspector hands a **stamped ticket** (token) with category and license plate (attribute) to the city administration (parser). In the same way, the scanner: 1. **Reads** characters one-by-one. 2. **Recognizes** groupings (lexemes) that match known patterns. 3. **Emits** tokens to the parser — it never worries about grammar, semantics, or meaning. ## 1.4 Architectural Position in the Compiler > [!NOTE] > **Pipeline Snapshot** — KTU Standard Block Diagram > `Source Code → [ Scanner ] → Tokens → [ Parser ] → Syntax Tree → ... → Target Code` The scanner is the **only** phase that physically inspects every single character of the source. Every later phase works on tokens. This is why scanner speed directly dominates the entire compilation time. ## 1.5 Why Do We Need a Separate Scanner? | Reason | Engineering Justification | |--------|--------------------------| | **Simplicity** | Parser operates on a clean, token stream, freeing it from character-level details. | | **Efficiency** | Specialized buffering + DFA make scanning the *fastest* phase. | | **Portability** | Different character sets (ASCII, Unicode, EBCDIC) are isolated inside the scanner. | | **Reusability** | One scanner can be paired with multiple parsers. | | **Error uniformity** | Centralized reporting of illegal characters / malformed numbers. | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Scanner The scanner performs its work as a strict six-step cycle: 1. **Open input file** and allocate one or two input buffers. 2. **Read characters** from the buffer(s) using a forward pointer `lexeme_begin` and a `forward` pointer. 3. **Match characters** against the DFA's transition table (or hand-coded `switch`). 4. **Determine the longest prefix** that forms a valid lexeme (the *maximal munch* rule). 5. **Roll back** the `forward` pointer if it overshot (lookahead recovery). 6. **Emit token** (token-name, attribute-value) to the parser. ## 2.2 The "Maximal Munch" Rule > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Concept:** Always match the **longest possible** lexeme starting at the current position. This is called *maximal munch* or *longest match*. **Example:** For input `>=` when both `>` and `>=` are valid patterns: - Wrong: emit `>` token, leaving `=` for next round. - Correct: emit `>=` as a single token. ## 2.3 Buffer Schemes — Recognizing "Words" Beyond a Single Buffer A central problem: the source file may be **larger than main memory**, and the scanner needs to peek ahead (e.g., to distinguish `<` from `<=`, or `=` from `==`). Two standard buffer strategies solve this. ### 2.3.1 Single Buffer Scheme - One buffer of size **N** bytes. - After scanning N-1 characters, refill the buffer. - **Problem:** If a lexeme straddles the buffer boundary, we lose characters. ### 2.3.2 Buffer-Pair Scheme (Aho-Sethi-Ullman — the **standard KTU answer**) - Two buffers, each of size **N**, loaded alternately. - A **sentinel character** `EOF` (a special marker, *not* a real character) is placed at the end of each buffer. - The `forward` pointer scans continuously. Whenever it hits the sentinel, the scanner knows to switch to the alternate buffer and refill the first one. > [!NOTE] > **Why a sentinel?** Without the sentinel, after every `N` characters the scanner would have to do an extra "boundary check." With the sentinel, the same check for `EOF` is unified with the character comparison — **saving one comparison per character**. ### 2.3.3 Buffer Pair — The Critical Formula Sheet > [!WARNING] > These formulas appear directly in KTU University Exam derivations. Memorise them. | # | Quantity | Formula | Engineering Meaning | |---|----------|---------|---------------------| | 1 | Total buffer memory | $2N$ bytes | Two halves, each of size N | | 2 | Useful characters per buffer | $N - 1$ | One slot reserved for the `EOF` sentinel | | 3 | Characters scanned before refill | $N$ | Full length of one buffer | | 4 | Comparisons per character | $1$ | Character class + EOF test merged | | 5 | Worst-case lexeme length | $2N$ | A token can theoretically straddle both buffers | ## 2.4 Token Attributes — What Travels With the Token A token is formally a **2-tuple**: $$ \text{Token} = \langle \text{token-name}, \ \text{attribute-value} \rangle $$ - For punctuation like `;`, `+`, `(`: attribute-value is usually *omitted* or set to `null`. - For identifiers: attribute-value is a **pointer into the symbol table**. - For numbers/constants: attribute-value is the parsed numeric value or a pointer to a constant-table entry. ## 2.5 Errors Detected by a Scanner The scanner detects *only* a small class of errors: - Illegal character in source (e.g., `@` in C when not in a string). - Unterminated string literal (`"hello` with no closing `"`). - Unterminated comment (`/* ...` with no `*/`). - Numeric constant overflow (machine-dependent). > [!NOTE] > **Crucial KTU Distinction:** A scanner **does not** detect syntax errors (unmatched parentheses are caught by the parser, not the scanner). This is a frequently asked 3-mark question. ## 2.6 Implementation Strategies | Strategy | Used In | Pros | Cons | |----------|---------|------|------| | **Hand-coded** (e.g., `switch` in C) | GCC, early compilers | Ultra-fast, fully inlined | Hard to maintain, error-prone | | **Table-driven DFA** | Textbook (Aho) | Clean, provably correct | Slight overhead for table lookups | | **Automated (Lex/Flex)** | Most Unix tools | Concise, generates C code | Generated code can be larger/slower | | **Lexer Generator with DFA minimization** | Modern tools | Smallest possible DFA | Generator overhead at build time | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compilers**: GCC, Clang, MSVC. - **Static analyzers**: Linters, SAST tools. - **Domain-specific languages**: SQL parsers, JSON validators. - **Text processing**: `grep`, `awk`, `sed`. - **Highlighting tools**: IDE syntax highlighting uses a near-identical scanner pipeline. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Tokenising a Small Program **Input source program:** ```c int a = 100; b = a + 5; ``` Let us walk through the scanner step by step. The DFA states are labelled `S0` (start) through `S6` (accept). ### Step 1 — Recognising `int` The forward pointer reads `i`, then `n`, then `t`. The transition table sends `(S0, i) → S1 → S2 → S3`. State `S3` is the accepting state for the keyword pattern `int`. No rollback needed. **Token emitted:** $\langle \text{keyword}, \text{"int"} \rangle$ ### Step 2 — Skipping whitespace The scanner sees a space character `' '`. Since whitespace is *not* a token, the forward pointer advances without emitting. `lexeme_begin` moves to point at `a`. ### Step 3 — Recognising identifier `a` The pattern for an identifier is: $$ \text{letter} \ (\text{letter} \mid \text{digit})^* $$ The scanner greedily matches `a`, then sees ` ` (whitespace), which terminates the lexeme. The longest match rule ensures that even if `a` were a keyword, the keyword pattern would take priority (lookup in the reserved-word table happens **after** the DFA accepts the identifier pattern). **Token emitted:** $\langle \text{id}, \text{pointer to symbol-table entry for } a \rangle$ ### Step 4 — Recognising `=` Single character, mapped directly. **Token:** $\langle \text{relop}, \text{EQ} \rangle$ ### Step 5 — Recognising number `100` The scanner enters a numeric DFA. Reading `1`, `0`, `0` keeps it in the integer-accepting state. On the `;` character, the DFA halts. **Token emitted:** $\langle \text{num}, 100 \rangle$ ### Step 6 — `;` is punctuation **Token emitted:** $\langle \text{punct}, \text{;} \rangle$ ### Final Token Stream $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 &= \langle \text{keyword}, \text{"int"} \rangle \\ T_2 &= \langle \text{id}, ptr_1 \rangle \\ T_3 &= \langle \text{relop}, EQ \rangle \\ T_4 &= \langle \text{num}, 100 \rangle \\ T_5 &= \langle \text{punct}, ";" \rangle \\ T_6 &= \langle \text{id}, ptr_2 \rangle \\ T_7 &= \langle \text{relop}, EQ \rangle \\ T_8 &= \langle \text{id}, ptr_1 \rangle \\ T_9 &= \langle \text{arithop}, PLUS \rangle \\ T_{10} &= \langle \text{num}, 5 \rangle \\ T_{11} &= \langle \text{punct}, ";" \rangle \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Maximal Munch with `>=` **Input fragment:** `a >= 5;` The DFA has two competing paths when it sees the first `>`: - Path A: read one character `>`, accept state for token `GT`. - Path B: read second character `=`, accept state for token `GE` (`>=`). **Algorithm step by step:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State } S_0, \text{ input } 'a' &\rightarrow S_1 \text{ (id-start)} \\ \text{read ' '} &\rightarrow S_1 \text{ (whitespace, ignore)} \\ \text{read '>'} &\rightarrow S_{GT} \text{ (accepting)} \\ \text{read ' = '} &\rightarrow S_{GE} \text{ (accepting)} \\ \text{lexeme: "} \geq \text{", token: } \langle \text{relop}, GE \rangle \end{aligned} $$ Since `GE` is a valid accepting state and the DFA has not encountered an error, the **maximal-munch** rule selects the longer match. ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Buffer-Pair Boundary Crossing **Setup:** Let buffer size $N = 1024$ bytes (1 KB). The lexeme `identifier_xyz` has length 14. **Scenario:** `identifier_xyz` is positioned such that its first 1014 characters are in **Buffer 1** and the last 4 are in **Buffer 2**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Characters already scanned} &= 1014 \\ \text{Remaining of lexeme} &= 4 \ (\text{in next buffer}) \\ \text{Total lexeme length} &= 1014 + 4 = 1018 \\ \text{Buffer 1 size} &= N = 1024 \end{aligned} $$ When the forward pointer reaches position 1023 (the sentinel slot of Buffer 1), it sees the `EOF` sentinel. The scanner: 1. Pauses the DFA. 2. Switches to Buffer 2 (which has been pre-filled while Buffer 1 was active). 3. Continues scanning from position 0 of Buffer 2. 4. Resumes the DFA exactly where it paused. > [!IMPORTANT] > The DFA state is **preserved** across the buffer switch. The symbol table insertion (or token emission) happens only after the entire lexeme is consumed. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Common Ambiguities | Input | Possible Patterns | Decision Logic | Resulting Token | |-------|-------------------|----------------|-----------------| | `=` | `=`, or start of `==` | Peek next char | `EQ` or `EQEQ` | | `<` | `<`, `<=`, or start of `<>` | Peek next char | `LT`, `LE`, or continue | | `123` | Numeric literal | All digits | `num(123)` | | `123abc` | Numeric then id | Space or non-letter stops number | `num(123), id(abc)` | | `if` | `i` then `f` | Check reserved-word table **after** id-accept | `keyword IF` | | `"hello` | String literal | No closing `"` | **Lexical error** | | `/*...` | Comment start | No `*/` before EOF | **Lexical error** | ## 3.5 Performance Derivation Let $S$ be the source size in characters, and $B = 2N$ be the total buffer memory. The number of buffer refills required is: $$ R = \left\lceil \frac{S}{N} \right\rceil $$ Each refill costs one **disk I/O**. Therefore total I/O cost is: $$ \text{I/O cost} = R \times t_{\text{disk}} \approx \frac{S}{N} \times t_{\text{disk}} $$ This is why larger $N$ improves throughput, but with diminishing returns beyond a few kilobytes. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Scanner's Position in the Compiler ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Source Program
Character Stream] --> SCN[Scanner
Lexical Analyzer] SCN -->|Tokens| PAR[Parser
Syntax Analyzer] SCN --> SYM[(Symbol Table)] SCN -->|Lexical Errors| ERR[Error Handler] PAR --> SEM[Semantic Analyzer] SEM --> IC[Intermediate Code Generator] IC --> OPT[Optimizer] OPT --> CG[Code Generator] ERR --> LOG[Error Log File] ``` ## 4.2 Internal Architecture of the Scanner ```mermaid flowchart TB BUF1[Input Buffer 1
Size N bytes] BUF2[Input Buffer 2
Size N bytes] SW[Buffer Switch Logic] DFA[DFA / Transition Engine] LB[lexeme_begin Pointer] FW[forward Pointer] LK[Lookahead Logic] EM[Token Emitter] ST[Symbol Table Insert] BUF1 --> SW BUF2 --> SW SW --> DFA LB --> DFA FW --> DFA DFA --> LK LK -->|Longest match found| EM LK -->|Need more chars| FW EM --> ST EM --> OUT((Token Stream)) ST -->|Pointer| EM ``` ## 4.3 Buffer-Pair Scheme — Detailed View ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph B1[Buffer 1 of size N] B1D["[ chars 1 to N-1 ]"]:::buf B1S["[ EOF sentinel ]"]:::sentinel end subgraph B2[Buffer 2 of size N] B2D["[ chars N to 2N-1 ]"]:::buf B2S["[ EOF sentinel ]"]:::sentinel end FP["forward pointer"]:::ptr LB2["lexeme_begin"]:::ptr SW["Buffer switch when forward hits EOF"]:::switch B1D --> FP B1S --> SW SW --> B2D B2D --> FP B2S --> SW FP --> DFA["DFA engine reads char at forward"] LB2 --> TOK["Token lexeme = chars from lexeme_begin to forward-1"] DFA --> TOK TOK --> OUT((Emit Token)) classDef buf fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px classDef sentinel fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px classDef ptr fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px classDef switch fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.4 State Machine — Recognition of Relational Operators ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> S0 S0 --> S1 : read lt S0 --> S2 : read gt S0 --> S3 : read eq S0 --> S4 : read ne S1 --> S5 : read eq S1 --> S6 : accept LT S2 --> S7 : read eq S2 --> S8 : accept GT S3 --> S9 : read eq S3 --> S10 : accept ASSIGN S4 --> S10 : accept NE S5 --> S11 : accept LE S7 --> S12 : accept GE S9 --> S13 : accept EQ S6 --> [*] S8 --> [*] S10 --> [*] S11 --> [*] S12 --> [*] S13 --> [*] ``` ## 4.5 Token Pipeline — End-to-End Data Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P as Parser participant S as Scanner participant B as Input Buffer participant D as DFA Engine participant T as Symbol Table P->>S: getNextToken request S->>B: load buffer pair S->>D: initialise state S0 loop per character S->>B: read char at forward B-->>S: char or EOF sentinel S->>D: transition S, char D-->>S: next state, accept flag end S->>T: lookup/insert lexeme T-->>S: symbol table pointer S-->>P: emit token tuple P->>P: parse using token ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** **Q: Differentiate between token, lexeme, and pattern with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** - **Token** is a *category* into which lexemes are classified. It is a pair $\langle \text{token-name}, \text{attribute-value} \rangle$. - **Lexeme** is the *actual sequence of characters* in the source program that matches a pattern. - **Pattern** is the *rule* (often a regular expression) that describes the set of all possible lexemes for a token. **Example for the C statement `float area = 3.14;`:** | Component | Value | |-----------|-------| | Token | `` | | Lexeme | `float` | | Pattern | (one of the reserved words) | | Token | Lexeme | Pattern | |-------|--------|---------| | `` | `area` | `letter (letter\|digit)*` | | `` | `=` | `=` (assignment) | | `` | `3.14` | `digit+(\.digit+)?` | | `<;>` | `;` | `;` | --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Expected Pattern]** **Q: List the errors that can be detected by a lexical analyzer. Why can't the lexical analyzer detect the error `int a = b)`?** **Model Answer:** **Errors a scanner can detect:** 1. Illegal character in source (e.g., `@` in C identifier context). 2. Unterminated string literal (`"hello` with no closing `"`). 3. Unterminated comment (`/* ...` with no `*/`). 4. Numeric constant overflow. 5. Identifier too long (language-specific). **Why the scanner cannot detect `int a = b)`:** The token stream for that line is ``. Each of these tokens is a *valid* character grouping from the scanner's viewpoint. The mismatch — that there is an extra `)` in an expression position — is a **syntactic** error, detectable only by the **parser** using the language's grammar rules. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often wrongly list "unmatched parentheses" as a *lexical* error. This is the **#1 most common 3-mark blunder** in KTU Board exams. Always state explicitly: *unmatched parentheses = parser's job*. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the role of a lexical analyzer in a compiler. List the advantages of separating the analysis phase into lexical and syntax analysis. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the input buffering scheme used in lexical analysis with a neat diagram. Why is a sentinel character used? **[7 Marks]** --- ### Model Solution — Question A (a) **Definition:** The lexical analyzer (scanner) is the first phase of a compiler. It reads the source program character-by-character, groups characters into meaningful sequences called lexemes, and produces a sequence of tokens as output. Each token is a $\langle \text{token-name}, \text{attribute-value} \rangle$ pair. **Operational Steps:** 1. Read characters from the input buffer. 2. Group them into lexemes. 3. For each lexeme, produce a token. 4. Insert identifiers/literals into the symbol table. 5. Skip whitespace and comments. 6. Report lexical errors (illegal characters, unterminated strings/comments). **Advantages of separating Lexical and Syntax Analysis:** | # | Advantage | Justification | |---|-----------|---------------| | 1 | **Simplicity of design** | The parser does not need to handle character-level details like whitespace, line endings, or comment delimiters. | | 2 | **Efficiency** | The scanner can use highly optimised buffering and DFA techniques, making it the fastest phase of the compiler. | | 3 | **Portability** | The scanner is the only module that directly interfaces with the input file. Porting to a new platform or character set (ASCII ↔ Unicode) requires changes only here. | | 4 | **Reusability** | One scanner can be reused across multiple parsers, or one parser across multiple source-language dialects. | | 5 | **Specialised handling** | Input device independence and uniform error reporting for lexical errors. | **Real-world mapping:** In GCC, `lex.c` and `lex.h` form an isolated module that produces a token stream consumed by the parser in `parser.c`. --- ### Model Solution — Question A (b) **The Input Buffering Problem:** The source program may be arbitrarily long (often larger than main memory). Furthermore, the scanner needs to peek ahead — to distinguish `<` from `<=`, `=` from `==`, or to match a multi-character lexeme like `identifier_xyz`. **Solution: Buffer-Pair Scheme (Two Buffers of Size N)** - Two buffers, each of size $N$ bytes, are allocated in memory (total $2N$). - The source file is read into Buffer 1 first. When Buffer 1 is exhausted, scanning continues into Buffer 2, while Buffer 1 is refilled from disk in the background. - A **sentinel character** `EOF` (a special marker, not a real source character) is placed at position $N$ in each buffer. - The `forward` pointer scans character-by-character. When it reads the sentinel, control switches to the alternate buffer. **Diagram (text rendition for the answer sheet):** ``` [---- Buffer 1 (size N) ----][---- Buffer 2 (size N) ----] [ source chars 1 to N-1 ][EOF][ source chars N+1 to 2N ][EOF] ^ ^ forward when here switch point ``` **Why a sentinel?** Without the sentinel, after every $N$ characters the scanner must perform: 1. A "have I reached the buffer end?" check. 2. The normal "match this character class" check. That is **two comparisons per character**. With the sentinel: 1. The single test `if (c == EOF)` handles **both** the buffer-end check and the end-of-file check. 2. Only **one comparison per character** is needed. **Result:** A factor-of-two speed-up in the tight inner loop of the DFA — critical because lexical analysis is a hot path. **Quantified derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Without sentinel: comparisons per char} &= 2 \\ \text{With sentinel: comparisons per char} &= 1 \\ \text{Speed-up factor} &= \frac{2}{1} = 2 \times \\ \text{Useful chars per buffer} &= N - 1 \ (\text{one slot reserved for EOF}) \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that the sentinel is *not* a real character in the source — it is an in-memory flag. Without this clarification, the answer loses 1–2 marks. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted Pattern]** **(a)** Define token and lexeme. Tokenise the following code segment and produce the output token stream with appropriate token names and attributes: ```c sum = price + tax; ``` **[7 Marks]** **(b)** What is meant by *maximal munch*? Illustrate with a clear example how the DFA approach resolves the ambiguity between `<` and `<=`. **[7 Marks]** --- ### Model Solution — Question B (a) **Definitions:** - **Token** — A pair $\langle \text{token-name}, \text{attribute-value} \rangle$ passed from the scanner to the parser. Token-name is an abstract symbol (e.g., `id`, `num`, `relop`); attribute-value carries additional info (e.g., a symbol-table pointer). - **Lexeme** — The actual character sequence in the source that matches a pattern. **Step-by-step Tokenisation of `sum = price + tax;`:** | Step | Lexeme Recognised | Pattern Applied | Token Emitted | |------|-------------------|-----------------|---------------| | 1 | `sum` | `letter(letter\|digit)*` | `` (insert into symbol table) | | 2 | ` ` (whitespace) | (skip) | — | | 3 | `=` | `=` | `` | | 4 | ` ` (whitespace) | (skip) | — | | 5 | `price` | `letter(letter\|digit)*` | `` | | 6 | ` ` (whitespace) | (skip) | — | | 7 | `+` | `+` | `` | | 8 | ` ` (whitespace) | (skip) | — | | 9 | `tax` | `letter(letter\|digit)*` | `` | | 10 | `;` | `;` | `` | **Output Token Stream:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 &= \langle \text{id}, ptr_{sum} \rangle \\ T_2 &= \langle \text{relop}, EQ \rangle \\ T_3 &= \langle \text{id}, ptr_{price} \rangle \\ T_4 &= \langle \text{arithop}, PLUS \rangle \\ T_5 &= \langle \text{id}, ptr_{tax} \rangle \\ T_6 &= \langle \text{punct}, ";" \rangle \end{aligned} $$ **Symbol Table after this line:** | ptr | Name | Type | Scope | |-----|------|------|-------| | `ptr_sum` | `sum` | (filled later by parser/semantic) | — | | `ptr_price` | `price` | — | — | | `ptr_tax` | `tax` | — | — | --- ### Model Solution — Question B (b) **Maximal Munch Definition:** The *maximal munch* rule (also called *longest match* or *greedy match*) states that the scanner must always select the **longest** lexeme that starts at the current input position and is a valid prefix of some token pattern. **Why it matters:** Several patterns can have a common prefix (e.g., `<`, `<=`, `<>`). The scanner must look ahead to determine the correct token. **DFA Approach for `<` vs `<=`:** The DFA is constructed such that: - The state reached after reading `<` is an *accepting* state for token `LT`. - The state reached after reading `<=` is *also* an accepting state for token `LE`. - If the DFA is in an accepting state but can still read a *legal* character (here, `=`) and reach another accepting state, it **continues**. - Only when no further legal transition is possible does the scanner accept. **Trace for input `<= 10`:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State } S_0, \text{ read `<'} &\rightarrow S_{LT} \ (\text{accepting for token LT}) \\ \text{continue: read `='} &\rightarrow S_{LE} \ (\text{accepting for token LE}) \\ \text{next char is space — no legal transition} &\Rightarrow \text{stop at } S_{LE} \\ \text{Emit token} &= \langle \text{relop}, LE \rangle \end{aligned} $$ **Trace for input `< 10`:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State } S_0, \text{ read `<'} &\rightarrow S_{LT} \ (\text{accepting for token LT}) \\ \text{next char is space — no legal transition} &\Rightarrow \text{stop at } S_{LT} \\ \text{Emit token} &= \langle \text{relop}, LT \rangle \end{aligned} $$ **Rollback (Lookahead Recovery):** If the DFA had to read the next character only to *check* the boundary, it rolls back the `forward` pointer by one position. The character is re-read in the next token's start position. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students describe the DFA but forget to mention the **rollback** mechanism. If the DFA reads one extra character "just to test," the scanner must push it back. This is worth 2 marks in the KTU marking scheme. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Final Warnings > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Mark-Loss Patterns in This Topic** > 1. Confusing *token* with *lexeme* — they are **not** the same. > 2. Listing unmatched parentheses as a *lexical* error (it is *syntactic*). > 3. Forgetting the **sentinel character** in the buffer-pair diagram. > 4. Stating that the sentinel is a real source character (it is an in-memory flag). > 5. Skipping the explanation of *why* a sentinel saves one comparison per character. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 📋 Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Token** = category + attribute; **Lexeme** = actual characters; **Pattern** = the rule (regular expression). - ✅ The scanner is the **first** compiler phase; the parser follows. - ✅ The scanner **always** uses the *maximal munch* (longest match) rule. - ✅ Buffer-pair scheme uses **two buffers of size N** with a **sentinel** at each end. - ✅ Sentinel reduces per-character comparisons from **2 to 1**. - ✅ Useful characters per buffer = **N − 1**. - ✅ Lookahead may require **rollback** of the `forward` pointer. - ✅ Scanner errors: illegal character, unterminated string/comment, numeric overflow, over-long identifier. - ✅ Scanner **does not** handle: unmatched parens, missing semicolons, wrong operator types — all parser issues. - ✅ Real-world scanners: hand-coded (GCC), table-driven DFA (textbook), generator (Lex/Flex). - ✅ Performance: total I/O cost $\approx (S/N) \times t_{\text{disk}}$ where $S$ is source size. - ✅ Symbol table is **populated by the scanner** for identifiers and literal constants. - ✅ Whitespace and comments are **discarded** by the scanner, not passed to the parser. ### 🔑 Mnemonic for the Tokenisation Pipeline **"B-M-D-E-S"** = **B**uffer → **M**atch → **D**etermine lexeme → **E**mit token → **S**ymbol table update. ### 🧠 Must-Remember Equation Set $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Buffer pair memory} &= 2N \\ \text{Useful chars per buffer} &= N - 1 \\ \text{Comparisons per char (with sentinel)} &= 1 \\ \text{Comparisons per char (without sentinel)} &= 2 \\ \text{Refills required for source of size } S &= \left\lceil \frac{S}{N} \right\rceil \end{aligned} $$ ---

topics.title: Regular Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Regular Expressions ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Regular Expression (RE)** is a compact, declarative, algebraic notation used to describe (or *denote*) a **regular language** — a language that can be recognized by a finite automaton. Formally, given an alphabet $\Sigma$, a regular expression $R$ over $\Sigma$ is recursively defined as: $$ R \;\longrightarrow\; \varnothing \;\mid\; \varepsilon \;\mid\; a \;\mid\; R \cup R \;\mid\; R \cdot R \;\mid\; R^{*} $$ where $a \in \Sigma$. The language **denoted** by a regular expression $R$ is written as $L(R)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Definition:** A Regular Expression is a sequence of characters that defines a **search pattern**, mainly used for **lexical analysis** in the front-end of a compiler to identify tokens (keywords, identifiers, operators, literals). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module Mapping:** This topic is foundational for **Module 1 (Lexical Analysis)** and serves as a prerequisite to topics like *Finite Automata*, *Conversion from RE to NFA/DFA*, and *Lex tool* used in the construction of the lexer/lexical analyzer. ### 1.2 The Six Canonical Building Blocks of Regular Expressions | Symbol | Name | Meaning | Set Notation | |---|---|---|---| | $\varnothing$ | Empty Set RE | Denotes the **empty language** (no strings) | $L(\varnothing) = \{\}$ | | $\varepsilon$ | Epsilon | Denotes the **language containing the empty string** | $L(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$ | | $a$ | Literal Symbol | Denotes the language with the single symbol $a$ | $L(a) = \{a\}$ | | $R_1 \cup R_2$ | Union / Alternation ($+$) | Either $R_1$ OR $R_2$ | $L(R_1) \cup L(R_2)$ | | $R_1 \cdot R_2$ | Concatenation | $R_1$ followed by $R_2$ | $L(R_1) \cdot L(R_2)$ | | $R^{*}$ | Kleene Star / Closure | Zero or more repetitions of $R$ | $L(R)^{*}$ | > [!TIP] > **Precedence (Highest to Lowest):** Kleene Star $(^{*})$ → Concatenation $(\cdot)$ → Union $(\cup)$. Always parenthesize for safety: $a \cdot b \cup c$ means $(a \cdot b) \cup c$, NOT $a \cdot (b \cup c)$. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > **🎯 The "Recipe Analogy":** > Think of a Regular Expression as a **recipe** for what a valid word/token should look like. For example, an email address has a recipe: *one-or-more letters, then an `@`, then one-or-more letters, then a `.`, then two-or-more letters*. A regular expression captures this exact recipe using symbols, just like a chef captures a recipe using ingredients and steps. > **🔧 The "Password Validator" Analogy:** > Imagine a door that only opens if you type the right *pattern*. A regular expression is the **bouncer's rulebook**: *"At least one uppercase, followed by 3 digits, then any 2 symbols"*. The door (compiler's lexer) checks every input character by character against this rulebook and either **accepts** (token) or **rejects** (error). ### 1.4 Why Regular Expressions Matter in Compiler Design In the **front-end** of a compiler, the **Lexical Analyzer (Lexer/Scanner)** must break the raw source program into a stream of **tokens** (keywords, identifiers, constants, operators, punctuation). The pattern for each token class is described using a **regular expression**. The lexer then uses this RE to scan and emit tokens, which is why RE sits at the very heart of Phase 1 of compilation. $$ \text{Source Code} \;\xrightarrow[\text{pattern matching}]{\text{Regular Expressions}}\; \text{Tokens} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Syntax Analyzer (Parser)} $$ ### 1.5 Regular Language (The Set RE Describes) The **language** of a regular expression $R$ — denoted $L(R)$ — is the **set of all strings** over $\Sigma$ that match the pattern described by $R$. A language $L$ is called a **regular language** if and only if there exists at least one regular expression $R$ such that $L = L(R)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Favorite Result (Kleene's Theorem):** A language is *regular* $\iff$ it is recognized by some **DFA** $\iff$ it is recognized by some **NFA** $\iff$ it is denoted by some **Regular Expression**. These four characterizations are **equivalent**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Recursive Definition of Regular Expressions (Formal Construction Rules) Let $\Sigma$ be a finite alphabet. The set of **regular expressions over $\Sigma$** is defined recursively as the smallest set $RE(\Sigma)$ satisfying: 1. **Base Case 1 — Empty Set:** $\varnothing \in RE(\Sigma)$, with $L(\varnothing) = \emptyset$ 2. **Base Case 2 — Empty String:** $\varepsilon \in RE(\Sigma)$, with $L(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$ 3. **Base Case 3 — Single Symbol:** For every $a \in \Sigma$, $a \in RE(\Sigma)$, with $L(a) = \{a\}$ 4. **Inductive Case 1 — Union:** If $R_1, R_2 \in RE(\Sigma)$, then $(R_1 \cup R_2) \in RE(\Sigma)$, with $L(R_1 \cup R_2) = L(R_1) \cup L(R_2)$ 5. **Inductive Case 2 — Concatenation:** If $R_1, R_2 \in RE(\Sigma)$, then $(R_1 \cdot R_2) \in RE(\Sigma)$, with $L(R_1 \cdot R_2) = L(R_1) \cdot L(R_2)$ 6. **Inductive Case 3 — Kleene Star:** If $R \in RE(\Sigma)$, then $R^{*} \in RE(\Sigma)$, with $L(R^{*}) = L(R)^{*}$ > [!NOTE] > **Why recursive?** Because every regular expression can be decomposed into a *finite* combination of these three operations (union, concatenation, closure) on smaller regular expressions, mirroring the structure of an arithmetic expression tree. ### 2.2 Extended Regular Expression Operators (KTU 2024 — High-Yield) | Operator | Notation | Meaning | Set-Theoretic Definition | |---|---|---|---| | One or more | $R^{+}$ | $R \cdot R^{*}$ | $L(R^{+}) = L(R) \cdot L(R)^{*}$ | | Zero or one (Optional) | $R?$ | $\varepsilon \cup R$ | $L(R) \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ | | Exactly n times | $R^{\{n\}}$ | $R$ repeated $n$ times | $L(R)^{n}$ | | Range / Interval | $R^{\{m,n\}}$ | $R$ repeated $m$ to $n$ times | $\bigcup_{i=m}^{n} L(R)^{i}$ | | Character class | $[a-z]$ | Any one lowercase letter | $a \cup b \cup \ldots \cup z$ | | Complement | $\neg R$ or $\overline{R}$ | Strings NOT in $L(R)$ | $\Sigma^{*} \setminus L(R)$ | | Intersection | $R_1 \cap R_2$ | Strings in BOTH | $L(R_1) \cap L(R_2)$ | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Algebraic Identities of Regular Expressions) > [!IMPORTANT] > These identities are **exam gold**. They are routinely asked for 3–7 marks in KTU University Exams for simplifying REs. | # | Identity (Law) | Mathematical Form | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Commutative (Union)** | $R_1 \cup R_2 = R_2 \cup R_1$ | | 2 | **Associative (Union)** | $(R_1 \cup R_2) \cup R_3 = R_1 \cup (R_2 \cup R_3)$ | | 3 | **Associative (Concatenation)** | $(R_1 \cdot R_2) \cdot R_3 = R_1 \cdot (R_2 \cdot R_3)$ | | 4 | **Distributive ($\cdot$ over $\cup$)** | $R_1 \cdot (R_2 \cup R_3) = (R_1 \cdot R_2) \cup (R_1 \cdot R_3)$ | | 5 | **Distributive ($\cup$ over $\cdot$)** | $(R_1 \cup R_2) \cdot R_3 = (R_1 \cdot R_3) \cup (R_2 \cdot R_3)$ | | 6 | **Identity (Union)** | $R \cup \varnothing = R$ | | 7 | **Identity (Concatenation)** | $R \cdot \varepsilon = \varepsilon \cdot R = R$ | | 8 | **Annihilator (Concatenation)** | $R \cdot \varnothing = \varnothing \cdot R = \varnothing$ | | 9 | **Idempotent (Union)** | $R \cup R = R$ | | 10 | **Kleene Star (Empty)** | $\varnothing^{*} = \{\varepsilon\} = \varepsilon$ | | 11 | **Kleene Star (Epsilon)** | $\varepsilon^{*} = \varepsilon$ | | 12 | **Kleene Star of Union** | $(R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} = R^{*}$ | | 13 | **Kleene Star of Star** | $(R^{*})^{*} = R^{*}$ | | 14 | **Distribution of Star** | $R^{*} \cdot R = R \cdot R^{*}$ | | 15 | **Positive Closure Relation** | $R^{*} = R^{+} \cup \varepsilon$ and $R^{+} = R^{*} \cdot R$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall — Concatenation is NOT Commutative:** $R_1 \cdot R_2 \neq R_2 \cdot R_1$ in general. For example, $a \cdot b = ab$ but $b \cdot a = ba$. Students often lose marks by treating $\cdot$ like $+$. ### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where RE is Used Beyond Compilers | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Compiler Design (KTU Core)** | Tokenization, pattern matching in source code | | **Text Editors** (VS Code, Sublime) | Find/Replace, syntax highlighting | | **Operating Systems** | `grep`, `sed`, `awk` shell tools | | **Network Security** | Intrusion detection using signature matching | | **Databases** | SQL `LIKE` pattern matching | | **Bioinformatics** | DNA sequence pattern detection (e.g., `(ATG)([ACGT]{3})*(TAA|TAG|TGA)$`) | | **Web Development** | Form input validation (email, phone, password) | ### 2.5 Two-Phase Equivalence: RE ⇄ Finite Automaton The single most important theoretical result for KTU 2024 is that **Regular Expressions and Finite Automata are equivalent in expressive power**: $$ \text{RE} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Kleene's Theorem}}\; \text{FA} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{FA} \;\xrightarrow{\text{State Elimination / GNFA}}\; \text{RE} $$ The standard algorithmic chain taught in KTU Module 1 is: $$ \text{RE} \;\xrightarrow{\text{McNaughton-Yamada-Thompson}}\; \varepsilon\text{-NFA} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Subset Construction}}\; \text{DFA} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Minimization}}\; \text{Minimal DFA} $$ ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Example 1 — Construct RE for the Language of All Strings Over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ Starting with `a` and Ending with `b` **Step 1 — Decompose the language structurally.** A string that starts with `a` and ends with `b` has the form: $$ a \;(\text{middle})\; b $$ **Step 2 — Describe the middle.** The middle can be **any string** (including the empty string) over $\{a, b\}$, which is denoted by: $$ (a \cup b)^{*} $$ **Step 3 — Concatenate the three parts.** $$ R = a \cdot (a \cup b)^{*} \cdot b $$ **Step 4 — Verify against sample strings.** - $\varepsilon$: Rejected ✓ (does not start with $a$) - `ab`: Accepted (middle $=\varepsilon$) ✓ - `aab`: Accepted (middle $=a$) ✓ - `abbb`: Accepted (middle $=bb$) ✓ - `ba`: Rejected (does not start with $a$) ✓ - `a`: Rejected (does not end with $b$) ✓ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{R = a(a \cup b)^{*}b} $$ --- ### 3.2 Example 2 — Construct RE for Strings of Even Length Over $\Sigma = \{0,1\}$ **Step 1 — Recognize the structural property.** An even-length string has length $0, 2, 4, 6, \ldots$, i.e., length $= 2k$ for some $k \geq 0$. **Step 2 — Express as a union of even powers.** $$ L = \Sigma^{0} \cup \Sigma^{2} \cup \Sigma^{4} \cup \ldots = \bigcup_{k=0}^{\infty} \Sigma^{2k} $$ **Step 3 — Rewrite using the Kleene star of a pair.** $$ L = \left( (0 \cup 1)(0 \cup 1) \right)^{*} = \left( (0 \cup 1)^{2} \right)^{*} $$ **Step 4 — Alternative simpler form using the standard identity.** Since any two symbols can be any combination: $$ R = \big((00) \cup (01) \cup (10) \cup (11)\big)^{*} $$ **Step 5 — Even shorter using character class notation.** $$ R = (00 \cup 01 \cup 10 \cup 11)^{*} $$ **Verification:** - $\varepsilon$ (length 0): Accepted ✓ - `01` (length 2): Accepted ✓ - `1100` (length 4): Accepted ✓ - `0` (length 1, odd): Rejected ✓ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{R = (00 \cup 01 \cup 10 \cup 11)^{*}} $$ --- ### 3.3 Example 3 — Construct RE for Strings Over $\{a, b\}$ with At Least One `a` and At Least One `b` **Step 1 — Language decomposition.** The set of all strings containing both `a` and `b` = (All strings) − (Strings with no `a`) − (Strings with no `b`) + (Strings with neither — empty set). **Step 2 — Apply set operations.** $$ L = \Sigma^{*} \setminus \left(L_{\text{no } a} \cup L_{\text{no } b}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Compute $L_{\text{no } a} = b^{*}$, $L_{\text{no } b} = a^{*}$.** $$ L = (a \cup b)^{*} \setminus (a^{*} \cup b^{*}) $$ **Step 4 — Express as a positive (constructive) RE.** A string with at least one `a` and at least one `b` can be described as the concatenation of any one of three patterns: - An `a` appears first, then a `b`: $\;(a \cup b)^{*} a (a \cup b)^{*} b (a \cup b)^{*}$ - A `b` appears first, then an `a`: $\;(a \cup b)^{*} b (a \cup b)^{*} a (a \cup b)^{*}$ **Step 5 — Combine via union and simplify using identity $L_{\text{contains both}} = (a \cup b)^{*} (ab \cup ba) (a \cup b)^{*}$.** **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{R = (a \cup b)^{*} (ab \cup ba) (a \cup b)^{*}} $$ **Alternative (also correct):** $$ R = a^{+} b^{+} (a \cup b)^{*} \;\cup\; b^{+} a^{+} (a \cup b)^{*} $$ --- ### 3.4 Example 4 — Construct RE for Floating-Point Numbers (KTU 2024 Classic) **Step 1 — Identify the structure of a valid floating-point number.** A floating-point literal can be: `[optional sign] [digits] . [digits] [optional exponent]` where the exponent is `[E/e][optional sign][digits]`. **Step 2 — Build each component RE.** - Optional sign: $(\cup \cup -)$ (or simply `(+ \cup -)?`) - Integer part: `(digit)+` where `digit = (0 \cup 1 \cup 2 \cup 3 \cup 4 \cup 5 \cup 6 \cup 7 \cup 8 \cup 9)` - Decimal point: `.` - Fractional part: `(digit)+` - Exponent: `((E \cup e)(\cup \cup -)? (digit)^{+})?` **Step 3 — Combine.** $$ R = (\cup \cup -)? \cdot (digit)^{+} \cdot . \cdot (digit)^{+} \cdot ((E \cup e) \cdot (\cup \cup -)? \cdot (digit)^{+})? $$ **Step 4 — Substitute digit definition.** $$ \boxed{R = (\cup \cup -)?\, (0 \cup 1 \cup \ldots \cup 9)^{+}\, .\, (0 \cup 1 \cup \ldots \cup 9)^{+}\, ((E \cup e)(\cup \cup -)?\,(0 \cup 1 \cup \ldots \cup 9)^{+})?} $$ **Verification examples:** `+3.14`, `-0.5E-10`, `2.718`, `1.0E+5` all accepted ✓. --- ### 3.5 Example 5 — Simplify an RE Using Algebraic Identities (Prove) **Prove that:** $\;R = (1 \cup \varepsilon)(00)^{*}1 = (00)^{*}1 \cup (00)^{*}01\;$... Wait, let us do a more standard simplification. **Simplify:** $\;R = (a \cup \varepsilon) a^{*} b^{*}$ **Step 1 — Apply distributive law.** $$ (a \cup \varepsilon) a^{*} b^{*} = a \cdot a^{*} \cdot b^{*} \cup \varepsilon \cdot a^{*} \cdot b^{*} $$ **Step 2 — Apply identity $\varepsilon \cdot X = X$.** $$ a \cdot a^{*} \cdot b^{*} \cup a^{*} \cdot b^{*} $$ **Step 3 — Apply identity $R \cdot R^{*} = R^{+}$ (positive closure).** $$ a^{+} b^{*} \cup a^{*} b^{*} $$ **Step 4 — Factor: $a^{*} b^{*}$ already contains $a^{+} b^{*}$ when $a^{+}$ has $\geq 1$ `a`s (since $a^{+} \subseteq a^{*}$). So the union is absorbed.** $$ a^{*} b^{*} $$ **Step 5 — Final verification:** $$ \boxed{(a \cup \varepsilon) a^{*} b^{*} = a^{*} b^{*}} $$ --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Standard KTU 2024 RE Construction Problems | Language Description | Regular Expression | Length Property | |---|---|---| | All strings over $\{a,b\}$ | $(a \cup b)^{*}$ | Any | | Strings starting with `a` | $a(a \cup b)^{*}$ | $\geq 1$ | | Strings ending with `b` | $(a \cup b)^{*}b$ | $\geq 1$ | | Strings of length exactly 3 | $(a \cup b)(a \cup b)(a \cup b)$ | $= 3$ | | Strings of length $\leq 3$ | $\varepsilon \cup (a \cup b) \cup (a \cup b)^{2} \cup (a \cup b)^{3}$ | $\leq 3$ | | Strings with exactly one `a` | $b^{*} a b^{*}$ | $\geq 1$ | | Strings with at most two `a`s | $b^{*}(a \cup \varepsilon)b^{*}(a \cup \varepsilon)b^{*}$ | any | | Strings without two consecutive `a`s | $b^{*}(a \cup \varepsilon b)^{*} = b^{*}(ab \cup b)^{*}$ | any | | Strings where every `a` is followed by `b` | $(\varepsilon \cup ab)^{*}$ | any | | Strings divisible by 3 in length | $((a \cup b)(a \cup b)(a \cup b))^{*}$ | multiple of 3 | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** When asked *"Write an RE for L"*, always start by writing **3 sample accepted strings** and **2 sample rejected strings**, then look for a **common pattern** among accepted strings (start, end, structure, repetition). --- ### 3.7 KTU 2024 Important Note — RE Construction Strategy (The 5-Step Heuristic) When facing a *"Construct a Regular Expression for..."* question in the KTU exam, follow this exact 5-step heuristic for full marks: 1. **Identify the Alphabet** $\Sigma$ explicitly. 2. **List 3-4 sample strings** in the language. 3. **Identify the structural invariant** — does the string start with a fixed symbol? End with one? Have a middle part? Have a sub-pattern repeated? 4. **Compose the RE** using the three operators ($\cup$, $\cdot$, $^{*}$) following precedence rules. 5. **Verify** by testing 1-2 accepted and 1-2 rejected strings against your RE. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Role of Regular Expressions in the Compiler Front-End ```mermaid flowchart LR sourceCode["SOURCE CODE
Raw Program Text"] --> lexer["LEXICAL ANALYZER
SCANNER"] regexBank["REGULAR EXPRESSIONS
Pattern Definitions"] --> lexer lexer --> tokenStream["TOKEN STREAM
id, num, op, kw, ..."] tokenStream --> parser["SYNTAX ANALYZER
PARSER"] parser --> ast["ABSTRACT SYNTAX TREE
AST"] ast --> semantic["SEMANTIC ANALYZER"] semantic --> ir["INTERMEDIATE CODE
Three Address Code"] regexBank -.describes.-> lexer faEngine["FINITE AUTOMATON
DFA or NFA Engine"] -.simulates.-> lexer ``` **Reading the diagram:** The source code is the *input*. The regex bank holds the pattern definitions (e.g., `[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9]*` for identifiers). The lexical analyzer (typically implemented by a DFA generated from the RE) consumes characters and emits tokens. The token stream feeds the parser in Phase 2. --- ### 4.2 State-Transition Diagram — The Three Core RE Operators Visualized ```mermaid flowchart TD startA(["START"]) --> unionNode{"UNION
R1 or R2"} unionNode -->|matches R1| r1["R1 Sub-Expression
Finite Automaton"] unionNode -->|matches R2| r2["R2 Sub-Expression
Finite Automaton"] r1 --> acceptA([ACCEPT]) r2 --> acceptA startB(["START"]) --> concatNode{{"CONCATENATION
R1 followed by R2"}} concatNode --> r1b["R1 Sub-Automaton"] r1b --> r2b["R2 Sub-Automaton"] r2b --> acceptB([ACCEPT]) startC(["START"]) --> starNode{{"KLEENE STAR
R repeated 0+ times"}} starNode -->|"epsilon skip"| acceptC([ACCEPT]) starNode -->|"match R once or more"| rC["R Sub-Automaton"] rC -->|"loop back"| starNode ``` --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram — The Six Canonical Building Blocks of RE ```mermaid flowchart TB foundation["RECURSIVE DEFINITION OF RE
Smallest set closed under union, concat, star"] foundation --> empty["NULL EMPTY SET
phi
Denotes Empty Language"] foundation --> epsilon["EPSILON
epsilon
Denotes Language with Empty String"] foundation --> literal["LITERAL SYMBOL
a in Sigma
Denotes Language with one symbol"] foundation --> unionOp["UNION OPERATOR
R1 union R2
L R1 union L R2"] foundation --> concatOp["CONCATENATION
R1 dot R2
L R1 concatenated with L R2"] foundation --> starOp["KLEENE STAR
R star
Zero or more concatenations of R"] unionOp --> extended["EXTENDED OPERATORS
R+ R question mark R m to n"] concatOp --> extended starOp --> extended ``` --- ### 4.4 RE-to-FA Conversion Pipeline (KTU 2024 Critical Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR re["REGULAR EXPRESSION
R"] --> step1["STEP 1: Parse RE
Build syntax tree"] step1 --> step2["STEP 2: McNaughton-Yamada-Thompson
RE to epsilon-NFA"] step2 --> step3["STEP 3: Subset Construction
epsilon-NFA to DFA"] step3 --> step4["STEP 4: DFA Minimization
Hopcroft or Table-Filling"] step4 --> finalDFA["MINIMAL DFA
Used by Lexical Analyzer"] finalDFA --> lexerUse["LEXICAL ANALYZER
Token Recognition"] ``` --- ### 4.5 Tree-Structure Diagram — Building a Complex RE from Primitives ```mermaid flowchart TB root["RE: a union b STAR dot c PLUS
A complete regular expression"] root --> union1["union
R1 = a"] root --> concat1["dot
CONCATENATION"] union1 --> aNode["a
Literal Symbol"] concat1 --> star1["STAR
R2 = b STAR"] concat1 --> plus1["PLUS
R3 = c PLUS"] star1 --> bNode["b
Literal Symbol"] plus1 --> cNode["c
Literal Symbol"] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a Regular Expression. List the six components used in the recursive definition of Regular Expressions.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Regular Expression (RE)** is a compact, formal, algebraic notation used to denote a **regular language** — i.e., a language recognized by a finite automaton. The six components of the recursive definition over an alphabet $\Sigma$ are: | # | Component | Notation | Language Denoted | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Empty set | $\varnothing$ | $\emptyset$ | | 2 | Empty string | $\varepsilon$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ | | 3 | Literal symbol | $a \in \Sigma$ | $\{a\}$ | | 4 | Union | $R_1 \cup R_2$ | $L(R_1) \cup L(R_2)$ | | 5 | Concatenation | $R_1 \cdot R_2$ | $L(R_1) \cdot L(R_2)$ | | 6 | Kleene Star | $R^{*}$ | $L(R)^{*}$ | > [!TIP] > **3-Mark Marking Key:** Definition (1 mark) + listing all six components in a table (2 marks). --- #### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State and prove the identity: $R \cdot \varnothing = \varnothing$. Also explain its significance in compiler design.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Statement:** For any regular expression $R$, $R \cdot \varnothing = \varnothing$. **Proof (by structural induction on $R$):** *Base Case:* For $R = \varnothing$: $\varnothing \cdot \varnothing = \varnothing$ (trivially true by definition). *Inductive Case (Concatenation):* Assume $R = R_1 \cdot R_2$. Then: $$ R \cdot \varnothing = (R_1 \cdot R_2) \cdot \varnothing = R_1 \cdot (R_2 \cdot \varnothing) = R_1 \cdot \varnothing = \varnothing $$ *Inductive Case (Kleene Star):* Assume $R = S^{*}$. Then: $$ R \cdot \varnothing = S^{*} \cdot \varnothing = \varnothing $$ because no string from $L(S^{*})$ can be concatenated with anything from $\varnothing = \emptyset$ to produce a string. **Significance:** It is the **annihilator property** — concatenating *anything* with the empty set yields the empty set. In compilers, this is used in **RE simplification** during lexical-analyzer construction to prune unreachable/dead productions. --- ### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Construct Regular Expressions for the following languages over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$. State the structural property you used. **\[7 Marks\]** (i) $L_1$ = All strings **starting with `a` and ending with `b`**. (ii) $L_2$ = All strings of **length exactly 3**. (iii) $L_3$ = All strings **not containing two consecutive `a`s**. **(b)** Prove the identity $(R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} = R^{*}$ using algebraic laws of Regular Expressions. **\[7 Marks\]** --- **Model Solution (Question A):** **(a)(i) Strings starting with `a` and ending with `b`:** **[2 Marks]** A string with this property must begin with `a`, then have *any* middle, then end with `b`. The middle can be any string over $\Sigma$, including $\varepsilon$. $$ R_1 = a \cdot (a \cup b)^{*} \cdot b $$ **Verification:** `ab` (middle = $\varepsilon$) ✓, `aab` ✓, `abbb` ✓. `ba` ✗ (does not start with $a$). **(a)(ii) Strings of length exactly 3:** **[2 Marks]** Each of the 3 positions can be `a` or `b`. We concatenate three copies of $(a \cup b)$. $$ R_2 = (a \cup b)(a \cup b)(a \cup b) $$ **Verification:** `aaa`, `aba`, `bab`, `bbb` all accepted ✓. `ab` (length 2) ✗. **(a)(iii) Strings not containing two consecutive `a`s:** **[3 Marks]** **Strategy:** Every `a` in such a string must either (a) be at the end, or (b) be followed by a `b`. So every `a` appears as either `a` (at end) or `ab` (followed by `b`). **Construction:** $$ R_3 = b^{*} (a b \cup a)^{*} $$ Or equivalently, since the language can be partitioned into blocks of `b`s and isolated `a`s: $$ R_3 = b^{*}(ab \cup a \cup \varepsilon)^{*} $$ **Verification:** `b`, `ab`, `bab`, `abbab` ✓. `aa` ✗, `baa` ✗. **(b) Proof of $(R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} = R^{*}$:** **[7 Marks]** We prove both directions. **Direction 1: $R^{*} \subseteq (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*}$** Since $R \subseteq (R \cup \varepsilon)$, by the closure of Kleene star under supersets: $$ R^{*} = (R)^{*} \subseteq (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} $$ **Direction 2: $(R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} \subseteq R^{*}$** Any string $w \in L((R \cup \varepsilon)^{*})$ is the concatenation of zero or more strings, each either from $L(R)$ or $\{\varepsilon\}$. The $\varepsilon$ choices contribute nothing to the concatenation. Thus $w$ is the concatenation of some (possibly zero) strings from $L(R)$, which means $w \in L(R^{*})$. **Formal algebraic proof using identities:** $$ \begin{aligned} (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} &= \varepsilon \cup (R \cup \varepsilon)(R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} && \text{(by definition of star: } X^{*} = \varepsilon \cup X \cdot X^{*}) \\ &= \varepsilon \cup (R \cdot (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} \cup \varepsilon \cdot (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*}) && \text{(distributive law)} \\ &= \varepsilon \cup R \cdot (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} \cup (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} && \text{(identity } \varepsilon \cdot X = X) \\ &= (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} \cup R \cdot (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} && \text{(rearranging, idempotent union)} \\ &= (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} \cup R \cdot (R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} && \text{(this is a fixed-point equation)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ By the unique fixed-point property of Kleene star, this implies $(R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} = R^{*}$. $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Write Regular Expressions for the following languages over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$: **\[7 Marks\]** (i) $L_1$ = All strings that **end in `01`**. (ii) $L_2$ = All strings of **even length**. (iii) $L_3$ = All strings that **contain the substring `0110`**. **(b)** Using the algebraic identities of Regular Expressions, simplify the following RE step-by-step: $R = (a \cup b)^{*}(a \cup b)(a \cup b)^{*} \cup (a \cup b)^{*}$. **\[7 Marks\]** --- **Model Solution (Question B):** **(a)(i) Strings ending in `01`:** **[2 Marks]** A string ending in `01` has the form: *anything* + `01`. $$ R_1 = (0 \cup 1)^{*} \cdot 0 \cdot 1 = (0 \cup 1)^{*}01 $$ **Verification:** `01` (prefix = $\varepsilon$) ✓, `101` ✗ (ends in `01`? No, ends in `01`? Wait — `101` ends in `01`? Yes! It ends in `01`. ✓). `110` ✗. **(a)(ii) Strings of even length:** **[2 Marks]** Even length = length $0, 2, 4, \ldots$. We pair consecutive symbols. $$ R_2 = ((0 \cup 1)(0 \cup 1))^{*} $$ **Verification:** $\varepsilon$ ✓, `01` ✓, `0011` ✓, `0` ✗. **(a)(iii) Strings containing substring `0110`:** **[3 Marks]** The substring `0110` can appear anywhere — at the start, in the middle, or at the end. So surround it with arbitrary prefixes and suffixes. $$ R_3 = (0 \cup 1)^{*} \cdot 0 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 0 \cdot (0 \cup 1)^{*} = (0 \cup 1)^{*}0110(0 \cup 1)^{*} $$ **Verification:** `0110` ✓, `00110011` ✓, `01101` ✓. `00110` ✗. **(b) Simplify $R = (a \cup b)^{*}(a \cup b)(a \cup b)^{*} \cup (a \cup b)^{*}$:** **[7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Recognize the structure.** The first part is a non-empty string (positive closure) and the second part is the Kleene star (any string, including empty). $$ (a \cup b)^{*}(a \cup b)(a \cup b)^{*} = (a \cup b)^{+} \quad \text{(by definition of } R^{+} = R^{*} \cdot R \text{)} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute back:** $$ R = (a \cup b)^{+} \cup (a \cup b)^{*} $$ **Step 3 — Apply absorption (since $L((a \cup b)^{+}) \subseteq L((a \cup b)^{*})$):** The set of non-empty strings is a *subset* of all strings, so the union collapses to just the larger set. $$ R = (a \cup b)^{*} $$ **Step 4 — Final simplification result:** $$ \boxed{R = (a \cup b)^{*}} $$ **Verification:** $L(R) = \Sigma^{*}$ — the language of *all* strings. Original RE: $(a \cup b)^{*}(a \cup b)(a \cup b)^{*} \cup (a \cup b)^{*}$ — first part is non-empty strings, second part is all strings (including empty). The union is clearly all strings. ✓ --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing $\varnothing$ with $\varepsilon$** — Empty set $\varnothing$ denotes NO strings; $\varepsilon$ denotes ONE string (the empty string). Mixing these is a guaranteed mark deduction. > > 2. **Wrong Precedence** — Writing $ab \cup c$ when meaning $a(b \cup c)$. The default precedence is: star $>$ concatenation $>$ union. Always parenthesize. > > 3. **Treating concatenation as commutative** — $ab \neq ba$ in general. This violates the algebraic structure. > > 4. **Forgetting to verify the RE** — KTU examiners reward verification. Always test 2 accepted and 1 rejected string. > > 5. **Over-complicated REs** — If a simpler form exists, present it. e.g., $(a \cup b)^{*}01(a \cup b)^{*}$ is preferable to $(\varepsilon \cup a \cup b)^{+}01(\varepsilon \cup (a \cup b)(a \cup b)^{*})$. > > 6. **Missing the language precisely** — Read the question: "starts with `a`" vs "contains `a`" are very different. Under- or over-accepting strings will cost 1-2 marks. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Notes — Regular Expressions (KTU 2024)** - ✅ **RE = Compact notation for regular languages** — used in lexical analysis to define token patterns. - ✅ **Six building blocks**: $\varnothing$, $\varepsilon$, $a$, $\cup$, $\cdot$, $^{*}$. - ✅ **Precedence**: $^{*} > \cdot > \cup$ (use parentheses liberally). - ✅ **Kleene's Theorem**: RE $\Leftrightarrow$ NFA $\Leftrightarrow$ DFA $\Leftrightarrow$ regular language (all equivalent). - ✅ **$R^{+}$ vs $R^{*}$**: $R^{+} = RR^{*}$, $R^{*} = R^{+} \cup \varepsilon$. Use $R^{+}$ for "one or more". - ✅ **Key identities to memorize**: - $R \cup \varnothing = R$ - $R \cdot \varepsilon = R$ - $R \cdot \varnothing = \varnothing$ - $\varnothing^{*} = \varepsilon^{*}$ - $(R \cup \varepsilon)^{*} = R^{*}$ - $(R^{*})^{*} = R^{*}$ - $R^{*} \cdot R = R \cdot R^{*}$ - ✅ **Construction strategy**: Identify alphabet → list samples → find pattern → compose using 3 operators → verify. - ✅ **Extended operators** (KTU 2024): $R^{+}$, $R?$, $R^{\{m,n\}}$, character classes $[a-z]$. - ✅ **RE-to-FA chain** (Module 1 workflow): RE → $\varepsilon$-NFA (Thompson's construction) → DFA (subset construction) → Minimal DFA. - ✅ **Compiler context**: RE defines *patterns*, FA *recognizes* them. Lex tool (e.g., `lex`/`flex`) auto-generates the FA from REs. - ✅ **Common exam patterns**: - "Construct RE for L where L = ..." - "Prove identity X = Y using RE algebra" - "Simplify the given RE" - "State and explain Kleene's Theorem" - ✅ **Discipline**: Always parenthesize, always verify, always state structural property. > **End of Module 1 — Regular Expressions Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PECIT751 Compiler Design).**

topics.title: From Regular Expression to Scanner: FSA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** From Regular Expression to Scanner: FSA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: From Regular Expression to Scanner via FSA

A **Scanner** (also called a **Lexical Analyzer** or **Lexer**) is the first phase of a compiler. It reads the raw input source code as a stream of characters and groups them into meaningful sequences called **Lexemes**, producing **Tokens** as output. The scanner itself is typically implemented as a **Finite State Automaton (FSA)** — a mathematical model of computation that accepts or rejects a string by traversing through a finite number of states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** A *Finite State Automaton* is formally defined as a **5-tuple** $\mathbf{(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)}$ where $Q$ is a finite set of states, $\Sigma$ is a finite input alphabet, $\delta$ is the transition function, $q_0$ is the start state, and $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of accepting/final states.

The pipeline of moving from a **Regular Expression (RE)** to a working **Scanner** involves three well-defined engineering stages:

1. **Regular Expression $\rightarrow$ NFA** (Thompson's Construction / $\varepsilon$-NFA)
2. **NFA $\rightarrow$ DFA** (Subset Construction Algorithm)
3. **DFA $\rightarrow$ Minimized DFA** (Hopcroft's / Partition Refinement Algorithm)

The final minimized DFA is then encoded (by tools like **Lex** / **Flex**) into the executable scanner program.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Bouncer at a Club Entrance

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Nightclub Bouncer"**
>
> Imagine a bouncer standing at the door of a high-end nightclub. He has a **rule book** (the Regular Expression) that describes exactly what a valid "VIP Entry Pass" looks like — say, two uppercase letters followed by three digits (e.g., `AB123`).
>
> - The **bouncer's mental checklist** is the **FSA**: he reads one character at a time, and depending on what he sees, he moves between "moods" (states): *expecting letter 1* $\rightarrow$ *expecting letter 2* $\rightarrow$ *expecting digit 1* $\rightarrow$ *expecting digit 2* $\rightarrow$ *expecting digit 3* $\rightarrow$ *ACCEPT (open the door)*.
> - If any character violates the rule, he moves to a *DEAD state* (REJECT) and turns the guest away.
> - A **Scanner** in a compiler is exactly this bouncer, except instead of VIP passes, it recognizes language tokens like `if`, `while`, `123`, `"hello"`, or `>=`.
>
> The **beauty** of the FSA is that for any regular expression, we can mechanically construct a bouncer (automaton) that *guarantees* to accept exactly the strings described by the RE — no judgment calls, no ambiguity.

## 1.3 Why FSA? The Engineering Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not just use the Regular Expression directly?**
> 1. **Speed:** A DFA-based scanner is **$O(n)$** where $n$ is the input length. Direct RE matching via backtracking is **$O(2^n)$** in the worst case.
> 2. **Determinism:** A DFA has *exactly one transition per symbol per state* — making the implementation trivially a `switch-case` or table-lookup.
> 3. **Compiler Correctness:** The compiler must match patterns in **O(n)** to remain practical for million-line source files. The scanner must also handle the **longest match** rule and **rule priority** for tokens like `=` vs `==`.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Alphabet ($\Sigma$)** | A finite, non-empty set of input symbols (e.g., ASCII characters). |
| **String** | A finite sequence of symbols from $\Sigma$. |
| **Language ($L$)** | Any set of strings over $\Sigma$. |
| **Regular Expression (RE)** | A compact algebraic notation to denote regular languages. |
| **NFA** | Nondeterministic Finite Automaton — may have multiple transitions on the same symbol, or $\varepsilon$-transitions. |
| **DFA** | Deterministic Finite Automaton — exactly one transition per symbol per state. |
| **$\varepsilon$-closure** | The set of states reachable from a state using only $\varepsilon$-transitions. |
| **Lexeme** | The actual character sequence matched in the source code. |
| **Token** | A categorized pair `<token-name, attribute-value>` output by the scanner. |
| **Longest Match** | The scanner picks the longest prefix of input matching any pattern. |
| **Rule Priority** | If two patterns match equally long prefixes, the one declared first wins. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Conversion Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)
