**Step 2 — i = 1:** No earlier non-terminals. No substitution. $A_1$ has no immediate LR. ✓

**Step 3 — i = 2, j = 1:** $A_2 \rightarrow A_3\,d$ — leftmost is $A_3$, not $A_1$. No substitution. No immediate LR in $A_2$. ✓

**Step 4 — i = 3, j = 1:** $A_3 \rightarrow A_1\,e$ — substitute $A_1 \rightarrow A_2c \mid a$:

$$A_3 \rightarrow A_2 c\,e \mid a\,e$$

**Step 5 — i = 3, j = 2:** New $A_3 \rightarrow A_2 c e$ — substitute $A_2 \rightarrow A_3 d \mid b$:

$$A_3 \rightarrow A_3 d\,c\,e \mid b\,c\,e$$

Keep the other production:

$$A_3 \rightarrow a\,e$$

**Step 6 — Eliminate immediate LR in $A_3$:**

$A_3$ has $A_3 \rightarrow A_3 dce$ (LR) and $A_3 \rightarrow ae$, $A_3 \rightarrow bce$ (non-LR).

Introduce $A_3'$:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
A_1   &\rightarrow A_2\,c \mid a \\
A_2   &\rightarrow A_3\,d \mid b \\
A_3   &\rightarrow a\,e\,A_3' \mid b\,c\,e\,A_3' \\
A_3'  &\rightarrow d\,c\,e\,A_3' \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Distribution of 7 marks:**
- 1 mark: correct outer/inner loop execution
- 2 marks: two substitution steps
- 2 marks: classification of α/β
- 1 mark: introduction of A₃′
- 1 mark: final grammar correctness

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions — Question B

### Solution to B(a) — [7 Marks]

**Eliminating LR from E:**

$$E \rightarrow E+T \mid E-T \mid T$$

- $\alpha_1 = +T$, $\alpha_2 = -T$, $\beta_1 = T$.

Introduce **E′**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E  &\rightarrow T\,E' \\
E' &\rightarrow +T\,E' \mid -T\,E' \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Eliminating LR from T:**

$$T \rightarrow T*F \mid T/F \mid F$$

- $\alpha_1 = *F$, $\alpha_2 = /F$, $\beta_1 = F$.

Introduce **T′**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T  &\rightarrow F\,T' \\
T' &\rightarrow *F\,T' \mid /F\,T' \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final grammar (E, T replaced; F unchanged):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
E  &\rightarrow T\,E' \\
E' &\rightarrow +T\,E' \mid -T\,E' \mid \varepsilon \\
T  &\rightarrow F\,T' \\
T' &\rightarrow *F\,T' \mid /F\,T' \mid \varepsilon \\
F  &\rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Distribution of 7 marks:**
- 2 marks: LR elimination from E
- 2 marks: LR elimination from T
- 1 mark: correct introduction of E′ and T′
- 1 mark: ε productions
- 1 mark: F kept untouched (correct observation)

---

### Solution to B(b) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Draw the dependency graph.**

Nodes: $\{P, Q, R\}$.  
Edges are added for each production $X \rightarrow Y\alpha$:

| Production | Edge Added |
|------------|------------|
| $P \rightarrow QR$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ |
| $P \rightarrow s$ | (terminal, no edge) |
| $Q \rightarrow SP$ | $Q \rightarrow S$ |
| $Q \rightarrow t$ | (terminal) |
| $R \rightarrow Qa$ | $R \rightarrow Q$ |

We must also include **S** as a node. From $Q \rightarrow SP$, we get $Q \rightarrow S$. But $S$ has productions, so we check: the grammar has $P, Q, R$ — no production for $S$ is given. So $S$ is a dead-end.

**Step 2 — Detect cycles.**

- $P \rightarrow Q$ exists.
- $Q \rightarrow S$ exists (S is leaf).
- $R \rightarrow Q$ exists.
- Is there a path $Q \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow Q$? No.
- Is there a path $P \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow P$? No.
- Is there a path $R \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow R$? No.

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

**The grammar is NOT left-recursive.** There is no cycle in the dependency graph, and no non-terminal can derive itself through any number of steps. Top-down parsing can be attempted **without** left-recursion elimination, although other issues (like left factoring) may still arise.

**Distribution of 7 marks:**
- 2 marks: correct graph construction
- 2 marks: cycle analysis
- 2 marks: conclusion (with justification)
- 1 mark: clean diagram or tabular presentation

---

## 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ε production in A′.** Without $A' \rightarrow \varepsilon$, the grammar generates a **strict subset** of the original language. **–2 marks** typical deduction.
> 2. **Dropping a non-left-recursive β during substitution.** When you substitute $A_j$'s productions, students often forget to keep $A_i \rightarrow \beta_i$ intact. **–1 to –2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing the order of loops.** The j-loop **must run** for all $j < i$ **before** immediate-LR elimination of $A_i$. Reversing this order leaves indirect LR intact. **–2 marks**.
> 4. **Naming the new non-terminal incorrectly.** Must be a **fresh** symbol (e.g., A′ or A₁) not already in the grammar; reusing breaks the algorithm's correctness proof. **–1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing left recursion with left factoring.** Left recursion = A derives Aα. Left factoring = A has two productions with the same prefix. They require **different** transformations. **–1 mark** for mislabelling.
> 6. **Missing indirect recursion check.** For multi-non-terminal grammars, students often eliminate only immediate LR and claim "done". Full marks require the double-loop algorithm. **–3 marks**.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Eliminating Left Recursion**

- ✅ **Definition:** $A \Rightarrow^+ A\alpha$ — a non-terminal derives a sentential form beginning with itself.
- ✅ **Two types:** Immediate (1-step) and Indirect (multi-step / cycle).
- ✅ **Why eliminate?** Top-down parsers (LL(1), recursive-descent) cannot handle it — leads to infinite recursion.
- ✅ **Immediate-LR Template:**
  - Old: $A \rightarrow A\alpha_1 \mid \ldots \mid A\alpha_m \mid \beta_1 \mid \ldots \mid \beta_n$
  - New: $A \rightarrow \beta_1 A' \mid \ldots \mid \beta_n A'$
  - New: $A' \rightarrow \alpha_1 A' \mid \ldots \mid \alpha_m A' \mid \varepsilon$
- ✅ **General Algorithm:** Order non-terminals as $A_1, \ldots, A_n$. For each $i$, for each $j < i$, substitute $A_j$'s productions into $A_i$'s productions where $A_i \rightarrow A_j\gamma$; then eliminate immediate LR in $A_i$.
- ✅ **Fresh non-terminal naming** is mandatory (e.g., A′, A₁).
- ✅ **ε production** in the new non-terminal is essential to preserve zero-iteration case.
- ✅ **Language preservation:** $L(G_{old}) = L(G_{new})$.
- ✅ **Detection alternative:** Look for cycles in the **dependency graph** (edges $A \rightarrow B$ if $A \rightarrow B\alpha$).
- ✅ **Pair with Left Factoring** for full LL(1) compatibility.
- ✅ **Time complexity:** $O(n^2 \cdot \vert P\vert)$ for the general algorithm.
- ✅ **Apply to:** Arithmetic expressions, programming language statements, type expressions, parameter lists — any grammar used in LL-style parsers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backtrack-free Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Backtrack-free Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backtrack-free Parsing (Definition – KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)**
> Backtrack-free parsing is a deterministic top-down parsing strategy in which the parser, at every step, makes a unique and unambiguous decision about which production rule of the grammar to apply **without ever needing to undo a prior decision**. For a context-free grammar $G$, the grammar is said to be **backtrack-free** (or equivalently, **LL(1)**) if and only if the parser can identify the correct production rule to use by inspecting only the **single next input symbol (lookahead)**, without ever backtracking over previously consumed input.

In formal mathematical notation, a grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is backtrack-free if for every pair of distinct productions:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha \quad \text{and} \quad A \rightarrow \beta$$

that share the same left-hand non-terminal $A$, the following condition holds:

$$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset$$

Furthermore, if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, then:

$$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Syntax Analysis)**
> Backtrack-free parsing forms the **theoretical foundation** of Predictive Parsing and Recursive Descent Parsing. Without backtrack-free guarantees, the parser would suffer from exponential time complexity in the worst case, making it impractical for compilers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a **railway junction** with multiple tracks diverging ahead. You have a single railway worker standing at each switch with a flashlight pointing only at the **next single track segment (lookahead symbol)**.

- **Backtracking parsing** is like a confused worker who flips a switch, walks a few meters down the track, realizes it's the wrong route, walks all the way back, and tries another switch. This is **slow and wasteful**.
- **Backtrack-free parsing** is like a worker who has a **perfectly printed instruction manual** (the LL(1) parsing table) that, based on the current signal (lookahead symbol), immediately tells which switch to flip. **No going back. No guessing.**

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Non-terminal $A$** | A decision point (junction) |
| **Productions of $A$** | Available outgoing tracks |
| **Lookahead symbol $a$** | The signal/light seen at the junction |
| **Parsing Table $M[A, a]$** | The instruction manual entry |
| **Backtracking** | Trial-and-error, returning to the junction |
| **Backtrack-free** | One-shot, table-driven deterministic decision |

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in backtrack-free parsing:

- **Lookahead $k$** = **1** (single input symbol is sufficient).
- **Time complexity** = **$O(n)$** where $n$ is the length of the input string.
- **Parser class designation** = **LL(1)** — meaning Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1 symbol of lookahead.
- **Parsing table size** = $\vert V_N \vert \times \vert T \cup \{\$\} \vert$ entries, where $\vert V_N \vert$ is the number of non-terminals.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The backtrack-free parsing algorithm operates through a sequence of well-defined logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Input Buffer Initialization**: The input string is loaded into a buffer terminated by the right end-marker **\$** (dollar symbol). This marker is **not** a token of the source language; it signals the parser that the entire input has been consumed.

- **Step 2 — Stack Initialization**: A parsing stack is initialized with the **start symbol $S$** at the bottom and the **end marker \$** at the top. The stack operates on a **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** discipline.

- **Step 3 — Top-of-Stack Lookahead Match**: The parser examines the **top symbol $X$** on the stack and the **current input symbol $a$**:
  - If $X = a = \$$: parsing **terminates successfully** (accept).
  - If $X = a \neq \$$: the terminal $X$ is **matched and popped** from the stack; the input pointer is **advanced** to the next symbol.

- **Step 4 — Non-terminal Expansion (The Core Decision)**: If $X$ is a non-terminal, the parser consults the **parsing table entry $M[X, a]$**:
  - If $M[X, a] = \{X \rightarrow \alpha\}$: the non-terminal $X$ is popped, and the production's right-hand side $\alpha$ is pushed onto the stack **in reverse order** (so that the leftmost symbol of $\alpha$ becomes the new top).
  - If $M[X, a] = \text{error}$: the parser invokes the **error-recovery routine**.

- **Step 5 — Repeat**: Steps 3 and 4 are repeated until either acceptance or an error is reported.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is "Backtrack-free"**: The deterministic table lookup $M[X, a]$ yields at most **one production**. There is never a need to retract, undo, or retry any decision. Each move is committed and irreversible.

## 2.2 Conditions for Backtrack-free (LL(1)) Grammar

For a grammar to admit backtrack-free parsing, the following **three simultaneous conditions** must hold for every non-terminal $A$ with productions $A \rightarrow \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \cdots \mid \alpha_n$:

1. **Disjoint FIRST sets**:
$$\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) = \emptyset \quad \forall \, i \neq j$$

2. **No FIRST–FOLLOW overlap when $\varepsilon$ is involved**:
$$\text{If } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha_i), \text{ then } \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset \quad \forall \, j \neq i$$

3. **At most one $\varepsilon$-production** per non-terminal (i.e., $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is unique if it exists).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Function | Definition | KTU Exam Usage |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ | Set of terminals that begin strings derivable from $\alpha$ | Compute for all non-terminals and production RHS |
| $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Set of terminals that can immediately follow $A$ in some sentential form | Compute iteratively until fixed point |
| $\text{FIRST}^+(alpha)$ | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cup \text{FOLLOW}(A)$ if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, else $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ | Used in LL(1) table construction |
| $M[A, a]$ | Parsing table entry mapping (non-terminal, terminal) to a production | Built from FIRST⁺ sets |
| **\$** | End-of-input sentinel symbol | Always added to $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ where $S$ is start symbol |
| **LL(1)** | Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, **1** token lookahead | Class of grammars parsable backtrack-free |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Backtrack-free parsing underpins virtually **every modern production-quality compiler frontend**:

- **GCC** uses predictive parsing techniques in early phases.
- **LLVM's Clang** relies on a hand-written recursive-descent parser (a direct implementation of LL(k)-style backtrack-free logic) for C/C++/Objective-C.
- **JavaCC**, **ANTLR**, and **YACC+Bison (LALR(1))** all build on these principles.
- **IDE tools** (syntax highlighters, auto-indenters) require linear-time, deterministic parsers — backtrack-free guarantees make this practical on huge source files.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: When a grammar is **not** LL(1), compilers apply **left-factoring** and **left-recursion elimination** to transform it into an equivalent LL(1) grammar. This is a **high-weightage KTU exam topic** in Module 2.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Computing FIRST and FOLLOW Sets

We now derive the algorithms used to construct the **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets that populate the LL(1) parsing table. The derivations follow Aho/Sethi/Ullman conventions as adopted by KTU 2024 scheme.

### 3.1.1 FIRST Set — Formal Definition and Iterative Rules

For any grammar symbol $\alpha \in (V_N \cup V_T)^*$, the set $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ is defined as:

$$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \{ a \in V_T \;\vert\; \alpha \Rightarrow^* a\beta \} \cup \begin{cases} \{\varepsilon\} & \text{if } \alpha \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon \\ \emptyset & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Iterative Construction Rules:**

- **Rule F1**: If $X$ is a terminal, then $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{X\}$.

- **Rule F2**: If $X \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is a production, then add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.

- **Rule F3**: If $X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \cdots Y_k$ is a production, then add all symbols of $\text{FIRST}(Y_1)$ except $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$. If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1)$, then add all symbols of $\text{FIRST}(Y_2)$ except $\varepsilon$, and so on. If $\varepsilon$ is in the FIRST set of **all** $Y_i$, then add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.

### 3.1.2 FOLLOW Set — Formal Definition and Iterative Rules

For any non-terminal $A$, the set $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ is defined as:

$$\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{ a \in V_T \;\vert\; S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta \}$$

**Iterative Construction Rules:**

- **Rule FL1**: Place **\$** in $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ where $S$ is the start symbol.

- **Rule FL2**: If $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ is a production, then add everything in $\text{FIRST}(\beta)$ **except $\varepsilon$** to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

- **Rule FL3**: If $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ is a production, or if $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ and $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then add everything in $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

Consider the classic arithmetic expression grammar:

$$E \rightarrow T E'$$
$$E' \rightarrow + T E' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$
$$T \rightarrow F T'$$
$$T' \rightarrow * F T' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; \text{id}$$

**Step 1 — Compute FIRST sets:**

Apply Rule F1 to terminals: $\text{FIRST}(+) = \{+\}$, $\text{FIRST}(*) = \{*\}$, $\text{FIRST}(() = \{\{\}$, $\text{FIRST}()) = \{\}\}$, $\text{FIRST}(\text{id}) = \{\text{id}\}$.

Apply Rule F3 to $F$:
$$\text{FIRST}(F) = \text{FIRST}((E)) \cup \text{FIRST}(\text{id}) = \{\{\} \cup \{\text{id}\}\}$$

Apply Rule F3 to $T'$:
$$\text{FIRST}(T') = \{*\} \cup \{\varepsilon\} = \{*, \varepsilon\}$$

Apply Rule F3 to $T$:
$$\text{FIRST}(T) = \text{FIRST}(F) = \{\{, \text{id}\}$$

Apply Rule F3 to $E'$:
$$\text{FIRST}(E') = \{+\} \cup \{\varepsilon\} = \{+, \varepsilon\}$$

Apply Rule F3 to $E$:
$$\text{FIRST}(E) = \text{FIRST}(T) = \{\{, \text{id}\}$$

**Step 2 — Compute FOLLOW sets:**

Apply Rule FL1:
$$\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$\}$$

Apply Rule FL2 to $E \rightarrow T E'$:
$\text{FIRST}(E') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{+\}$, so add $+\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$.

Apply Rule FL3 (since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$): add $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(T')$.

Apply Rule FL2 to $T \rightarrow F T'$: $\text{FIRST}(T') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{*\}$, so add $*\ \text{to } \text{FOLLOW}(F)$.

Apply Rule FL3 (since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$): add $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(F)$.

Now compute $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$: apply Rule FL2 to $E' \rightarrow +T E'$ — $\text{FIRST}(E') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{+\}$, so add $+\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$. Then by Rule FL3, add $\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{\$, )\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$ (we will derive $\text{FOLLOW}(E')$ next).

Compute $\text{FOLLOW}(E')$: Apply Rule FL2 to $F \rightarrow (E)$ — $\text{FIRST}()) = \{)\}$, so add $)\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(E)$. Also, since $E$ is the entire RHS body, add everything in $\text{FOLLOW}(F)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(E)$ later. Initially, $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$, )\}$ (from $E' \rightarrow +TE'$ by Rule FL3, and original FL1).

So $\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+, \$, )\}$, and thus $\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{*, +, \$, )\}$ (Rule FL2 gave $*$, Rule FL3 propagated $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$).

**Final Consolidated FIRST/FOLLOW Table:**

| Non-Terminal | FIRST | FOLLOW |
|---|---|---|
| $E$ | $\{ (, \text{id} \}$ | $\{ \$, ) \}$ |
| $E'$ | $\{ +, \varepsilon \}$ | $\{ \$, ) \}$ |
| $T$ | $\{ (, \text{id} \}$ | $\{ +, \$, ) \}$ |
| $T'$ | $\{ *, \varepsilon \}$ | $\{ +, \$, ) \}$ |
| $F$ | $\{ (, \text{id} \}$ | $\{ *, +, \$, ) \}$ |

### 3.1.4 LL(1) Parsing Table Construction

For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$:

- For every terminal $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, set $M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$.
- If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, then for every $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, set $M[A, b] = A \rightarrow \alpha$.

For our example, the resulting LL(1) parsing table is:

| | **id** | **+** | **\*** | **(** | **)** | **\$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$E$** | $E \rightarrow T E'$ | | | $E \rightarrow T E'$ | | |
| **$E'$** | | $E' \rightarrow +TE'$ | | | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| **$T$** | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | | | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | | |
| **$T'$** | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $T' \rightarrow *FT'$ | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| **$F$** | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | | | $F \rightarrow (E)$ | | |

**Result**: No cell contains more than one production ⇒ **the grammar is LL(1) ⇒ backtrack-free parsing is possible**. Every table lookup yields a **unique, deterministic** decision.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture: Backtrack-free Predictive Parser

The following block-level functional architecture shows the complete data flow of a backtrack-free predictive parser, with all major components and their interactions:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Buffer] -->|current symbol a| B0{Parser Driver}
    B0 -->|push, pop, compare| C0[Parse Stack]
    C0 -->|top symbol X| B0
    B0 -->|query M X a| D0[LL1 Parsing Table]
    D0 -->|production A to alpha| B0
    B0 -->|accept or error| E0[Output Module]
    E0 --> F0[Accept]
    E0 --> G0[Error Handler and Recovery]
    H0[FIRST Set Builder] --> D0
    I0[FOLLOW Set Builder] --> D0
    J0[Grammar Input G] --> H0
    J0 --> I0
```

## 4.2 Detailed Control-Flow Diagram of the Parsing Algorithm

The diagram below shows the **runtime decision flow** during parsing — the backtrack-free property is visualized as a **single, non-branching decision path** at every step:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Initialize stack with S and dollar] --> B1[Read top X from stack and current input a]
    B1 --> C1{Is X equal to dollar}
    C1 -->|Yes| D1{Is a equal to dollar}
    D1 -->|Yes| E1[ACCEPT - Parsing successful]
    D1 -->|No| F1[ERROR - Extra input after parse]
    C1 -->|No| G1{Is X equal to a}
    G1 -->|Yes| H1[Match: pop X and advance input]
    G1 -->|No| I1{Is X a terminal}
    I1 -->|Yes| J1[ERROR - Mismatch]
    I1 -->|No| K1[Consult table M of X comma a]
    K1 -->|M X a is empty| L1[ERROR - No valid production]
    K1 -->|M X a equals X to alpha| M1[Pop X from stack]
    M1 --> N1[Push alpha in reverse order onto stack]
    N1 --> B1
    H1 --> B1
```

## 4.3 LL(1) Grammar Verification Flow

This diagram captures the **offline grammar-analysis process** that determines *whether* a given grammar admits backtrack-free parsing:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[Context Free Grammar G] --> A2[Step 1: Eliminate Left Recursion]
    A2 --> B2[Step 2: Perform Left Factoring]
    B2 --> C2[Step 3: Compute FIRST sets]
    C2 --> D2[Step 4: Compute FOLLOW sets]
    D2 --> E2[Step 5: Compute FIRST PLUS sets]
    E2 --> F2[Step 6: Populate LL1 Table M]
    F2 --> G2{Any table cell has multiple entries}
    G2 -->|Yes| H2[NOT LL1 - Not backtrack free]
    G2 -->|No| I2[Grammar is LL1 - Backtrack free parsing possible]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Note**: The parser driver loop has **no backward edge** from the table-consultation step to any earlier state. This is the structural embodiment of the "backtrack-free" property. Every cycle either matches a terminal (forward progress on input) or expands a non-terminal (forward progress on the derivation tree). There is no path that retracts either.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`
**Define backtrack-free parsing. State the conditions under which a context-free grammar admits backtrack-free (LL(1)) parsing.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backtrack-free Parsing**: A top-down parsing technique in which the parser makes a **unique, deterministic** choice of production rule for any non-terminal using **only one lookahead symbol**, without ever needing to undo a prior decision.

**Conditions for LL(1) (backtrack-free) grammar:**
- For every non-terminal $A$ with productions $A \rightarrow \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \cdots \mid \alpha_n$, the sets $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$ must be **pairwise disjoint**.
- If any $\alpha_i$ is nullable ($\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$), then $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$ for all $j \neq i$.
- At most **one** $\varepsilon$-production per non-terminal.
- The grammar must be **free of left recursion** and **left-factored**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`
**Differentiate between recursive descent parsing and predictive parsing. How does predictive parsing ensure backtrack-free operation?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Recursive Descent Parsing | Predictive Parsing |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | A set of mutually recursive procedures, one per non-terminal | Table-driven, single generic algorithm |
| **Backtracking** | May backtrack unless grammar is LL(1) | Inherently backtrack-free by table design |
| **Code size** | Large (one procedure per non-terminal) | Compact (single driver) |
| **Maintainability** | Difficult for large grammars | Easy — only the table changes |
| **Lookahead** | Implicit (1-token) | Explicit, indexed into table $M$ |

Predictive parsing ensures backtrack-free operation because the **parsing table $M$** is constructed so that **every entry $M[A, a]$ contains at most one production**. The parser driver, on consulting $M[A, a]$, has only one choice — hence no need to backtrack. The grammar analysis (FIRST/FOLLOW computation) **guarantees** that this uniqueness property holds for all reachable $(A, a)$ pairs.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **one** of two internal-choice alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 2]`

Consider the following grammar:

$$S \rightarrow aBDh$$
$$B \rightarrow bB \;\vert\; c$$
$$D \rightarrow EF$$
$$E \rightarrow eE \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$
$$F \rightarrow fF \;\vert\; g$$

**(a)** Compute the **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets for all non-terminals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct the **LL(1) parsing table** and verify that the grammar is backtrack-free. Show the **parsing moves** for the input string `abcfg`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**FIRST Sets** (apply iterative rules F1, F2, F3):

Apply Rule F1 to terminals: $\text{FIRST}(a)=\{a\}$, $\text{FIRST}(b)=\{b\}$, $\text{FIRST}(c)=\{c\}$, $\text{FIRST}(d)=\{d\}$, $\text{FIRST}(h)=\{h\}$, $\text{FIRST}(e)=\{e\}$, $\text{FIRST}(f)=\{f\}$, $\text{FIRST}(g)=\{g\}$.

Apply Rule F3 to $F$:
$$\text{FIRST}(F) = \{f\} \cup \{g\} = \{f, g\}$$

Apply Rule F3 to $E$:
$$\text{FIRST}(E) = \{e\} \cup \{\varepsilon\} = \{e, \varepsilon\}$$

Apply Rule F3 to $D$:
$\text{FIRST}(D) = \text{FIRST}(E) \cup \text{FIRST}(F) = \{e, \varepsilon\} \cup \{f, g\}$. Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E)$, include $\text{FIRST}(F)$. Since not all of $EF$ are nullable, exclude $\varepsilon$.
$$\text{FIRST}(D) = \{e, f, g\}$$

Apply Rule F3 to $B$:
$$\text{FIRST}(B) = \{b\} \cup \{c\} = \{b, c\}$$

Apply Rule F3 to $S$:
$$\text{FIRST}(S) = \text{FIRST}(a) \cup \text{FIRST}(B) \cup \cdots = \{a\}$$

**FOLLOW Sets** (apply rules FL1, FL2, FL3):

Apply Rule FL1: $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$.

Apply Rule FL2 to $S \rightarrow aBDh$: $\text{FIRST}(h) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{h\}$, so $h \in \text{FOLLOW}(D)$.

Apply Rule FL3 to $S \rightarrow aBDh$: since $h$ is terminal and the only symbol after $D$, $\text{FOLLOW}(D) = \{h\}$ initially.

Apply Rule FL2 to $B \rightarrow bB$: nothing follows $B$ in this production, so skip. Apply Rule FL3: $B$ is followed by $D$, so since $D$ is non-terminal, use FL2 path: $\text{FIRST}(D) = \{e, f, g\}$, so add these to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

Apply Rule FL2 to $D \rightarrow EF$: $\text{FIRST}(F) = \{f, g\}$ added to $\text{FOLLOW}(E)$. Also $F$ is the last symbol, so by FL3, add $\text{FOLLOW}(D) = \{h\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(F)$.

Apply Rule FL2 to $E \rightarrow eE$: nothing follows $E$ in this production, so add $\text{FOLLOW}(E)$ (already accumulated) by FL3.

Apply Rule FL2 to $F \rightarrow fF$: nothing follows $F$, so add $\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{h\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(F)$ (idempotent).

**Final FOLLOW sets:**

| Non-Terminal | FIRST | FOLLOW |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | $\{a\}$ | $\{\$\}$ |
| $B$ | $\{b, c\}$ | $\{e, f, g\}$ |
| $D$ | $\{e, f, g\}$ | $\{h\}$ |
| $E$ | $\{e, \varepsilon\}$ | $\{f, g, h\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{f, g\}$ | $\{h\}$ |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Parsing Table Construction** — for each production, populate $M[A, a]$ using the rule: for every $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, $M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$; if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, also for every $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.

| | **a** | **b** | **c** | **d** | **e** | **f** | **g** | **h** | **\$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$S$** | $S \rightarrow aBDh$ | | | | | | | | |
| **$B$** | | $B \rightarrow bB$ | $B \rightarrow c$ | | | | | | |
| **$D$** | | | | | $D \rightarrow EF$ | $D \rightarrow EF$ | $D \rightarrow EF$ | | |
| **$E$** | | | | | $E \rightarrow eE$ | $E \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $E \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $E \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | |
| **$F$** | | | | | | $F \rightarrow fF$ | $F \rightarrow g$ | | |

**Verification of Backtrack-free property**: Every cell in the table contains **at most one production**. No two productions for the same non-terminal share a FIRST set element. Therefore, the grammar **is LL(1)** and admits backtrack-free parsing.

**Parsing moves for input `abcfg$`**:

| Step | Stack (top→bottom) | Input | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S\$$ | `abcfg$` | $M[S, a] = S \rightarrow aBDh$; pop $S$, push $hDBa$ |
| 2 | $aDBh\$$ | `abcfg$` | Match $a$; pop $a$, advance input |
| 3 | $DBh\$$ | `bcfg$` | $M[B, b] = B \rightarrow bB$; pop $B$, push $Bb$ |
| 4 | $bBh\$$ | `bcfg$` | Match $b$; pop $b$, advance input |
| 5 | $Bh\$$ | `cfg$` | $M[B, c] = B \rightarrow c$; pop $B$, push $c$ |
| 6 | $ch\$$ | `cfg$` | Match $c$; pop $c$, advance input |
| 7 | $h\$$ | `fg$` | **ERROR** — top is $h$, input is $f$ |

The parser **rejects** `abcfg` as a syntactically invalid string. No backtracking was attempted — the error was detected immediately and deterministically.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **left recursion** and **left factoring**. Why must these transformations be applied before constructing an LL(1) parsing table? Demonstrate with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the grammar:
$$A \rightarrow aB \;\vert\; bC$$
$$B \rightarrow bB \;\vert\; d$$
$$C \rightarrow cC \;\vert\; a$$

Construct the LL(1) parsing table and parse the input string `abbd$`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Left Recursion**: A grammar is left-recursive if there exists a non-terminal $A$ such that $A \Rightarrow^+ A\alpha$ for some string $\alpha$. Direct left recursion has the form $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$. It causes top-down parsers to enter an **infinite loop** because the parser keeps expanding $A$ without consuming any input.

**Elimination**: For $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$, replace with:
$$A \rightarrow \beta A'$$
$$A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$$

**Left Factoring**: When two productions of a non-terminal share a common prefix, the parser cannot decide between them with one lookahead. For $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$, factor to:
$$A \rightarrow \alpha A'$$
$$A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$$

**Why necessary for backtrack-free parsing**: These transformations ensure that the FIRST sets of competing productions are **disjoint** (the LL(1) condition). Without them, table cells would have **multiple entries**, violating the backtrack-free guarantee.

**Demonstration Example**:
Original: $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$
- Left-recursive → causes infinite loop.
- After elimination: $E \rightarrow T E'$, $E' \rightarrow +TE' \mid \varepsilon$.
- Now $\text{FIRST}(T) \cap \text{FIRST}(+) = \emptyset$ — backtrack-free.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**FIRST sets**:
- $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$, $\text{FIRST}(b) = \{b\}$, $\text{FIRST}(c) = \{c\}$, $\text{FIRST}(d) = \{d\}$.
- $\text{FIRST}(B) = \{b, d\}$.
- $\text{FIRST}(C) = \{c, a\}$.
- $\text{FIRST}(A) = \{a, b\}$.

**FOLLOW sets**:
- $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{\$\}$ (start symbol, Rule FL1).
- From $A \rightarrow aB$: $B$ is last in $A$'s RHS, so $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{\$\}$ propagates to $B$. Similarly $\text{FOLLOW}(C) = \{\$\}$.
- $\text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{\$\}$, $\text{FOLLOW}(C) = \{\$\}$.

**LL(1) Parsing Table**:

| | **a** | **b** | **c** | **d** | **\$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$A$** | $A \rightarrow aB$ | $A \rightarrow bC$ | | | |
| **$B$** | | $B \rightarrow bB$ | | $B \rightarrow d$ | |
| **$C$** | $C \rightarrow a$ | | $C \rightarrow cC$ | | |

No cell has multiple entries ⇒ **grammar is LL(1) ⇒ backtrack-free**.

**Parsing `abbd$`**:

| Step | Stack (top→bottom) | Input | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A\$$ | `abbd$` | $M[A, a] = A \rightarrow aB$; pop, push $Ba$ |
| 2 | $Ba\$$ | `abbd$` | Match $a$ |
| 3 | $B\$$ | `bbd$` | $M[B, b] = B \rightarrow bB$; pop, push $Bb$ |
| 4 | $bB\$$ | `bbd$` | Match $b$ |
| 5 | $B\$$ | `bd$` | $M[B, b] = B \rightarrow bB$; pop, push $Bb$ |
| 6 | $bB\$$ | `bd$` | Match $b$ |
| 7 | $B\$$ | `d$` | $M[B, d] = B \rightarrow d$; pop, push $d$ |
| 8 | $d\$$ | `d$` | Match $d$ |
| 9 | $\$$ | `$` | **ACCEPT** — input is syntactically valid |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> - **Skipping the \$ end-marker in FOLLOW(S)**: forgetting to add \$ to FOLLOW of the start symbol is a **frequent 1-mark deduction**.
> - **Ignoring $\varepsilon$ in FIRST propagation**: when a non-terminal is nullable, its FOLLOW set must be merged into downstream FIRST⁺ calculations.
> - **Forgetting to check multiple entries in the table**: KTU examiners **always** test backtrack-free verification by checking that no two productions share a cell.
> - **Confusing LL(1) with LR(1)**: LL(1) uses top-down (leftmost derivation) with 1 lookahead; LR(1) uses bottom-up (rightmost derivation in reverse) with 1 lookahead. Mixing the two loses marks.
> - **Not showing the parsing-moves trace**: KTU board examiners expect a step-by-step move sequence (stack + input + action) for full marks on parsing problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Backtrack-free Parsing**

- **Definition**: A deterministic top-down parsing strategy with **no backtracking**, requiring only **1 lookahead symbol**. Grammars supporting this are called **LL(1)**.
- **LL(1) Uniqueness Condition**: For any non-terminal $A$ with multiple productions, the sets $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$ must be **pairwise disjoint**.
- **FIRST Set Rule of Thumb**: A terminal is in FIRST($\alpha$) iff it can begin a string derived from $\alpha$; $\varepsilon$ is in FIRST($\alpha$) iff $\alpha \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$.
- **FOLLOW Set Rule of Thumb**: A terminal is in FOLLOW($A$) iff it can appear immediately after $A$ in some sentential form; always add **\$** to FOLLOW(start symbol).
- **Parsing Table Size**: $\vert V_N \vert \times (\vert V_T \vert + 1)$ entries (the +1 accounts for \$).
- **Time Complexity**: $O(n)$ per input symbol — **linear**, suitable for production compilers.
- **Preprocessing Required**: (1) Eliminate left recursion, (2) Left-factor the grammar, (3) Compute FIRST & FOLLOW, (4) Populate table, (5) Verify uniqueness.
- **Stack Discipline**: Initial stack = $S\$$; productions pushed in **reverse order**; **\$** is matched at termination.
- **Error Detection**: Occurs deterministically when $M[X, a]$ is empty — **no silent failures, no infinite loops** (provided left recursion was eliminated).
- **LL(k) Generalization**: Backtrack-free parsing extends to $k > 1$, but the table size grows as $\vert V_T \vert^k$, making LL(1) the practical sweet spot.
- **Relation to Other Parser Classes**: LL(1) ⊂ LL(k) ⊂ Context-Free; LL(1) ⊂ SLR(1) ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ LR(1) (the latter three being bottom-up).
- **Engineering Reality**: Hand-written recursive-descent parsers (Clang, V8) are essentially LL(k) backtrack-free parsers with $k \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ for selected constructs.
- **KTU Exam Weightage**: Backtrack-free parsing, FIRST/FOLLOW computation, and LL(1) table construction are **guaranteed high-weightage topics** — typically a 7- or 14-mark question per exam cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Left-Factoring to Eliminate Backtracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Left-Factoring to Eliminate Backtracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Left-Factoring** is a grammar transformation technique used in the **front-end of a compiler** (specifically during the **Syntax Analysis Phase**) to modify a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** so that it becomes suitable for **predictive top-down parsing (LL(1))** without requiring backtracking.

Mathematically, given a non-terminal $A$ with productions:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \mid \alpha\beta_3 \mid \dots \mid \alpha\beta_n \mid \gamma$$

where $\alpha$ is the **longest common prefix (LCP)** shared among two or more alternatives and $\gamma$ represents productions that do **not** share this prefix, the left-factored grammar is constructed as:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \gamma$$

$$A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \beta_3 \mid \dots \mid \beta_n$$

Here, $A'$ is a freshly introduced non-terminal (also called a *factored non-terminal* or *auxiliary symbol*) that absorbs the divergent suffixes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Office Reception Desk**
> Imagine a college office reception where two students walk in and both start with the same question: *"Sir, I want to apply for..."* (a) *"...a bonafide certificate."* and (b) *"...a course withdrawal form."*
> 
> The receptionist is confused — *should I give the bonafide form first and then ask, or the withdrawal form first?* That confusion is exactly **backtracking**.
> 
> **Left-factoring** is like a smart receptionist who says: *"Tell me the common part ONCE, then I'll ask you the specific part."* So the receptionist first asks: *"Do you want to apply for a FORM?"* Once the student says yes, the receptionist then asks: *"Is it a bonafide or a withdrawal?"*
> 
> **The "common part" is factored out** so the decision is deferred to a single, clean sub-question.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in Compilers (KTU Context)

- **Standard Top-Down Parsers** (like **Recursive Descent** and **LL(1) Predictive Parsers**) require the parser to **decide which production to apply by looking at the first input token** (the *lookahead*).
- If two productions for the same non-terminal start with the **same terminal symbol**, the parser **cannot disambiguate** using a single token of lookahead → it must **backtrack**, which is:
  - **Exponentially slow** in the worst case,
  - **Difficult to implement** in modern linear-time parsers,
  - **Impossible to convert to a table-driven LL(1) parser**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Left-factoring is **Step 2** in the canonical grammar-preparation pipeline for LL(1) parsing. **Step 1** is **Left-Recursion Elimination** (covered separately in Module 2). Both are essential preprocessing steps taught in KTU's PECIT751 syllabus before constructing the **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets and the **LL(1) Parsing Table**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in This Topic

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Lookahead Token Count | $k$ | For LL(1), $k=1$; the parser uses **1 token** to decide the production |
| Non-terminal Count | $|N|$ | Total number of non-terminals in the grammar |
| Production Count | $|P|$ | Total number of productions |
| Longest Common Prefix Length | $\vert\alpha\vert$ | Number of symbols in the common prefix |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 When Exactly Do We Need Left-Factoring?

A grammar $G$ requires left-factoring when there exist **two or more productions of the same non-terminal whose right-hand sides share a non-empty common prefix**.

Formally, a production pair $(A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1, A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_2)$ where:

- $A \in N$ (non-terminals),
- $\alpha \in (N \cup \Sigma)^{+}$, $\alpha \neq \varepsilon$ (the common prefix is non-empty),
- $\beta_1, \beta_2 \in (N \cup \Sigma)^{*}$ (the divergent suffixes).

For **predictive parsing**, the condition is stricter — the parser fails when:

$$\text{FIRST}(\alpha\beta_1) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha\beta_2) \neq \emptyset$$

This is the precise mathematical reason for the backtracking conflict.

## 2.2 The Left-Factoring Algorithm (KTU Board Step-Wise Format)

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm: Left-Factor_Grammar($G$)**
> This is the standard KTU-board presentation format.

**Input:** A context-free grammar $G = (N, \Sigma, P, S)$ that may cause backtracking.

**Output:** An equivalent left-factored grammar $G'$ that supports predictive parsing.

**Step 1.** For **each non-terminal $A \in N$**, examine all productions of the form:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \dots \mid \alpha_n$$

**Step 2.** Identify the **longest common prefix $\alpha$** shared by a maximal subset of these productions.

- Let $P_{\alpha} = \{ \alpha_i \mid \alpha_i = \alpha\beta_i \}$ be the set of productions sharing the prefix $\alpha$.
- Let $P_{\neg\alpha}$ be the remaining productions (those that do **not** share $\alpha$).

**Step 3.** Replace the original productions of $A$ with:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \gamma_1 \mid \gamma_2 \mid \dots \mid \gamma_m$$

where $\gamma_j$ are the RHS of $P_{\neg\alpha}$ (unchanged), and add a new non-terminal $A'$ with:

$$A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \dots \mid \beta_k$$

**Step 4.** **Recursively** apply Steps 1–3 to the newly created non-terminal $A'$, since $A'$ itself may now have productions with a common prefix (factoring is *recursive* in nature).

**Step 5.** Repeat for all non-terminals until **no non-terminal** has two productions sharing a non-empty common prefix.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important criterion to remember for KTU exams:**

$$\text{Grammar } G \text{ is LL(1) after left-factoring} \iff \text{For every non-terminal } A,\ \forall i \neq j:\ \text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) = \emptyset$$

| S.No. | Concept | Formula / Condition | Unit / Symbol |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Backtracking Exists | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) \neq \emptyset$ | Set intersection |
| 2 | Backtracking Eliminated | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) = \emptyset$ | Set disjoint |
| 3 | Left-Factoring Transformation | $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \implies A \rightarrow \alpha A',\ A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$ | Production rewrite |
| 4 | LCP Definition | $\alpha = \text{LCP}(\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \dots, \alpha_n)$ | String operation |
| 5 | Lookahead Tokens | $k = 1$ for **LL(1)** | Token count |
| 6 | New Non-terminals Introduced | $\Delta N' = \text{Number of distinct common prefixes eliminated}$ | Count |
| 7 | Language Equivalence | $L(G) = L(G')$ | Set equality |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Left-Factoring** with **Left-Recursion Elimination**. They are **two completely different transformations**:
> - **Left-Recursion:** $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$ (recursion on the **left**)
> - **Left-Factoring:** $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$ (common **prefix** on the left)
> 
> Both are required (in this order: Left-Recursion first, then Left-Factoring) to make a grammar LL(1).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Left-factoring is not just a textbook exercise — it is the **foundation of every modern compiler's parser-generator tool**:

1. **ANTLR** (ANother Tool for Language Recognition) — used in Java, JavaScript, Python language tooling — automatically applies left-factoring when generating LL(k\*) parsers.
2. **YACC/Bison** (LALR parsers) — internally rely on equivalent transformation principles to resolve shift/reduce ambiguities.
3. **IDE tooling** — language servers (LSP) use LL(1)/LL(k) grammars transformed via left-factoring to provide real-time syntax highlighting and error recovery.
4. **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs)** — SQL, JSON, YAML parsers are all built on left-factored grammars for linear-time performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Work-through Mandate:** Every production transformation is shown step-by-step, with the underlying rationale written out explicitly. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic Two-Production Case

### Original Grammar $G_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow iE t S \mid iE t S e S \mid a \\
E &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify the Non-Terminal With Common Prefix

We examine each non-terminal in turn:

- **For $S$:** The productions are
  - $S \rightarrow iE t S$
  - $S \rightarrow iE t S e S$
  - $S \rightarrow a$
  
  Comparing the first two, we compute the **longest common prefix**:
  - Compare `iE t S` vs `iE t S e S`:
    - Position 1: `i` = `i` ✓
    - Position 2: `E` = `E` ✓
    - Position 3: `t` = `t` ✓
    - Position 4: `S` = `S` ✓
    - Position 5: end-of-string vs `e` ✗
  - **Longest common prefix $\alpha = iE t S$**

- **For $E$:** Only one production, no factoring needed.

### Step 2 — Apply the Left-Factoring Transformation

The productions of $S$ are split as:

- $P_\alpha = \{ S \rightarrow iE t S,\ S \rightarrow iE t S e S \}$
- $P_{\neg\alpha} = \{ S \rightarrow a \}$

So the new form becomes:

$$S \rightarrow \alpha S' \mid a$$

Replacing $\alpha$ with the literal string:

$$S \rightarrow iE t S\ S' \mid a$$

And the new non-terminal $S'$ captures the divergent suffixes:

$$S' \rightarrow \varepsilon \mid e S$$

### Step 3 — Resulting Left-Factored Grammar $G_1'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow iE t S\ S' \mid a \\
S' &\rightarrow \varepsilon \mid e S \\
E &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:** Now the parser sees the lookahead token `i`, knows to apply the production $S \rightarrow iE t S\ S'$, and only **after** matching `iE t S` does it consult $S'$ to decide between $\varepsilon$ and `e S` — using a single extra lookahead token. **No backtracking needed.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multiple Common Prefixes (Recursive Factoring)

### Original Grammar $G_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow ab \mid ac \mid ad \mid b
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Longest Common Prefix

Examining all productions of $A$:

| Production | First Symbol |
|---|---|
| $A \rightarrow ab$ | `a` |
| $A \rightarrow ac$ | `a` |
| $A \rightarrow ad$ | `a` |
| $A \rightarrow b$ | `b` |

Three productions begin with `a`. The common prefix is $\alpha = a$.

### Step 2 — First Round of Factoring

The set $P_\alpha = \{A \rightarrow ab,\ A \rightarrow ac,\ A \rightarrow ad\}$ and $P_{\neg\alpha} = \{A \rightarrow b\}$.

We introduce a new non-terminal $A'$ and rewrite:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow aA' \mid b \\
A' &\rightarrow b \mid c \mid d
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Check $A'$ for Further Factoring

The productions of $A'$ are $A' \rightarrow b \mid c \mid d$. Each begins with a **distinct terminal** (`b`, `c`, `d`). **No further factoring is needed.**

### Step 4 — Final Factored Grammar $G_2'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow aA' \mid b \\
A' &\rightarrow b \mid c \mid d
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursive nature:** If $A'$ had been $A' \rightarrow bx \mid by \mid bz$, we would have applied factoring **again** to obtain $A' \rightarrow bA''$ and $A'' \rightarrow x \mid y \mid z$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Three-Way Common Prefix (Classic KTU Pattern)

### Original Grammar $G_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &\rightarrow abc \mid abd \mid aef \\
Y &\rightarrow c
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Analyze Productions of $X$

- $X \rightarrow abc$
- $X \rightarrow abd$
- $X \rightarrow aef$

Comparing all three pairwise, the **first two symbols** `a`, `b` appear in `abc` and `abd`, but `aef` diverges at position 2 (b vs e). Therefore, the **longest common prefix among ALL three** is $\alpha = a$.

But notice — between `abc` and `abd` alone, the common prefix is `ab`. This is a *nested* factoring situation.

### Step 2 — First Round of Factoring (on $\alpha = a$)

We separate productions sharing prefix `a`:

- $P_\alpha = \{ X \rightarrow abc,\ X \rightarrow abd,\ X \rightarrow aef \}$
- $P_{\neg\alpha} = \emptyset$ (no other productions)

So we obtain:

$$X \rightarrow aX'$$

$$X' \rightarrow bc \mid bd \mid ef$$

### Step 3 — Second Round of Factoring (Recursive)

Now examine $X'$:
- $X' \rightarrow bc$
- $X' \rightarrow bd$
- $X' \rightarrow ef$

The first two share prefix `b`. The third does not. So we factor again with $\alpha' = b$:

- $P_{\alpha'} = \{X' \rightarrow bc,\ X' \rightarrow bd\}$
- $P_{\neg\alpha'} = \{X' \rightarrow ef\}$

Resulting in:

$$X' \rightarrow bX'' \mid ef$$

$$X'' \rightarrow c \mid d$$

### Step 4 — Final Grammar $G_3'$ (Fully Left-Factored):

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &\rightarrow aX' \\
X' &\rightarrow bX'' \mid ef \\
X'' &\rightarrow c \mid d \\
Y &\rightarrow c
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification using FIRST sets:**
> - $\text{FIRST}(aX') = \{a\}$ — only one production of $X$ begins with `a` ✓
> - $\text{FIRST}(bX'') = \{b\}$ and $\text{FIRST}(ef) = \{e\}$ — disjoint ✓
> - $\text{FIRST}(c) = \{c\}$ and $\text{FIRST}(d) = \{d\}$ — disjoint ✓
> 
> **Grammar is now LL(1).** Predictive parsing is possible without backtracking.

---

## 3.4 Summary Matrix — Transformation Patterns

| Original Pattern | Factored Result | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$ | $A \rightarrow \alpha A',\ A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$ | Basic two-way split |
| $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \mid \gamma$ | $A \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \gamma,\ A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$ | Mixed — some share, some don't |
| $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \mid \alpha\beta_3$ | $A \rightarrow \alpha A',\ A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \beta_3$ | Multi-way split |
| Nested: $A' \rightarrow \gamma\beta_1 \mid \gamma\beta_2$ (after first round) | $A' \rightarrow \gamma A'',\ A'' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$ | **Recursive** factoring |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Left-Factoring Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Input Grammar G]) --> loop1{For each non-terminal A in N}
    loop1 --> extract[Collect all productions of A: A -> alpha1, alpha2, ..., alphan]
    extract --> findLCP[Find Longest Common Prefix alpha among productions]
    findLCP --> checkAlpha{Is alpha non-empty AND shared by 2 or more productions?}
    checkAlpha -- No --> nextNT[Move to next non-terminal]
    nextNT --> loop1
    checkAlpha -- Yes --> split[Partition productions into P_alpha and P_notAlpha]
    split --> rewrite[Rewrite A -> alpha A-prime OR gamma1 OR gamma2 OR ...]
    rewrite --> addNew[Add new non-terminal A-prime with productions: A-prime -> beta1 OR beta2 OR ... OR betan]
    addNew --> recurse{Does A-prime itself have a common prefix?}
    recurse -- Yes --> findLCP
    recurse -- No --> nextNT
    loop1 --> doneAll{All non-terminals processed?}
    doneAll -- No --> loop1
    doneAll -- Yes --> finalCheck{Verify: For every A, FIRST of all RHS are pairwise disjoint?}
    finalCheck -- Yes --> outputG([Output: Left-Factored Grammar G-prime suitable for LL1 predictive parsing])
    finalCheck -- No --> loop1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at the top with the input grammar. The algorithm iterates over each non-terminal, finds the LCP, and rewrites. The recursive arrow back to `findLCP` represents the **recursive** nature of factoring (when the new non-terminal itself needs factoring). The final verification step ensures the grammar is LL(1)-ready.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Effect of Left-Factoring

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE Left-Factoring (Backtracking Required)"]
        direction TB
        A1[Non-terminal A] --> P1[Production 1: alpha beta1]
        A1 --> P2[Production 2: alpha beta2]
        A1 --> P3[Production 3: gamma]
        P1 -.->|"Parser sees 'a' → tries P1 → fails → backtracks"| fail[Backtracking Overhead]
        P2 -.->|"Then tries P2 → succeeds"| fail
    end

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER Left-Factoring (No Backtracking)"]
        direction TB
        A2[Non-terminal A] --> Q1[Production 1: alpha A-prime]
        A2 --> Q3[Production 3: gamma]
        A3[New Non-terminal A-prime] --> R1[beta1]
        A3 --> R2[beta2]
        Q1 -->|"Parser sees 'a' → applies A → alpha A-prime"| ok[Single-Pass Decision]
        A3 -->|"Then A-prime decides between beta1 and beta2"| ok
    end

    BEFORE ==>|"Apply Left-Factoring Transformation"| AFTER
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the Block Diagram:** The "Before" side illustrates the backtracking cost — the parser must **try**, **fail**, and **retry**. The "After" side illustrates the linear decision path: one production is chosen based on the first token, and any remaining ambiguity is delegated to a fresh non-terminal $A'$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree — Recursive Factoring Visualized

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["X -> abc OR abd OR aef"]
    root --> r1["X -> a X-prime"]
    root --> x1["X-prime -> bc OR bd OR ef"]
    x1 --> x2["X-prime -> b X-doubleprime OR ef"]
    x2 --> x3["X-doubleprime -> c OR d"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. Define Left-Factoring. Why is it required in top-down parsing? (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Left-factoring is a grammar transformation in which common prefixes of the right-hand sides of productions belonging to the same non-terminal are extracted into a new non-terminal.

> [!NOTE]
> For a non-terminal $A$ with productions $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$, the factored form is:
> $$A \rightarrow \alpha A' \quad \text{and} \quad A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$$

**Why required:** In top-down predictive parsing, the parser must decide which production of a non-terminal to apply using only a **single lookahead token**. If two productions start with the **same terminal**, the parser **cannot disambiguate** and must backtrack. Left-factoring **defers** the decision to a new non-terminal, ensuring the parser can make a **single-pass, backtrack-free** choice.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently omit the **"why"** part and only define the transformation. Marks are split as: **1 mark** for the formal definition, **2 marks** for the reason (single-lookahead constraint). Always include the FIRST-set justification: *"The FIRST sets of the RHS must be pairwise disjoint for LL(1)."*

---

### Q2. Consider the grammar:
$$S \rightarrow aSb \mid aS \mid \varepsilon$$
**Is left-factoring required? Justify your answer.** (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

**Yes, left-factoring is required** for the productions $S \rightarrow aSb$ and $S \rightarrow aS$, which share the common prefix `a`.

**Step 1 — Compute FIRST sets of the RHS:**

- $\text{FIRST}(aSb) = \{a\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(aS) = \{a\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$

**Step 2 — Check intersection:**

$$\text{FIRST}(aSb) \cap \text{FIRST}(aS) = \{a\} \neq \emptyset$$

This non-empty intersection means a predictive parser **cannot decide** between the two productions on seeing `a`. Hence, **left-factoring is required**.

**Step 3 — Factored Form:**

$$S \rightarrow aS' \mid \varepsilon$$

$$S' \rightarrow Sb \mid S$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Note that this grammar is also **left-recursive** (in $S' \rightarrow Sb \mid S$, the production $S' \rightarrow S$ creates left-recursion). After factoring, **left-recursion elimination** must be applied as the **next** step. Students who factor but stop here lose **1 mark**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B includes **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

#### (a) What is left-factoring? Explain with a suitable example the steps involved in performing left-factoring on a context-free grammar. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Left-factoring is a syntactic transformation applied to a context-free grammar $G$ that replaces productions of a non-terminal sharing a common left-side prefix with factored equivalents, eliminating the need for backtracking in top-down predictive parsers.

Mathematically, for $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \mid \gamma$:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \gamma, \quad A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$$

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

1. For each non-terminal $A$, list all productions.
2. Find the longest common prefix $\alpha$ shared by two or more productions.
3. Replace those productions with $A \rightarrow \alpha A'$ and introduce $A' \rightarrow \beta_i$ for each divergent suffix $\beta_i$.
4. Recursively apply to $A'$ if it has its own common prefixes.
5. Verify that all FIRST sets are pairwise disjoint.

**Example (2 marks):**

Original: $A \rightarrow ab \mid ac \mid b$

Factored:
$$A \rightarrow aA' \mid b, \quad A' \rightarrow b \mid c$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Forgetting to recursively apply factoring to $A'$.

---

#### (b) Consider the following grammar and perform left-factoring. Also verify that the resulting grammar is suitable for predictive parsing. (7 Marks)

$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid E - T \mid T$$

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Check for Left-Recursion (1 mark):**

Yes, $E \rightarrow E + T$ and $E \rightarrow E - T$ are **left-recursive** (E appears as the leftmost symbol on the RHS). Left-factoring alone **cannot handle left-recursion**. We must **first eliminate left-recursion**.

**Step 2 — Eliminate Left-Recursion (2 marks):**

Using the standard transformation $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta \implies A \rightarrow \beta A',\ A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$:

Here, $E \rightarrow E(+T) \mid E(-T) \mid T$, so $\alpha_1 = +T$, $\alpha_2 = -T$, $\beta = T$.

$$E \rightarrow T E'$$
$$E' \rightarrow +T E' \mid -T E' \mid \varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Apply Left-Factoring (2 marks):**

The productions $E' \rightarrow +TE'$ and $E' \rightarrow -TE'$ share a common prefix structure in their *operators*. The LCP of `+TE'` and `-TE'` is empty (operators differ), so the productions are **already distinct** in their first terminal. **No further left-factoring is needed.**

**Step 4 — Verify via FIRST Sets (2 marks):**

- $\text{FIRST}(T E') = \text{FIRST}(T) = \{(, id\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(+T E') = \{+\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(-T E') = \{-\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$

The set $\{+,\ -,\ (, id\}$ is **pairwise disjoint** (with $\varepsilon$ handled via FOLLOW). Grammar is **LL(1) and suitable for predictive parsing**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often skip Step 1 (left-recursion check) and try to apply left-factoring directly. This is a **wrong ordering** and the factored form will still have left-recursion. Marks lost: **up to 3**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### (a) Explain the concept of backtracking in top-down parsing. How does left-factoring eliminate it? Illustrate with an example. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Backtracking (3 marks):** Backtracking in top-down parsing occurs when, after the parser commits to a production based on the lookahead, it finds a mismatch with the input and must **rewind** to try an alternative production. This makes the parser **exponential in worst case** ($O(k^n)$ where $k$ is the average branching factor and $n$ is the input length).

**Example of backtracking:**

Grammar: $S \rightarrow aSb \mid aS \mid \varepsilon$, input: `aab`

- Parser sees `a` and tries $S \rightarrow aSb$, then $S \rightarrow aS$, then $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.
- After matching `a`, it recursively tries to match the rest, fails, and **backtracks**.

**Elimination via Left-Factoring (4 marks):** After factoring to $S \rightarrow aS' \mid \varepsilon$ and $S' \rightarrow Sb \mid S$:

- Parser sees `a` → applies $S \rightarrow aS'$ (no ambiguity, single choice).
- Then for $S'$, it sees the next token and decides deterministically.
- **No backtracking, linear time** $O(n)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write the backtracking example but forget to show the **linear-time complexity** claim with the factored grammar. Examiners expect an explicit mention of the time-complexity improvement.

---

#### (b) Perform left-factoring on the following grammar and write the final factored grammar. Show the FIRST sets for verification. (7 Marks)

$$S \rightarrow iE t S \mid iE t S e S \mid a$$
$$E \rightarrow b$$

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify Common Prefix in $S$ (2 marks):**

Productions of $S$:
- $S \rightarrow iE t S$
- $S \rightarrow iE t S e S$
- $S \rightarrow a$

LCP of first two: $\alpha = iE t S$. Third production is unshared.

**Step 2 — Apply Factoring (2 marks):**

$$S \rightarrow iE t S\ S' \mid a$$

$$S' \rightarrow \varepsilon \mid e S$$

**Step 3 — Recursive Check on $S'$ (1 mark):**

Productions $S' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ and $S' \rightarrow eS$ start with **different terminals** ($\varepsilon$ and `e`). No further factoring needed.

**Step 4 — Verify via FIRST Sets (2 marks):**

- $\text{FIRST}(iE t S\ S') = \{i\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(eS) = \{e\}$

All sets are pairwise disjoint. **Grammar is LL(1).**

**Final Factored Grammar:**

$$S \rightarrow iE t S\ S' \mid a$$

$$S' \rightarrow \varepsilon \mid e S$$

$$E \rightarrow b$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students frequently miss that $S'$ has only two productions and skip the recursive check. Even if it appears trivial, **always state explicitly** that no further factoring is needed (1 mark is reserved for this verification).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-Year-Before-Exam Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- [ ] **Left-Factoring Definition:** A grammar transformation that extracts common prefixes of RHS of productions sharing the same LHS non-terminal.
- [ ] **Purpose:** Makes grammar suitable for **LL(1) predictive parsing** by ensuring **disjoint FIRST sets**.
- [ ] **Transformation Rule:** $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \implies A \rightarrow \alpha A',\ A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$.
- [ ] **Order of Transformations (KTU Mandatory):** **(1)** Eliminate Left-Recursion → **(2)** Left-Factor → **(3)** Compute FIRST/FOLLOW → **(4)** Build LL(1) Table.
- [ ] **Recursive Application:** After factoring, the new non-terminal $A'$ may itself have productions with a common prefix — **apply factoring again**.
- [ ] **Termination:** Factoring terminates because the total length of RHS strictly decreases (the LCP is "moved out") in each round.
- [ ] **Language Preservation:** $L(G) = L(G')$ — left-factoring **does not change the language**, only the structure.
- [ ] **Backtracking Symptom:** $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) \neq \emptyset$ for two productions of the same non-terminal.
- [ ] **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing left-factoring with left-recursion elimination — they are **different transformations**.
- [ ] **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to add the **unshared** productions (the $\gamma$ terms) when rewriting.
- [ ] **Common Mistake #3:** Stopping after one round of factoring when $A'$ still has a common prefix.
- [ ] **LL(1) Verdict Criterion:** Final grammar is LL(1) $\iff$ for every non-terminal, FIRST sets of all RHS are pairwise disjoint.
- [ ] **Real-World Tools:** ANTLR, YACC, Bison, JavaCC — all use variants of left-factoring.
- [ ] **Examiner Hot-Question:** *"Show the FIRST sets to prove the factored grammar is LL(1)"* — always include this in your answer for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursive Descent Parsers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recursive Descent Parsers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 MODULE 2: Recursive Descent Parsers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

A **Recursive Descent Parser (RDP)** is a top-down, syntax-analysis technique in which the parser is implemented as a set of mutually recursive procedures (or functions) — one procedure for every non-terminal of the grammar. The parser attempts to derive the input string by starting with the start symbol's procedure and recursively expanding each non-terminal by selecting a production whose right-hand side matches the next tokens in the input buffer.

When the parser uses **one-symbol lookahead** and selects productions deterministically (no backtracking) based on a precomputed **Predictive Parsing Table**, it is called a **Predictive Recursive Descent Parser** or **LL(1) Parser**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Recursive Descent Parsing belongs to the family of **top-down parsers** and operates on the **LL(k)** class of grammars. For KTU evaluation, the most tested variant is **LL(1)** — i.e., one-token lookahead.

### 🌱 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are reading a **recipe** (the grammar) step by step. Each step says *"if you see ingredient X, add ingredient Y"*. As you cook (parse), you keep a small notepad (lookahead) showing the **next ingredient** you'll need.

- If the recipe tells you to "add either salt or sugar" and your notepad says **salt**, you confidently pick **salt** (no backtracking).
- If the recipe says "add salt, then pepper" but your notepad shows **sugar**, you raise an error.

A recursive descent parser works exactly like this:
- **Each non-terminal** = a sub-recipe (a procedure).
- **The lookahead token** = the small notepad.
- **The parsing table** = the precomputed "decision chart" telling you which sub-recipe to use.

### 📌 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights)

- **LL(1) Grammar** — A grammar parsed by scanning input **L**eft-to-right, producing a **L**eftmost derivation, using **1** token of lookahead.
- **Predictive Parsing Table** — A 2-D table $M[\text{Non-terminal}, \text{Terminal}]$ indexing the production to apply.
- **Backtracking** — A naive approach where, on mismatch, the parser undoes work and tries another production (KTU exams prefer the **non-backtracking** predictive variant).
- **Mutual Recursion** — Procedures calling one another in a cycle, mirroring the recursive structure of grammar rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-requisite Grammar Transformations**
> Before applying recursive descent, the input grammar **must** be:
> 1. **Free of Left Recursion** (direct & indirect).
> 2. **Left-Factored** (to remove common prefixes).
> A grammar failing either condition leads to infinite recursion or ambiguous table entries.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 Architecture of a Recursive Descent Parser

A predictive recursive descent parser consists of three tightly-coupled components:

### ⚙️ Component 1: Input Buffer
- Holds the input string followed by the **end-marker $**.
- A pointer advances one token at a time; the **lookahead** is the token currently pointed to.

### ⚙️ Component 2: Stack
- Initially contains the start symbol $S$ on top of the marker `$`.
- Symbols (terminals & non-terminals) are popped; terminals are **matched** with the lookahead, non-terminals are **expanded** via the parsing table.

### ⚙️ Component 3: Parsing Table $M[A, a]$
- Rows → non-terminals.
- Columns → terminals (including `$`).
- Cell contents → a production $A \to \alpha$ OR **error**.

### 🔁 The Predictive Parsing Algorithm (High-Level)

For each step:
1. Let $X$ = top of stack, $a$ = current lookahead.
2. If $X = a = \$$ → **Accept** (parsing successful).
3. If $X = a \neq \$$ → **pop** $X$, **advance** input pointer.
4. If $X$ is a non-terminal → consult $M[X, a]$:
   - If $M[X, a] = X \to Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$ → **pop** $X$, **push** $Y_k, \dots, Y_2, Y_1$ (rightmost first).
   - If $M[X, a] = \text{error}$ → invoke **error-recovery routine**.

### 🧠 Why FIRST and FOLLOW?

The parsing table is built using two sets: **FIRST(α)** and **FOLLOW(A)**.

**Definition 1 — FIRST(α):** The set of terminals that begin any string derived from $\alpha$. If $\alpha \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$, then $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$.

**Definition 2 — FOLLOW(A):** The set of terminals that can appear **immediately to the right** of non-terminal $A$ in some sentential form. If $A$ can be the rightmost symbol, then $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.

### 📐 Rules for Computing FIRST

1. If $X$ is a terminal → $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{ X \}$.
2. If $X \to \varepsilon$ is a production → add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
3. If $X \to Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_n$ → add $\text{FIRST}(Y_1) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$. If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1)$, also add $\text{FIRST}(Y_2) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$, and so on. If all $Y_i$ derive $\varepsilon$, add $\varepsilon$.

### 📐 Rules for Computing FOLLOW

1. Place `$` in $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ where $S$ is the start symbol.
2. For production $A \to \alpha B \beta$ → add $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
3. If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$ (or $\beta$ is empty) → add $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

### 📐 Rule for Filling the Parsing Table

$$
M[A, a] =
\begin{cases}
A \to \alpha & \text{if } a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \\
A \to \alpha & \text{if } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \text{ and } a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A) \\
\text{error} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conflict Detection in KTU Exams**
> A grammar is **NOT LL(1)** if any table cell $M[A, a]$ ends up with **more than one production**. This is the classic "table-conflict" question in KTU boards.

---

## 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula / Rule Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Direct Left Recursion | $A \to A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta$ replaced by $A \to \beta A'$, $A' \to \alpha A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$ | $\alpha, \beta$ do not start with $A$ |
| 2 | Left Factoring | $A \to \alpha\beta_1 \;\vert\; \alpha\beta_2$ replaced by $A \to \alpha A'$, $A' \to \beta_1 \;\vert\; \beta_2$ | Common prefix $\alpha$ extracted |
| 3 | FIRST Terminal | $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$ | $a$ is terminal |
| 4 | FIRST Non-terminal | $\text{FIRST}(A) = \bigcup \text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$ for all $A \to \alpha_i$ | Iterate until fixed point |
| 5 | FOLLOW Start | $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ | Always true |
| 6 | FOLLOW from RHS | $A \to \alpha B\beta$ → $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Recursion rule |
| 7 | FOLLOW Inherit | $A \to \alpha B$ or $A \to \alpha B\beta$ with $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$ → $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Spillover rule |
| 8 | Table Entry | $M[A, a]$ = production if $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ or ($\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ and $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$) | Core fill rule |
| 9 | LL(1) Condition | Each $M[A, a]$ has at most one production | Conflict ⇒ not LL(1) |
| 10 | Stack Push Order | Push RHS symbols **in reverse** | Rightmost symbol on top |

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recursive descent parsers power many production-grade tools:
- **ANTLR-generated parsers** (used in Hive, Pig, Shopify's GraphQL stack).
- **JSON / YAML / XML parsers** in browsers and IDEs.
- **Expression evaluators** in spreadsheets and calculators.
- **Compiler front-ends** for languages like Java, Python (early phases), and DSLs in industry.
- **Static analyzers** (ESLint, SonarQube) that walk the AST.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 🎯 Canonical KTU Example — Arithmetic Expression Grammar

Consider the classic grammar (with left recursion) for arithmetic expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\to E + T \;\mid\; T \\
T &\to T * F \;\mid\; F \\
F &\to ( E ) \;\mid\; \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

We will convert it to LL(1) form and build a predictive parser.

---

## 📝 Step 1 — Eliminate Left Recursion

Apply the rule $A \to A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta \;\Rightarrow\; A \to \beta A',\;\; A' \to \alpha A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$.

**For $E$:**
- $\alpha = +T$, $\beta = T$
- $E \to T E'$
- $E' \to +T E' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$

**For $T$:**
- $\alpha = *F$, $\beta = F$
- $T \to F T'$
- $T' \to *F T' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$

**Resulting LL(1) Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E  &\to T\,E' \\
E' &\to +T\,E' \;\mid\; \varepsilon \\
T  &\to F\,T' \\
T' &\to *F\,T' \;\mid\; \varepsilon \\
F  &\to (E) \;\mid\; \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **No left factoring required** here because no non-terminal has two productions sharing a common prefix. In other problems, KTU may give a grammar like $A \to ab \;\vert\; ac$ requiring factoring into $A \to aA'$, $A' \to b \;\vert\; c$.

---

## 📝 Step 2 — Compute FIRST Sets

Applying the rules iteratively until convergence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FIRST}(F) &= \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\} \\
\text{FIRST}(T') &= \{\,*,\ \varepsilon\,\} \\
\text{FIRST}(T)  &= \text{FIRST}(F) \cup \big(\text{FIRST}(T') \setminus \{\varepsilon\}\big) = \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\} \\
\text{FIRST}(E') &= \{\,+,\ \varepsilon\,\} \\
\text{FIRST}(E)  &= \text{FIRST}(T) = \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each step:**
- $\text{FIRST}(F)$: From $F \to (E)$ we get `(`; from $F \to \text{id}$ we get `id`.
- $\text{FIRST}(T')$: From $T' \to *F T'$ we get `*`; from $T' \to \varepsilon$ we get $\varepsilon$.
- $\text{FIRST}(T)$: First symbol on RHS of $T \to F T'$ is $F$, so FIRST($T$) starts with FIRST($F$). Then we add FIRST($T'$) minus $\varepsilon$, which is just `*`. But `*` is not in FIRST($F$), so it joins. However, since $F$ does **not** derive $\varepsilon$, we don't add anything further.
- Wait — recheck: $F$ doesn't yield $\varepsilon$, so FIRST($T$) is **just** FIRST($F$) = `{, id}`.

**Corrected:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FIRST}(F) &= \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\} \\
\text{FIRST}(T') &= \{\,*,\ \varepsilon\,\} \\
\text{FIRST}(T)  &= \text{FIRST}(F) = \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\} \quad \text{(no spillover as } F \not\Rightarrow^* \varepsilon\text{)} \\
\text{FIRST}(E') &= \{\,+,\ \varepsilon\,\} \\
\text{FIRST}(E)  &= \text{FIRST}(T) = \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 📝 Step 3 — Compute FOLLOW Sets

**Start symbol rule:** $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(E)$.

**Rule from $E \to T\,E'$:** Everything in FIRST($E'$) except $\varepsilon$ goes to FOLLOW($T$).
- $\text{FIRST}(E') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{+\}$ → add to FOLLOW($T$).
- Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$, also add FOLLOW($E$) to FOLLOW($T$).

**Rule from $E' \to +T\,E'$:** FIRST($E'$) \ $\{\varepsilon\}$ goes to FOLLOW($T$). Then FOLLOW($E'$) goes to FOLLOW($T$) only if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$, which it is.
- But we also need to push FOLLOW($E'$) into FOLLOW($E'$) (no-op).

**Rule from $E \to T\,E'$ (the $E'$ part):** FOLLOW($E$) goes to FOLLOW($E'$) because the RHS ends with $E'$. (This is the "spillover" rule.)

**Rule from $T \to F\,T'$:** Add FIRST($T'$) \ $\{\varepsilon\} = \{*\}$ to FOLLOW($F$). Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$, add FOLLOW($T$) to FOLLOW($F$).

**Rule from $T' \to *F\,T'$:** Add FIRST($T'$) \ $\{\varepsilon\}$ to FOLLOW($F$); add FOLLOW($T'$) to FOLLOW($F$).

**Rule from $F \to (E)$:** Add `)` to FOLLOW($E$).

Compiling everything:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOLLOW}(E)   &= \{\,\$,\ \text{)}\,\} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(E')  &= \text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\,\$,\ \text{)}\,\} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(T)   &= \{+\} \cup \text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{+,\ \$,\ \text{)}\} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(T')  &= \text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+,\ \$,\ \text{)}\} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(F)   &= \{*\} \cup \text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{*,\ +,\ \$,\ \text{)}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 📝 Step 4 — Build the Predictive Parsing Table $M$

Using the rule: for each production $A \to \alpha$, fill $M[A, a]$ for all $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, and if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, also for $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.

| Non-terminal | **+** | **\*** | **(** | **)** | **id** | **\$** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **E**  | error | error | $E \to T\,E'$ | error | $E \to T\,E'$ | error |
| **E'** | $E' \to +T\,E'$ | error | error | $E' \to \varepsilon$ | error | $E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| **T**  | error | error | $T \to F\,T'$ | error | $T \to F\,T'$ | error |
| **T'** | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to *F\,T'$ | error | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | error | $T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| **F**  | error | error | $F \to (E)$ | error | $F \to \text{id}$ | error |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No cell has more than one production** → the grammar **is LL(1)**. If two productions appear in a cell, the grammar fails and a KTU answer must state: *"Grammar is not LL(1); recursive descent not directly applicable."*

---

## 📝 Step 5 — Trace the Input `id + id * id`

Each step: **Stack (top on right) | Input | Action**

| # | Stack | Remaining Input | Action / Production |
|---|-------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | `$E`     | `id+id*id$`   | $E \to T\,E'$ |
| 2 | `$E'T`   | `id+id*id$`   | $T \to F\,T'$ |
| 3 | `$E'T'F` | `id+id*id$`   | $F \to \text{id}$ |
| 4 | `$E'T'id`  | `id+id*id$`   | match `id` |
| 5 | `$E'T'`   | `+id*id$`     | $T' \to \varepsilon$ (since $+$ ∈ FOLLOW($T'$)) |
| 6 | `$E'`     | `+id*id$`     | $E' \to +T\,E'$ |
| 7 | `$E'T+`   | `+id*id$`     | match `+` |
| 8 | `$E'T`    | `id*id$`      | $T \to F\,T'$ |
| 9 | `$E'T'F`  | `id*id$`      | $F \to \text{id}$ |
| 10 | `$E'T'id`  | `id*id$`      | match `id` |
| 11 | `$E'T'`   | `*id$`        | $T' \to *F\,T'$ |
| 12 | `$E'T'F*` | `*id$`        | match `*` |
| 13 | `$E'T'F`  | `id$`         | $F \to \text{id}$ |
| 14 | `$E'T'id`  | `id$`         | match `id` |
| 15 | `$E'T'`   | `$`           | $T' \to \varepsilon$ ($ \in \text{FOLLOW}(T')$) |
| 16 | `$E'`     | `$`           | $E' \to \varepsilon$ ($ \in \text{FOLLOW}(E')$) |
| 17 | `$`       | `$`           | **ACCEPT** ✓ |

The string `id + id * id` is **syntactically valid** with respect to the grammar.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Diagram 1: Predictive Recursive Descent Parser — Top-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Buffer: w$] --> B[Lookahead Pointer]
    B --> C{Predictive Parsing Algorithm}
    C -->|Consult| D[Parsing Table M]
    D -->|Production| E[Stack Manipulation]
    E --> F{Top of Stack Symbol X}
    F -->|Terminal matches lookahead| G[Pop X, Advance Input]
    F -->|Non-terminal A| H[Lookup M[A, a]]
    H -->|Production A to alpha| I[Pop A, Push alpha reversed]
    H -->|Error Entry| J[Invoke Error Recovery]
    G --> K{Terminal equals $ and Input Empty}
    I --> K
    K -->|Yes| L[Accept: Parsing Successful]
    K -->|No| C
```
