(3AC / SSA / CFG)"] src --> lex lex --> tok tok --> parser parser --> pt pt --> astb astb --> ast ast --> sem sem --> ann ann --> ir ``` **Reading guide:** - The **Parse Tree** is produced by the parser and is an *intermediate* artifact. - The **AST Builder** consumes the parse tree and emits the AST. - From the AST onwards, all later compiler phases (semantic analysis, IR generation, optimization, code generation) operate on the AST or its descendants, never on the parse tree. ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Parse Tree or AST? Use this flowchart during exam revision to decide which tree is being asked for in a problem. ```mermaid graph TD q["Question: Construct the tree for input w"] q --> q1{"Does the question
show every terminal
symbol including
punctuation?"} q1 -- Yes --> ptOut["Answer is a Parse Tree
(CST)"] q1 -- No --> q2{"Does the question
ask for grouping
structure or operator
precedence only?"} q2 -- Yes --> astOut["Answer is an AST
(Syntax Tree)"] q2 -- No --> q3{"Does the question
use a 'tree node'
per construct
(e.g. If, While)?"} q3 -- Yes --> astOut q3 -- No --> ptOut ``` > [!NOTE] > When a KTU question says "**syntax tree**" without further qualification, treat it as the **AST** unless it explicitly says "concrete" or "parse" tree. The two terms are sometimes used loosely in textbooks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each) ### Q1. Differentiate between a parse tree and an abstract syntax tree. (3 marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECIT751, Module 3]** **Model Answer (3 key points — full credit mapping):** | # | Point | Marks | |---|-------|-------| | 1 | A **parse tree** is a concrete representation showing *every* detail of the derivation, including all terminals, non-terminals, and punctuation; every node corresponds to a grammar symbol. | 1 | | 2 | An **AST** is an *abstracted* tree in which punctuation, keywords used purely for grouping, and chain productions are eliminated; only essential operators and operands remain as nodes. | 1 | | 3 | A parse tree has size up to $O(n^2)$ and is grammar-specific; an AST has size $O(n)$ and is closer to the program's semantics, making it the preferred working IR for the rest of the compiler. | 1 | ### Q2. Why is an abstract syntax tree preferred over a parse tree as the intermediate representation in the later phases of compilation? (3 marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECIT751, Module 3]** **Model Answer:** 1. The AST is **compact and smaller** in size (typically $O(n)$) compared to a parse tree (up to $O(n^2)$), because chain rules and syntactic noise have been removed. 2. The AST is **language-agnostic at the operator level**, which makes it easier to plug in different front-ends to a single back-end (e.g., GCC's GIMPLE). 3. The AST directly represents the **semantic structure** of the program — operator precedence, operand flow, statement types — which the semantic analyser and code generator can traverse efficiently. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks, internal choice) ### Question A (14 marks) **(a)** Consider the following grammar $G$ for a simple block-structured language: **[7 marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\to \text{if } ( C ) \; S \;\mid\; \text{if } ( C ) \; S \;\text{else } S \;\mid\; \text{id} = E ; \\ C &\to E \; \text{relop} \; E \\ E &\to E + T \;\mid\; T \\ T &\to T * F \;\mid\; F \\ F &\to ( E ) \;\mid\; \text{id} \;\mid\; \text{num} \\ \text{relop} &\to \;<\;\mid\;>\;\mid\;== \end{aligned} $$ Construct the **parse tree** for the input string: $$ w = \text{if } ( x \;>\; 0 ) \; y = x + 1 ; \text{ else } y = x ; $$ **Model Solution (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1 — Choose the leftmost derivation.** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( C ) \; S \; \text{else } S \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( E \; \text{relop} \; E ) \; S \; \text{else } S \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( T \; \text{relop} \; E ) \; S \; \text{else } S \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( F \; \text{relop} \; E ) \; S \; \text{else } S \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( \text{id} \; \text{relop} \; E ) \; S \; \text{else } S \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( x \;>\; E ) \; S \; \text{else } S \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( x \;>\; T ) \; S \; \text{else } S \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( x \;>\; F ) \; S \; \text{else } S \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( x \;>\; \text{num} ) \; S \; \text{else } S \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( x \;>\; 0 ) \; \text{id} = E ; \text{else } \text{id} = E ; \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( x \;>\; 0 ) \; \text{id} = E + T ; \text{else } \text{id} = E ; \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( x \;>\; 0 ) \; \text{id} = T + T ; \text{else } \text{id} = E ; \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( x \;>\; 0 ) \; \text{id} = F + F ; \text{else } \text{id} = E ; \\ &\Rightarrow \text{if } ( x \;>\; 0 ) \; y = \text{id} + \text{num} ; \text{else } y = \text{id} ; \end{aligned} $$ **Check yield:** `if ( x > 0 ) y = id + num ; else y = id ;` ≈ input. ✓ **Step 2 — Sketch the parse tree structure (described for the answer sheet):** - Root `S` → `if`, `(`, `C`, `)`, `S`, `else`, `S`. - `C` → `E`, `relop`, `E`; each `E` chain-folds down to `x` and `0` via `E → T → F → id` and `E → T → F → num`. - First inner `S` (then-branch) → `id`, `=`, `E`, `;`; with `E → E + T → T + T → F + F → id + num`. - Second inner `S` (else-branch) → `id`, `=`, `E`, `;`; with `E → T → F → id`. **Step 3 — Node count.** Derivation length = 14 ⇒ internal non-terminal nodes = 15. Leaves = the 16 terminal symbols. Total $\vert T_{PT} \vert = 15 + 16 = 31$ nodes. **(b)** Construct the **abstract syntax tree (AST)** for the same input string. List the operations performed during AST construction. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply transformations from parse tree:** 1. **Folding:** Collapse all chain productions $E \to T \to F \to \text{id}$ and $E \to T \to F \to \text{num}$. 2. **Pruning:** Remove the parentheses `(`, `)`, the semicolons `;`, and the keywords `if`, `else` — they are absorbed into the *type* of the AST node. 3. **Promotion:** Convert the terminal `>` into a relational operator node, `=` into an assignment operator node, `+` into an arithmetic operator node. **Step 2 — AST shape (described):** - Root: `IfElse` - Child 1 (condition): `>` (relational operator) - Left: leaf `x` - Right: leaf `0` - Child 2 (then-branch): `=` - Left: leaf `y` - Right: `+` - Left: leaf `x` - Right: leaf `1` - Child 3 (else-branch): `=` - Left: leaf `y` - Right: leaf `x` **Step 3 — Node count.** Internal nodes: 5 (`IfElse`, `>`, `=`, `=`, `+`). Leaves: 6 (`x`, `0`, `y`, `x`, `1`, `y`). Total $\vert T_{AST} \vert = 11$ nodes. **Comparison:** $\vert T_{PT} \vert - \vert T_{AST} \vert = 31 - 11 = 20$ nodes eliminated (≈ 64.5% reduction). --- ### Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** What is an intermediate representation? List and briefly describe three different forms of IR used in compilers. **[7 marks]** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECIT751, Module 3]** **Model Answer:** An **intermediate Representation (IR)** is a data structure internal to a compiler that represents the source program after front-end analysis but before target code generation. It serves as the bridge between the front-end (language-specific) and the back-end (target-specific). **Three forms of IR:** 1. **Graphical IRs — Syntax Trees (Parse Tree / AST):** Hierarchical tree structures that mirror the syntactic/semantic structure of the program. The AST is widely used in the front-end for semantic analysis and lightweight optimizations. 2. **Linear IRs — Three-Address Code (3AC):** A sequence of simple instructions of the form `x = y op z`, where each instruction has at most one operator on the right-hand side. Examples include the **stack-machine code** of JVM and the **register-transfer lists** of GCC's RTL. Easy to optimize using data-flow analysis. 3. **Graphical IRs — Control Flow Graphs (CFG) / Data Flow Graphs (DFG):** Directed graphs where nodes are basic blocks (or instructions) and edges represent control flow (or data dependencies). Used heavily in optimization phases. **LLVM IR** and **GIMPLE** are modern examples; the CFG form is essential for loop optimizations, dead-code elimination, and register allocation. **(b)** Explain the construction of an AST from a parse tree with a suitable example. How does the AST aid in subsequent compiler phases? **[7 marks]** **Model Answer:** **AST Construction Algorithm (Post-Order Transformation):** ``` function buildAST(parseNode): if parseNode is a leaf (terminal): if terminal is a meaningful operand (id, num, literal): return new LeafNode(value = parseNode.value) else: // punctuation, semicolons, parentheses -> discarded return null if parseNode has a single child (chain production): return buildAST(parseNode.firstChild) opNode = new OperatorNode(operator = parseNode.symbol) for each child of parseNode (left to right): subAST = buildAST(child) if subAST != null: opNode.addChild(subAST) return opNode ``` **Example:** For the parse tree of `id + id * id` (Section 4.1), the algorithm collapses chain rules, discards `+` and `*` as terminals, and produces the AST of Section 4.2. **How the AST aids later compiler phases:** 1. **Semantic analysis / type checking:** A single recursive walk over the AST can propagate types bottom-up and check for type errors (e.g., `int + float`). 2. **Optimization:** Patterns like constant folding (`2 + 3 → 5`) and algebraic identities (`x * 1 → x`) are easy to detect on ASTs via pattern matching. 3. **Code generation:** Tree-walking code generators emit target code directly from the AST. Even when a 3AC/CFG IR is built later, the AST is the natural starting point. 4. **Language retargeting:** The AST abstracts away the source syntax, allowing one back-end to serve multiple front-ends. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks in KTU valuation for "Graphical IRs — Syntax-Related Trees":** > > 1. **Confusing Parse Tree and AST in the answer:** If the question asks for the *AST* and you draw a *parse tree* (with chain rules intact and punctuation as leaves), you may lose **up to 4 of 7 marks** in that sub-part. Always re-read the question. > 2. **Forgetting to verify the yield:** Examiners award a mark for explicitly checking that the leaves of the parse tree, read left-to-right, match the input string. Skipping this step costs 1 mark. > 3. **Wrong operator precedence in the AST:** Drawing `*` shallower than `+` in an AST (instead of deeper) signals that you do not understand how tree depth encodes precedence. Cost: **2–3 marks**. > 4. **Missing chain-rule collapsing:** Leaving `E → T → F → id` as four levels instead of one leaf wastes space in the diagram and loses 1 mark for *clarity*. > 5. **Omitting the start symbol in the parse tree root:** The root must be the *start symbol* (e.g., `S` or `E`); using a different non-terminal costs 1 mark. > 6. **No arrow/line labels:** In Mermaid/textual diagrams, edge directions must be visible. An undirected mess loses 1 mark for *neatness*. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision checklist for "Graphical IRs — Syntax-Related Trees":** - **Parse Tree (CST):** Root = start symbol; every node = grammar symbol; leaves yield the input string; size up to $O(n^2)$. - **AST (Syntax Tree):** Internal nodes = operators/constructs; leaves = operands; punctuation and chain rules removed; size $O(n)$. - **Tree depth = operator precedence:** Deeper node = lower precedence (binds looser). - **Derivation order:** A *leftmost* (or rightmost) derivation uniquely determines a parse tree for an unambiguous grammar. - **Yield check:** $\text{Yield}(T_{PT}) = w$ confirms $w \in L(G)$. - **AST construction = post-order traversal** of the parse tree with chain-folding and punctuation-pruning. - **Position in the front-end:** Lexer → Parser (Parse Tree) → AST Builder (AST) → Semantic Analyser → IR Generator. - **Real-world examples:** GCC's GENERIC/GIMPLE, LLVM/Clang AST, Eclipse JDT AST. - **Difference in node count:** $\vert T_{AST} \vert \le \vert T_{PT} \vert$; the gap = nodes eliminated (chain rules + punctuation). - **Examiner's hint:** When asked to "draw the syntax tree," draw the **AST** unless told otherwise. Always re-read the question word-by-word. > **End of Notes — Graphical IRs: Syntax-Related Trees (KTU 2024 Scheme, PECIT751, Module 3)**

topics.title: Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Graphs # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Compiler Design (Intermediate Code Generation)**, a **Graph** refers to a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** used as an intermediate representation of a basic block of source code. A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** for an expression identifies the common subexpressions within it. It is a directed graph that contains no cycles, and it is typically used to represent an expression where: - **Leaf nodes** represent identifiers, names, or constants. - **Interior nodes** represent operators. - **Directed edges** connect an operator node to its operands (children). Unlike a syntax tree, a **DAG** has the property that a node representing a common subexpression is created **only once** and is **reused** whenever the same subexpression appears again. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) is a critical data structure used during Intermediate Code Generation to enable **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** and **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**, forming the foundation of machine-independent optimization. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Memo Pad" Imagine you are a chef following a recipe, but instead of writing each step from scratch, you maintain a **memo pad** of intermediate mixtures (a paste, a sauce, a dough) and simply refer back to the memo whenever the same mixture is needed again. - A **leaf node** = a raw ingredient (flour, water). - An **interior node** = a cooking operation (mixing, kneading). - A **directed edge** = an arrow showing "this operation produced that, which is now used here." - **Reusing a node** = realizing "Hey, I already kneaded the dough on line 2, I don't need to knead it again on line 5." This is exactly what a DAG does for the compiler: it avoids recomputing the same expression multiple times, dramatically speeding up the generated code. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** A **Syntax Tree** represents a *single derivation* of an expression, possibly duplicating identical subtrees. A **DAG** collapses all *identical* subtrees into a *single* shared node — saving both memory and execution time. ## 1.3 Syntax Tree vs. DAG — A Quick Comparison | Property | Syntax Tree | Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Cycle** | Acyclic (always) | Acyclic (always) | | **Node Uniqueness** | Every subexpression gets a new node | Identical subexpressions share one node | | **Structure** | Hierarchical tree | Directed graph with possible multiple parents | | **Primary Purpose** | Syntactic verification | Intermediate code generation & optimization | | **CSE Capability** | ❌ None | ✅ Built-in | | **Space Efficiency** | Lower | Higher | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Formal Node Representation Each node $N$ in a DAG can be represented as a record with the following fields: $$ N = \{\, \text{label}(N),\ \text{left}(N),\ \text{right}(N)\, \} $$ Where: - $\text{label}(N)$ is the operator symbol (or identifier/constant for leaves). - $\text{left}(N)$ and $\text{right}(N)$ are pointers (or indices) to the operand nodes. For **unary operators**, only $\text{left}(N)$ is used; for **leaf nodes** (variables/constants), both pointers are typically `null`. ## 2.2 Three-Address Statement Forms Recognized by a DAG The DAG constructor converts a basic block consisting of three-address statements. The forms handled are: | Statement Form | DAG Action | | :--- | :--- | | $x = y\ \text{op}\ z$ | Node $N$ corresponds to operation `op`; check if such node exists. If not, create. | | $x = y$ | No new node is created; node descriptor for $x$ points to the node of $y$. | | $x = \text{op}\ y$ | Unary operation; create/check node for `op(y)`. | | $\text{param}\ y$ | $y$ is added to the parameter list (no DAG node). | | $\text{if}\ x\ \text{relop}\ y\ \text{goto}\ L$ | Conditional jump; nodes for $x$ and $y$ are constructed. | ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Construction Algorithm (KTU Board-Favorite) For each three-address statement $x = y\ \text{op}\ z$ in the basic block: 1. **Resolve Left Operand:** Find/create a node $N_y$ corresponding to the current value of $y$ by checking the identifier table. 2. **Resolve Right Operand:** Find/create a node $N_z$ for $z$ similarly. 3. **Check for Existing Operation:** Search the DAG to see if there is already a node $M$ such that $\text{label}(M) = \text{op}$, $\text{left}(M) = N_y$, and $\text{right}(M) = N_z$. 4. **Decide Action:** - If $M$ **exists** → reuse $M$. - If $M$ **does not exist** → create a new node $M$ with the above fields. 5. **Update Identifier Table:** Update the entry for $x$ to point to node $M$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **"Why" behind Step 3:** The search is typically implemented using a **hash table** keyed on the tuple $(\text{op}, N_y, N_z)$. This makes common subexpression detection $O(1)$ on average, which is critical for compiler efficiency. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Expression / Rule | Used For | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Node Definition** | $N = \{\,\text{label},\,\text{left},\,\text{right}\,\}$ | Formal DAG specification | | **Acyclicity Constraint** | $\nexists\ v_i \to v_j \to \cdots \to v_i$ | Defining a valid DAG | | **Common Subexpression** | $E_1 = E_2 \implies \text{single node shared}$ | Detecting CSE | | **Identifier Binding** | $\text{node}(x_i) = M$ where $M$ is the latest definition of $x$ | Tracking variable values | | **Number of Nodes (Expression)** | $N_{\text{nodes}} = \text{Number of distinct subexpressions}$ | Measuring DAG compactness | | **Optimization Gain** | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{DAG nodes}}}{N_{\text{tree nodes}}}$ | Quantifying CSE savings | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility DAGs are not merely academic tools. They are the backbone of: - **LLVM / GCC Optimization Passes:** Every `opt` pass for CSE uses a DAG-based value numbering technique. - **Hardware Synthesis (VLSI):** FPGA design tools compile Boolean expressions into DAGs for circuit minimization. - **Network Routing Algorithms:** Shortest-path computations use DAG representations of dependency graphs. - **Database Query Optimizers:** Operator trees in SQL engines are converted into DAGs to find shared subqueries. - **Build Systems (Make, Bazel):** Dependency graphs are DAGs that prevent redundant compilation of source files. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a DAG from a Basic Block **Given Basic Block:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= 4 \times i \\ t_2 &= a[t_1] \\ t_3 &= 4 \times i \\ t_4 &= b[t_3] \\ t_5 &= t_2 \times t_4 \\ t_6 &= \text{prod} + t_5 \\ \text{prod} &= t_6 \\ t_7 &= i + 1 \\ i &= t_7 \end{aligned} $$ We will construct the DAG step by step. ### Step 1: Process $t_1 = 4 \times i$ - Search for node labeled `4`: not found → **create leaf node `4`**. - Search for node labeled `i`: not found → **create leaf node `i`**. - Search for node $(\times, 4, i)$: not found → **create interior node `×(4, i)`**. - Update $\text{node}(t_1) = \text{node}(\times)$. ### Step 2: Process $t_2 = a[t_1]$ - Search for `a`: **create leaf node `a`**. - Search for $t_1$: found, returns node of $\times(4, i)$. - Search for node $(\text{[]}, a, \times(4,i))$: not found → **create interior node `[]`** with children `a` and `×(4,i)`. - Update $\text{node}(t_2) = \text{node}([])$. ### Step 3: Process $t_3 = 4 \times i$ - Node `4` and node `i` **already exist**. - Search for node $(\times, 4, i)$: **FOUND** → reuse the existing `×(4, i)` node. - Update $\text{node}(t_3) = \text{node}(\times)$. *(No new node created — this is CSE!)* ### Step 4: Process $t_4 = b[t_3]$ - Search for `b`: **create leaf node `b`**. - Search for $t_3$: same as $t_1$, points to `×(4, i)`. - Search for $(\text{[]}, b, \times)$: not found → **create interior node `[]`** with children `b` and `×(4,i)`. - Update $\text{node}(t_4) = \text{node}([])$. ### Step 5: Process $t_5 = t_2 \times t_4$ - $t_2$ and $t_4$ are known nodes. - Search for $(\times, \text{node}(t_2), \text{node}(t_4))$: not found → **create interior node `×`** with those children. - Update $\text{node}(t_5) = \text{node}(\times)$. ### Step 6: Process $t_6 = \text{prod} + t_5$ - Create leaf `prod`. - Search for $(+, \text{prod}, t_5)$: not found → **create interior node `+`**. - Update $\text{node}(t_6) = \text{node}(+)$. ### Step 7: Process $\text{prod} = t_6$ - No new node — update the identifier `prod` to point to $\text{node}(+)$ (the latest definition of $t_6$). ### Step 8: Process $t_7 = i + 1$ - Create leaf `1` (or reuse if exists). - Search for $(+, i, 1)$: not found → **create interior node `+`** with children `i` and `1`. - Update $\text{node}(t_7) = \text{node}(+)$. ### Step 9: Process $i = t_7$ - Update `i` to point to $\text{node}(t_7)$, the latest `+` node. ## 3.2 Optimization Gain Calculation For the expression `a + a * (b - c) + (b - c) * d`, a syntax tree would contain **two identical** subtrees for `(b - c)`. The DAG contains only **one**. $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{tree}} &= 7 \text{ interior nodes} \\ N_{\text{DAG}} &= 6 \text{ interior nodes} \\ \eta &= 1 - \frac{6}{7} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 14.29\% \end{aligned} $$ This represents a **14.29%** reduction in computation nodes. ## 3.3 Numerical Verification of Algorithm Correctness Let us verify the **CSE** on a slightly different example: $$ E = (a + b) \times (a + b) + c $$ - **Tree nodes (interior):** 4 (two `+`, two `×` if fully expanded... actually: 2 `+`, 1 `×`, 1 final `+` = 4 interior) - **DAG nodes (interior):** 3 — one `+` shared, one `×`, one final `+`. - **Saved nodes:** 1. $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{tree}} &= 4 \\ N_{\text{DAG}} &= 3 \\ \text{CSE Savings} &= 4 - 3 = 1\ \text{node eliminated} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The generated three-address code would compute `(a + b)` only **once**, storing it in a temporary, and reuse that temporary in the multiplication — exactly as a human programmer would do manually. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Final DAG for the Worked Example The diagram below represents the complete DAG after processing all 9 statements of the basic block from Section 3.1. ```mermaid graph TD leaf4["CONST: 4"] leafI["ID: i"] leafA["ID: a"] leafB["ID: b"] leafProd["ID: prod"] leaf1["CONST: 1"] nodeMul1["OP: x (4, i)"] nodeArrA["OP: [] (a, nodeMul1)"] nodeArrB["OP: [] (b, nodeMul1)"] nodeMul2["OP: x (nodeArrA, nodeArrB)"] nodeAdd1["OP: + (leafProd, nodeMul2)"] nodeAdd2["OP: + (leafI, leaf1)"] t1Ref["t1, t3"]:::ref t2Ref["t2"]:::ref t4Ref["t4"]:::ref t5Ref["t5"]:::ref t6Ref["t6, prod_new"]:::ref t7Ref["t7, i_new"]:::ref leaf4 --> nodeMul1 leafI --> nodeMul1 leafI --> nodeAdd2 leafA --> nodeArrA nodeMul1 --> nodeArrA leafB --> nodeArrB nodeMul1 --> nodeArrB nodeArrA --> nodeMul2 nodeArrB --> nodeMul2 leafProd --> nodeAdd1 nodeMul2 --> nodeAdd1 leaf1 --> nodeAdd2 nodeMul1 -.-> t1Ref nodeArrA -.-> t2Ref nodeArrB -.-> t4Ref nodeMul2 -.-> t5Ref nodeAdd1 -.-> t6Ref nodeAdd2 -.-> t7Ref classDef ref fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The dashed edges with a pink-magenta style represent the **identifier table bindings** — i.e., which temporaries or names point to which DAG node. Notice how `t1` and `t3` both point to the **same** `x(4, i)` node, visually demonstrating Common Subexpression Elimination. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Basic Block
(Three-Address Code)"] --> B["Lexical &
Identifier Lookup"] B --> C{"Node
Exists?"} C -- Yes --> D["Reuse Existing
DAG Node"] C -- No --> E["Create New
DAG Node"] D --> F["Update Identifier
Descriptor Table"] E --> F F --> G{"More
Statements?"} G -- Yes --> B G -- No --> H["Optimized
DAG Output"] H --> I["CSE Pass"] I --> J["DCE Pass"] J --> K["Final Optimized
Three-Address Code"] style A fill:#e1f5ff style K fill:#d4edda style C fill:#fff3cd ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Diagram: Syntax Tree vs. DAG ```mermaid graph TD subgraph ST["SYNTAX TREE - Two Duplicate Subtrees"] STroot1["x"]:::tree STleft1["+"]:::tree STright1["a, b"]:::tree STroot1 --> STleft1 STroot1 --> STright1 STroot2["x"]:::tree STleft2["+"]:::tree STright2["a, b"]:::tree STroot2 --> STleft2 STroot2 --> STright2 end subgraph DAG["DAG - Shared Subexpression"] DAGroot["x"]:::dag DAGleft["x"]:::dag DAGplus["+"]:::dag DAGab["a, b"]:::dag DAGroot --> DAGleft DAGroot --> DAGplus DAGleft --> DAGplus DAGplus --> DAGab end classDef tree fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000 classDef dag fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#008800 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The syntax tree on the left has **two independent** `+` nodes — one for each occurrence of `a + b`. The DAG on the right has **only one** `+` node, which both `x` operations reference. This single structural difference is the source of all optimization gains. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) in the context of intermediate code generation. How does it differ from a syntax tree?** **Model Answer:** A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** is a representation of a basic block in which the common subexpressions are identified and shared. It contains leaf nodes (identifiers/constants) and interior nodes (operators), with no directed cycles. **Difference from Syntax Tree:** | Aspect | Syntax Tree | DAG | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Common subexpressions | Duplicated | Shared (single node) | | Space complexity | Higher | Lower | | Optimization support | None | CSE & DCE enabled | The DAG enables **common subexpression elimination**, which is impossible with a pure syntax tree. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **What is the role of an identifier table in DAG construction?** **Model Answer:** The **identifier table** (or name table) maintains, for each variable name `x` appearing in the basic block, a pointer to the most recent DAG node that defines the value of `x`. When a statement of the form `x = y op z` is processed: 1. The current nodes for `y` and `z` are looked up in the table. 2. After computing `y op z`, the entry for `x` is **updated** to point to the new (or reused) result node. This allows the DAG constructor to resolve operand values at any point in the block — a critical requirement for detecting common subexpressions across non-consecutive statements. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** Construct the **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** for the following basic block and list all nodes with their children. $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a + b \\ t_2 &= a - b \\ t_3 &= t_1 \times t_2 \\ t_4 &= t_1 + t_2 \\ t_5 &= t_3 \times t_4 \\ x &= t_5 \end{aligned} $$ **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** and **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)** using DAG. Show how the DAG constructed in part (a) can be used to perform these optimizations. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Statement-by-Statement Construction:** | Step | Statement | Action Taken | Resulting Node | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | $t_1 = a + b$ | Create leaves `a`, `b`; create `+` | $N_1 = +(a, b)$ | | 2 | $t_2 = a - b$ | Reuse `a`, `b`; create `-` | $N_2 = -(a, b)$ | | 3 | $t_3 = t_1 \times t_2$ | Reuse $N_1$, $N_2$; create `×` | $N_3 = \times(N_1, N_2)$ | | 4 | $t_4 = t_1 + t_2$ | Check $(+, N_1, N_2)$ — already exists as $N_1$? **No**, different operator. Create new `+` | $N_4 = +(N_1, N_2)$ | | 5 | $t_5 = t_3 \times t_4$ | Reuse $N_3$, $N_4$; create `×` | $N_5 = \times(N_3, N_4)$ | | 6 | $x = t_5$ | `x` points to $N_5$ | — | **Final DAG Node List:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_1 &= +(a, b) \\ N_2 &= -(a, b) \\ N_3 &= \times(N_1, N_2) \\ N_4 &= +(N_1, N_2) \\ N_5 &= \times(N_3, N_4) \end{aligned} $$ **Visual DAG Structure:** ```mermaid graph TD A["ID: a"]:::leaf B["ID: b"]:::leaf N1["N1: + (a, b)"]:::op N2["N2: - (a, b)"]:::op N3["N3: x (N1, N2)"]:::op N4["N4: + (N1, N2)"]:::op N5["N5: x (N3, N4)"]:::op A --> N1 B --> N1 A --> N2 B --> N2 N1 --> N3 N2 --> N3 N1 --> N4 N2 --> N4 N3 --> N5 N4 --> N5 classDef leaf fill:#fff9c4 classDef op fill:#c8e6c9 ``` **Identifier Bindings:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{node}(t_1) &= N_1,\quad \text{node}(t_2) = N_2,\quad \text{node}(t_3) = N_3,\\ \text{node}(t_4) &= N_4,\quad \text{node}(t_5) = N_5,\quad \text{node}(x) = N_5 \end{aligned} $$ **Total Interior Nodes:** $5$ (versus $6$ in a syntax tree → saves $1$ redundant addition). --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution **1. Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE):** In the DAG above, although $t_1 = a + b$ and the **subterm** $a$ / $b$ appear in $t_2$ as operands, $t_1$ and $t_2$ are **not common subexpressions** because they are computed with **different operators** (`+` vs. `-`). Thus, no CSE is applied to $N_1$ and $N_2$. **However**, if the input had been (for instance) two statements both of the form `t_i = a + b`, the DAG algorithm would automatically detect the second as a CSE and avoid recomputation. This is the **core value** of DAGs over plain syntax trees. **Code without DAG (naive):** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a + b \quad (1\ \text{add}) \\ t_2 &= a - b \quad (1\ \text{sub}) \\ t_3 &= t_1 \times t_2 \quad (1\ \text{mul}) \\ t_4 &= t_1 + t_2 \quad (1\ \text{add}) \\ t_5 &= t_3 \times t_4 \quad (1\ \text{mul}) \end{aligned} $$ **Code with DAG (optimized):** The DAG reuses $N_1$ and $N_2$ in computing both $N_3$ and $N_4$, but no CSE is applicable here as the operators differ. The DAG-based code generator would still produce semantically identical code, but the **DAG is the data structure** that enables CSE whenever the opportunity arises. **2. Dead Code Elimination (DCE):** A statement is "dead" if its result is **never used** outside the basic block. From the DAG, we identify which variables' last definition is "used" (i.e., a `use` list). - `t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4` are intermediate — their last uses are within the block. - `x` is used **outside** the block (it is the block's output). - $\Rightarrow$ All temporaries are needed for computing `x`. - $\Rightarrow$ No dead code in this particular block. **To illustrate DCE**: had there been a statement `t_6 = a * 7` that was never used (no `use` of `t_6`), it would be removed because its node would have no outgoing identifier bindings. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — CSE Misidentification:** > Students often confuse **same operands with different operators** as a CSE. For example, `a + b` and `a - b` are **NOT** common subexpressions, even though they share operands. CSE requires **identical operator AND identical operands**. Always state this explicitly in your answer to avoid losing **2 marks**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **(a)** Construct the DAG for the following block. Show the contents of the identifier table after each statement. $$ \begin{aligned} a &= b + c \\ b &= a - d \\ c &= b + c \\ d &= a - d \end{aligned} $$ **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is the role of **value numbering** in DAG-based optimization? Explain with an example. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Important Catch:** The block reassigns `b` and `d`, so earlier uses must refer to **old** values stored in separate identifier-table entries (often subscripted as $b_1, b_2$, etc.). | Step | Statement | Action | DAG Created | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $a = b + c$ | Create `b`, `c`, `+` node | $N_1 = +(b, c)$; $\text{node}(a) = N_1$ | | 2 | $b = a - d$ | `a` is $N_1$, create `d`, new `-` node | $N_2 = -(N_1, d)$; $\text{node}(b_{\text{new}}) = N_2$ | | 3 | $c = b + c$ | **CRITICAL:** `b` here is the **NEW** `b` = $N_2$. `c` is still the original `c` leaf. Create `+`. | $N_3 = +(N_2, c)$; $\text{node}(c_{\text{new}}) = N_3$ | | 4 | $d = a - d$ | `a` is $N_1$ (unchanged). New `-` check: $(-, N_1, d)$? **EXISTS** as $N_2$! | Reuse $N_2$; $\text{node}(d_{\text{new}}) = N_2$ | **Final DAG Nodes:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_1 &= +(b_{\text{old}}, c) \\ N_2 &= -(N_1, d_{\text{old}}) \\ N_3 &= +(N_2, c) \end{aligned} $$ **Identifier Table:** | Variable | Points To | | :---: | :---: | | $a$ | $N_1$ | | $b$ | $N_2$ | | $c$ | $N_3$ | | $d$ | $N_2$ | **Result:** $d$ is **eliminated** as a separate definition — the compiler recognizes that $a - d$ has already been computed for $b$, and reuses it. ```mermaid graph TD bOld["b_old"]:::leaf cLeaf["c"]:::leaf dOld["d_old"]:::leaf N1["N1: + (b_old, c)"]:::op N2["N2: - (N1, d_old)"]:::op N3["N3: + (N2, c)"]:::op bOld --> N1 cLeaf --> N1 N1 --> N2 dOld --> N2 N2 --> N3 cLeaf --> N3 aRef["a"]:::ref bRef["b"]:::ref cRef["c"]:::ref dRef["d"]:::ref N1 -.-> aRef N2 -.-> bRef N2 -.-> dRef N3 -.-> cRef classDef leaf fill:#fff9c4 classDef op fill:#c8e6c9 classDef ref fill:#e1bee7 ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Reassignment Blindness:** > A very common mistake (worth losing **3 marks**!) is to use the **new** value of `b` in the statement `a = b + c` on line 1 retroactively, or to assume `b` in line 3 still refers to the **old** `b`. Always strictly track the **identifier-table binding** at the point of evaluation. Use subscripts like $b_1, b_2$ to make this crystal clear in the exam. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Value Numbering:** **Value numbering** is a technique closely related to DAG construction in which every computed subexpression is assigned a unique **integer "value number"** that serves as its canonical name. Two subexpressions with the same value number are known to be **semantically equivalent**. **Algorithm Sketch:** 1. Maintain a hash table mapping each distinct subexpression $(\text{op}, v_1, v_2)$ → a unique value number, where $v_1, v_2$ are value numbers of the operands. 2. When a new subexpression is encountered: - If its triple is already in the table → reuse the existing value number (**CSE detected**). - Else → assign a fresh value number and insert. **Example:** For the block: $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a + b \\ t_2 &= a + b \\ t_3 &= t_1 \times t_2 \end{aligned} $$ - Statement 1: $(+, a, b)$ → new value number **V1**. $\text{node}(t_1) = V_1$. - Statement 2: $(+, a, b)$ → **already in table** → reuse **V1**. $\text{node}(t_2) = V_1$. - Statement 3: $(\times, V_1, V_1)$ → new value number **V2**. $\text{node}(t_3) = V_2$. **Result:** $t_2$ is immediately identified as a CSE of $t_1$ and the compiler emits only **one** addition instruction. **Role in DAG:** The DAG is essentially a **visual, structural** representation of value numbering. Internally, compilers like **LLVM** use a value-numbering scheme to perform CSE in $O(1)$ amortized time using hash lookups. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Confusing "Value Numbering" with "Value in Register":** > "Value numbering" is **NOT** about register allocation. It is a compile-time analysis that assigns abstract integers to expressions. Examiners specifically test this distinction — confusing the two will cost you **2 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — DAGs (Graphs in Module 3)** - ✅ **DAG** = Directed Acyclic Graph used for **intermediate code representation** with **shared** common subexpressions. - ✅ **Leaf nodes** = identifiers / constants; **Interior nodes** = operators. - ✅ A DAG is **always acyclic** — its defining mathematical property. - ✅ **Key difference from syntax tree:** Common subexpressions are merged into a **single node** (DAG has multiple parents; tree has only one). - ✅ **Construction Algorithm** uses an **identifier table** to look up current operand nodes and a **hash table** for fast CSE detection. - ✅ **Three statement types handled:** Binary assignment `x = y op z`, copy `x = y`, unary `x = op y`. - ✅ **Reassignment matters:** When `b = a - d` is reassigned, the **new** `b` and the **old** `b` are tracked separately in the identifier table (use subscripts!). - ✅ **CSE** (Common Subexpression Elimination) and **DCE** (Dead Code Elimination) are the two primary DAG-based optimizations. - ✅ **Value numbering** is the hash-based, integer-labeling technique underlying DAG construction. - ✅ **Real-world use:** LLVM, GCC, database query optimizers, FPGA synthesis tools, and build systems. - ✅ **Reuse detection rule:** Operator and **both** operands (by value number) must match for CSE; same operands with **different operators** do **not** qualify. - ✅ **Dead Code Test:** If a node has no identifier-table binding pointing to it as the *latest* definition, it is dead and can be removed. - ✅ **Time complexity** of DAG construction is $O(n)$ amortized (using hashing), where $n$ is the number of statements in the basic block. ---

topics.title: Linear IRs - Stack-Machine Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Linear IRs - Stack-Machine Code # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Linear Intermediate Representations (IRs) A **Linear IR** is a sequence of instructions (a flat, linear list) that represents the program's computation. Unlike graphical IRs (such as Control Flow Graphs or Abstract Syntax Trees), a linear IR lacks an explicit hierarchical or graph structure — it is simply a **straight-line list of instructions** that the compiler walks from top to bottom. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Linear IR):** > A Linear IR is a notational intermediate representation in which the program is expressed as a sequential, ordered list (or sequence) of instructions. Examples include Three-Address Code (TAC), Stack-Machine Code, and Java Bytecode. The two principal members of the linear IR family used in Module 3 are: 1. **Three-Address Code (TAC)** — Instructions of the form `x = y op z` with at most one operator per instruction. 2. **Stack-Machine Code** — Postfix-style instructions that operate implicitly on an **evaluation stack** (a Last-In-First-Out data structure). ## 1.2 Stack-Machine Code — Formal Definition **Stack-Machine Code** (also called **Bytecode** or **Stack-Based IR**) is a linear IR in which every instruction is a short, fixed-size operation that: - **Pushes** a value onto the evaluation stack, OR - **Pops** operands from the stack, computes a result, and **pushes** the result back onto the stack. There are **no explicit operand names** in arithmetic instructions — the operands are implicitly taken from the **top of the stack (TOS)**. This is the same model used by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**, the **.NET CLR**, and the **WebAssembly (Wasm) interpreter**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight:** Stack-machine code is essentially a **postfix (Reverse Polish) notation** where each "postfix token" has been replaced by a named instruction opcode. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cafeteria Tray Counter" Imagine a cafeteria service line: - A **tray** acts as the evaluation stack. - A **customer (instruction)** can either **place a dish on the tray** (PUSH) or **take the top two dishes, mix them, and place the result back** (BINARY OP). - The **chef at the end** only ever looks at the **top of the tray** — never at a specific dish by name. Similarly, in a stack machine: - The compiler issues `PUSH` instructions to place operands on the stack. - Arithmetic instructions (`ADD`, `MUL`, etc.) consume the top values and push the result. - The location of intermediate values is **always implicit** — the next free slot on the stack. ## 1.4 Why Use Stack-Machine Code? - **Compactness:** Instructions are short (often 1 byte opcode). Java's `.class` files famously use this compactness for network transfer. - **Portability:** The same stack code can run on any hardware with a stack-based interpreter (the famous "Write Once, Run Anywhere" promise of Java). - **Simplicity of Code Generation:** Translating an expression tree into stack code is straightforward — a postorder traversal. - **No Register Allocation Required:** The stack *is* the storage — no need for complex graph-coloring register allocators. > [!TIP] > **KTU Favourite Question:** Examiners love asking *"Why is Java bytecode considered a stack-machine code?"* The answer revolves around: **(1) all operands are pushed/popped from an operand stack**, and **(2) the JVM spec explicitly defines `push`, `pop`, `add`, `mul`, etc. as stack operations.** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Stack-Machine Instruction A typical stack-machine instruction has one of three forms: | Instruction Form | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | `PUSH v` | Push the constant/variable `v` onto the stack | `PUSH 5` | | `POP x` | Pop the TOS into variable `x` (store) | `POP x` | | `OP` | Pop two operands, apply operation, push result | `ADD`, `MUL`, `SUB`, `DIV` | **Key Properties:** - The stack is unbounded (conceptually) — but in practice, the compiler tracks the **maximum stack depth** for code generation. - No instruction refers to a memory location *except* `LOAD` and `STORE` (which move between stack and named variables). - All arithmetic is **binary** (two operands → one result), matching standard CPU semantics. ## 2.2 Stack Operations — Operator Catalogue | Opcode | Effect on Stack (Top on the Right) | Mathematical Meaning | |---|---|---| | `PUSH c` | `… → …, c` | Push constant `c` | | `LOAD x` | `… → …, val(x)` | Push value of variable `x` | | `STORE x` | `…, v → …` and `val(x) := v` | Pop TOS into `x` | | `ADD` | `…, a, b → …, a+b` | Pop two, push sum | | `SUB` | `…, a, b → …, a-b` | Pop two, push difference | | `MUL` | `…, a, b → …, a*b` | Pop two, push product | | `DIV` | `…, a, b → …, a/b` | Pop two, push quotient | | `NEG` | `…, a → …, -a` | Unary negation | | `EQ, NE, LT, GT, LE, GE` | `…, a, b → …, bool(a op b)` | Comparisons | | `JMP L` | Unconditional branch to label `L` | Control flow | | `JMPZ L` | `…, cond → …`, jump if TOS == 0 | Conditional branch | | `LABEL L:` | Marks target of jump | — | > [!NOTE] > **Operand Order Convention (KTU Standard):** In KTU textbooks, when `ADD` is executed, the **second-from-top** operand is the LEFT operand. So if the stack is `…, a, b` (with `b` on top), `ADD` produces `a + b`. This matches postfix: `a b +`. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Conversion Rule | Stack-Machine Translation | |---|---|---| | 1 | Translate a constant `c` | `PUSH c` | | 2 | Translate a variable `x` | `LOAD x` | | 3 | Translate `E1 + E2` | `code(E1)`, `code(E2)`, `ADD` | | 4 | Translate `E1 - E2` | `code(E1)`, `code(E2)`, `SUB` | | 5 | Translate `E1 * E2` | `code(E1)`, `code(E2)`, `MUL` | | 6 | Translate `E1 / E2` | `code(E1)`, `code(E2)`, `DIV` | | 7 | Translate `-E` (unary minus) | `code(E)`, `NEG` | | 8 | Assignment `x = E` | `code(E)`, `STORE x` | | 9 | Access array element `a[i]` | `code(a)`, `code(i)`, `INDEX` | | 10 | Function call `f(x,y)` | `ARGS x`, `ARGS y`, `CALL f` | | Property | Value / Description | |---|---| | **Instruction size** | Typically **1 byte opcode** (plus optional operand) | | **Operand naming** | None — implicit (top of stack) | | **Stack depth metric** | Maximum height of stack during execution = **code-size optimization parameter** | | **Real-world example** | **Java Bytecode**, **LLVM was not stack-based** (uses SSA), **CPython Bytecode** | | **Number of operands in `ADD`** | **Two** (one result) | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded systems & interpreters:** Stack machines are cheap to implement in hardware (Forth chips, Java processors like the **aJ-100**). - **Network transmission:** Stack bytecode is small → ideal for **applets** and **mobile code**. - **JIT compilation:** Modern JVMs (HotSpot, OpenJ9) **interpret** stack bytecode first, then JIT-compile it to native register code for hot methods. - **WebAssembly (Wasm):** While Wasm is technically a register machine, it uses an explicit **value stack** — the same conceptual model as stack-machine code. ## 2.5 Why is the Stack Implicit? — The 'Why' Behind the Design By making operand locations implicit (always at the stack top), the compiler **never needs to compute data-flow information** to know where operands come from. The next instruction's source is *always* the previous instruction's destination. This makes: - **Code generation trivial** (just a postorder tree walk). - **Verification easy** (stack depth can be statically computed at every program point). - **Interpreters simple** (a single switch statement on the opcode). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Translating a Simple Expression **Problem:** Translate the arithmetic expression $$E = (a + b) \times (c - d)$$ into stack-machine code. **Step 1 — Parse into an expression tree (mental model):** The expression is parsed into a tree where `*` is the root, `+` and `-` are children, and `a, b, c, d` are leaves. **Step 2 — Apply the translation rules from the Formula Sheet:** We must translate sub-expression `a + b` first (left sub-tree of `*`): - Translate `a` → `LOAD a` - Translate `b` → `LOAD b` - Combine with `+` → append `ADD` This produces: $$\text{code}(a+b) = \{\text{LOAD } a,\ \text{LOAD } b,\ \text{ADD}\}$$ Similarly, translate `c - d`: - Translate `c` → `LOAD c` - Translate `d` → `LOAD d` - Combine with `-` → append `SUB` This produces: $$\text{code}(c-d) = \{\text{LOAD } c,\ \text{LOAD } d,\ \text{SUB}\}$$ **Step 3 — Concatenate and add the root operator:** Concatenate the two sub-codes, then append `MUL`: $$\begin{aligned} \text{code}(E) &= \text{code}(a+b)\ \Vert\ \text{code}(c-d)\ \Vert\ \{\text{MUL}\} \\ &= \{\text{LOAD } a,\ \text{LOAD } b,\ \text{ADD},\ \text{LOAD } c,\ \text{LOAD } d,\ \text{SUB},\ \text{MUL}\} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Trace the stack to verify:** | Instruction | Stack (bottom → top) | Comment | |---|---|---| | `LOAD a` | `[a]` | Push value of `a` | | `LOAD b` | `[a, b]` | Push value of `b` | | `ADD` | `[a+b]` | Pop `a, b`, push sum | | `LOAD c` | `[a+b, c]` | Push value of `c` | | `LOAD d` | `[a+b, c, d]` | Push value of `d` | | `SUB` | `[a+b, c-d]` | Pop `c, d`, push difference | | `MUL` | `[(a+b)*(c-d)]` | Pop two, push product | **Result:** The TOS contains the value of `E` — exactly as required. ✅ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Precedence Handling (No Parentheses Needed!) **Problem:** Translate the unbracketed expression $$E = a + b \times c + d$$ into stack-machine code, correctly honouring operator precedence (`×` binds tighter than `+`). **Step 1 — Identify sub-expressions based on precedence:** Standard precedence gives the parse: $$E = a + (b \times c) + d$$ **Step 2 — Code the inner-most sub-expression first:** Translate `b × c`: $$\text{code}(b \times c) = \{\text{LOAD } b,\ \text{LOAD } c,\ \text{MUL}\}$$ Translate `a + (b × c)`: $$\text{code}(\text{left}) = \{\text{LOAD } a,\ \text{LOAD } b,\ \text{LOAD } c,\ \text{MUL},\ \text{ADD}\}$$ Translate `(left result) + d`: $$\text{code}(E) = \{\text{LOAD } a,\ \text{LOAD } b,\ \text{LOAD } c,\ \text{MUL},\ \text{ADD},\ \text{LOAD } d,\ \text{ADD}\}$$ **Step 3 — Stack trace to verify correctness:** | # | Instruction | Stack | TOS Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `LOAD a` | `[a]` | — | | 2 | `LOAD b` | `[a, b]` | — | | 3 | `LOAD c` | `[a, b, c]` | — | | 4 | `MUL` | `[a, b*c]` | Multiplication computed first ✓ | | 5 | `ADD` | `[a+b*c]` | Left addition done | | 6 | `LOAD d` | `[a+b*c, d]` | — | | 7 | `ADD` | `[a+b*c+d]` | Final result ✓ | **Critical observation:** Because we followed the **parse tree** (not the linear text), the stack machine correctly evaluates `b × c` before adding `a`, **even though no parentheses were written in the source**. This is the elegance of tree-to-stack-code translation. ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Assignment Statement **Problem:** Translate `x = (a - b) * 2` into stack-machine code. **Step 1 — Code the right-hand side:** For the expression `(a - b) * 2`: $$\text{code}((a-b)\times 2) = \{\text{LOAD } a,\ \text{LOAD } b,\ \text{SUB},\ \text{PUSH } 2,\ \text{MUL}\}$$ **Step 2 — Append `STORE x` to capture the result into the variable:** $$\text{code}(x = (a-b) \times 2) = \{\text{LOAD } a,\ \text{LOAD } b,\ \text{SUB},\ \text{PUSH } 2,\ \text{MUL},\ \text{STORE } x\}$$ **Step 3 — Stack trace:** | # | Instruction | Stack | |---|---|---| | 1 | `LOAD a` | `[a]` | | 2 | `LOAD b` | `[a, b]` | | 3 | `SUB` | `[a-b]` | | 4 | `PUSH 2` | `[a-b, 2]` | | 5 | `MUL` | `[(a-b)*2]` | | 6 | `STORE x` | `[]` (variable `x` now holds the result) | ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — TAC vs Stack-Machine Code | Property | Three-Address Code (TAC) | Stack-Machine Code | |---|---|---| | Instruction count (for `a+b*c`) | 2 (`t1 = b*c`, `t2 = a+t1`) | 4 (LOAD, LOAD, LOAD, MUL, ADD) | | Explicit temporaries needed? | **Yes** (`t1, t2, …`) | **No** (stack holds intermediates) | | Operand naming | **Explicit** (`x, y, z, t1`) | **Implicit** (always TOS) | | Code-generation algorithm | Next-use / liveness analysis | Simple postorder tree walk | | Register allocation | Required (graph coloring) | Not required | | Compactness of object code | Lower | **Higher** (shorter opcodes) | | Verification complexity | Medium (SSA / dataflow) | **Low** (stack depth is local) | | Real-world use | **LLVM IR**, GCC GIMPLE | **JVM Bytecode**, CPython Bytecode | ## 3.5 Algorithm — `genStackCode(E)` (Recursive) The compiler uses a simple **recursive descent** over the AST: $$\begin{aligned} &\text{genStackCode}(E \to \text{const } c): \quad \text{emit } \texttt{PUSH } c \\ &\text{genStackCode}(E \to \text{id } x): \quad \text{emit } \texttt{LOAD } x \\ &\text{genStackCode}(E \to E_1 \text{ op } E_2): \quad \text{genStackCode}(E_1);\ \text{genStackCode}(E_2);\ \text{emit } \texttt{op} \\ &\text{genStackCode}(E \to -E_1): \quad \text{genStackCode}(E_1);\ \text{emit } \texttt{NEG} \\ &\text{genStackCode}(S \to x = E): \quad \text{genStackCode}(E);\ \text{emit } \texttt{STORE } x \end{aligned}$$ > [!TIP] > **For KTU board answers:** Always show the **recursive definition** of `genStackCode` *before* applying it to an example. This earns full methodology marks. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Stack Machine Execution Engine The following block-level functional architecture illustrates how a stack-machine interpreter executes stack bytecode. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Bytecode Stream
Instruction Pointer IP] --> B[Fetch Unit
Reads Next Opcode] B --> C{Opcode Type?} C -- PUSH / LOAD --> D[Operand Stack
Push Value on Top] C -- ADD / SUB / MUL / DIV --> E[ALU Unit
Pops Two Operands] C -- STORE --> F[Local Variable Table
Pop TOS into Slot] C -- JMP / JMPZ --> G[Branch Unit
Update IP] E --> D D --> H[Program Counter
IP = IP + 1] F --> H G --> H H --> A ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **Fetch Unit** reads the next opcode from the linear bytecode stream. - The **Opcode Decoder** (`{Opcode Type?}`) routes the instruction to the correct functional block. - The **Operand Stack** is the central data structure — every PUSH/LOAD grows it, every binary op shrinks it by 1 (pops 2, pushes 1). - The **Local Variable Table** is where `STORE` and `LOAD` access named variables. - After execution, the **Program Counter** advances and the cycle repeats. ## 4.2 Flowchart — Translating an Expression to Stack-Machine Code ```mermaid flowchart TD S([Start: Input AST]) --> P{Is Node a
Leaf?} P -- Yes, Constant c --> PC[emit PUSH c] P -- Yes, Variable x --> PL[emit LOAD x] P -- No, Binary Op --> R1[Recurse on Left Child E1] R1 --> R2[Recurse on Right Child E2] R2 --> PO[emit op code
ADD or SUB or MUL or DIV] PC --> END([End: Stack Code Generated]) PL --> END PO --> END ``` **Interpretation:** This is a **postorder traversal** of the AST. Leaf nodes generate single push/load instructions; internal nodes recurse on children *before* emitting the operator — this guarantees operands are on the stack before the operator executes. ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Stack Evolution During Evaluation of `a + b * c` ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1["Step 1: LOAD a"] A1[Stack: a] end subgraph S2["Step 2: LOAD b"] A2[Stack: a, b] end subgraph S3["Step 3: LOAD c"] A3[Stack: a, b, c] end subgraph S4["Step 4: MUL"] A4[Stack: a, b times c] end subgraph S5["Step 5: ADD"] A5[Stack: a + b times c] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 ``` **Reading the diagram:** At each step, the topmost values on the stack are the *operands that will be consumed by the next operator*. This visualizes why the parse tree's postorder traversal is exactly what the stack machine expects. ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Position of Stack-Machine Code in the Compiler Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Source Program
High Level Language] --> LEX[Lexical Analyzer
Tokens] LEX --> PARSE[Syntax Analyzer
Parse Tree or AST] PARSE --> SEM[Semantic Analyzer
Annotated AST] SEM --> IRGEN[IR Generator
Stack Machine Code] IRGEN --> OPT[Optimizer
Optional Peephole] OPT --> CG[Code Generator
Target Machine Code] ``` **Highlight:** The **IR Generator** box is where this module's topic lives. The input is an AST; the output is a linear stream of stack opcodes. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define Stack-Machine Code. Why is it classified as a Linear IR?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Stack-Machine Code is a linear intermediate representation in which instructions operate on an implicit evaluation stack. Operands are pushed onto the stack by `PUSH` / `LOAD` instructions, and arithmetic operations (e.g., `ADD`, `MUL`) pop their operands from the top of the stack and push the result back. **Why Linear (1 mark):** It is classified as a *linear* IR because the program is represented as a **single, sequential list of instructions** with no hierarchical or graph structure. Unlike an AST or CFG, there is no branching in the representation itself — the control flow is encoded only by explicit jump instructions (`JMP`, `JMPZ`). --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Compare Three-Address Code (TAC) and Stack-Machine Code with respect to operand representation and the need for temporaries.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | TAC | Stack-Machine Code | |---|---|---| | **Operand representation** | Explicit (named variables/temporaries) | Implicit (always at TOS) | | **Need for temporaries** | Yes — `t1, t2, …` are required | No — the stack holds intermediates | (3 marks for correct comparative table and explanation.) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the concept of **Stack-Machine Code** as a linear intermediate representation. Discuss its **advantages** and **disadvantages** compared to Three-Address Code. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Generate stack-machine code for the following expressions. Show the **stack trace** after each instruction: **(7 marks)** $$\text{(i) } x = (a + b) \times c - d \qquad \text{(ii) } y = (p - q) / (r + s \times t)$$ --- #### 📝 Model Answer — Question A **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **Definition (2 marks):** Stack-Machine Code is a linear IR where each instruction manipulates an implicit evaluation stack. Operands are pushed via `PUSH` (constants) or `LOAD` (variables). Binary operators (`ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV`) pop two operands from the stack top, compute, and push the result. The stack depth is bounded and statically computable. **Advantages over TAC (3 marks):** 1. **No temporaries required** — TAC needs `t1, t2, …` for every sub-expression; the stack holds them implicitly. 2. **Compact object code** — operands are 1 byte opcodes, no address fields. Smaller files (e.g., JARs). 3. **Simpler code generation** — a postorder tree walk produces the code; no need for next-use dataflow analysis. 4. **Easier interpretation** — a single `switch(opcode)` loop is sufficient; no symbolic register lookup. **Disadvantages (2 marks):** 1. **Stack manipulation overhead** — every `LOAD`/`STORE` adds explicit stack traffic that a register machine can avoid. 2. **Harder to optimize at IR level** — most classical optimizations (common subexpression elimination, copy propagation) are designed for TAC/SSA and need translation. --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Expression (i): `x = (a + b) × c - d`** Parse tree: `((a + b) × c) - d` (left-associative) Stack-machine code: $$\text{code}(x) = \{\ \text{LOAD } a,\ \text{LOAD } b,\ \text{ADD},\ \text{LOAD } c,\ \text{MUL},\ \text{LOAD } d,\ \text{SUB},\ \text{STORE } x\ \}$$ **Stack trace:** | # | Instruction | Stack (bottom → top) | |---|---|---| | 1 | `LOAD a` | `[a]` | | 2 | `LOAD b` | `[a, b]` | | 3 | `ADD` | `[a+b]` | | 4 | `LOAD c` | `[a+b, c]` | | 5 | `MUL` | `[(a+b)*c]` | | 6 | `LOAD d` | `[(a+b)*c, d]` | | 7 | `SUB` | `[(a+b)*c - d]` | | 8 | `STORE x` | `[]`, `x = (a+b)*c - d` ✅ | --- **Expression (ii): `y = (p - q) / (r + s × t)`** Parse tree: `((p - q)) / ((r + (s × t)))` (precedence: × before +) Stack-machine code: $$\text{code}(y) = \{\ \text{LOAD } p,\ \text{LOAD } q,\ \text{SUB},\ \text{LOAD } r,\ \text{LOAD } s,\ \text{LOAD } t,\ \text{MUL},\ \text{ADD},\ \text{DIV},\ \text{STORE } y\ \}$$ **Stack trace:** | # | Instruction | Stack | |---|---|---| | 1 | `LOAD p` | `[p]` | | 2 | `LOAD q` | `[p, q]` | | 3 | `SUB` | `[p-q]` | | 4 | `LOAD r` | `[p-q, r]` | | 5 | `LOAD s` | `[p-q, r, s]` | | 6 | `LOAD t` | `[p-q, r, s, t]` | | 7 | `MUL` | `[p-q, r, s*t]` | | 8 | `ADD` | `[p-q, r+s*t]` | | 9 | `DIV` | `[(p-q)/(r+s*t)]` | | 10 | `STORE y` | `[]`, `y = (p-q)/(r+s*t)` ✅ | --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat **block diagram**, describe the architecture of a **stack machine**. Explain how the operand stack, instruction pointer, and instruction stream interact during execution. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Translate the following code segment into **stack-machine code** and trace the stack at every step. Identify the **maximum stack depth** required: **(7 marks)** ``` A = 10 B = 20 C = A + B D = A * B + C E = D - A ``` --- #### 📝 Model Answer — Question B **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1. Key components: - **Instruction Stream** — Linear sequence of bytes (the bytecode). - **Instruction Pointer (IP)** — Points to the next opcode to execute; advances by 1 (or by operand size) after fetch. - **Fetch Unit** — Reads opcode at `IP`, increments IP. - **Decoder** — Classifies opcode (PUSH, LOAD, ALU op, STORE, JUMP). - **Operand Stack** — LIFO buffer holding intermediate values. Grows on PUSH/LOAD, shrinks on binary ops and STORE. - **ALU** — Performs arithmetic when opcodes `ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV` are decoded. - **Local Variable Table** — Named storage accessed by `LOAD x` and `STORE x`. **Interaction (4 marks):** 1. **Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle** repeats for every instruction. 2. On `PUSH c` — constant is loaded onto TOS; IP advances. 3. On `LOAD x` — value of `x` from variable table is pushed; IP advances. 4. On `ADD` — ALU pops top two, computes sum, pushes back. **Net stack change: -1.** 5. On `STORE x` — TOS is popped into variable `x`. **Net stack change: -1.** 6. On `JMP L` — IP is set to label `L` (no stack change). 7. The cycle continues until a `HALT` or end-of-method is reached. --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Stack-machine translation:** | Source Line | Stack-Machine Code | |---|---| | `A = 10` | `PUSH 10`, `STORE A` | | `B = 20` | `PUSH 20`, `STORE B` | | `C = A + B` | `LOAD A`, `LOAD B`, `ADD`, `STORE C` | | `D = A * B + C` | `LOAD A`, `LOAD B`, `MUL`, `LOAD C`, `ADD`, `STORE D` | | `E = D - A` | `LOAD D`, `LOAD A`, `SUB`, `STORE E` | **Full program:** ``` PUSH 10 STORE A PUSH 20 STORE B LOAD A LOAD B ADD STORE C LOAD A LOAD B MUL LOAD C ADD STORE D LOAD D LOAD A SUB STORE E ``` **Stack trace for the line `D = A * B + C`:** | # | Instruction | Stack | |---|---|---| | 1 | `LOAD A` | `[A]` | | 2 | `LOAD B` | `[A, B]` | | 3 | `MUL` | `[A*B]` | | 4 | `LOAD C` | `[A*B, C]` | | 5 | `ADD` | `[A*B + C]` | | 6 | `STORE D` | `[]` ✅ | **Maximum stack depth analysis:** - For `A = 10` line: depth = **1** (after `PUSH 10`). - For `C = A + B` line: depth = **2** (after `LOAD A`, `LOAD B`). - For `D = A * B + C` line: depth = **2** (after `LOAD A`, `LOAD B`). - For `E = D - A` line: depth = **2** (after `LOAD D`, `LOAD A`). **Maximum stack depth required by the program = 2** ✅ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Operand order in `SUB` and `DIV`:** Many students incorrectly write `(a, b) → (a+b)` for `ADD` but forget that for `SUB`, the stack `[a, b]` (b on top) produces `a - b`, **not** `b - a`. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this. > 2. **Forgetting `STORE` for assignments:** When asked to translate `x = E`, students often emit only `code(E)` and forget the final `STORE x`. This costs the full 1 mark of the assignment line. > 3. **Missing precedence in `a + b * c`:** If you code it as `LOAD a, LOAD b, ADD, LOAD c, MUL`, you have evaluated `(a+b)*c` — **wrong**! The correct parse is `a + (b*c)`. Always follow the **parse tree**, not the source text. > 4. **No stack trace:** A translation without a verifying stack trace gets only **partial** marks (3 out of 7 for the translation part). The trace demonstrates understanding. > 5. **Confusing PUSH and LOAD:** `PUSH` is for **literals/constants**, `LOAD` is for **variables**. Examiners will deliberately include a constant like `PUSH 5` to test this. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Linear IR = sequence of instructions** (no tree/graph structure in the IR itself). - ✅ **Stack-Machine Code = postfix-style IR** where operands are implicit at TOS. - ✅ **Two instruction categories:** data-movement (`PUSH, LOAD, STORE`) and arithmetic (`ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, NEG`). - ✅ **Operand order:** TOS is the **right** operand. Stack `[a, b]` after `ADD` yields `a + b`. - ✅ **Code generation algorithm:** Recursive postorder traversal of the AST. - ✅ **No temporaries needed** — the stack *is* the temporary storage. - ✅ **Maximum stack depth** is a code-generation optimization metric. - ✅ **Real-world examples:** Java Bytecode, CPython Bytecode, .NET CIL (Conceptual — actually register-based, but value-stack model). - ✅ **Comparison to remember:** TAC uses `t1, t2, …`; Stack-Machine uses implicit stack slots. - ✅ **Always draw the stack trace** in exam answers — it earns bonus methodology marks. - ✅ **Precedence is preserved automatically** if you follow the parse tree's postorder traversal. - ✅ **For assignments:** always end the code with `STORE x` after `code(RHS)`. > **Final Note:** The key insight is that **stack-machine code is the most natural compilation of postfix expressions** — a fact Aho, Sethi, and Ullman emphasize in the *Dragon Book* (which is the prescribed reference for KTU's Compiler Design syllabus). Master the postorder translation rule, and every exam problem becomes a mechanical application.

topics.title: Three-Address Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Three-Address Code

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Address Code (TAC)** is a linearized intermediate representation of a source program in which each instruction contains **at most three operands** — typically one operator (binary or unary) and up to two operand addresses — with results stored in **temporaries** (compiler-generated named variables such as `t1`, `t2`, `t3`...).

The general canonical form is:

$$
x := y \;\text{op}\; z
$$

where:
- $x$ is the **result location** (target)
- $y$ and $z$ are **source operands** (variables, constants, or temporaries)
- $\text{op}$ is a **binary operator**

A variant form for unary operations is $x := \text{op}\; y$, and for copies $x := y$.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3 — Intermediate Code Generation), TAC is treated as the **canonical IR** that bridges the gap between the front-end's syntax tree/semantic analysis and the back-end's optimization/code generation phases.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" View

Imagine a complex recipe (the source program) being broken down into a series of **single-step instruction cards** that any apprentice cook can follow linearly:

- The **front-end** of the compiler is like parsing a long paragraph recipe.
- The **three-address code** is rewriting that paragraph as a stack of small index cards, where **each card has only ONE action** (e.g., "mix $y$ and $z$ using a whisk and label the bowl $x$").
- The **back-end** is the apprentice executing those cards one-by-one, no matter how big the recipe was.

This linearity and uniformity make TAC **easy to optimize** (you can shuffle cards) and **easy to translate** to target machine code (each card maps neatly to a few instructions).

## 1.3 Why TAC? — The 3-Stage Justification

| Stage | Role of TAC |
|-------|-------------|
| **Front-end (Analysis)** | Decouples source-language syntax from the back-end. |
| **Optimizer** | Works on a small, fixed set of TAC instructions (≈ 10–15 forms). |
| **Back-end (Synthesis)** | Emits native code for x86 / ARM / RISC-V using a simple pattern-matching translator. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric used in KTU textbook examples**: A typical TAC translation of an expression with $n$ operators produces **at most $n$ temporaries** and **at most $n$ instructions** (one per operator), assuming left-associative evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Taxonomy of Three-Address Instructions (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to enumerate the **instruction forms** by memory. Below is the complete taxonomy.

### 2.1.1 Assignment Forms
- **Binary arithmetic/logic:** $\;x := y \;\text{op}\; z \quad$ where $\text{op} \in \{+,\ -,\ *,\ /,\ \%,\ \&\&,\ \vert\vert,\ ==,\ !=,\ <,\ >,\ \leq,\ \geq\}$
- **Unary operation:** $\;x := \text{op}\; y \quad$ where $\text{op} \in \{-,\ !,\ \sim\}$
- **Simple copy:** $\;x := y$

### 2.1.2 Memory / Pointer Forms
- **Indexed copy (array access):** $\;x := y[i] \quad$ and $\quad x[i] := y$
- **Address and dereference:** $\;x := \&y \quad$ and $\quad x := *y \quad$ and $\quad *x := y$

### 2.1.3 Control-Flow Forms
- **Unconditional jump:** $\;\textbf{goto}\; L$
- **Conditional jump:** $\;\textbf{if}\; x \;\text{relop}\; y \;\textbf{goto}\; L$
- **Conditional jump (single operand):** $\;\textbf{if}\; x \;\textbf{goto}\; L$  /  $\;\textbf{ifFalse}\; x \;\textbf{goto}\; L$

### 2.1.4 Procedure / Function Forms
- **Parameter pass:** $\;\textbf{param}\; x$
- **Procedure call:** $\;\textbf{call}\; p,\; n \quad$ (call procedure $p$ with $n$ parameters)
- **Return value:** $\;x := \textbf{call}\; p$
- **Indexed / pointer return:** $\;x := y[i] \quad$ as part of function return protocols

## 2.2 The Three Standard TAC Representations (KTU 2024 High-Yield!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 3 explicitly tests the comparison between **Quadruples, Triples, and Indirect Triples**. This is a guaranteed 7-mark question.

| Representation | Structure per Instruction | Storage Form | Reordering Friendly? |
|----------------|---------------------------|--------------|----------------------|
| **Quadruple** | `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | 4 fields per row, results named via temporaries | **No** — temporaries are explicit |
| **Triple** | `(op, arg1, arg2)` | 3 fields; the *result* is the **position number** of the instruction | Yes — position numbers change on reorder |
| **Indirect Triple** | A `code` array of pointers to a pool of triples | Triple pool + indirection list | **Yes** — reorder only the pointer list |

### 2.2.1 Quadruple Representation
For $a := b * -c + b * -c$, the quadruples are:

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|----|------|------|--------|
| (0) | uminus | c | — | t1 |
| (1) | * | b | t1 | t2 |
| (2) | uminus | c | — | t3 |
| (3) | * | b | t3 | t4 |
| (4) | + | t2 | t4 | t5 |
| (5) | := | t5 | — | a |

### 2.2.2 Triple Representation
The result is encoded as the instruction *position*. Argument that is the position of another instruction is written `(i)`.

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|----|------|------|
| (0) | uminus | c | — |
| (1) | * | b | (0) |
| (2) | uminus | c | — |
| (3) | * | b | (2) |
| (4) | + | (1) | (3) |
| (5) | := | a | (4) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-referencing pitfall**: Triple (2) duplicates the uminus of $c$. To avoid recomputation, **DAG construction** is preferred — see Section 3.

### 2.2.3 Indirect Triple Representation
A **separate pointer list** references an immutable pool of triples. Re-ordering the list is sufficient to re-order the program — the pool itself is untouched. Cost: an extra level of indirection on every fetch.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Bound | Engineering Utility |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Max temporaries for an expression of $n$ operators | $T_{\max} = n$ | Worst-case stack allocation in code generator |
| TAC instructions for an $n$-operator expression | $I = n$ | Bounds optimizer's input size |
| Quadruple memory per instruction | $4$ fields × 4 bytes = **16 B** (typical) | Buffer sizing in compiler IR |
| Triple memory per instruction | $3$ fields × 4 bytes = **12 B** | Saves 25 % memory vs quadruple |
| Indirect triple cost | $1$ pointer + $1$ triple fetch = **16 B** logical | Trades memory for reorderability |
| Short-circuit AND of $n$ conditions | $n$ jumps + $n - 1$ labels | Used in Boolean code generation |
| Backpatch list length (worst) | $O(n)$ for an $n$-statement block | Used in KTU backpatching algorithm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Callout** — Never use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside markdown table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value) — this is a **mandatory syntax rule** of the KTU 2024 digital answer sheet rendering engine.

## 2.4 Why TAC Matters in Real Engineering

- **LLVM IR**: Modern production compilers (Clang, Rust, Swift) use a TAC-flavored **Static Single Assignment (SSA)** form — a direct descendant of classical three-address code.
- **JVM Bytecode**: Although stack-based, every `iload`, `iadd`, `istore` is conceptually a TAC instruction broken into its 3-operand form for verification.
- **GCC GIMPLE**: GCC's intermediate representation is essentially a constrained TAC that allows tree-level and RTL-level optimizations.
- **Embedded cross-compilers**: TAC makes retargeting trivial — the same IR emits ARM, RISC-V, or x86 with minimal change.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Foundational Procedure: Translating an Expression to TAC

We need a *systematic* rule. KTU 2024 examiners favor the **SDD (Syntax-Directed Definition)** approach: synthesize TAC as we parse the expression grammar:

$$
E \rightarrow E_1 + T \;\mid\; E_1 - T \;\mid\; T
$$

$$
T \rightarrow T_1 * F \;\mid\; T_1 / F \;\mid\; F
$$

$$
F \rightarrow (E) \;\mid\; \text{id}
$$

**Semantic rule** (synthesized attribute `E.addr` = the location holding $E$'s value):

$$
E \rightarrow E_1 + T \quad\Longrightarrow\quad E.\text{addr} := \text{newtemp}()\;;\;\text{emit}(E.\text{addr},\, '=',\, E_1.\text{addr},\, '+',\, T.\text{addr})
$$

### 3.1.1 Worked Example — A KTU-Favourite Expression

Translate: $\;a := -b * (c + d) - e\uparrow f\;\;$ (where $\uparrow$ denotes exponentiation, evaluated as repeated multiplication).

**Step 1 — Parse and synthesize `addr` bottom-up:**

| Sub-expression | TAC emitted | Comment |
|----------------|-------------|---------|
| `-b` | `t1 := uminus b` | Unary minus |
| `c + d` | `t2 := c + d` | Innermost parenthesis |
| `t1 * t2` | `t3 := t1 * t2` | Left multiplication |
| `e ↑ f` | `t4 := e * e * …` (f-times) | Exponentiation unfolds |
| `t3 - t4` | `t5 := t3 - t4` | Final subtraction |
| `a := t5` | `a := t5` | Assignment to root |

**Step 2 — Full TAC Listing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&t_1 := \text{uminus}\; b \\
&t_2 := c + d \\
&t_3 := t_1 * t_2 \\
&t_4 := e * e \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad (f = 2;\; \text{generalise as needed}) \\
&t_5 := t_3 - t_4 \\
&a := t_5
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Temporaries Count Verification
- Operators used: 1 (uminus) + 1 (+) + 1 (*) + 1 (-) = **4 unary/binary** plus exponentiation cost.
- Generated temporaries: $t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4, t_5$ → **5 temporaries** for the 4 primitive operators. This matches the **upper bound** $T_{\max} = n + 1$ when a final copy/assignment is required.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Control-Flow Translation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module 3 places strong emphasis on the translation of **Boolean expressions** and **control flow** to TAC. The following matrix is the canonical answer template.

### 3.2.1 Translation of `if E then S1 else S2`

**SDT (Syntax-Directed Translation Scheme):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1.}\;\; E.\text{true} := \text{newlabel}() \\
&\text{2.}\;\; E.\text{false} := \text{newlabel}() \\
&\text{3.}\;\; E.\text{code} \;\Vert\; \text{label}(E.\text{true})\!:\; S_1.\text{code} \\
&\text{4.}\;\; \text{goto}\; S.\text{next} \\
&\text{5.}\;\; \text{label}(E.\text{false})\!:\; S_2.\text{code} \\
&\text{6.}\;\; \text{label}(S.\text{next})\!:\;
\end{aligned}
$$

**TAC Output Template:**

| Line | TAC Instruction |
|------|-----------------|
| 100 | (code to evaluate $E$ → jumps to 200 if true, 300 if false) |
| 200 | $\langle$ code for $S_1\rangle$ |
| 250 | **goto** 400 |
| 300 | $\langle$ code for $S_2\rangle$ |
| 400 | (next statement) |

### 3.2.2 Translation of `while E do S`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{label}(\text{begin})\!:\;\; E.\text{code} \\
&\text{label}(E.\text{true})\!:\;\; S.\text{code} \\
&\text{goto}\;\; \text{begin} \\
&\text{label}(E.\text{false})\!:\;\;\;\;
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Translation of `for (i := e1; i <= e2; i := i + 1) S`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&i := e_1 \\
&\text{label}(\text{begin})\!:\;\; t_1 := e_2 \\
&\textbf{if}\; i \leq t_1 \;\textbf{goto}\;\; \text{body} \\
&\textbf{goto}\;\; \text{end} \\
&\text{label}(\text{body})\!:\;\; \langle S.\text{code} \rangle \\
&i := i + 1 \\
&\textbf{goto}\;\; \text{begin} \\
&\text{label}(\text{end})\!:\;\;\;\;
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Boolean Expression Translation — Short-Circuit Code

Consider $a < b \;\text{or}\; c < d \;\text{and}\; e < f$.

**Precedence (high → low):** `not`, `and`, `or`. Apply short-circuit semantics.

### 3.3.1 Two Standard Code Styles

**Style 1 — Numerical Evaluation (treats Boolean as 0/1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&t_1 := a < b \\
&t_2 := c < d \\
&t_3 := e < f \\
&t_4 := t_2 \;\text{and}\; t_3 \\
&t_5 := t_1 \;\text{or}\; t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Style 2 — Short-Circuit Jumps (used by modern compilers):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{if}\; a < b \;\textbf{goto}\; L_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad (\text{true} \to \text{skip second clause}) \\
&\textbf{ifFalse}\; c < d \;\textbf{goto}\; L_2 \quad (\text{false} \to \text{result false}) \\
&\textbf{ifFalse}\; e < f \;\textbf{goto}\; L_2 \\
&L_1\!:\;\; t := 1 \\
&\textbf{goto}\;\; L_3 \\
&L_2\!:\;\; t := 0 \\
&L_3\!:\;\;\;\;
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 3 specifically recommends the **short-circuit (jumping) code** form because it is a pre-requisite for the **Backpatching algorithm** covered in Module 3's second half.

## 3.4 The Backpatching Algorithm (Complete Derivation)

The naïve approach of `newlabel()` followed by `emit(goto L)` leaves dangling forward references. **Backpatching** solves this with a list of pending jumps.

### 3.4.1 Two Key Lists (Synthesized Attributes)

- `B.truelist` — list of jump instruction indices that must be patched with the label of the **true** exit of a Boolean expression $B$.
- `B.falselist` — list of jump instruction indices that must be patched with the label of the **false** exit of $B$.

### 3.4.2 Three Helper Functions

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{makelist}(i) := \{i\} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad (\text{create a singleton}) \\
&\text{merge}(p_1, p_2) := \text{concatenate the lists pointed by } p_1, p_2 \\
&\text{backpatch}(p, i) := \text{for each instruction } k \text{ in list } p,\; \text{set target of } k \text{ to } i
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.3 Production Rules with Backpatching

**Production 1:** $B \rightarrow B_1 \;\text{or}\; M\; B_2$

- Generate `M.code` which produces the next available label index.
- $B.\text{truelist} := \text{merge}(B_1.\text{truelist},\; B_2.\text{truelist})$
- $B.\text{falselist} := B_2.\text{falselist}$
- $\text{backpatch}(B_1.\text{falselist},\; M.\text{quad})$

**Production 2:** $B \rightarrow B_1 \;\text{and}\; M\; B_2$

- $B.\text{truelist} := B_2.\text{truelist}$
- $B.\text{falselist} := \text{merge}(B_1.\text{falselist},\; B_2.\text{falselist})$
- $\text{backpatch}(B_1.\text{truelist},\; M.\text{quad})$

**Production 3:** $B \rightarrow \text{not}\; B_1$

- $B.\text{truelist} := B_1.\text{falselist}$
- $B.\text{falselist} := B_1.\text{truelist}$

**Production 4:** $B \rightarrow (B_1)$

- $B.\text{truelist} := B_1.\text{truelist}$
- $B.\text{falselist} := B_1.\text{falselist}$

**Production 5:** $B \rightarrow E_1 \;\text{relop}\; E_2$

- Emit: `if E1.addr relop E2.addr goto _` and `goto _`.
- $B.\text{truelist} := \text{makelist}(\text{nextquad} - 2)$
- $B.\text{falselist} := \text{makelist}(\text{nextquad} - 1)$

### 3.4.4 Worked Backpatching Example

Translate: $\; \textbf{if}\; a < b \;\text{or}\; c < d \;\text{then}\; x := 1\;\textbf{else}\; x := 0$

| nextquad | TAC emitted | Backpatch actions |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 100 | `if a < b goto _` | push 100 to $B_1$.truelist (tentative) |
| 101 | `goto _` | push 101 to $B_1$.falselist |
| 102 | $M$ — label 102 generated | — |
| 103 | `if c < d goto _` | push 103 to $B_2$.truelist |
| 104 | `goto _` | push 104 to $B_2$.falselist |
| 105 | $L_1 =$ `102:` (after backpatch of $B_1$.falselist → 102) | `backpatch({101}, 102)` |
| 106 | `t := 1` | — |
| 107 | `goto _` (S.next) | — |
| 108 | $L_2 =$ backpatch target for $B_2$.falselist | `backpatch({100,104}, 108)` |
| 109 | `t := 0` | — |
| 110 | $L_3 =$ S.next (backpatch 107 → 110) | `backpatch({103, 107}, 110)` |

> [!NOTE]
> This example demonstrates that **backpatching removes the need for two passes** by keeping the unresolved jump targets in a list and resolving them when the target label is finally known.

## 3.5 DAG-Based Translation (Optimizer Pre-Cursor)

A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** captures common subexpressions. For $a := b * -c + b * -c$:

**DAG Construction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Leaf nodes:}\; a, b, c \\
