Example: `(+, a, b, t1)` for `t1 = a + b`. 2. **Triples** — uses instruction indices instead of temporaries. Example: `(0): * b c` referencing itself by position. 3. **Indirect Triples** — an auxiliary list maps execution order to triple indices, enabling easy reordering during optimisation. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for correct example.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is the role of `newtemp()` and `gen()` in SDT-based expression translation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **`newtemp()`** — generates and returns a **fresh symbolic temporary name** (e.g., `$t_1, t_2, \dots$` or `t1, t2, \dots`) every time it is invoked. It maintains an internal counter that increments monotonically. Used to hold intermediate results of sub-expressions. *(1.5 marks)* - **`gen(quad)`** — appends a newly constructed TAC instruction (typically a quadruple `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`) to the **instruction stream** that will become the final intermediate code. *(1.5 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Do NOT** emit TAC for parentheses `(` `)` — they are free. Common mistake: students write `t = (a + b)`, wasting 1 mark. > 2. **Always** show the temporary counter starting from `t1`. Skipping the subscript makes the answer un-readable and loses 1 mark. > 3. **Operator precedence matters** — `a + b * c` is **not** `(a+b)*c`. Wrong grouping = 4-mark penalty. > 4. For **array** expressions, students often forget the `load` quadruple. Examiner strictly deducts 2 marks. > 5. In **type-mixed** expressions, omitting `inttofloat` quads costs 2 marks. --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]` **(a)** For the expression `a - b + c * d / e`, construct the **annotated parse tree** using the standard arithmetic grammar and write the **SDT scheme** (semantic actions) for the relevant productions. Show that your scheme is **conflict-free** for an LALR(1) parser. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using your SDT scheme, translate the expression into **Three-Address Code** (quadruple form) and **Postfix notation**. Display the temporary count after each step. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks** Grammar: $$ \begin{aligned} E &\rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; E - T \;\vert\; T \\ T &\rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; T / F \;\vert\; F \\ F &\rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; \text{id} \end{aligned} $$ Annotated SDT (actions in `{}` braces): $$ \begin{aligned} E &\rightarrow E_1 + T \quad \{ E.\text{place} = \text{newtemp}();\;\text{gen}(E_1.\text{place} + T.\text{place} = E.\text{place}) \} \\ E &\rightarrow E_1 - T \quad \{ E.\text{place} = \text{newtemp}();\;\text{gen}(E_1.\text{place} - T.\text{place} = E.\text{place}) \} \\ E &\rightarrow T \quad \{ E.\text{place} = T.\text{place} \} \\ T &\rightarrow T_1 * F \quad \{ T.\text{place} = \text{newtemp}();\;\text{gen}(T_1.\text{place} * F.\text{place} = T.\text{place}) \} \\ T &\rightarrow T_1 / F \quad \{ T.\text{place} = \text{newtemp}();\;\text{gen}(T_1.\text{place} / F.\text{place} = T.\text{place}) \} \\ T &\rightarrow F \quad \{ T.\text{place} = F.\text{place} \} \\ F &\rightarrow (E) \quad \{ F.\text{place} = E.\text{place} \} \\ F &\rightarrow \text{id} \quad \{ F.\text{place} = \text{id}.\text{name} \} \end{aligned} $$ **LALR(1) conflict check:** No action references a symbol to its left in a way that requires an inherited attribute on the right side. All attributes (`place`) are **synthesised** — therefore the SDT is **S-attributed**, which is always LALR(1)-compatible. No shift-reduce or reduce-reduce conflicts arise. *(+2 marks for conflict-free proof.)* **Part (b) — 7 marks** Parse tree application (bottom-up): | Step | Reduction | Action | TAC Emitted | Temp Counter | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F \rightarrow d$ | $F.\text{place}=d$ | — | 0 | | 2 | $F \rightarrow e$ | $F.\text{place}=e$ | — | 0 | | 3 | $T \rightarrow T/F$ | `newtemp` → $t_1$ | `t1 = d / e` | 1 | | 4 | $F \rightarrow c$ | $F.\text{place}=c$ | — | 1 | | 5 | $T \rightarrow T*F$ | `newtemp` → $t_2$ | `t2 = c * t1` | 2 | | 6 | $E \rightarrow T$ | $E.\text{place}=t_2$ | — | 2 | | 7 | $F \rightarrow b$ | $F.\text{place}=b$ | — | 2 | | 8 | $T \rightarrow F$ | $T.\text{place}=b$ | — | 2 | | 9 | $E \rightarrow E-T$ | `newtemp` → $t_3$ | `t3 = t2 - b` | 3 | | 10 | $F \rightarrow a$ | $F.\text{place}=a$ | — | 3 | | 11 | $T \rightarrow F$ | $T.\text{place}=a$ | — | 3 | | 12 | $E \rightarrow E+T$ | `newtemp` → $t_4$ | `t4 = t3 + a` | 4 | **Final TAC (quadruple form):** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `/` | `d` | `e` | `t1` | | 2 | `*` | `c` | `t1` | `t2` | | 3 | `-` | `t2` | `b` | `t3` | | 4 | `+` | `t3` | `a` | `t4` | **Postfix notation:** `d e / c * b - a +` *(Marks: 1 for parse table; 2 for TAC correctness; 1 for quadruple table; 1 for temp counter discipline; 1 for postfix derivation; 1 for grammar precision.)* --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Expected Pattern]` **(a)** Translate the expression `x = A[i * 2] + B[j - 1]` into Three-Address Code, assuming arrays `A` and `B` start at lower bound `0` with element width `w` bytes. Show all **address-arithmetic** quadruples explicitly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Demonstrate how the same translator would handle a **mixed-type** expression `f = (x + y) * z` where `x, y` are `int` and `z` is `float`. Provide the **complete SDT rule** for the multiplication production with type coercion. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks** For `A[i*2]`: - $\text{addr}(A[i*2]) = \text{base}(A) + (i \cdot 2 - 0) \cdot w = \text{base}(A) + i \cdot 2 \cdot w$ For `B[j-1]`: - $\text{addr}(B[j-1]) = \text{base}(B) + (j - 1) \cdot w$ **Complete TAC (quadruple form):** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `*` | `i` | `2` | `t1` | Index for A | | 2 | `*` | `t1` | `w` | `t2` | Offset for A | | 3 | `+` | `base(A)` | `t2` | `t3` | Address of A[i*2] | | 4 | `load` | `t3` | — | `t4` | Read A[i*2] | | 5 | `-` | `j` | `1` | `t5` | Index for B | | 6 | `*` | `t5` | `w` | `t6` | Offset for B | | 7 | `+` | `base(B)` | `t6` | `t7` | Address of B[j-1] | | 8 | `load` | `t7` | — | `t8` | Read B[j-1] | | 9 | `+` | `t4` | `t8` | `t9` | Sum | | 10 | `assign` | `t9` | — | `x` | Store in x | *(Award 2 marks for correct address formula usage, 2 for `load` quads, 1 for correct indexing math, 1 for final assignment, 1 for clean formatting.)* **Part (b) — 7 marks** The canonical SDT production for type-coerced multiplication: $$ T \rightarrow T_1 * F $$ **Type-checking action (pseudocode):** ``` if (T1.type == int) and (F.type == float) then u = newtemp() gen(inttofloat, T1.place, _, u) T.place = newtemp() gen(*, u, F.place, T.place) T.type = float else if (T1.type == float) and (F.type == int) then u = newtemp() gen(inttofloat, F.place, _, u) T.place = newtemp() gen(*, T1.place, u, T.place) T.type = float else T.place = newtemp() gen(*, T1.place, F.place, T.place) T.type = max(T1.type, F.type) // assuming a type lattice end if ``` **Step-by-step translation of `f = (x + y) * z`:** | Step | Reduction | TAC Emitted | |---|---|---| | 1 | Compute `x + y` (both int) | `t1 = x + y` (type: int) | | 2 | Detect int × float mismatch | `t2 = inttofloat t1` | | 3 | Apply `*` with coercion | `t3 = t2 * z` (type: float) | | 4 | Final assignment | `f = t3` | **Final TAC:** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `+` | `x` | `y` | `t1` | | 2 | `inttofloat` | `t1` | — | `t2` | | 3 | `*` | `t2` | `z` | `t3` | | 4 | `assign` | `t3` | — | `f` | *(Award 2 marks for the SDT pseudocode; 2 marks for explicit `inttofloat` quad; 1 mark for each correct reduction step; 1 mark for final TAC formatting.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Read 1 hour before the exam.** - ✅ **TAC rule:** every instruction has at most **3 operands** and exactly **1 operator** (unary or binary). - ✅ **One binary op ⇒ one quad ⇒ one new temporary.** This invariant is your sanity check. - ✅ **Parentheses are free** — never emit a quad for `(`, `)`. - ✅ **Type coercion is mandatory** in mixed expressions; missing `inttofloat` = lost marks. - ✅ **Array address formula:** $\text{base} + (i - \text{low}) \cdot w$ — write it from memory. - ✅ **Postfix conversion** uses the operator stack: pop on higher-or-equal precedence, push current. - ✅ **L-attributed vs S-attributed** — for expression translation, the SDT is **always S-attributed** (synthesised only) ⇒ LALR(1)-safe. - ✅ **Temporary counter convention:** starts at `t1`, increments to `t2`, `t3`, …, never reused. - ✅ **Quadruple vs Triple:** quadruples store `result` explicitly (easier for optimisers); triples use instruction indices (compact but fragile under reordering). - ✅ **Boolean expressions** are translated using **backpatching** with labels (covered in next sub-topic; remember to revise `truelist` and `falselist`). - ✅ **The Dragon Book (Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman)** is the canonical reference; KTU frequently draws exam questions from Sections **6.4 – 6.6** of that text. > **Final Exam Tip:** When asked *"Translate the expression into TAC"*, always begin by **(i)** restating the grammar, **(ii)** drawing the parse tree, **(iii)** tabulating the SDT reductions, and **(iv)** listing the quadruple table. This four-step structure scores 100% on a typical 7-mark sub-question.

topics.title: Translating Control-Flow Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Translating Control-Flow Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Translating Control-Flow Statements** is the intermediate code generation phase in which high-level structured statements (`if-then`, `if-then-else`, `while`, `for`, `repeat-until`, etc.) are systematically converted into a sequence of three-address code (TAC) instructions using a **Syntax-Directed Translation (SDT) scheme**. The translation preserves the program's semantic order of execution by emitting explicit **conditional jumps** (`if … goto L`) and **unconditional jumps** (`goto L`) that simulate the structured control constructs of the source language.

In KTU 2024 terminology, this phase is treated as an **intermediate code generation strategy** for flow-of-control statements using either:
1. **Direct (One-Pass) Translation** — using inherited/synthesized attributes such as `S.next`, `E.true`, `E.false`, or
2. **Backpatching (Multi-Pass) Translation** — using `truelist` and `falselist` attributes that defer the filling of jump targets.

The output is typically represented as **quadruples, triples, or indirect triples** forming the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** implicitly.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Railway Signal Controller's Job Sheet**
> 
> Imagine a railway controller who receives a list of instructions written in plain English:
> *"If the green signal is ON, allow train A to pass and continue on the main line; else, divert train A to the siding."*
> 
> The controller must convert this rulebook into a **paper log of explicit, numbered signal steps**:
> 1. Check green signal (Step 1).
> 2. If OFF, jump directly to Step 7 (the siding).
> 3. If ON, set the main-line switch (Step 2, 3, 4, 5, 6).
> 4. At the end, go to Step 8 (resume main schedule).
> 
> The structured English rule becomes a **flat sequence of unconditional and conditional jumps**. This is exactly what the compiler does when it translates `if`, `while`, and `for` statements: structured control is "flattened" into a stream of three-address code with explicit `goto` targets.

## 1.3 Key Entities and Attributes

| Entity | Attribute | Meaning | Type |
|--------|-----------|---------|------|
| Statement `S` | `S.next` | Label to jump to *after* S completes | Inherited |
| Boolean `E` | `E.true` | Label to jump to *if* E evaluates to **true** | Inherited |
| Boolean `E` | `E.false` | Label to jump to *if* E evaluates to **false** | Inherited |
| Boolean `E` | `E.truelist` | List of not-yet-filled `goto`-true targets | Synthesized |
| Boolean `E` | `E.falselist` | List of not-yet-filled `goto`-false targets | Synthesized |
| Statement `S` | `S.begin` | Label marking *entry* of S (loops) | Inherited |
| Loop `L` | `L.next` | Exit label of the loop | Inherited |
| Temporary | `newlabel()` | Generates a fresh symbolic label | Function |

## 1.4 Core Constructs Covered in KTU 2024 Module 3

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes the following control-flow translation patterns:
> - `if E then S`
> - `if E then S1 else S2`
> - `while E do S`
> - `for S1 → E1 → E2 → E3 do S`
> - `repeat S until E`
> - **Boolean expressions** `E → E or E`, `E → E and E`, `E → not E`, `E → (E)`, `E → id relop id`, `E → true`, `E → false`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The translation of control-flow statements relies on **two key mechanisms**:

### Mechanism A: Direct (One-Pass) Translation using Attributes
1. **Pre-traversal Phase** — Allocate labels (`newlabel()`) for each decision/join point in the SDT scheme.
2. **Attribute Propagation** — Pass `S.next`, `S.begin`, `E.true`, `E.false` as **inherited attributes** (top-down).
3. **Code Generation** — Emit three-address code as **synthesized action** at each production.
4. **Symbolic Labels** — Use symbolic labels (not numeric addresses) which are later resolved to actual addresses.

### Mechanism B: Backpatching (Multi-Pass) Translation
1. **Code Emission Phase** — Generate TAC with **placeholder jump targets** (often `0` or `_`).
2. **List Aggregation** — Collect jump instructions into `truelist` / `falselist` during bottom-up parsing.
3. **Backpatch Phase** — At join points, `backpatch(list, label)` fills all placeholders with the correct target label.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Backpatching?** In a one-pass scheme with `S.next`, the parser must often look ahead to determine the *next* statement's label. **Backpatching sidesteps this by delaying the target resolution** until the join point is reached, making the scheme suitable for **bottom-up parsers** like LALR(1).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Construct

### 2.2.1 `if E then S`
- **Why:** Need to evaluate `E`; if false, skip the body `S`.
- **How:**
  - Evaluate `E`. If true, fall through into `S`. If false, `goto S.next`.
  - After `S` finishes, fall through to `S.next`.

### 2.2.2 `if E then S1 else S2`
- **Why:** Two paths must reconverge.
- **How:**
  - Evaluate `E`. If true → `S1` → `goto S.next`. If false → `S2` → `S.next`.

### 2.2.3 `while E do S`
- **Why:** A condition is tested before each iteration.
- **How:**
  - Mark loop entry. Test `E`. If true, execute `S` and jump back to entry. If false, exit to `S.next`.

### 2.2.4 `for S1 → E1 → E2 → E3 do S`
- **Why:** Index variable management (init, limit, step).
- **How:**
  - Generate `S1`. Allocate loop label. Evaluate `E1`. Store limit `E2`. Compare index with limit. If true, execute `S`, then `E3`, then jump to test. If false, exit.

### 2.2.5 `repeat S until E`
- **Why:** Body executes at least once.
- **How:**
  - Execute `S`. Evaluate `E`. If false, jump back. If true, fall through (exit).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / SDD Cheat Sheet (One-Pass Scheme)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU 2024 Board Exams — Memorize These Productions**

| Production | Semantic Rules (Inherited + Synthesized) |
|------------|------------------------------------------|
| `S → if E then S1` | `E.true = newlabel(); E.false = S.next; S1.next = S.next; S.code = E.code \vert\vert gen(E.true ':') \vert\vert S1.code` |
| `S → if E then S1 else S2` | `E.true = newlabel(); E.false = newlabel(); S1.next = S.next; S2.next = S.next; S.code = E.code \vert\vert gen(E.true ':') \vert\vert S1.code \vert\vert gen('goto' S.next) \vert\vert gen(E.false ':') \vert\vert S2.code` |
| `S → while E do S1` | `S.begin = newlabel(); E.true = newlabel(); E.false = S.next; S1.next = S.begin; S.code = gen(S.begin ':') \vert\vert E.code \vert\vert gen(E.true ':') \vert\vert S1.code \vert\vert gen('goto' S.begin)` |
| `S → S1 ; S2` | `S1.next = newlabel(); S2.next = S.next; S.code = S1.code \vert\vert gen(S1.next ':') \vert\vert S2.code` |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Backpatching Scheme

| Production | Semantic Rules |
|------------|----------------|
| `S → if E then S1` | `E.truelist = makelist(); E.falselist = makelist(); backpatch(E.truelist, S.next); S.code = E.code \vert\vert S1.code` |
| `S → if E then S1 else S2` | `E.truelist = makelist(); E.falselist = makelist(); backpatch(E.truelist, S1.begin); backpatch(E.falselist, S2.begin); S.nextlist = merge(S1.nextlist, S2.nextlist, makelist(nextinstr))` ... followed by `gen('goto' _)` |
| `S → while E do S1` | `S.begin = nextinstr; E.truelist = makelist(); E.falselist = makelist(); backpatch(S1.nextlist, S.begin); backpatch(E.truelist, S.begin); S.nextlist = E.falselist` |
| `E → E1 or E2` | `backpatch(E1.falselist, E2.begin); E.truelist = merge(E1.truelist, E2.truelist); E.falselist = E2.falselist` |
| `E → E1 and E2` | `backpatch(E1.truelist, E2.begin); E.truelist = E2.truelist; E.falselist = merge(E1.falselist, E2.falselist)` |
| `E → not E1` | `E.truelist = E1.falselist; E.falselist = E1.truelist` |
| `E → id1 relop id2` | `E.truelist = makelist(nextinstr); E.falselist = makelist(nextinstr+1); gen('if' id1 relop id2 'goto _'); gen('goto _')` |
| `E → true` | `E.truelist = makelist(nextinstr); gen('goto _')` |
| `E → false` | `E.falselist = makelist(nextinstr); gen('goto _')` |

## 2.5 Helper Functions Used

| Function | Definition | Return Type |
|----------|------------|-------------|
| `newlabel()` | Returns a fresh symbolic label (e.g., `L1`, `L2`, …) | Label |
| `nextinstr` | Index of the next TAC instruction to be emitted | Integer |
| `makelist(i)` | Creates a new list containing only instruction index `i` | List of integers |
| `merge(p1, p2)` | Concatenates two instruction lists | List of integers |
| `backpatch(p, i)` | Fills target `i` into every `goto _` listed in `p` | void |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> In modern compiler toolchains (LLVM IR, GCC GIMPLE, MSVC IL), control-flow translation is the foundation of:
> - **Static Single Assignment (SSA) construction**
> - **Dead code elimination** and **constant propagation** at IR level
> - **Profile-guided optimization (PGO)** using the CFG
> - **JIT compilation** in virtual machines (JVM, CLR) which use structured bytecode jumps
> 
> Mastery of this topic is essential for careers in **compiler engineering, language tooling, performance optimization, and static analysis**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Translation of `if E then S1 else S2` (One-Pass Scheme)

**Source Construct:**
```
if (x < y) then
    a = b + c
else
    d = e - f
```

### Step-by-Step Derivation

> **Step 1 — Allocate inherited attributes for `S`.**
> The statement `S` has `S.next` pointing to the next statement's label after the if-then-else block.
> Let `S.next = L1`.

> **Step 2 — Allocate true and false labels for `E`.**
> `E.true = L2` (label where code continues if E is true).
> `E.false = L3` (label where code continues if E is false).

> **Step 3 — Allocate next labels for both branches.**
> `S1.next = L1` (after then-branch, go to S.next).
> `S2.next = L1` (after else-branch, go to S.next).

> **Step 4 — Apply the production `S → if E then S1 else S2`.**
> According to the SDD rule:
> $$S.code = E.code \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(E.true : ) \; \vert\vert \; S_1.code \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(\text{goto } S.next) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(E.false : ) \; \vert\vert \; S_2.code$$

> **Step 5 — Substitute concrete values.**
> $$S.code = E.code \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(L2 :) \; \vert\vert \; S_1.code \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(\text{goto } L1) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(L3 :) \; \vert\vert \; S_2.code$$

> **Step 6 — Generate `E.code`.**
> For `E → id1 relop id2`, the boolean expression is translated as:
> $$E.code = \text{gen}(\text{if } x < y \text{ goto } L2) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(\text{goto } L3)$$

> **Step 7 — Generate `S1.code` and `S2.code` (assignment statements).**
> For an assignment `a = b + c`:
> $$S_1.code = \text{gen}(t_1 = b + c) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(a = t_1)$$
> For an assignment `d = e - f`:
> $$S_2.code = \text{gen}(t_2 = e - f) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(d = t_2)$$

> **Step 8 — Concatenate all segments to produce the final three-address code.**

### Final Three-Address Code Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{if } x < y \texttt{ goto } L2 \\
&\texttt{goto } L3 \\
&L2 : \quad t_1 = b + c \\
&\quad\quad a = t_1 \\
&\texttt{goto } L1 \\
&L3 : \quad t_2 = e - f \\
&\quad\quad d = t_2 \\
&L1 :
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Translation of `while E do S` (One-Pass Scheme)

**Source Construct:**
```
while (i < 100) do
    sum = sum + i
    i = i + 1
```

### Step-by-Step Derivation

> **Step 1 — Allocate `S.begin` and `S.next`.**
> `S.begin = L1` (entry of loop).
> `S.next = L2` (exit of loop, falls through to next statement).

> **Step 2 — Allocate `E.true` and `E.false`.**
> `E.true = L3` (where to go if E is true).
> `E.false = S.next = L2` (where to go if E is false — exit loop).

> **Step 3 — Set `S1.next` (next-statement of loop body).**
> After body executes, jump back to loop start. Hence `S1.next = S.begin = L1`.

> **Step 4 — Apply the production `S → while E do S1`.**
> According to the SDD rule:
> $$S.code = \text{gen}(S.begin :) \; \vert\vert \; E.code \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(E.true :) \; \vert\vert \; S_1.code \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(\text{goto } S.begin)$$

> **Step 5 — Substitute concrete values.**
> $$S.code = \text{gen}(L1 :) \; \vert\vert \; E.code \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(L3 :) \; \vert\vert \; S_1.code \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(\text{goto } L1)$$

> **Step 6 — Generate `E.code`.**
> $$E.code = \text{gen}(\text{if } i < 100 \texttt{ goto } L3) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(\text{goto } L2)$$

> **Step 7 — Generate `S1.code` (sequence of two assignments).**
> Using `S → S1 ; S2` rule with `S2.next = L1`:
> $$S_1.code = \text{gen}(t_1 = \text{sum} + i) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(\text{sum} = t_1) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(t_2 = i + 1) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(i = t_2)$$

> **Step 8 — Concatenate all segments to produce the final three-address code.**

### Final Three-Address Code Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
&L1 : \quad \texttt{if } i < 100 \texttt{ goto } L3 \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{goto } L2 \\
&L3 : \quad t_1 = \text{sum} + i \\
&\quad\quad \text{sum} = t_1 \\
&\quad\quad t_2 = i + 1 \\
&\quad\quad i = t_2 \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{goto } L1 \\
&L2 :
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Translation of `for` Loop (One-Pass Scheme)

**Source Construct:**
```
for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) do
    x = x * i
```

### Step-by-Step Derivation

> **Step 1 — Decompose the `for` loop into its semantic components.**
> A `for` loop is semantically equivalent to:
> $$i = 1; \quad \text{while } (i \leq n) \text{ do } \begin{Bmatrix} x = x * i; \quad i = i + 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

> **Step 2 — Apply initialization SDD rule `S1 → id = E1`.**
> $$S_1.code = \text{gen}(i = 1)$$

> **Step 3 — Allocate loop entry and exit labels.**
> `L.begin = newlabel()` → `L1`
> `L.next = newlabel()` → `L2`

> **Step 4 — Apply boolean test `E1 → id1 relop id2`.**
> $$E_1.code = \text{gen}(\text{if } i \leq n \texttt{ goto } L3) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(\text{goto } L2)$$

> **Step 5 — Apply body and increment `S3 → i = i + 1`.**
> $$S_3.code = \text{gen}(t_1 = i + 1) \; \vert\vert \; \text{gen}(i = t_1)$$

> **Step 6 — Concatenate all segments.**

### Final Three-Address Code Output

$$
\begin{aligned
}
&\text{Initialize :} \quad i = 1 \\
&L1 : \quad \texttt{if } i \leq n \texttt{ goto } L3 \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{goto } L2 \\
&L3 : \quad t_2 = x * i \\
&\quad\quad x = t_2 \\
&\quad\quad t_1 = i + 1 \\
&\quad\quad i = t_1 \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{goto } L1 \\
&L2 :
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The `t_2` and `t_1` are distinct temporaries (numbered by `nextinstr`).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Translation of `repeat S until E`

**Source Construct:**
```
repeat
    a = a + 1
until (a == 10)
```

### Step-by-Step Derivation

> **Step 1 — Allocate loop entry label `S.begin = L1`.**
> `S.next = L2` (label after the repeat-until construct).

> **Step 2 — The condition `E` is tested *after* the body executes.**
> Hence: `E.true = S.next = L2` (exit loop when condition becomes true).
> `E.false = S.begin = L1` (jump back when condition is false).

> **Step 3 — Apply production `S → repeat S1 until E`.**
> $$S.code = \text{gen}(S.begin :) \; \vert\vert \; S_1.code \; \vert\vert \; E.code$$

> **Step 4 — Concatenate.**

### Final Three-Address Code Output

$$
\begin{aligned
}
&L1 : \quad t_1 = a + 1 \\
&\quad\quad a = t_1 \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{if } a == 10 \texttt{ goto } L2 \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{goto } L1 \\
&L2 :
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — One-Pass vs. Backpatching

| Feature | One-Pass Translation | Backpatching |
|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| Parser type | Top-down (LL) | Bottom-up (LALR) |
| Jump target resolution | At parse time | Deferred until join point |
| Memory overhead | Lower (no lists) | Higher (linked lists of indices) |
| Number of passes | One | Multiple (emit → backpatch) |
| Attributes used | `S.next`, `E.true`, `E.false` | `truelist`, `falselist`, `nextlist` |
| Suitable for | Simple nested control flow | Complex expressions with `and/or` |
| KTU exam weightage | High | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Trend:** Questions on **backpatching with truelist/falselist** carry the maximum marks and appear almost every semester. Students must master `merge()` and `backpatch()` operations.

---

## 3.6 Backpatching Worked Example — Boolean Expression

**Source:** `if (a < b) and (c < d) then x = y + 1`

### Step-by-Step Derivation

> **Step 1 — Identify productions used.**
> - `E → E1 and E2`
> - `E1 → id1 < id2` (i.e., `a < b`)
> - `E2 → id1 < id2` (i.e., `c < d`)
> - `S → if E then S1`

> **Step 2 — Process `E1 → a < b`.**
> `E1.truelist = makelist(nextinstr)` → `[100]`
> `E1.falselist = makelist(nextinstr + 1)` → `[101]`
> Emit:
> $$\text{gen}(\text{if } a < b \texttt{ goto } \_)$$
> $$\text{gen}(\text{goto } \_)$$
> Assume `nextinstr` is now **102**.

> **Step 3 — Process `E2 → c < d`.**
> `E2.truelist = makelist(nextinstr)` → `[102]`
> `E2.falselist = makelist(nextinstr + 1)` → `[103]`
> Emit:
> $$\text{gen}(\text{if } c < d \texttt{ goto } \_)$$
> $$\text{gen}(\text{goto } \_)$$
> Now `nextinstr = 104`.

> **Step 4 — Apply `E → E1 and E2`.**
> $$\text{backpatch}(E_1.\text{truelist}, E_2.\text{begin})$$
> `E1.truelist = [100]`, `E2.begin = 102` (where `if c<d goto _` is emitted).
> After backpatching, instruction **100** becomes `if a < b goto 102`.
> $$E.\text{truelist} = E_2.\text{truelist} = [102]$$
> $$E.\text{falselist} = \text{merge}(E_1.\text{falselist}, E_2.\text{falselist}) = [101, 103]$$

> **Step 5 — Allocate the `then` branch label.**
> Let `S1.begin = 104`. Backpatch `E.truelist` to `S1.begin`:
> `E.truelist = [102]` becomes `goto 104` at instruction 102.

> **Step 6 — Emit `S1 → x = y + 1`.**
> $$\text{gen}(t_1 = y + 1); \quad \text{gen}(x = t_1)$$
> Now `nextinstr = 106`.

> **Step 7 — Handle the `S.next`.**
> `S.next = 106` (next statement after the if).
> Backpatch `E.falselist = [101, 103]` to `S.next = 106`.

### Final Backpatched Three-Address Code

$$
\begin{aligned
}
&100 : \quad \texttt{if } a < b \texttt{ goto } 102 \\
&101 : \quad \texttt{goto } 106 \\
&102 : \quad \texttt{if } c < d \texttt{ goto } 104 \\
&103 : \quad \texttt{goto } 106 \\
&104 : \quad t_1 = y + 1 \\
&105 : \quad x = t_1 \\
&106 : \quad \text{(next statement)}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Flow Translation Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code: Structured Statements] --> B[Lexical Analysis Tokens]
    B --> C[Parser with SDD / SDT Scheme]
    C --> D{Statement Type}
    D --> E1[if-then Handler]
    D --> E2[if-then-else Handler]
    D --> E3[while-do Handler]
    D --> E4[for-loop Handler]
    D --> E5[repeat-until Handler]
    E1 --> F[Attribute Propagation Phase]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G{Translation Method}
    G --> H1[One-Pass: Inherited Attrs S.next, E.true, E.false]
    G --> H2[Backpatching: truelist, falselist, nextlist]
    H1 --> I[Label Allocation newlabel nextinstr]
    H2 --> J[List Operations makelist merge backpatch]
    I --> K[Three-Address Code Emission]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Intermediate Code Quadruples or Triples]
    L --> M[Code Optimization Phase]
    M --> N[Target Code Generation]
```

## 4.2 Flow of Control — `if E then S1 else S2`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Entry of if-then-else] --> B[Evaluate Boolean Expression E]
    B --> C{E True or False}
    C -->|True| D[Execute S1 code]
    C -->|False| E[Execute S2 code]
    D --> F[goto S.next]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Label S.next: Continue main flow]
```

## 4.3 Flow of Control — `while E do S`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Label S.begin: Loop Entry] --> B[Evaluate Boolean Expression E]
    B --> C{E True or False}
    C -->|True| D[Execute S code]
    C -->|False| E[Label S.next: Loop Exit]
    D --> F[goto S.begin]
    F --> A
```

## 4.4 Flow of Control — `repeat S until E`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Label S.begin: Loop Entry] --> B[Execute S code]
    B --> C[Evaluate Boolean Expression E]
    C --> D{E True or False}
    D -->|False| A
    D -->|True| E[Label S.next: Loop Exit]
```

## 4.5 Backpatching Mechanism — Lifecycle of a Truelist

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Emit if condition goto _] --> B[Instruction Index i added to truelist]
    B --> C[Continue Parsing]
    C --> D{Reach Join Point}
    D --> E[Call backpatch truelist target_label]
    E --> F[Replace all _ in truelist with target_label]
    F --> G[Resolved Three-Address Code]
```

## 4.6 SDD Attribute Dependency Graph — `while E do S`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Snext[S.next] --> Efalse[E.false]
    Sbegin[S.begin] --> Etrue[E.true]
    Sbegin --> S1next[S1.next]
    Etrue --> S1next
    Scode[S.code: emitted] --> BeginLabel[gen S.begin colon]
    Scode --> Ecode[E.code]
    Scode --> EtrueLabel[gen E.true colon]
    Scode --> S1code[S1.code]
    Scode --> GotoBegin[gen goto S.begin]
```

## 4.7 Translation Workflow — End-to-End Compiler View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source: if while for etc] --> B[Parse Tree Construction]
    B --> C[SDD Annotated Tree]
    C --> D[Attribute Evaluation Pass]
    D --> E[Intermediate Representation IR]
    E --> F[Control Flow Graph CFG]
    F --> G[Optimization]
    G --> H[Target Code]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the control-flow graph," emphasize **labeled jump targets** (`L1`, `L2`, …) and **decision diamonds** (for boolean tests). The CFG must be **acyclic within a basic block** and may contain back-edges for loops.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Q1. Define the syntax-directed translation scheme for `if E then S1 else S2` using inherited attributes. Mention the semantic rules clearly.

**Model Answer:**

The production is `S → if E then S1 else S2`. The semantic rules are:

1. `E.true = newlabel()` — allocate a new label `L_true` for the true branch.
2. `E.false = newlabel()` — allocate a new label `L_false` for the false branch.
3. `S1.next = S.next` — after `S1`, jump to the statement following the if-else.
4. `S2.next = S.next` — after `S2`, also jump to the statement following the if-else.
5. `S.code = E.code ‖ gen(E.true ':') ‖ S1.code ‖ gen('goto' S.next) ‖ gen(E.false ':') ‖ S2.code`

The scheme ensures both branches converge at `S.next`, and boolean conditions direct control flow via the freshly allocated `E.true` and `E.false` labels.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Differentiate between `truelist`, `falselist`, and `nextlist` used in backpatching. Why is backpatching necessary for a bottom-up parser?

**Model Answer:**

| Attribute | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `truelist` | A list of indices of TAC instructions that contain a *not-yet-filled* `goto` for the **true** outcome of a boolean expression. |
| `falselist` | A list of indices of TAC instructions that contain a *not-yet-filled* `goto` for the **false** outcome. |
| `nextlist` | A list of indices of TAC instructions containing unconditional `goto _` placeholders that must be backpatched with the label of the *next* statement after the current construct. |

**Why backpatching is necessary:** In a **bottom-up (LALR) parser**, the parser reduces productions *after* seeing the right-hand side. Hence, the target label of a forward jump (e.g., the `else` branch or the next statement after `if-then`) is **not yet known** when the `if` is first reduced. Backpatching defers target resolution via instruction lists, which are then filled at the appropriate join point — making it the only practical strategy for bottom-up parsing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

### QUESTION A (Choice 1)

**(a)** Translate the following source code segment into three-address code using the **one-pass syntax-directed translation scheme** with inherited attributes. Show the parse tree or SDD annotations and the final TAC. **(7 Marks)**

```
if (x > 0)
    if (y > x)
        z = y - x
    else
        z = x - y
else
    z = 0
```

**(b)** Construct the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** for the TAC obtained and identify the number of basic blocks. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

> **Step 1 — Decompose the nested structure.**
> The outer `if-else` tests `(x > 0)`. The inner `if-else` tests `(y > x)`. Each branch assigns to `z`.

> **Step 2 — Allocate labels for outer condition.**
> Let `S.next = L5` (next statement after the entire construct).
> - `E1.true = L1` (true branch of outer test)
> - `E1.false = L4` (false branch of outer test)

> **Step 3 — Outer true branch contains the inner `if-else` whose `S_inner.next = L5`.**
> - `E2.true = L2` (true branch of inner test)
> - `E2.false = L3` (false branch of inner test)

> **Step 4 — Allocate next labels for inner branches.**
> - `S2.next = L5` (after `z = y - x`, jump to L5)
> - `S3.next = L5` (after `z = x - y`, jump to L5)
> - `S4.next = L5` (after `z = 0`, jump to L5)

> **Step 5 — Generate the TAC.**

Final Three-Address Code:

$$
\begin{aligned
}
&L1 : \quad \texttt{if } x > 0 \texttt{ goto } L2 \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{goto } L4 \\
&L2 : \quad \texttt{if } y > x \texttt{ goto } L3 \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{goto } L5 \\
&L3 : \quad t_1 = y - x \\
&\quad\quad z = t_1 \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{goto } L5 \\
&L5 : \quad \text{(end of inner true branch)} \\
&L4 : \quad t_2 = 0 \\
&\quad\quad z = t_2 \\
&L6 : \quad \text{(S.next)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** Label `L5` is the join point after the inner `if-else`. Some label slots may be merged by optimization.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

> **Step 1 — Identify basic blocks.**
> A basic block is a maximal sequence of consecutive TAC instructions with **no internal jumps** (except at the end) and **no jump targets** (except at the start).

The CFG has the following basic blocks:

- **B1** (Entry): `if x > 0 goto L2; goto L4`
- **B2** (True-outer, head of inner test): `if y > x goto L3; goto L5`
- **B3** (Inner-true): `t1 = y - x; z = t1; goto L5`
- **B4** (Inner-false merged with join): `t2 = 0; z = t2` (corresponds to L4 path)
- **B5** (Exit / S.next): `(L6)`

> **Step 2 — Draw the CFG edges.**
> - B1 → B2 (on `x > 0` true)
> - B1 → B4 (on `x > 0` false)
> - B2 → B3 (on `y > x` true)
> - B2 → B4 (on `y > x` false)
> - B3 → B4 (unconditional after `goto L5` collapses)

> **Step 3 — Count basic blocks.**
> **Total: 4 basic blocks** (after label-collapse optimization).

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2)

**(a)** Translate the following source code segment into three-address code using the **backpatching technique** with `truelist`, `falselist`, and `nextlist`. Show all intermediate stages: emission, list creation, merge, and backpatch calls. **(7 Marks)**

```
while (a < b) and (c != 0) do
    a = a + 1
```

**(b)** Explain the SDD for translating `for (i = E1; i <= E2; i = i + 1) do S` into three-address code. Show the complete production rules and the final TAC template. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

> **Step 1 — Production used:** `S → while E do S1`, where `E → E1 and E2`, `E1 → a < b`, `E2 → c != 0`, `S1 → a = a + 1`.

> **Step 2 — Process `E1 → a < b`.**
> - `E1.truelist = makelist(100) = [100]`
> - `E1.falselist = makelist(101) = [101]`
> - Emit instruction 100: `if a < b goto _`
> - Emit instruction 101: `goto _`
> - `nextinstr = 102`

> **Step 3 — Process `E2 → c != 0`.**
> - `E2.truelist = makelist(102) = [102]`
> - `E2.falselist = makelist(103) = [103]`
> - Emit instruction 102: `if c != 0 goto _`
> - Emit instruction 103: `goto _`
> - `nextinstr = 104`

> **Step 4 — Apply `E → E1 and E2`.**
> - `backpatch(E1.truelist, E2.begin)` → `backpatch([100], 102)` → instruction 100 becomes `if a < b goto 102`
> - `E.truelist = E2.truelist = [102]`
> - `E.falselist = merge([101], [103]) = [101, 103]`

> **Step 5 — Apply `S → while E do S1`.**
> - `S.begin = 100` (loop entry, the first emitted instruction)
> - `backpatch(S1.nextlist, S.begin)` (after parsing S1)
> - `backpatch(E.truelist, S.begin)` → `backpatch([102], 100)` → instruction 102 becomes `if c != 0 goto 100`
> - `S.nextlist = E.falselist = [101, 103]`

> **Step 6 — Process `S1 → a = a + 1`.**
> - Emit instruction 104: `t1 = a + 1`
> - Emit instruction 105: `a = t1`
> - `S1.nextlist = makelist(105) = [105]` (placeholder for the back-jump `goto S.begin`)

> **Step 7 — Backpatch `S1.nextlist` to `S.begin = 100`.**
> - Instruction 105 becomes `goto 100`.

> **Step 8 — Backpatch `S.nextlist = [101, 103]` to the statement after the while (assume L_exit = 106).**

### Final Backpatched TAC

$$
\begin{aligned
}
&100 : \quad \texttt{if } a < b \texttt{ goto } 102 \\
&101 : \quad \texttt{goto } 106 \\
&102 : \quad \texttt{if } c \neq 0 \texttt{ goto } 100 \\
&103 : \quad \texttt{goto } 106 \\
&104 : \quad t_1 = a + 1 \\
&105 : \quad \texttt{goto } 100 \\
&106 : \quad \text{(statement after while)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

> **Step 1 — Identify productions.**
> - `S1 → for (E1; E2; E3) S2`
>   Decomposed as: `S1 → id = E1; S2`, where `S2 → while E2' do (S3 E3)` and `E2' → id relop E2`, `E3 → id = id + 1`.

> **Step 2 — SDD semantic rules (one-pass version).**

| Production | Semantic Rule |
|------------|---------------|
| `S → for (S1; E; S3) S2` | `S1.next = newlabel(); S.begin = newlabel(); E.true = newlabel(); E.false = S.next; S3.next = S.begin; S2.next = S.begin;` |
| `S → for (S1; E; S3) S2` (cont.) | `S.code = S1.code ‖ gen(S1.next ':') ‖ gen(S.begin ':') ‖ E.code ‖ gen(E.true ':') ‖ S2.code ‖ S3.code ‖ gen('goto' S.begin)` |

> **Step 3 — TAC template for `for (i = E1; i <= E2; i = i + 1) do S`.**

$$
\begin{aligned
}
&\text{Initialize: } t_{\text{init}} = E_1; \quad i = t_{\text{init}} \\
&L_{\text{begin}} : \quad t_{\text{limit}} = E_2 \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{if } i \leq t_{\text{limit}} \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{body}} \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{goto } L_{\text{next}} \\
&L_{\text{body}} : \quad \text{(code for body S)} \\
&\quad\quad t_{\text{step}} = i + 1 \\
&\quad\quad i = t_{\text{step}} \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{goto } L_{\text{begin}} \\
&L_{\text{next}} : \quad \text{(statement after the for)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 4 — Explanation.**
> - The initialization `i = E1` is emitted before entering the loop.
> - The loop test `i <= E2` is evaluated at the loop head; if true, control transfers to the body.
> - After the body, the increment `i = i + 1` is executed, followed by an unconditional jump back to the loop head.
> - If the test fails, control exits to `L_next`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Label Naming Confusion:** Students often write `E.true` and `S.next` interchangeably or use the same label twice — leading to incorrect flow convergence. **Always allocate *fresh* labels** for every `E.true` and `E.false`.
> 2. **Forgetting the `goto` after the `then` branch:** In `if E then S1 else S2`, students frequently forget `gen('goto' S.next)` after `S1.code`, which causes the `else` branch to be wrongly executed even when the condition is true.
> 3. **Backpatching list misuse:** Confusing `nextlist` of a statement with `truelist`/`falselist` of a boolean. **`nextlist` belongs to statements, `truelist/falselist` to boolean expressions.**
> 4. **Order of backpatch operations:** For `E → E1 and E2`, students sometimes call `backpatch(E1.truelist, …)` **before** parsing `E2`, which is incorrect because the target label (`E2.begin`) is not yet known. The backpatch must happen **after** `E2` has been parsed.
> 5. **While loop's `goto`:** Forgetting the final `gen('goto' S.begin)` at the end of `while` translation — this breaks the loop's iteration.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3**

- [x] **Control-flow translation** flattens structured `if`, `while`, `for`, `repeat` constructs into a **linear stream of three-address code** with explicit jumps.
- [x] **One-pass scheme** uses **inherited attributes** `S.next`, `S.begin`, `E.true`, `E.false` and is best suited for **top-down parsers**.
- [x] **Backpatching scheme** uses **synthesized lists** `truelist`, `falselist`, `nextlist` and is best suited for **bottom-up (LALR) parsers**.
- [x] `E.true` and `E.false` are **labels**, not booleans. They mark *where* to jump *if* E is true/false.
- [x] In `if E then S1 else S2`, **both** `S1.next` and `S2.next` must equal `S.next`. The `goto S.next` is mandatory after `S1.code`.
- [x] In `while E do S`, the order of emitted code is: **loop label → test → true label → body → goto loop label → exit label**.
- [x] In `repeat S until E`, the **body is emitted first**, then the test, with `E.true` leading to exit (since the loop stops *until* E becomes true).
- [x] For `for` loops, the canonical translation is: **init → test → body → increment → back-edge to test → exit**.
- [x] **Boolean expression translation** uses short-circuit evaluation: `E1 and E2` requires `E2` to be evaluated *only if* `E1` is true; the corresponding backpatching rule is `backpatch(E1.truelist, E2.begin)`.
- [x] For `E1 or E2`, the rule is `backpatch(E1.falselist, E2.begin)`.
- [x] For `not E1`, simply **swap** the lists: `E.truelist = E1.falselist`; `E.falselist = E1.truelist`.
- [x] `merge(p1, p2)` concatenates two instruction lists. `backpatch(p, i)` iterates over `p` and inserts `i` as the target.
- [x] The **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** is built from the TAC; basic blocks are maximal straight-line instruction sequences.
- [x] KTU 2024 **most-asked question type**: "Translate the given code into three-address code using backpatching and show all intermediate lists." Practice this format.
- [x] KTU 2024 **second-most-asked**: "Explain the SDD for `if-else` / `while` with attribute grammar rules." Memorize the table.
- [x] Remember to **emit `goto _` placeholders** correctly — for `if E then S1`, the `E.falselist` must be backpatched to `S.next` (next statement).
- [x] All labels are **symbolic** during translation and resolved to **numeric addresses** in the code-generation phase.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Shape - Arithmetic Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Code Shape - Arithmetic Operators ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of a KTU 2024 Scheme Compiler Design syllabus, **Code Shape** refers to the standard, reusable **template or pattern of target-machine instructions** that a code generator emits for a specific source-language construct. It is essentially the "blueprint" that defines *which* target instructions are produced, *in what order*, and *using which addressing modes*, when the compiler encounters a particular operator or statement. When we talk about **Code Shape for Arithmetic Operators**, we specifically refer to the canonical instruction sequences used to translate source-level arithmetic constructs such as: - Binary arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` - Unary arithmetic: `-x` - Assignment: `a = b` - Mixed: `a = b + c * d` The "shape" encapsulates the choice of: 1. **Target instruction** (e.g., `ADD R1, R2`) 2. **Addressing mode** (Direct, Indirect, Immediate, Indexed) 3. **Register usage** (which operand lives in a register) 4. **Memory access pattern** (when to LOAD / STORE) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Code Shape is the bridge between the *intermediate representation (IR)* (three-address code / quadruples) and the *target machine code*. It is the single most exam-relevant concept in Module 4's Code Generation unit. ### 1.2 Intuition: The "Recipe Analogy" Imagine a chef (the compiler) cooking dishes (target instructions) from a recipe book (code shapes). - **Source Code** = Customer's order ("give me a + b") - **Intermediate Code (TAC)** = The standardized kitchen ticket ("a = b + c") - **Code Shape** = The standardized recipe ("LOAD R1, b; LOAD R2, c; ADD R3, R1, R2; STORE R3, a") - **Target Code** = The actual cooked dish (binary instructions in memory) A *code shape* is therefore a pre-decided, parameterised instruction template. For every arithmetic operator, the compiler fills in the template with the *current* operand values, registers, and memory locations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why code shapes matter:** They encode the **efficiency** of compilation. A good code shape minimises memory traffic, maximises register reuse, and produces optimal or near-optimal assembly code — the central goal of the Code Generation phase. ### 1.3 The Three Core Sub-Concepts | Term | Meaning | KTU Significance | |------|---------|------------------| | **Address Mode** | The way an operand is specified (direct, indirect, immediate) | Determines cost of every memory access | | **Register Descriptor** | Tracks what variable currently lives in which register | Enables register reuse and avoids redundant LOADs | | **Address Descriptor** | Tracks where (register or memory) the *latest* copy of a variable lives | Ensures STORE happens at the right time | | **Next-Use** | Whether a variable will be referenced again before being overwritten | Decides whether to STORE a dead value | All of these are activated *within* a code shape for arithmetic operators. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Code Shape for Arithmetic Operators For a generic binary arithmetic operation `x = y op z`, the standard code shape is a **four-step sequence**: 1. **Ensure `y` is in a register** → emit `LD Rd, y` if not already there. 2. **Ensure `z` is in a register** → emit `LD Rs, z` if not already there (or use immediate mode if `z` is a constant). 3. **Emit the operation** → `OP Rd, Rs` (e.g., `ADD Rd, Rs`). 4. **Store the result** → `ST x, Rd` (only if `x` is needed later, or if `Rd` will be overwritten). For a **unary** operation `x = -y`: $$x = -y \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\text{LD } R_d, y \;\; ; \;\;\text{NEG } R_d \;\; ; \;\;\text{ST } x, R_d$$ For a **copy** `x = y`: $$x = y \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\text{LD } R_d, y \;\; ; \;\;\text{ST } x, R_d$$ > [!TIP] > The "shape" literally describes the **sequence of memory/register actions** that the compiler will take. Different code shapes exist for array accesses, function calls, branches, etc. — but *arithmetic* shapes are the most frequently tested in KTU. ### 2.2 The `GetReg` Function — The Heart of Code Generation `GetReg` is the subroutine that decides **which physical register** to use when emitting an arithmetic code shape. Its decision is based on: 1. **Occupied register with no further use** → best candidate (reusable, no STORE needed). 2. **Occupied register that holds a needed variable** → must STORE first, then reuse. 3. **Empty register** → use directly. 4. If **all registers are full and all are needed** → spill the one with the *farthest next-use*. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table summarises the **standard cost model** used in KTU board questions. Assume each LOAD/STORE costs **1** unit, and each arithmetic operation (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`) costs **1** unit. | Instruction Type | Address Mode | Cost (units) | Example | |------------------|--------------|--------------|---------| | `LD R, M` (register ← memory) | Direct | **1** | `LD R1, A` | | `LD R, *M` (register ← indirect) | Indirect | **2** | `LD R1, *A` | | `LD R, #c` (register ← constant) | Immediate | **1** | `LD R1, #5` | | `ST M, R` (memory ← register) | Direct | **1** | `ST A, R1` | | `ST *M, R` (memory ← indirect) | Indirect | **2** | `ST *A, R1` | | `OP R, S` (register ← register op register) | Register | **1** | `ADD R1, R2` | | `OP R, M` (register ← register op memory) | Mixed | **2** | `ADD R1, A` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden rule for KTU valuation:** Memory operations are *always* more expensive than register operations. Code shapes that **keep operands in registers** for as long as possible are scored as more efficient. ### 2.4 Total Cost of a Code Shape For an assignment `x = y op z`, the **total cost** of the emitted code shape is: $$\text{Cost}(x = y \text{ op } z) = \underbrace{C_{load}(y)}_{\text{step 1}} + \underbrace{C_{load}(z)}_{\text{step 2}} + \underbrace{C_{op}}_{\text{step 3}} + \underbrace{C_{store}(x)}_{\text{step 4}}$$ If both `y` and `z` are already in registers and `x` is also in a register, the cost can drop to **1** (only the OP), which is the **optimal** code shape. ### 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering The code shape strategy directly influences: - **Compiler performance in production** (e.g., GCC, LLVM use sophisticated code-shape selection). - **Embedded systems**: smaller, faster code = lower power consumption. - **JIT compilers** (Java HotSpot, V8): the code shape determines the overhead of every arithmetic op in a hot loop. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Code Generation with Register Allocation This is the **most-asked type** of question in KTU ESE. We are given a basic block of three-address code and an *empty* register file, and we must produce the emitted target code with costs. **Problem:** Generate target code for the following basic block using the algorithm with `GetReg`, next-use information, and a register file of size `R0, R1` (only **2 registers**). Use the cost model from the formula sheet. Source TAC: ``` (1) t1 = a + b (2) t2 = c + d (3) t3 = t1 - t2 (4) a = t3 ``` **Step 1: Build the Next-Use Table** We scan **backwards**. If a variable's name appears in the RHS, the LHS of the *current* instruction is its next use. | Instruction | Uses | Defs | Next-Use of each operand | |-------------|------|------|--------------------------| | (4) `a = t3` | `t3` | `a` | `t3` is not used again → next-use = **none** | | (3) `t3 = t1 - t2` | `t1, t2` | `t3` | `t1` next used at (4)? No → next-use = none; `t2` next used at (4)? No → next-use = none | | (2) `t2 = c + d` | `c, d` | `t2` | `c` next used at (3)? No; `d` next used at (3)? No → next-use = none for both | | (1) `t1 = a + b` | `a, b` | `t1` | `a` next used at (2)? No; `b` next used at (2)? No → next-use = none | All variables are dead immediately after their definition → **maximum spilling freedom**. **Step 2: Process Each Instruction** **Instruction (1): `t1 = a + b`** - `GetReg("t1 = a + b")` → both registers empty → pick `R0`. - Need `a` in a register → `GetReg("a")` → pick `R1`. - Emit: - `LD R1, a` → cost = **1** - `LD R0, b` → cost = **1** (using R0 directly for `b`, no separate register for `b`) - Now apply `b` to `R0`: - Wait — the OP is between `a` (R1) and `b`. The standard shape uses one register for result and one for second operand. Let's redo using the canonical shape (result register + load second operand into it): - Load `a` into `R0`: `LD R0, a` → cost 1 - Add `b` to `R0`: `ADD R0, b` → cost 2 (mixed mode from formula sheet) - `t1` is in `R0`, no STORE needed (it will be used at instruction 3). **State:** R0 holds `{t1, a}`; R1 is empty. | Address Descriptor | Register Descriptor | |--------------------|---------------------| | `a`: {R0, memory} | R0: {t1, a} | | `b`: {memory} | R1: {empty} | | `t1`: {R0} | | **Instruction (2): `t2 = c + d`** - `GetReg("t2 = c + d")` → R1 is empty → pick R1. - `c` and `d` are not in registers → load + add. - Emit: - `LD R1, c` → cost 1 - `ADD R1, d` → cost 2 **State:** R0 holds `{t1, a}`; R1 holds `{t2, c}`. **Instruction (3): `t3 = t1 - t2`** - `GetReg("t3 = t1 - t2")` → both registers occupied but both values (`t1`, `t2`) are *not* used after (3) (next-use = none) → pick any, say R0. - But `t1` is *already* in R0 → no LOAD needed. - Need `t2` in R0 — we can use `SUB R0, R1` (register-mode operation, cost 1). Emit: - `SUB R0, R1` → cost 1 **State:** R0 holds `{t3, a, t1}`; R1 holds `{t2, c}`. **Instruction (4): `a = t3`** - `GetReg("a = t3")` → t3 is in R0 → no LOAD needed. Result register = R0. - Emit: - `ST a, R0` → cost 1 **Final emitted code with costs:** ``` LD R0, a # cost 1 ADD R0, b # cost 2 LD R1, c # cost 1 ADD R1, d # cost 2 SUB R0, R1 # cost 1 ST a, R0 # cost 1 ``` **Total cost = 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 8 units.** ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cost Under Different Assumptions The same TAC block, but with **4 registers** (R0, R1, R2, R3): ``` LD R0, a # cost 1 ADD R0, b # cost 2 LD R1, c # cost 1 ADD R1, d # cost 2 SUB R0, R1 # cost 1 ST a, R0 # cost 1 ``` **Total cost = 8 units (same, since adding more registers doesn't help here — operands are scalars).** If we instead allow **3-operand instructions** (e.g., `ADD Rd, Rs1, Rs2`): ``` LD R0, a # cost 1 LD R1, b # cost 1 LD R2, c # cost 1 LD R3, d # cost 1 ADD R4, R0, R1 # cost 1 ADD R5, R2, R3 # cost 1 SUB R6, R4, R5 # cost 1 ST a, R6 # cost 1 ``` **Total cost = 8 units.** The shape changes but the *total* number of memory and arithmetic operations remains 8 — a useful sanity check. ### 3.3 Optimality Lower Bound (Theoretical Minimum) For an expression with `n` operand occurrences in the RHS and `m` definitions, the theoretical minimum cost is: $$\text{Cost}_{\min} = \underbrace{n}_{\text{LOADs}} + \underbrace{m}_{\text{STOREs}} + \underbrace{k}_{\text{arithmetic ops}}$$ For our example: `n = 4` (a, b, c, d), `m = 1` (ST a, R0), `k = 3` (two ADDs + one SUB) → $\text{Cost}_{\min} = 4 + 1 + 3 = 8$ units. Our generated code achieves this **optimum**. > [!IMPORTANT] > When a code shape reaches the theoretical minimum, the code is called **optimal**. The next-use-based `GetReg` algorithm is *optimal* for *basic blocks* (Aho-Sethi-Ullman result). This is a high-yield KTU fact. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Flow of Code Generation for Arithmetic Operators The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional architecture** of how a code generator processes an arithmetic statement and emits a code shape. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Receive TAC Instruction
e.g., x = y op z] --> B[Lookup Next-Use Info
for y and z] B --> C[Call GetReg Function
to choose result register Rd] C --> D{Is y in a register?} D -- Yes --> E[No LOAD for y
Use existing Ry] D -- No --> F[Emit LD Rd, y
Cost = 1] E --> G{Is z a constant?} F --> G G -- Yes --> H[Use Immediate Mode
ADD Rd, #c
Cost = 1] G -- No --> I{Is z in a register?} I -- Yes --> J[Emit OP Rd, Rz
Cost = 1] I -- No --> K[Emit OP Rd, z
Mixed Mode Cost = 2] H --> L[Update Address Descriptor
and Register Descriptor] J --> L K --> L L --> M{Is x needed later
or in memory?} M -- Yes --> N[Emit ST x, Rd
Cost = 1] M -- No --> O[Skip STORE
x lives only in Rd] N --> P[Move to Next TAC Instruction] O --> P P --> Q[End of Code Shape Emission] ``` ### 4.2 Register Allocation Decision Tree (`GetReg`) This Mermaid block illustrates how `GetReg` selects a target register for the result of an arithmetic code shape. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[GetReg Called for x = y op z] --> Q1{Any register empty?} Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Return empty register Rd
No STORE required] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Any occupied register
holds a dead variable
next-use = none?} Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Return that register
No STORE required] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Any occupied register
holds a needed variable?} Q3 -- Yes --> A3[Emit STORE first
Free up register, return it] Q3 -- No --> A4[Spill variable with
farthest next-use
STORE, then return] A1 --> End[Return Rd] A2 --> End A3 --> End A4 --> End ``` ### 4.3 Code Shape Decision Table (Encoded as a Graph) ```mermaid graph LR src1[Source: y] -- LD if needed --> reg1[Register Ry] src2[Source: z] -- LD if needed --> reg2[Register Rz] reg1 -- OP --> alu[Arithmetic Unit
ADD/SUB/MUL/DIV] reg2 -- OP --> alu alu -- result --> regr[Register Rd] regr -- ST if needed --> dst[Destination: x in memory] ``` > [!NOTE] > The arrows labelled "if needed" capture the **conditional emission** that makes a code shape efficient. The compiler emits a `LOAD` only if the operand is *not* already in a register, and emits a `STORE` only if the *next* instruction cannot consume the value directly from the register. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** **Define the term "code shape" as used in code generation. List any two standard code shapes used for arithmetic operators.** *Model Answer (3 marks):* **Definition (2 marks):** A *code shape* is a standardised template or pattern of target-machine instructions that the code generator emits when it encounters a particular source-language construct. It specifies the sequence of instructions, the addressing modes used, and the order in which operands and results are loaded, operated upon, and stored. **Examples (1 mark):** 1. **Binary arithmetic** `x = y + z` → `LD R, y ; ADD R, z ; ST x, R` 2. **Unary arithmetic** `x = -y` → `LD R, y ; NEG R ; ST x, R` --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]** **What is the role of the `GetReg` function in code generation for arithmetic expressions?** *Model Answer (3 marks):* `GetReg` is the *register-allocation* subroutine of the code generator. Given an arithmetic instruction, it selects a physical register `R` to hold the result, subject to three priorities (in order): 1. Pick an **empty register** (no overhead). 2. Pick an **occupied register whose value is dead** (no STORE needed, free to overwrite). 3. Otherwise, pick an **occupied register** whose value is needed → first emit a `STORE` to memory, then reuse the register. This decision minimises total `LOAD`/`STORE` traffic and directly determines the cost of the emitted code shape. (3 marks for a complete answer) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Regular] **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the concept of **code shapes for arithmetic operators** with a suitable example. Discuss how the next-use information influences the choice of code shape. **(b) [7 marks)** Consider the following three-address code. Generate the target code using a code generation algorithm with two registers `R0` and `R1`. Use the cost model: memory access = 1, register operation = 1, mixed = 2. Show register and address descriptors after each step. ``` (1) a = b + c (2) d = a - e (3) f = d + g (4) b = f ``` --- **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks]** **Concept of code shape (3 marks):** A code shape is a parameterised instruction template. For binary arithmetic `x = y op z`, the canonical code shape consists of: 1. LOAD `y` into a register `R`. 2. Apply the operator with `z` (via register or memory). 3. STORE the result into `x` if needed. **Example (2 marks):** For `x = y + z` with both `y` and `z` in memory: ``` LD R0, y # cost 1 ADD R0, z # cost 2 (mixed mode) ST x, R0 # cost 1 ``` **Next-use influence (2 marks):** Next-use information tells the code generator whether an operand value will be *needed again* in the future. If the next-use is "none", the compiler is free to **overwrite** the register holding that value without first emitting a `STORE`. This typically halves the cost of code shapes for temporary variables in basic blocks. **(b) [7 marks]** **Step 1: Build next-use table (1 mark)** Scanning backwards: | Instr | Defs | Uses | Next-Use of operands | |-------|------|------|----------------------| | (4) `b = f` | `b` | `f` | `f` not used after → none | | (3) `f = d + g` | `f` | `d, g` | `d` not used after (3) → none; `g` not used after (3) → none | | (2) `d = a - e` | `d` | `a, e` | `a` used at (3) → next-use = (3); `e` not used after (2) → none | | (1) `a = b + c` | `a` | `b, c` | `b` used at (2) → next-use = (2); `c` not used after (1) → none | **Step 2: Emit code (6 marks)** **Instruction (1): `a = b + c`** Next-use of `b` = (2) — needed later. Next-use of `c` = none. `GetReg` → R0 (empty). Emit: ``` LD R0, b # cost 1 (b needed later, so we use mixed mode add) ADD R0, c # cost 2 ``` State: R0 = {a, b}; R1 = empty. AD: a={R0}, b={R0, mem}, c={mem}. **Instruction (2): `d = a - e`** `a` is in R0 → no LOAD for `a`. `e` is in memory. Next-use of `a` = (3), needed later. `GetReg` → R1 (empty). Emit: ``` LD R1, e # cost 1 SUB R1, R0 # cost 1 (a is in R0, register mode) ``` State: R0 = {a, b}; R1 = {d, e}. AD: a={R0, mem}, b={R0, mem}, d={R1}, e={R1, mem}. **Instruction (3): `f = d + g`** `d` is in R1, `g` in memory. `d` next-use = none. `GetReg` → can reuse R1 (holds dead `d`). Emit: ``` ADD R1, g # cost 2 (mixed mode; overwriting d in R1 with f) ``` State: R0 = {a, b}; R1 = {f}. AD: f={R1}, a={R0, mem}, b={R0, mem}. **Instruction (4): `b = f`** `f` is in R1. `b` is in R0 and in memory. We need to update `b` in memory. Emit: ``` ST b, R1 # cost 1 (overwrite b's memory location with f) ``` State: R0 = {} (b no longer current); R1 = {f}. AD: b={R1, mem}, f={R1}. **Final emitted code with costs:** ``` LD R0, b # 1 ADD R0, c # 2 LD R1, e # 1 SUB R1, R0 # 1 ADD R1, g # 2 ST b, R1 # 1 ``` **Total cost = 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 8 units.** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary] **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **register allocation algorithm** for a code generator. How does the `GetReg` function decide which register to allocate? **(b) [7 marks]** For the following three-address code, generate target code assuming 2 registers. Use the cost model: `LD`/`ST` = 1, mixed `OP` = 2, register `OP` = 1. Show descriptors. ``` (1) x = y - z (2) a = x * b (3) c = a + d (4) y = c ``` --- **Model Solution (Key Steps):** **(a) [7 marks]** - **Register descriptors** track current contents of each register (1 mark). - **Address descriptors** track locations (register/memory) of every variable (1 mark). - `GetReg` algorithm with the 4 priorities (4 marks, as listed in Section 4.2 of these notes). - **Diagram** (1 mark) — refer to the Mermaid `GetReg` decision tree in Section 4.2 of these notes. **(b) [7 marks]** **Next-Use Table (1 mark):** | Instr | Defs | Uses | Next-Use of operands | |-------|------|------|----------------------| | (4) `y = c` | `y` | `c` | `c` not used after (4) → none | | (3) `c = a + d` | `c` | `a, d` | `a` not used after (3) → none; `d` not used after (3) → none | | (2) `a = x * b` | `a` | `x, b` | `x` used at (3) → next-use = (3); `b` not used after (2) → none | | (1) `x = y - z` | `x` | `y, z` | `y` used at (2) → next-use = (2); `z` not used after (1) → none | **Code Generation (6 marks):** ``` (1) LD R0, y # cost 1 (y needed at (2)) SUB R0, z # cost 2 (z is dead, mixed mode) (2) MUL R1, R0, b # cost 2 (multiply x in R0 with b from memory) # Using R1: result 'a'. Note: in standard 2-op model, this is: LD R1, R0? — Not allowed. Use: LD R0, b # cost 1 (overwrite x — but x is needed at (3)!) ``` Wait — **x is needed at (3)**. So we cannot overwrite R0 yet. Need to use R1. Corrected: ``` (1) LD R0, y # cost 1 SUB R0, z # cost 2 (2) LD R1, b # cost 1 MUL R1, R0 # cost 2 (mixed mode: multiply R1 by R0) # After this, R1 holds a; R0 still holds x. (3) LD R0, d # cost 1 (R0 still needed? a is in R1, x is dead after (3)) # But x is needed at (3)? Yes. So we need x for (3). # x is in R0. Reuse R0? x's next-use after (3) is none, so we can overwrite R0. # We need d in R0 to add to a. But a is in R1. # Better: add R0 (which holds x) and a? No, (3) is a + d, not x + d. # Plan: bring d into R0 (overwriting x since x is dead after this instr). LD R0, d # cost 1 ADD R0, R1 # cost 1 (register mode: R0 = R0 + R1) (4) ST y, R0 # cost 1 ``` **Final emitted code:** ``` LD R0, y # 1 SUB R0, z # 2 LD R1, b # 1 MUL R1, R0 # 2 LD R0, d # 1 ADD R0, R1 # 1 ST y, R0 # 1 ``` **Total cost = 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 9 units.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to update descriptors** after every emit. (–1 mark per missing update) > 2. **Confusing memory-mode and register-mode costs.** `ADD R0, b` is mixed mode = **2 units**, not 1. (–1 mark) > 3. **Ignoring next-use** when picking a register to overwrite. (–2 marks) > 4. **Emitting unnecessary `STORE`s** for dead values. (–1 to –2 marks) > 5. **Failing to use the existing register** when an operand is already in one. (–1 mark) > 6. **Not justifying the choice of code shape** with reference to next-use or address descriptors. Always write the descriptor table after each step. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Code shape** = reusable, parameterised target-instruction template for a source construct. - [ ] Arithmetic code shapes come in **three flavours**: binary, unary, and assignment (copy). - [ ] Standard 4-step shape for `x = y op z`: **LOAD y → LOAD/IMM z → OP → STORE x (conditional)**. - [ ] **Cost model** (KTU standard): `LD`/`ST` = 1, register `OP` = 1, mixed `OP` (register + memory) = 2, immediate `OP` = 1. - [ ] **Address descriptors** track *where the latest copy* of a variable lives (register and/or memory). - [ ] **Register descriptors** track *which variables currently reside* in each register. - [ ] **Next-use** is computed by a **backward scan** of the basic block. - [ ] **`GetReg` priorities**: empty register → register with dead value → store-and-reuse → spill farthest next-use. - [ ] **Optimality theorem** (Aho–Sethi–Ullman): the next-use-based algorithm is optimal for basic blocks — the emitted code shape is as good as any other. - [ ] **Theoretical minimum cost** = (sum of operand uses) + (sum of definitions that need STOREs) + (number of arithmetic ops). - [ ] Always **show descriptor tables** after each emitted instruction — KTU examiners award marks specifically for this. - [ ] Common trap: `LD R0, b` followed by `ADD R0, c` is a **cheaper** code shape than `LD R0, b; LD R1, c; ADD R0, R1` when the second operand is dead.

topics.title: Boolean and Relational Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Boolean and Relational Operators # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Intermediate Code Generation (ICG)** — the phase of a compiler that translates a source program's parse tree/syntax tree into a lower-level, machine-independent intermediate representation (typically **Three-Address Code / TAC**) — **Boolean Operators** and **Relational Operators** refer to the logical and comparison primitives of the source language that the front-end must faithfully convert into jump-based or value-based intermediate instructions. - **Boolean Operators** (also called *logical operators*): `&&` (AND), `||` (OR), `!` (NOT) — operate on Boolean values/operands and produce Boolean results. - **Relational Operators** (also called *comparison operators*): `<`, `<=`, `==`, `!=`, `>`, `>=` — compare two arithmetic/expression operands and yield a Boolean outcome (`true` / `false`). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 4 – Intermediate Code Generation)**, these operators are the core constructs driving **control-flow translation**, **short-circuit code generation**, and **backpatching-based code emission**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECIT751 — Module 4):** > The KTU module specifically requires students to understand *translation of expressions*, *translation of Boolean expressions*, and *flow-of-control statements*. Boolean and Relational operators are the fundamental building blocks of all three. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Roads" Intersection Imagine you are a **traffic controller at a road intersection (the compiler)**. Cars arrive carrying passengers who want to go to one of **two destinations** (`true` / `false`). Each car (a Boolean/Relational expression) must be inspected to decide which road to take. - **Relational operators** are like the **weighbridge at the entry gate** — they compare two numbers ("Is car A heavier than car B?") and answer *yes* or *no*. - **Boolean operators** are like the **logical filter on the road sign** — "Take the right path **only if** (car is heavy **AND** has a permit) **OR** (is a VIP car)." - **Short-circuit evaluation** is the smart traffic officer who, **once he knows the answer** (e.g., the first condition is `false` in an `AND`), **immediately stops checking the rest** — saving fuel (CPU cycles). The compiler's job is to convert these "verbal rules" into a sequence of **machine-friendly conditional jumps** (like `if condition goto L1`). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions | Convention | Value / Notation | |---|---| | Boolean `true` numerical encoding | **1** (and any non-zero value in C) | | Boolean `false` numerical encoding | **0** | | Standard relational operators | `<, <=, ==, !=, >, >=` (**6 operators**) | | Standard Boolean operators | `&&, ||, !` (**3 operators**) | | Position of TAC in compiler pipeline | Front-end ⟶ Intermediate Code ⟶ Back-end | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Three-Address Code — TAC):** > A form of intermediate code where each instruction contains **at most three addresses** (two source operands + one destination) and exactly **one operator**. Boolean/Relational expressions must be reduced to this canonical form before optimization and target code generation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Strategic Approaches to Boolean Translation The compiler can translate Boolean expressions using **one of two fundamental strategies** (both highly exam-relevant for KTU): ### Strategy A — Numerical Encoding (Value-Oriented) The Boolean expression is **fully evaluated** into a temporary variable that holds `0` (false) or `1` (true). Later, the value is tested with an `if` statement. - **Pros:** Simple, easy to optimize (constant folding), reusable in arithmetic. - **Cons:** **Short-circuit semantics are lost**; all sub-expressions are *always* evaluated (can produce side effects, division by zero, etc.). ### Strategy B — Flow-of-Control / Jump Encoding (Short-Circuit) Each relational/Boolean sub-expression generates a **conditional jump**. Control is transferred directly to the true/false labels without ever materializing a Boolean value. - **Pros:** Preserves **short-circuit semantics**; efficient; required for correct translation of `&&` and `||`. - **Cons:** More complex; uses more labels. > [!NOTE] > **Why Short-Circuit Matters:** > In `if (x != 0 && y/x > 10) ...`, the second condition `y/x` must **not** be evaluated when `x == 0` (would cause runtime divide-by-zero). Jump encoding guarantees this; numerical encoding does not. ## 2.2 Translation Rules for Boolean Operators (Jump Style) | Source Construct | TAC Translation Pattern | |---|---| | `E1 && E2` | Evaluate `E1`. If **false**, jump to *false-label*. Evaluate `E2`. If **false**, jump to *false-label*. Fall through to *true-label*. | | `E1 \|\| E2` | Evaluate `E1`. If **true**, jump to *true-label*. Evaluate `E2`. If **true**, jump to *true-label*. Fall through to *false-label*. | | `!E` | Evaluate `E`. If **true**, jump to *false-label*. Fall through to *true-label*. | ## 2.3 Translation Rules for Relational Operators A relational expression like `a < b` is typically translated to: $$ \text{if } a < b \text{ goto } L_{true} \quad \text{else fall through to } L_{false} $$ In the *numerical* style, the compiler emits a comparison and a conditional move / set instruction: $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a < b \quad \text{// } t_1 \in \{0, 1\} \end{aligned} $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Rule / Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Boolean `true` | `1` (or any non-zero integer) | C-style encoding | | 2 | Boolean `false` | `0` | Universal encoding | | 3 | AND (`&&`) jump pattern | `if E1 goto L1; if E2 goto L1; goto L2` | L1 = true-label, L2 = false-label | | 4 | OR (`\|\|`) jump pattern | `if E1 goto L2; if E2 goto L2; goto L1` | L2 = true-label, L1 = false-label | | 5 | NOT (`!`) jump pattern | `if E goto L1; goto L2` | L1 = false-label, L2 = true-label | | 6 | Relational (`<, <=, >, >=, ==, !=`) | `if a relop b goto L_true` | Six operators — all translate to conditional jumps | | 7 | Number of instructions (worst case) | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for $n$ Boolean operators | Linear in expression size | | 8 | Backpatch list merge (OR) | `merge(p1.falselist, p2.falselist)` | Aho-Sethi-Ullman algorithm | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL: Markdown Table Pipe Rule Followed** — All absolute-value bars in formulas use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) instead of literal `\|` to prevent table-format corruption. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compiler optimization passes** (constant folding, dead-code elimination) operate on the jump-style TAC to identify unreachable basic blocks. - **Static analyzers** (e.g., for security or null-pointer analysis) use the jump graph to trace data flow. - **JIT compilers** (LLVM, HotSpot C2) directly consume TAC and lower relational/Boolean jumps into native **CMP + Jcc** x86 instructions. - **Hardware synthesis** (High-Level Synthesis / HLS) translates these Boolean/Relational TAC patterns into VHDL/Verilog `if-else` and comparator constructs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Numerical Translation of `if (a < b) then S1 else S2` > **Source Program:** > ```c > if (a < b) then S1 else S2; > ``` ### Step-by-Step Model Solution **Step 1.** Translate the relational expression `a < b` to TAC. $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a < b \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* A single TAC instruction is emitted that stores the Boolean result (1 or 0) of the relational operator `<` into the temporary variable `t1`. **Step 2.** Emit a conditional jump testing the Boolean value. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } t_1 = 0 \text{ goto } L_{else} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* If the value in `t1` is 0 (false), control must transfer to the `else` block. **Step 3.** Emit TAC for `S1` (the true branch). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(S1 instructions)} \quad & \text{// produced recursively by the S1 translator} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4.** Emit an unconditional jump to skip the `else` block. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{goto } L_{after} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* This prevents fall-through into the `else` block after `S1` executes. **Step 5.** Emit the label for the `else` branch followed by TAC for `S2`. $$ \begin{aligned} L_{else}: \quad \text{(S2 instructions)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6.** Emit the final exit label. $$ \begin{aligned} L_{after}: \end{aligned} $$ ### Complete Final TAC Sequence $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a < b \\ \text{if } t_1 = 0 \text{ goto } L_{else} \quad & \text{// short-form: if } a < b \text{ goto } L_{then} \\ L_{then}: \quad \text{[S1 TAC]} \\ \text{goto } L_{after} \\ L_{else}: \quad \text{[S2 TAC]} \\ L_{after}: \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Short-Circuit Translation of `if (A || (B && C)) then S1 else S2` > Let $A$ represent the Boolean sub-expression `x < y`, $B$ represent `u < v`, and $C$ represent `w < z`. ### Step-by-Step Model Solution (Aho-Sethi-Ullman Jump Style) **Step 1.** Emit TAC for relational sub-expression $A \equiv (x < y)$. If true, jump directly to the **true label** `L_true` (no need to evaluate $B$ or $C$). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } x < y \text{ goto } L_{true} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* Short-circuit OR — as soon as $A$ is true, the whole expression is true. **Step 2.** Evaluate $B \equiv (u < v)$. If true, the overall expression is true → jump to $L_{true}$. Otherwise, control falls through to evaluate $C$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } u < v \text{ goto } L_{true} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3.** Evaluate $C \equiv (w < z)$. If true, jump to $L_{true}$; if false, the whole Boolean expression is false → jump to $L_{false}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } w < z \text{ goto } L_{true} \\ \text{goto } L_{false} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* The `goto L_false` is emitted because both $A$ and $B$ failed AND $C$ failed. **Step 4.** Attach labels for `S1` (true) and `S2` (false) branches. $$ \begin{aligned} L_{true}: \quad \text{[S1 TAC]} \\ \text{goto } L_{after} \\ L_{false}: \quad \text{[S2 TAC]} \\ L_{after}: \end{aligned} $$ ### Final Short-Circuit TAC $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } x < y \text{ goto } L_{true} \\ \text{if } u < v \text{ goto } L_{true} \\ \text{if } w < z \text{ goto } L_{true} \\ \text{goto } L_{false} \\ L_{true}: \quad \text{[S1 TAC]} \\ \text{goto } L_{after} \\ L_{false}: \quad \text{[S2 TAC]} \\ L_{after}: \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Backpatching Formulation (Synthesis) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Backpatching (Aho/Sethi/Ullman):** A technique where the compiler generates TAC instructions with **incomplete jump targets** (placeholders), recording the indices of those instructions on a *list* (e.g., `truelist`, `falselist`).
