Once the target label is known, all instructions in the list are *patched* (filled in) using a `backpatch(list, label)` utility. ### Two Key Functions $$ \begin{aligned} \text{backpatch}(p.truelist, \; L_{true}) \quad & \text{— fills in true-targets} \\ \text{backpatch}(p.falselist, \; L_{false}) \quad & \text{— fills in false-targets} \end{aligned} $$ ### Merge Function for Lists $$ \begin{aligned} \text{merge}(p_1, p_2) \quad \equiv \quad \text{concatenate lists } p_1 \text{ and } p_2 \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cost & Behaviour | Translation Method | # Instructions (avg) | Preserves Short-Circuit? | Allows Constant Folding? | Backpatching Required? | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Numerical Encoding** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | **No** | **Yes** | No | Arithmetic-heavy languages, simple expressions | | **Short-Circuit / Jump Style** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ to $\mathcal{O}(2n)$ | **Yes** | **Partial** | **Yes** | C, Java, C++ `&&` and `\|\|` | | **Backpatching-based Jump Style** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | **Yes** | **Yes** (after patching) | **Yes** (single-pass generation) | Production compilers (GCC, LLVM front-end) | Where $n$ = number of Boolean/Relational operators in the expression. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Boolean/Relational Translation The following flow diagram captures the **functional architecture** of a Boolean/Relational expression translator inside the compiler's intermediate code generator. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Parse Tree Node
Boolean or Relational Expression] --> B{Is it a
Relational Operator?} B -- Yes --> C[Emit Comparison TAC
if a relop b goto Ltrue] C --> D[Add instr to truelist or falselist] B -- No --> E{Is it AND?} E -- Yes --> F[Evaluate Left Operand] F --> G[Evaluate Right Operand] G --> H[backpatch left.truelist with right start label] H --> I[Result.truelist = right.truelist] I --> J[Result.falselist = merge left.falselist with right.falselist] E -- No --> K{Is it OR?} K -- Yes --> L[Evaluate Left Operand] L --> M[Evaluate Right Operand] M --> N[backpatch left.falselist with right start label] N --> O[Result.falselist = right.falselist] O --> P[Result.truelist = merge left.truelist with right.truelist] K -- No --> Q{Is it NOT?} Q -- Yes --> R[Evaluate Operand] R --> S[Result.truelist = operand.falselist] S --> T[Result.falselist = operand.truelist] D --> U[Return Translation to Parent] J --> U P --> U T --> U U --> V[Continue TAC Generation
for enclosing construct] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Short-Circuit Control Flow Trace ```mermaid flowchart LR node1([Start: Evaluate A]) --> node2{A: x less than y} node2 -- True --> node3([Jump to Ltrue
S1 executes]) node2 -- False --> node4{B: u less than v} node4 -- True --> node3 node4 -- False --> node5{C: w less than z} node5 -- True --> node3 node5 -- False --> node6([Jump to Lfalse
S2 executes]) node3 --> node7([End of if-then-else]) node6 --> node7 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — TAC Instruction Layout (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TD inst1["if x less than y goto Ltrue"] --> inst2["if u less than v goto Ltrue"] inst2 --> inst3["if w less than z goto Ltrue"] inst3 --> inst4["goto Lfalse"] inst4 --> inst5["Ltrue: S1 TAC instructions"] inst5 --> inst6["goto Lafter"] inst6 --> inst7["Lfalse: S2 TAC instructions"] inst7 --> inst8["Lafter: continue program"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safeguards Followed:** All node identifiers are purely alphanumeric and prefix-lettered (e.g., `node1`, `inst1`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `style`) are used as bare node IDs. All labels inside double quotes are free of bold/italic markers or HTML tags. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** *What are the two methods used by a compiler to translate Boolean expressions? Briefly explain each.* #### Model Answer (3 Marks): The two methods are: 1. **Numerical Encoding (Value-Oriented Method):** The Boolean expression is evaluated *completely* and the result (0 or 1) is stored in a temporary variable. The `if` statement later tests this numerical value. *Disadvantage:* short-circuit semantics are lost; all sub-expressions are always evaluated. 2. **Flow-of-Control / Short-Circuit Method (Jump Style):** Each relational/Boolean sub-expression is translated into a **conditional jump** (`if a < b goto L_true`). Control is transferred immediately upon knowing the result, preserving short-circuit semantics. This is the standard production-compiler technique. *(1 mark — naming both; 1 mark — first method; 1 mark — second method with short-circuit emphasis.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Why must the compiler preserve short-circuit semantics when translating `&&` and `||`? Give one example.* #### Model Answer (3 Marks): The compiler must preserve short-circuit semantics because the source language guarantees that the **right-hand operand is evaluated only if necessary**. This matters for: - **Correctness:** Side effects (e.g., function calls, pointer dereferences) in the right operand must not occur when the left operand already determines the result. - **Safety:** Prevents runtime errors such as divide-by-zero. **Example:** In `if (x != 0 && y/x > 5)`, when `x == 0`, the right operand `y/x` must **not** be evaluated (would cause divide-by-zero). Short-circuit jump-code generation guarantees this; numerical encoding does not. *(1 mark — semantic reason; 1 mark — safety/correctness; 1 mark — example.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** One full question (14 marks) with two sub-parts of 7 marks each, or two questions of 7 marks with internal choice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### (a) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(7 Marks)* *Translate the following `C` code segment into three-address code (TAC) using the **short-circuit (jump) method**:* ```c if ((a < b) || (c < d && e < f)) x = 1; else x = 0; ``` ##### Step-by-Step Model Solution: **Step 1.** Allocate labels: `L_true` (where `x = 1` is placed), `L_false` (where `x = 0` is placed), and `L_after` (continuation). **Step 2.** Translate `a < b` — if true, the whole expression is true (short-circuit OR), so jump to `L_true`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } a < b \text{ goto } L_{true} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3.** Translate `c < d` — if false, the entire `AND` sub-expression is false, so jump to `L_false`. (If true, evaluate the second operand of AND.) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } c < d \text{ goto } L_{next} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4.** Translate `e < f` — if true, the entire Boolean expression is true, so jump to `L_true`. $$ \begin{aligned} L_{next}: \quad \text{if } e < f \text{ goto } L_{true} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5.** If neither relational succeeded, the expression is false — jump to `L_false`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{goto } L_{false} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6.** Emit TAC for true and false branches. $$ \begin{aligned} L_{true}: \quad x = 1 \\ \text{goto } L_{after} \\ L_{false}: \quad x = 0 \\ L_{after}: \end{aligned} $$ **Final Complete TAC Sequence:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } a < b \text{ goto } L_{true} \\ \text{if } c < d \text{ goto } L_{next} \\ L_{next}: \quad \text{if } e < f \text{ goto } L_{true} \\ \text{goto } L_{false} \\ L_{true}: \quad x = 1 \\ \text{goto } L_{after} \\ L_{false}: \quad x = 0 \\ L_{after}: \end{aligned} $$ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Correct label allocation — 1 mark; correct translation of first relational — 1 mark; correct AND short-circuit — 2 marks; correct OR short-circuit — 1 mark; correct true/false branch TAC — 1 mark; final label and presentation — 1 mark. #### (b) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(7 Marks)* *Explain the **backpatching technique** for translating Boolean expressions. How does it differ from the multi-pass approach?* ##### Step-by-Step Model Solution: **Definition (2 marks):** Backpatching is a *single-pass* code-generation technique where jump instructions whose target labels are not yet known are emitted with **placeholders** (e.g., empty jump fields). The indices of such incomplete instructions are maintained in **lists** (typically `truelist` and `falselist`). When the target label is finally defined, the `backpatch(list, label)` routine walks the list and fills in the target. **Key Lists Maintained:** - `truelist` — list of instruction indices that must jump to the *true* label. - `falselist` — list of instruction indices that must jump to the *false* label. **Translation Rules (3 marks):** - For $E \to E_1 \; \&\& \; E_2$: backpatch `E1.truelist` with the label of the *start* of $E_2$'s code. The result's truelist = $E_2.truelist$, falselist = `merge(E1.falselist, E2.falselist)`. - For $E \to E_1 \; \mid\mid \; E_2$: backpatch `E1.falselist` with the label of the *start* of $E_2$'s code. The result's truelist = `merge(E1.truelist, E2.truelist)`, falselist = $E_2.falselist$. - For $E \to \text{relop}$: emit `if ... goto _` and append its index to `truelist`; emit `goto _` and append to `falselist`. **Difference from Multi-Pass (2 marks):** Multi-pass approaches first generate TAC with symbolic labels and then perform a second pass to resolve them. Backpatching collapses this into a **single left-to-right pass**, with placeholder resolution deferred only until the actual target label is encountered. This reduces compilation time and is well-suited for one-pass compilers. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative to Question A* #### (a) **[KTU University Exam – June 2022]** *(7 Marks)* *Generate three-address code for the following expression using the **numerical encoding method**:* ```c if (a + b < c * d && e == f) goto L1; ``` ##### Step-by-Step Model Solution: **Step 1.** Compute sub-expressions into temporaries. $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a + b \\ t_2 &= c * d \\ t_3 &= e == f \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2.** Compute the two relational results. $$ \begin{aligned} t_4 &= t_1 < t_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3.** Apply Boolean AND to obtain the final Boolean value. $$ \begin{aligned} t_5 &= t_4 \,\&\, t_3 \quad \text{// bitwise AND since both are 0/1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4.** Test the Boolean value and jump. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } t_5 = 1 \text{ goto } L_1 \end{aligned} $$ **Final TAC:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a + b \\ t_2 &= c * d \\ t_3 &= e == f \\ t_4 &= t_1 < t_2 \\ t_5 &= t_4 \,\&\, t_3 \\ \text{if } t_5 = 1 \text{ goto } L_1 \end{aligned} $$ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Sub-expression evaluation — 2 marks; relational computation — 1 mark; Boolean AND logic — 1 mark; conditional jump — 1 mark; complete & correct sequence — 2 marks. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > ⚠️ In numerical encoding, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by: (i) using `&&` directly inside a single TAC instruction (illegal — TAC allows only one operator per instruction); (ii) using the wrong Boolean encoding (`true` = 1, `false` = 0 must be explicitly handled); (iii) forgetting to test the final Boolean value with an `if` jump. Always decompose into temporaries! #### (b) **[KTU University Exam – June 2022]** *(7 Marks)* *Construct the **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** and corresponding **three-address code** for the Boolean expression:* $$ E \;=\; (a < b) \; \&\& \; (c < d) \; \mid\mid \; (e < f) $$ ##### Step-by-Step Model Solution: **Step 1.** Identify the three relational sub-expressions and the Boolean structure. - $R_1 = a < b$ - $R_2 = c < d$ - $R_3 = e < f$ - The Boolean structure is: $(R_1 \;\&\&\; R_2) \; \mid\mid \; R_3$ **Step 2.** Using the **short-circuit jump method**, emit TAC. **Step 3.** Translate $R_1$: if true, evaluate $R_2$ (because of AND). If false, jump to false-label of the AND, which is the false-label of the overall OR — i.e., jump to $L_{overall\_false}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } a < b \text{ goto } L_1 \quad & \text{// evaluate AND's second operand} \\ \text{goto } L_{overall\_false} \quad & \text{// short-circuit: AND is false} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4.** At $L_1$, evaluate $R_2$: if true, the AND is true → the OR is true → jump to $L_{overall\_true}$. If false, the AND is false → the OR needs to evaluate $R_3$. $$ \begin{aligned} L_1: \quad \text{if } c < d \text{ goto } L_{overall\_true} \\ \text{goto } L_2 \quad & \text{// fall through to evaluate R3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5.** At $L_2$, evaluate $R_3$: if true → $L_{overall\_true}$; else → $L_{overall\_false}$. $$ \begin{aligned} L_2: \quad \text{if } e < f \text{ goto } L_{overall\_true} \\ \text{goto } L_{overall\_false} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6.** Define the target labels. $$ \begin{aligned} L_{overall\_true}: \quad \text{[TRUE branch TAC]} \\ \text{goto } L_{after} \\ L_{overall\_false}: \quad \text{[FALSE branch TAC]} \\ L_{after}: \end{aligned} $$ **Final TAC:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } a < b \text{ goto } L_1 \\ \text{goto } L_{overall\_false} \\ L_1: \quad \text{if } c < d \text{ goto } L_{overall\_true} \\ \text{goto } L_2 \\ L_2: \quad \text{if } e < f \text{ goto } L_{overall\_true} \\ \text{goto } L_{overall\_false} \\ L_{overall\_true}: \quad \text{[TRUE]} \\ \text{goto } L_{after} \\ L_{overall\_false}: \quad \text{[FALSE]} \\ L_{after}: \end{aligned} $$ **DAG Sketch (textual representation):** | Node | Children | Operation | |---|---|---| | $n_1$ | $a, b$ | $<$ | | $n_2$ | $c, d$ | $<$ | | $n_3$ | $e, f$ | $<$ | | $n_4$ | $n_1, n_2$ | $\&\&$ | | $n_5$ | $n_4, n_3$ | $\mid\mid$ | *Root node = $n_5$ representing the entire Boolean expression.* **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Boolean decomposition — 1 mark; correct AND short-circuit — 2 marks; correct OR short-circuit — 1 mark; complete TAC sequence with labels — 2 marks; DAG construction — 1 mark. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > ⚠️ **Most common error:** Students confuse the direction of short-circuit jumps. **Remember** — in `A && B`, the *false* branch dominates; in `A || B`, the *true* branch dominates. Getting these reversed causes full mark deduction on both sub-parts. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Boolean Operators** in source code: `&&` (AND), `||` (OR), `!` (NOT) — must be translated to short-circuit jump code to preserve language semantics. - ✅ **Relational Operators**: exactly **six** standard ones — `<, <=, ==, !=, >, >=` — all translate to a single `if a relop b goto L` instruction. - ✅ **Numerical Encoding** stores Boolean result in a temporary as `0` (false) or `1` (true); loses short-circuit semantics. - ✅ **Short-Circuit / Jump Encoding** uses conditional jumps and is the production-grade method. - ✅ **Three-Address Code (TAC)** constraint: maximum **one operator per instruction**, with at most **three addresses** (two sources + one destination). - ✅ **Backpatching** = single-pass technique using `truelist`, `falselist`, `merge()`, and `backpatch()` to defer jump-target resolution. - ✅ **AND short-circuit rule:** if $E_1$ is false, skip $E_2$ — backpatch $E_1.\text{falselist}$ with start of $E_2$; result's falselist = `merge($E_1$.falselist, $E_2$.falselist)`. - ✅ **OR short-circuit rule:** if $E_1$ is true, skip $E_2$ — backpatch $E_1.\text{truelist}$ with start of $E_2$; result's truelist = `merge($E_1$.truelist, $E_2$.truelist)`. - ✅ **NOT rule:** truelist and falselist are *swapped*. - ✅ Always allocate **three labels** per `if-then-else`: `L_true`, `L_false`, `L_after`. - ✅ **Why relational operators matter:** they are the *primitives* of every control-flow decision and form the bridge between arithmetic expressions and Boolean logic. - ✅ **Exam trap:** TAC allows only ONE operator per instruction — never combine `<` and `&&` into a single line. - ✅ **Production compilers** (GCC, LLVM) use backpatching-based short-circuit translation as the default.

topics.title: Control-Flow Constructs - Conditional Execution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control-Flow Constructs - Conditional Execution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control-Flow Constructs – Conditional Execution** in compiler design refers to the systematic translation of high-level conditional statements (such as `if-then`, `if-then-else`, `while`, `for`, `switch-case`, and Boolean expressions) into **Three-Address Code (TAC)** using a combination of **conditional jumps** and **unconditional jumps**. The compiler must accurately capture the program's control flow by emitting jump instructions whose targets are either fixed labels or placeholders to be filled later (via backpatching).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic falls under **Intermediate Code Generation** and is fundamental to producing a **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** — the backbone of later optimizations and target code generation.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Three-Address Code (TAC)** | An intermediate representation where each instruction has at most three operands |
| **Conditional Jump** | An instruction that transfers control based on a Boolean condition (e.g., `if x < y goto L1`) |
| **Unconditional Jump** | An instruction that always transfers control (e.g., `goto L1`) |
| **Backpatching** | A two-pass technique to fill in jump targets after labels are known |
| **Short-Circuit Evaluation** | Skipping the evaluation of the right operand when the result is determined by the left |
| **Label** | A symbolic name for an instruction address |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Signal Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Railway Signal Box:** Imagine a railway signal operator (the compiler) who must guide a train (program execution) through a junction. Conditional execution is like a set of track switches: the operator checks a condition ("Is the express line clear?") and flips the switch accordingly. If the condition is **true**, the train moves to Track A; if **false**, it moves to Track B. The **labels** in the code are the destination stations, and **backpatching** is like the operator waiting for the station master to confirm the platform number before writing it on the signal — the signal (jump) is prepared in advance, but the final destination is filled in only when the layout is fully known.

This analogy helps us understand that the compiler must **defer** certain decisions (the jump target) until all parts of the program have been seen — exactly the motivation behind **backpatching**.

### 1.4 Why Conditional Execution Matters in Compilers

- **Correctness:** Conditional jumps are the only mechanism by which branching logic survives translation to linear intermediate code.
- **Optimization:** The CFG built from these jumps is the substrate for dead-code elimination, loop optimization, and constant folding.
- **Target Code Generation:** The backend uses the jump pattern to emit compare-and-branch instructions (e.g., `CMP`, `JNE`, `JE` in x86).
- **Static Analysis:** Tools for verification, profiling, and security analysis all rely on accurate control-flow reconstruction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Forms of Conditional Translation

A conditional statement in a high-level language can be expressed in TAC using one of two fundamental patterns:

#### Pattern A — **Direct Conditional Jump** (for `if` without else)

For a statement `if (E) S` where `E` is a Boolean expression and `S` is a statement:

1. Evaluate `E` to a Boolean result.
2. Emit `if False E goto L_after`
3. Translate `S`
4. Label `L_after:`

#### Pattern B — **Jump-to-Else Translation** (for `if-then-else`)

For `if (E) S1 else S2`:

1. Evaluate `E`
2. Emit `if False E goto L_else`
3. Translate `S1`
4. Emit `goto L_after`
5. Label `L_else:`
6. Translate `S2`
7. Label `L_after:`

### 2.2 Boolean Expression Translation

Boolean expressions built from relational operators (`<`, `=`, `>`, etc.) connected by logical AND (`&&`), OR (`||`), and NOT (`!`) require special handling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Evaluation Strategies:**
> - **Non-Short-Circuit (Complete):** Evaluate every subexpression, then combine. Simpler but may produce extra code.
> - **Short-Circuit:** As soon as the outcome is determined, skip remaining subexpressions. Preferred for efficiency and side-effect preservation.

**Example of Short-Circuit Translation of `A && B`:**

| Step | TAC Instruction | Comment |
|------|----------------|---------|
| 1 | `if False A goto L_false` | If A is false, skip B |
| 2 | `if False B goto L_false` | If B is false, result is false |
| 3 | `t1 = 1` | True path |
| 4 | `goto L_next` | Skip false path |
| 5 | `L_false: t1 = 0` | False path |
| 6 | `L_next:` | Continue |

### 2.3 The Backpatching Technique

**Motivation:** In a single-pass translation, jump targets are often not yet known when a jump is emitted. Backpatching solves this by using **lists of pending jumps** and filling in targets after labels are created.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Key Lists Maintained by the Compiler:**
> - **`true_list`** — jumps to be patched when the Boolean expression evaluates to `true`
> - **`false_list`** — jumps to be patched when the Boolean expression evaluates to `false`

**Algorithm (Synthesized Attributes `B.truelist` and `B.falselist`):**

1. For `B → B1 || B2`:
   - Patch `B1.falselist` to point to the start of `B2`'s code.
   - `B.truelist = merge(B1.truelist, B2.truelist)`
   - `B.falselist = B2.falselist`

2. For `B → B1 && B2`:
   - Patch `B1.truelist` to point to the start of `B2`'s code.
   - `B.truelist = B2.truelist`
   - `B.falselist = merge(B1.falselist, B2.falselist)`

3. For `B → !B1`:
   - `B.truelist = B1.falselist`
   - `B.falselist = B1.truelist`

4. For `B → E1 rel E2`:
   - Generate `if E1 rel E2 goto _` and append to `B.truelist`
   - Generate `goto _` and append to `B.falselist`

5. For `S → if (B) S1`:
   - `S.nextlist = merge(B.falselist, S1.nextlist)`

6. For `S → if (B) S1 else S2`:
   - Backpatch `B.truelist` to start of `S1`
   - Backpatch `B.falselist` to start of `S2`
   - `S.nextlist = merge(S1.nextlist, S2.nextlist)`

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Use Case |
|---------|----------------|----------|
| Number of TAC instructions for `if-then-else` | **n + 4** (n = TAC for condition) | Code size estimation |
| Number of TAC instructions for `if-then` | **n + 2** | Code size estimation |
| Number of new labels in backpatching | **⌈k/1⌉ where k = Boolean operators** | Label allocation |
| Short-circuit evaluation saves | **1 + (avg short-circuit savings)** | Optimization metric |
| Control flow edges added by `if-then-else` | **+2 edges** in CFG | CFG construction |
| CFG node count for `if-then-else` | **+2 basic blocks** | CFG analysis |
| Time complexity of backpatching | **O(n)** linear | Complexity analysis |
| Label creation pattern | `L1: , L2: , L3: ...` generated sequentially | Label generator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on syntax:** All math notations have been written with LaTeX-style vertical bars via `\vert` rather than `|` to ensure clean rendering in markdown tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilers** (e.g., LLVM, HotSpot) heavily use conditional jump translation for speculative execution.
- **Static Analyzers** (e.g., Coverity, SonarQube) reconstruct control flow to detect unreachable code.
- **Hardware Synthesis** (HDL compilers) translate high-level `if` into hardware muxes and conditional clock gating.
- **Cybersecurity:** Control-flow integrity (CFI) defenses rely on accurate CFG reconstruction from TAC.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Translation of `if (a < b || c > d) x = 1; else x = 2;`

We will translate this using **backpatching with synthesized attributes** step by step.

#### Step 1 — Initialize Symbol Table Entries

Let each relational expression and Boolean operation produce TAC instructions with placeholders that we will backpatch.

#### Step 2 — Translation of `B1: a < b`

Generate:

$$
\text{if } a < b \text{ goto } \_\_\_
$$

$$
\text{goto } \_\_\_
$$

Append the first to `B1.truelist`, second to `B1.falselist`.

#### Step 3 — Translation of `B2: c > d`

Generate:

$$
\text{if } c > d \text{ goto } \_\_\_
$$

$$
\text{goto } \_\_\_
$$

Append the first to `B2.truelist`, second to `B2.falselist`.

#### Step 4 — Translation of `B: B1 || B2`

Apply the rule:

$$
B.\text{truelist} = \text{merge}(B1.\text{truelist},\ B2.\text{truelist})
$$

$$
B.\text{falselist} = B2.\text{falselist}
$$

Backpatch `B1.falselist` to point to the **next instruction to be generated** (the start of `B2`).

#### Step 5 — Translation of `S1: x = 1`

Generate:

$$
x = 1
$$

Add an unconditional jump `goto ___` to `S1.nextlist` so we can jump past `S2` to `L_after`.

#### Step 6 — Translation of `S2: x = 2`

Generate:

$$
L\_else: \quad x = 2
$$

#### Step 7 — Final Backpatching

- Backpatch `B.truelist` to `L_then` (start of `S1`)
- Backpatch `B.falselist` to `L_else` (start of `S2`)
- Backpatch `S1.nextlist` to `L_after` (instruction after `S2`)

#### Step 8 — Final Three-Address Code

The fully backpatched TAC is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&100: \quad \text{if } a < b \text{ goto } 103 \quad &\text{(was } B1.\text{truelist, backpatched)} \\
&101: \quad \text{goto } 102 \quad &\text{(B1.falselist, backpatched to } B2 \text{ start)} \\
&102: \quad \text{if } c > d \text{ goto } 103 \quad &\text{(B2.truelist, backpatched)} \\
&103: \quad \text{goto } 104 \quad &\text{(B2.falselist, backpatched to } L\_\text{else)} \\
&104: \quad x = 1 \quad &\text{(} L\_\text{then)} \\
&105: \quad \text{goto } 107 \quad &\text{(S1.nextlist, backpatched to } L\_\text{after)} \\
&106: \quad x = 2 \quad &\text{(} L\_\text{else)} \\
&107: \quad \text{--- end of statement ---} \quad &\text{(} L\_\text{after)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Conditional Constructs and Their TAC Patterns

| Source Construct | TAC Pattern | Number of Jumps |
|------------------|-------------|-----------------|
| `if (E) S` | `if False E goto L_after; S; L_after:` | **1** |
| `if (E) S1 else S2` | `if False E goto L_else; S1; goto L_after; L_else: S2; L_after:` | **2** |
| `while (E) S` | `L_begin: if False E goto L_after; S; goto L_begin; L_after:` | **2** |
| `do S while (E)` | `L_body: S; L_test: if E goto L_body` | **1** |
| `for (init; E; step) S` | `init; L_begin: if False E goto L_after; S; step; goto L_begin; L_after:` | **2** |
| `switch (E) {case v1: S1; ...}` | `t = E; if t != v1 goto L2; S1; goto L_after; L2: if t != v2 goto L3; ...` | **n + 1** for n cases |
| `A && B` | `if False A goto L_false; if False B goto L_false; ...` | **2** |
| `A \|\| B` | `if True A goto L_true; if True B goto L_true; ...` | **2** |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step — Translation of a `while` Loop with Backpatching

Consider: `while (a < 10) { a = a + 1; }`

**Step 1:** Generate label `L_begin`.
**Step 2:** Translate Boolean `a < 10` → produces two jump instructions with backpatch lists.
**Step 3:** The `B.truelist` must be backpatched to the start of the loop body.
**Step 4:** Translate `a = a + 1`.
**Step 5:** Emit `goto L_begin` — append this jump to `S.nextlist`.
**Step 6:** Backpatch `B.falselist` to `L_after` (the label after the loop).
**Step 7:** Backpatch `S.nextlist` (the trailing `goto`) to point to `L_begin` (loop-back).

**Resulting TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&200: \quad L\_begin: \\
&201: \quad \text{if } a < 10 \text{ goto } 203 \quad &\text{(B.truelist, backpatched)} \\
&202: \quad \text{goto } 205 \quad &\text{(B.falselist, backpatched to } L\_\text{after)} \\
&203: \quad t1 = a + 1 \quad &\text{(loop body)} \\
&204: \quad a = t1 \\
&205: \quad \text{goto } 200 \quad &\text{(S.nextlist, backpatched to } L\_\text{begin)} \\
&206: \quad L\_after: \quad \text{--- next statement ---}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Complexity Analysis of Backpatching

- **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ where $n$ is the number of Boolean and jump instructions. Each backpatch operation is a constant-time list traversal and address substitution.
- **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for storing the true/false lists until they are backpatched.
- **Advantage over Multi-Pass:** Backpatching achieves single-pass translation, reducing I/O and memory overhead compared to classical two-pass approaches that scan the code twice.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Control Flow Diagram for `if-then-else`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Entry: Evaluate Condition E] --> B{E is True?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Execute S1 - Then Block]
    C --> D[Jump to L_after]
    B -- No --> E[Execute S2 - Else Block]
    E --> F[Label L_after]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Continue with Next Statement]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Backpatching Translation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lexical Tokens] --> B[Parser: Builds AST]
    B --> C[Semantic Analyzer]
    C --> D[Intermediate Code Generator]
    D --> E[Boolean Expression Handler]
    D --> F[Statement Translator]
    E --> G[TrueList Backpatch Manager]
    E --> H[FalseList Backpatch Manager]
    F --> I[NextList Backpatch Manager]
    G --> J[Label Generator and Allocator]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Final Three-Address Code TAC]
    K --> L[Control Flow Graph Builder]
    L --> M[Optimizer and Code Generator]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Translation of `if (A && B) S1 else S2`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Entry: if Statement] --> EvalA[Evaluate A]
    EvalA -->|A is False| GoFalse1[goto L_false]
    GoFalse1 --> GoFalse2
    EvalA -->|A is True| EvalB[Evaluate B]
    EvalB -->|B is False| GoFalse2[goto L_false]
    GoFalse2 --> ElseBlock[S2: Else Block]
    EvalB -->|B is True| ThenBlock[S1: Then Block]
    ThenBlock --> JumpAfter[goto L_after]
    ElseBlock --> LabelAfter
    JumpAfter --> LabelAfter[Label L_after]
    LabelAfter --> Continue[Continue Program]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Control Flow Graph for `while` Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Init[Initialization] --> Begin[L_begin: Evaluate Condition E]
    Begin -->|True| Body[Loop Body S]
    Body --> Step[Step Increment]
    Step --> Begin
    Begin -->|False| After[L_after: Exit Loop]
    After --> Next[Next Statement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above follow alphanumeric naming (e.g., `EvalA`, `GoFalse1`) and use plain uppercase text inside labels to avoid any markdown rendering conflicts.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Three-Address Code. Why is it preferred for representing conditional control flow during intermediate code generation?**

**Model Answer:**
Three-Address Code (TAC) is an intermediate representation in which each instruction contains at most three operands — typically of the form `x = y op z` for binary operations or `x = op y` for unary operations. It is preferred for conditional control flow because:
1. **Explicit jumps** (both conditional and unconditional) make the control flow directly visible, simplifying CFG construction.
2. **Linear instruction stream** is easy to optimize, analyze, and translate to target machine code.
3. **Labels** can be assigned, backpatched, and manipulated symbolically without committing to actual memory addresses.
4. **Decoupling from source syntax** allows uniform handling of `if`, `while`, `for`, and `switch` constructs.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the difference between short-circuit and non-short-circuit evaluation of Boolean expressions? Show the TAC for both for the expression `A && B`.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Non-Short-Circuit Evaluation:** Both `A` and `B` are always evaluated, and the result is computed by combining the Boolean values (e.g., using `AND` instruction on 0/1 values).
- **Short-Circuit Evaluation:** As soon as the result is determined, evaluation stops. For `A && B`, if `A` is `false`, `B` is never evaluated.

**TAC Comparison for `A && B`:**

| Strategy | TAC |
|----------|-----|
| **Non-Short-Circuit** | `t1 = A; t2 = B; t3 = t1 AND t2` |
| **Short-Circuit** | `if False A goto L_false; if False B goto L_false; t3 = 1; goto L_next; L_false: t3 = 0; L_next:` |

Short-circuit is preferred when subexpressions have side effects or are expensive to compute.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the translation scheme for `if-then-else` and `while` statements using Three-Address Code with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **backpatching technique** for generating code for Boolean expressions. Illustrate with the translation of the expression `if (a < b || c == d && e > f) x = 1; else x = 2;` using synthesized attributes `truelist` and `falselist`. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**`if-then-else` Translation Scheme:**

For a statement `if (E) S1 else S2`, the standard translation scheme is:

1. Evaluate Boolean expression `E`. If `E` is false, jump to the start of `S2`.
2. Translate `S1`.
3. Emit unconditional jump to skip past `S2`.
4. Mark label for `S2`.
5. Translate `S2`.
6. Mark label for continuation.

**Example — `if (x > 0) y = 1; else y = -1;`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{if } x > 0 \text{ goto } L1 \\
&\text{goto } L2 \\
&L1: \quad y = 1 \\
&\text{goto } L3 \\
&L2: \quad y = -1 \\
&L3: \quad \text{--- continue ---}
\end{aligned}
$$

**`while` Translation Scheme:**

For `while (E) S`:

1. Mark label `L_begin` at the start.
2. Evaluate `E`. If `E` is false, jump to `L_after`.
3. Translate `S`.
4. Emit unconditional jump back to `L_begin`.
5. Mark label `L_after`.

**Example — `while (i < n) { sum = sum + a[i]; i++; }`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&L1: \quad \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L2 \\
&\quad\quad \text{goto } L3 \\
&L2: \quad t1 = i \times 4 \\
&\quad\quad t2 = a + t1 \\
&\quad\quad t3 = \text{load } t2 \\
&\quad\quad t4 = \text{sum} + t3 \\
&\quad\quad \text{sum} = t4 \\
&\quad\quad i = i + 1 \\
&\quad\quad \text{goto } L1 \\
&L3: \quad \text{--- end of while ---}
\end{aligned}
$$

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Backpatching Technique Overview:**

Backpatching is a technique used during single-pass code generation to handle forward references in jump instructions. It maintains two lists for each Boolean expression:
- **`truelist`**: list of jumps to be patched when expression evaluates to true
- **`falselist`**: list of jumps to be patched when expression evaluates to false
- **`nextlist`**: list of jumps to be patched at the end of a statement

**Translation of the Given Expression:**

We translate `if (a < b || c == d && e > f) x = 1; else x = 2;` using backpatching.

**Step 1 — Translate `B1: a < b`:**
- Emit: `if a < b goto _` → add to `B1.truelist`
- Emit: `goto _` → add to `B1.falselist`

**Step 2 — Translate `B2: c == d`:**
- Emit: `if c == d goto _` → add to `B2.truelist`
- Emit: `goto _` → add to `B2.falselist`

**Step 3 — Apply AND rule for `B3: B2 && (e > f)`:**
- Translate `e > f`: `if e > f goto _` → `B3_inner.truelist`
- Backpatch `B2.truelist` to point to the first instruction of `e > f` translation
- `B3.truelist = B3_inner.truelist`
- `B3.falselist = merge(B2.falselist, B3_inner.falselist)`

**Step 4 — Apply OR rule for `B: B1 || B3`:**
- Backpatch `B1.falselist` to point to the first instruction of `B3` translation
- `B.truelist = merge(B1.truelist, B3.truelist)`
- `B.falselist = B3.falselist`

**Step 5 — Translate `S1: x = 1`:**
- Emit: `x = 1`
- Emit: `goto _` → add to `S1.nextlist`

**Step 6 — Translate `S2: x = 2` and `L_after`:**
- Mark `L_else` label, emit: `x = 2`
- Mark `L_after` label

**Step 7 — Final Backpatching:**
- Backpatch `B.truelist` to `L_then` (start of `S1`)
- Backpatch `B.falselist` to `L_else`
- Backpatch `S1.nextlist` to `L_after`

**Final Backpatched TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&100: \quad \text{if } a < b \text{ goto } 110 \quad &\text{(B1.truelist backpatched)} \\
&101: \quad \text{goto } 102 \quad &\text{(B1.falselist backpatched to B3 start)} \\
&102: \quad \text{if } c == d \text{ goto } 103 \quad &\text{(B2.truelist backpatched to e > f)} \\
&103: \quad \text{if } e > f \text{ goto } 110 \quad &\text{(B3.truelist backpatched)} \\
&104: \quad \text{goto } 105 \quad &\text{(B3.falselist backpatched to L_else)} \\
&110: \quad x = 1 \quad &\text{(L_then)} \\
&111: \quad \text{goto } 112 \quad &\text{(S1.nextlist backpatched to L_after)} \\
&105: \quad x = 2 \quad &\text{(L_else)} \\
&112: \quad \text{--- continue ---} \quad &\text{(L_after)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the translation of the `switch` (or `case`) statement into Three-Address Code. What are the advantages and disadvantages of using a jump table versus a sequence of `if-else` comparisons? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Translate the following code segment into Three-Address Code, showing each step clearly:

```c
int i = 0;
int sum = 0;
while (i < 10) {
    if (i % 2 == 0)
        sum = sum + i;
    i = i + 1;
}
```

**(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Translation of `switch` Statement:**

A `switch` statement tests an expression against a list of constant values and transfers control to the matching case. Translation strategies include:

1. **Sequence of `if-else`:** Each case is tested with a conditional jump.
2. **Jump Table:** A table indexed by case value is constructed at translation time; the code computes the case value, checks range, and indirectly jumps to the appropriate case label.

**Diagram — Jump Table Translation:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Evaluate Switch Expression E into t] --> B{Is t in Range min to max?}
    B -- No --> C[goto Default Case]
    B -- Yes --> D[Lookup JumpTable t - min]
    D --> E[Indirect Jump to Selected Case]
    C --> F[L_after Label]
    E --> F
```

**Sequence-of-If-Else Translation for `switch (E) { case 1: S1; case 2: S2; case 3: S3; }`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&t = E \\
&\text{if } t \neq 1 \text{ goto } L2 \\
&\text{--- S1 ---} \\
&\text{goto } L\_\text{after} \\
&L2: \quad \text{if } t \neq 2 \text{ goto } L3 \\
&\text{--- S2 ---} \\
&\text{goto } L\_\text{after} \\
&L3: \quad \text{if } t \neq 3 \text{ goto } L\_\text{default} \\
&\text{--- S3 ---} \\
&\text{goto } L\_\text{after} \\
&L\_\text{default}: \quad \text{--- default ---} \\
&L\_\text{after}:
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Strategy | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Best For |
|----------|----------------|------------------|----------|
| **Sequence of if-else** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ where $n$ = number of cases | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ jump instructions | Sparse, non-contiguous case values |
| **Jump Table** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ for the jump | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for the table | Dense, contiguous case values |

**Advantages of Jump Table:** Constant-time dispatch, smaller code size for many cases.
**Disadvantages:** Requires contiguous case values; extra memory for the table; range check needed.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Source Code:**

```c
int i = 0;
int sum = 0;
while (i < 10) {
    if (i % 2 == 0)
        sum = sum + i;
    i = i + 1;
}
```

**Step 1 — Translate declarations and initializations:**

$$
i = 0
$$

$$
\text{sum} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Translate `while` loop header `L_begin` and condition `i < 10`:**

Mark `L_begin:`, then emit:

$$
\text{if } i < 10 \text{ goto } L\_\text{body}
$$

$$
\text{goto } L\_\text{after}
$$

**Step 3 — Translate `if (i % 2 == 0) sum = sum + i;`:**

Inside the loop body, translate the conditional:

$$
t1 = i \bmod 2
$$

$$
\text{if } t1 \neq 0 \text{ goto } L\_\text{endif}
$$

$$
t2 = \text{sum} + i
$$

$$
\text{sum} = t2
$$

Mark `L_endif:`

**Step 4 — Translate `i = i + 1;`:**

$$
t3 = i + 1
$$

$$
i = t3
$$

**Step 5 — Emit jump back to loop start:**

$$
\text{goto } L\_\text{begin}
$$

**Step 6 — Mark `L_after` for loop exit.**

**Complete Final TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&i &= 0 \\
&\text{sum} &= 0 \\
&L\_\text{begin}: \quad &\text{if } i < 10 \text{ goto } L\_\text{body} \\
&\text{goto } L\_\text{after} \\
&L\_\text{body}: \quad &t1 = i \bmod 2 \\
&\text{if } t1 \neq 0 \text{ goto } L\_\text{endif} \\
&t2 = \text{sum} + i \\
&\text{sum} = t2 \\
&L\_\text{endif}: \quad &t3 = i + 1 \\
&i = t3 \\
&\text{goto } L\_\text{begin} \\
&L\_\text{after}: \quad &\text{--- end of while ---}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the unconditional jump after `S1` in `if-then-else`** — Many students forget to emit `goto L_after` after translating `S1`, causing control to fall through into `S2`. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Confusing `truelist` and `falselist` for `&&` and `||`** — For `&&`, patch `B1.truelist`; for `||`, patch `B1.falselist`. Reversing them yields completely wrong code.
> 3. **Forgetting backpatching of `S.nextlist`** in `while` and `if` statements — The trailing `goto` must be backpatched to the loop entry label, not left dangling.
> 4. **Wrong operator in short-circuit translation** — Use `if False A goto` for `&&` and `if True A goto` for `||`. Mixing them up is a **fatal logical error**.
> 5. **Omitting label declarations** — In KTU, each label must be clearly marked with a colon and a unique name. Omitting labels loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> 6. **Switch case without `goto L_after` after each case** — Causes fall-through that may be unintended.
> 7. **Modulo and division operations** — KTU expects the TAC `i % 2` to be expanded into `t1 = i MOD 2` or equivalent explicit three-address form, not left as a high-level expression.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Control-Flow Constructs: Conditional Execution**

- **Three-Address Code (TAC)** uses at most three operands per instruction and is the standard intermediate form for control flow.
- **Conditional jumps** like `if x relop y goto L` are emitted for relational and Boolean expressions.
- **`if-then`** requires **1 conditional jump** plus the body; **`if-then-else`** requires **2 jumps** (one conditional, one unconditional).
- **`while` loop** translates to: `L_begin: if False E goto L_after; body; goto L_begin; L_after:`.
- **Backpatching** is a single-pass technique that defers the resolution of jump targets using `truelist`, `falselist`, and `nextlist`.
- **Short-circuit evaluation** of Boolean expressions avoids unnecessary subexpression evaluation and preserves side effects.
- **Switch statements** can be translated as a sequence of `if-else` (sparse cases) or a **jump table** (dense, contiguous cases).
- **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** is the direct product of conditional translation and is essential for optimization and analysis.
- **Time and space complexity** of backpatching is $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — linear in the number of instructions.
- **For `&&`:** patch `B1.truelist`; `B.truelist = B2.truelist`; `B.falselist = merge(B1.falselist, B2.falselist)`.
- **For `||`:** patch `B1.falselist`; `B.truelist = merge(B1.truelist, B2.truelist)`; `B.falselist = B2.falselist`.
- **For `!`:** simply swap `truelist` and `falselist`.
- **Labels** in TAC must be unique, sequentially numbered or named, and backpatched before final code emission.
- **Common KTU 14-mark question pattern:** translate a complex Boolean expression with mixed `&&` and `||` using backpatching, showing all `truelist`/`falselist` manipulations.
- **Valuation tip:** Always show intermediate steps of backpatching — KTU examiners award marks for the process, not just the final TAC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loops and Iteration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Loops and Iteration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Loops in Compiler Design Context

In the context of **Compiler Design (PECIT751)**, the term **"Loops"** does **not** refer to programming constructs in a high-level language (e.g., `for`, `while`). Instead, it refers to the compiler's internal representation of iterative program structures within a **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** for the purpose of **code optimization**.

A **loop** in a compiler is formally defined as a strongly connected region of a flow graph where execution may repeat a sequence of instructions multiple times. The compiler identifies, classifies, and transforms these regions to improve **runtime efficiency, register usage, and memory access patterns**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A loop is a set of nodes in a control flow graph $G = (N, E, n_0)$ such that for every node in the loop, there exists a path from the loop header back to itself, making it a region of strongly connected components dominated by a single entry node (the loop header).

### 1.2 Iteration — The Execution Repetition Mechanism

**Iteration** in the compiler's optimization domain refers to:
- The **count of executions** of statements inside a loop body.
- The **frequency of variable updates** per loop traversal.
- The basis for **Frequency Reduction** — the principle that **a statement executed $n$ times inside a loop is $n$ times more expensive** than a statement executed once outside.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Golden Rule (Frequency Reduction Principle):**  
> If a computation is performed inside a loop and its result remains unchanged across iterations, the computation can be moved outside the loop, reducing its execution cost from $O(n)$ to $O(1)$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Conveyor Belt"

Imagine a **factory conveyor belt** in a manufacturing unit:
- The **belt** itself is the **loop** — the same physical path is traversed repeatedly.
- The **worker standing beside the belt** performing the same calculation on every product is the **invariant computation** (e.g., recomputing `sqrt(x)` where `x` doesn't change inside the loop).
- The **factory manager's optimization** is to move this calculation **outside the belt loop** — perform it once and write the result on a board, so the worker simply reads the value.
- The **belt's repeating motion** represents **iteration** — every revolution is a new instance of the same process.

This is precisely what the compiler does with **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)**.

### 1.4 Why Loops Are Critical Optimization Targets

| Reason | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|
| **90/10 Rule** | Programs spend ~90% of execution time in ~10% of code, primarily **loops** |
| **Amplification Effect** | A small inefficiency multiplied by $n$ iterations becomes a large slowdown |
| **Memory Locality** | Loops dominate cache behavior; optimizing them improves **cache hit ratio** |
| **Register Pressure** | Long loops stress the register allocator; optimizations reduce **spill code** |
| **Parallelism** | Loops expose **data parallelism** for vectorization and parallelization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU 2024 Valuation:**  
> - **Time Complexity Reduction** from $O(n \cdot k)$ to $O(n + k)$ (for $n$ iterations, $k$ cost reduction).  
> - **Space Complexity** maintained at $O(1)$ for most loop optimizations.  
> - **Transformation Correctness** — the optimization must preserve program semantics (proved via **Available Expressions Analysis**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Loop Detection Framework — The 3-Stage Pipeline

To optimize a loop, the compiler must first **detect** it. KTU 2024 syllabus expects mastery of the following 3-stage detection pipeline:

**Stage 1: Dominator Computation**
- A node $d$ **dominates** node $n$ if **every** path from the entry node $n_0$ to $n$ must pass through $d$.
- The **immediate dominator** of $n$ is the closest strict dominator of $n$.
- Computed using the iterative dataflow equation:

$$
\text{Dom}(n) = \{n\} \cup \left( \bigcap_{p \in \text{pred}(n)} \text{Dom}(p) \right)
$$

- For the entry node: $\text{Dom}(n_0) = \{n_0\}$.

**Stage 2: Back Edge Identification**
- A back edge is an edge $n \rightarrow h$ where $h$ **dominates** $n$.
- The target $h$ of a back edge becomes the **loop header** (also called the **loop entry**).

**Stage 3: Natural Loop Construction**
- Given a back edge $n \rightarrow h$, the **natural loop** consists of:
  - The header $h$.
  - All nodes that can reach $n$ without going through $h$.
- Formally: $\text{Loop}(h) = \{h\} \cup \{m \mid \exists \text{ path } m \rightsquigarrow n \text{ not passing through } h\}$.

### 2.2 The Five Major Loop Optimization Techniques (KTU 2024 Core Syllabus)

#### 2.2.1 Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)

**Definition:** A statement $s$ inside a loop is **loop-invariant** if all operands of $s$ are either:
- **Constants**, OR
- Defined **outside** the loop, OR
- Have exactly **one definition** inside the loop that is itself loop-invariant.

**Algorithm (4 Steps):**
1. Identify all loop-invariant statements using **reaching definitions** analysis.
2. Mark a statement $s$ as **movable** if it dominates all exits of the loop and all uses of operands of $s$ inside the loop are reached only by the definition inside $s$.
3. Hoist $s$ to a **pre-header** block created explicitly for this purpose.
4. Update the control flow graph.

#### 2.2.2 Strength Reduction (Induction Variable Transformation)

**Definition:** Replace expensive operations (e.g., multiplication) with cheaper ones (e.g., addition) by recognizing **induction variables** — variables whose values form an **arithmetic progression** in each iteration.

**Key Formula:**

$$
x_{i+1} = x_i + c \quad \text{(replaces } x_{i+1} = x_i \cdot c \text{ or similar)}
$$

where $c$ is a **loop-invariant constant**.

#### 2.2.3 Induction Variable Elimination

If a loop has **two or more induction variables**, we can:
- Keep only the **basic induction variable** (the one updated by $i = i + 1$).
- Express other induction variables as **linear functions** of the basic one.
- Replace references to eliminated variables with the linear expression.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$
y_i = a \cdot x_i + b
$$

where $a, b$ are loop-invariant, and $x_i$ is the basic induction variable.

#### 2.2.4 Loop Unrolling

**Definition:** Replicate the loop body $k$ times, reducing the **trip count** from $n$ to $n/k$.

**Trade-off:**

$$
\text{Execution time} = n \cdot T_{\text{body}} \quad \xrightarrow{\text{unroll by } k} \quad \frac{n}{k} \cdot (k \cdot T_{\text{body}}) = n \cdot T_{\text{body}}
$$

The **overhead saved** is the per-iteration cost of **branch + increment + comparison** $\times (n - n/k)$.

#### 2.2.5 Loop Jamming (Fusion)

**Definition:** Combine two adjacent loops with **identical iteration space** into a single loop, reducing:
- **Loop control overhead** by a factor of 2.
- **Cache misses** due to better data locality.

**Example Transformation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before: } & \text{for } (i=0; i<n; i++) \;\; A[i] = B[i]; \\
& \text{for } (i=0; i<n; i++) \;\; C[i] = A[i] + 1; \\
\text{After: } & \text{for } (i=0; i<n; i++) \;\; \{ A[i]=B[i]; \;\; C[i] = A[i]+1; \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dominator Set | $\text{Dom}(n) = \{n\} \cup \bigcap_{p \in \text{pred}(n)} \text{Dom}(p)$ | Loop header detection |
| 2 | Back Edge Condition | $h \in \text{Dom}(n)$ for edge $n \to h$ | Loop identification |
| 3 | Loop Invariant Test | $\text{Operand } x \text{ invariant} \iff x$ defined outside OR constant | LICM applicability |
| 4 | Strength Reduction | $x_{i+1} = x_i + c$ | Replace $x \cdot c$ with additions |
| 5 | Linear Induction Var | $y_i = a \cdot x_i + b$ | IV elimination |
| 6 | Frequency Reduction | $T_{\text{new}} = \frac{T_{\text{old}}}{n}$ for invariant code | Justification of LICM |
| 7 | Unroll Gain | $G = (n-1) \cdot T_{\text{overhead}} \cdot (1 - \frac{1}{k})$ | Compute speedup of unrolling |
| 8 | Dataflow Gen-Kill | $\text{IN}[B] = \bigcup_{P \in \text{pred}(B)} \text{OUT}[P]$ | Reaches analysis for LICM |
| 9 | Available Expression | $AE_{\text{out}}[B] = \text{Gen}[B] \cup (AE_{\text{in}}[B] - \text{Kill}[B])$ | Verify LICM safety |
| 10 | Natural Loop | $\text{Loop}(h) = \{h\} \cup \{m \mid m \rightsquigarrow n,\; h \notin \text{path}\}$ | Loop construction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** All loop optimizations are justified using the **frequency reduction principle**: a computation executed inside a loop runs **$n$ times more often** than outside, so any reduction in per-iteration cost is amplified by $n$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Loop Optimization |
|---|---|
| **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** | Tight inner loops in matrix multiplication, FFT kernels |
| **Embedded Systems** | DSP filters, real-time signal processing |
| **Database Engines** | Query loops over tuples, join algorithms |
| **Machine Learning** | Gradient descent iterations, convolution operations |
| **Graphics** | Vertex/fragment shader inner loops, ray tracing |
| **Compilers (GCC, LLVM)** | Loop unroll, vectorize, LICM passes in `-O2`/`-O3` |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Strength Reduction on a Multiplication Induction

Consider the following loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{for } (i = 1; \;\; i \leq n; \;\; i++) \;\{\\
& \quad x = 4 \cdot i;\\
& \quad \text{use}(x);\\
& \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Basic Induction Variable:**

The variable $i$ is updated by $i_{k+1} = i_k + 1$, with initial value $i_0 = 1$.

**Step 2 — Observe the Dependent Induction Variable:**

The variable $x = 4 \cdot i$ is also an induction variable. It forms the sequence $x_1 = 4, x_2 = 8, x_3 = 12, \ldots$

**Step 3 — Derive the Recurrence for $x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= 4 \cdot i_{k+1} \\
&= 4 \cdot (i_k + 1) \\
&= 4 \cdot i_k + 4 \\
&= x_k + 4
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Substitute the recurrence of $i$ into the definition of $x$, then factor.

**Step 4 — Initialize the Transformed Variable:**

Set $x_0 = 4 \cdot 1 = 4$ (computed once, outside the loop).

**Step 5 — Transformed Loop (strength-reduced):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x = 4;\\
& \text{for } (i = 1; \;\; i \leq n; \;\; i++) \;\{\\
& \quad \text{use}(x);\\
& \quad x = x + 4;\\
& \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Cost Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original cost per iteration} &= T_{\text{multiply}}(4 \cdot i) + T_{\text{increment}}(i++) \\
\text{Transformed cost per iteration} &= T_{\text{add}}(x + 4) + T_{\text{increment}}(i++) \\
\text{Savings per iteration} &= T_{\text{multiply}} - T_{\text{add}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n$ iterations, total savings $= n \cdot (T_{\text{multiply}} - T_{\text{add}})$.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Loop Invariant Code Motion (LICM) Cost Justification

Consider a loop with the invariant computation `x = y + z` where `y` and `z` are defined outside the loop.

**Original Cost (Without LICM):**

$$
T_{\text{original}} = n \cdot (T_{\text{add}} + T_{\text{store}})
$$

where $n$ is the trip count.

**Transformed Cost (With LICM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{hoisted}} &= T_{\text{add}} + T_{\text{store}} + n \cdot 0 \\
&= T_{\text{add}} + T_{\text{store}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Hoisting moves the add and store **outside** the loop. The values of `y` and `z` do not change inside, so the add is performed once. Each iteration simply **reads** the precomputed value.

**Speedup Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{\text{original}}}{T_{\text{hoisted}}} \\
&= \frac{n \cdot (T_{\text{add}} + T_{\text{store}})}{T_{\text{add}} + T_{\text{store}}} \\
&= n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** LICM provides a theoretical **$n$-fold speedup** for purely invariant computations — a massive gain that justifies the KTU 2024 emphasis on this technique.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Loop Unrolling Gain

Let $T_b$ = time of one loop body execution, $T_o$ = per-iteration overhead (branch, increment, compare), $n$ = trip count, $k$ = unroll factor.

**Original Loop Cost:**

$$
T_{\text{orig}} = n \cdot (T_b + T_o)
$$

**Unrolled Loop Cost (assuming $n$ is divisible by $k$):**

$$
T_{\text{unrolled}} = \frac{n}{k} \cdot (k \cdot T_b + T_o) = n \cdot T_b + \frac{n}{k} \cdot T_o
$$

**Net Gain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= T_{\text{orig}} - T_{\text{unrolled}} \\
&= n \cdot T_b + n \cdot T_o - n \cdot T_b - \frac{n}{k} \cdot T_o \\
&= n \cdot T_o \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The body work $n \cdot T_b$ is identical; the saving comes from **fewer overhead executions** — the branch/compare/increment is performed $n/k$ times instead of $n$ times.

**Limiting Case (Infinite Unroll):**

As $k \to n$, $G \to n \cdot T_o \cdot (1 - 1/n) = (n-1) \cdot T_o$, which is the **maximum possible** gain (all overhead eliminated).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Apply Each Optimization

| Optimization | Precondition | Speedup | Code Size | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LICM** | Computation operand-invariant | Up to $n\times$ | $\pm 0$ | Low |
| **Strength Reduction** | Induction variable exists | $O(n) \cdot (T_m - T_a)$ | Small $\uparrow$ | Low |
| **IV Elimination** | Multiple IVs present | $O(n) \cdot T_{\text{op}}$ | Small $\downarrow$ | Medium |
| **Loop Unrolling** | Constant/small trip count | $n \cdot T_o \cdot (1 - 1/k)$ | Large $\uparrow$ | High (code bloat) |
| **Loop Jamming** | Adjacent same-range loops | Up to $2\times$ overhead | $\downarrow$ | Medium |
| **Loop Peeling** | First/last iter different | Branch elim. | Slight $\uparrow$ | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Loop Optimization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source IR / CFG] --> B[Stage 1: Dominator Computation]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Back Edge Detection]
    C --> D{Loop Detected?}
    D -- No --> Z[Pass to Next Optimization]
    D -- Yes --> E[Stage 3: Natural Loop Construction]
    E --> F[Loop Header Identified]
    F --> G[Reach Defs Analysis]
    G --> H[Find Invariant Statements]
    H --> I{Invariant Found?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Apply LICM to Pre-header]
    I -- No --> K[Check Induction Variables]
    J --> L[Apply Strength Reduction]
    K --> M{IVs Present?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Apply IV Elimination]
    M -- No --> O[Check Trip Count]
    N --> P[Apply Loop Unrolling]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Apply Loop Jamming]
    Q --> R[Verify Available Expressions]
    R --> S{Optimization Safe?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Update CFG with Optimized Code]
    S -- No --> U[Revert Transformation]
    T --> V[Emit Optimized IR]
    U --> V
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Natural Loop Identification Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0[Entry Node n0] --> N1[Block A]
    N1 --> N2[Block B Loop Header h]
    N1 --> N3[Block C Exit]
    N2 --> N3
    N2 --> N4[Block D Inside Loop]
    N4 --> N2
    N3 --> N5[Block E]
    N5 --> N6[Block F Loop Latch n]
    N6 --> N2
    style N2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style N6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style N4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**

| Element | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Node N2 (Yellow)** | Loop Header $h$ — entry to the natural loop |
| **Node N6 (Green)** | Loop Latch $n$ — source of the back edge $n \to h$ |
| **Node N4 (Blue)** | Loop Body — strictly inside the natural loop |
| **Back Edge** | $N6 \to N2$, where $N2$ dominates $N6$ |
| **Natural Loop Set** | $\{N2, N4, N6\}$ — header, body, and latch |

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Dataflow for LICM (Reaching Definitions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pre-header Block] --> B[Loop Header]
    B --> C[Loop Body Iter 1]
    C --> D[Loop Body Iter k]
    D --> E[Loop Exit]
    E --> F[Post-Exit Code]
    subgraph LICM_Region[LICM Target Region]
        C
        D
    end
    G[Reaching Defs Analysis] --> C
    G --> D
    H[Invariant Test] --> C
    H --> D
    H -- All Invariant --> I[Hoist Statement to Pre-header A]
    I --> A
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Induction Variable Strength Reduction Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original: x = 4*i in Loop] --> B[Step 1: Identify Basic IV i]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Linear Relation x = 4*i]
    C --> D[Step 3: Derive Recurrence x_next = x + 4]
    D --> E[Step 4: Initialize x = 4 outside loop]
    E --> F[Step 5: Replace multiply with add]
    F --> G[Transformed: x = x + 4 in Loop]
    G --> H[Cost Reduction: T_multiply replaced with T_add]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**
**Q: Define a "natural loop" in a control flow graph. How is a back edge identified?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A natural loop is a strongly connected subgraph of a control flow graph that has a single entry node called the **loop header**, and the entry dominates all nodes in the loop. Formally, given a back edge $n \to h$, the natural loop consists of the header $h$ and all nodes that can reach $n$ without passing through $h$.

A back edge is identified as follows: an edge $n \to h$ is a back edge if and only if $h$ **dominates** $n$ in the control flow graph. The dominator relation is computed using:

$$
\text{Dom}(n) = \{n\} \cup \bigcap_{p \in \text{pred}(n)} \text{Dom}(p)
$$

The target $h$ of the back edge becomes the loop header. **(1 mark) Definition, (1 mark) Back edge condition, (1 mark) Construction.**

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Similar Pattern]**
**Q: What is the "frequency reduction" principle? Why is it central to loop optimization?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The frequency reduction principle states that a computation executed inside a loop runs $n$ times more often (where $n$ is the trip count) than if it were executed outside. Therefore, any reduction in the per-iteration cost of an instruction is **amplified by a factor of $n$** when summed over the entire loop execution.

This principle is central to loop optimization because:
1. Programs spend the majority of execution time in loops (**90/10 rule**).
2. It justifies moving invariant computations outside the loop (**LICM**).
3. It justifies replacing expensive operations with cheaper ones (**strength reduction**).
4. It quantifies the gain: a 1-cycle reduction per iteration over $n = 10^6$ iterations saves $10^6$ cycles.

**(1 mark) Principle statement, (1 mark) Amplification effect, (1 mark) Justification with example.**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** with a suitable example. State the conditions for a statement to be considered loop-invariant. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Strength Reduction** and **Induction Variable Elimination** as loop optimization techniques. Show with an example how multiplication can be replaced with addition inside a loop. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) is an optimization that identifies computations within a loop whose results do not change across iterations and moves them outside the loop to execute exactly once.

**Conditions for a statement to be invariant (3 marks):**
1. All operands of the statement are **constants**.
2. All operands are **defined outside** the loop.
3. All operands have **exactly one definition** inside the loop, and that definition is itself loop-invariant.

**Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
1. Build the dataflow analysis using **reaching definitions**.
2. Identify invariant statements.
3. Verify that moving the statement preserves the original program semantics (using **available expressions** analysis to ensure no new uses appear).
4. Move the statement to a **pre-header** block (a newly created empty block that dominates the loop header).

**Example (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Original:} \\
& \text{for } (i = 0; i < 100; i++) \;\{\\
& \quad t = 2 \cdot x + y; \quad \text{(x, y defined outside)}\\
& \quad a[i] = t \cdot i;\\
& \}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{After LICM:} \\
& t = 2 \cdot x + y;\\
& \text{for } (i = 0; i < 100; i++) \;\{\\
& \quad a[i] = t \cdot i;\\
& \}
\end{aligned}
$$

The expression `2*x + y` is now computed once, saving $99$ redundant computations.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Strength Reduction Definition (2 marks):** Strength reduction is a compiler optimization that replaces expensive operations (like multiplication) with equivalent cheaper operations (like addition) when applied to induction variables. An **induction variable** is a variable whose value evolves in a fixed pattern (usually arithmetic progression) with each loop iteration.

**Derivation with Example (3 marks):**

Consider the loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{for } (i = 1; i \leq 100; i++) \;\{\\
& \quad y = 5 \cdot i + 3;\\
& \quad \text{use}(y);\\
& \}
\end{aligned}
$$

The variable $i$ is the basic induction variable with recurrence $i_{k+1} = i_k + 1$. The variable $y$ is a derived induction variable. The relationship $y_k = 5 \cdot i_k + 3$ allows us to derive:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{k+1} &= 5 \cdot i_{k+1} + 3 \\
&= 5 \cdot (i_k + 1) + 3 \\
&= (5 \cdot i_k + 3) + 5 \\
&= y_k + 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transformed Code (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& y = 8; \quad \text{(initialized to } 5 \cdot 1 + 3 = 8\text{)}\\
& \text{for } (i = 1; i \leq 100; i++) \;\{\\
& \quad \text{use}(y);\\
& \quad y = y + 5;\\
& \}
\end{aligned}
$$

The multiplication `5 * i` has been replaced with addition `y + 5`, achieving a per-iteration speedup.

**Induction Variable Elimination (1 mark):** If a loop has multiple induction variables, all but the basic one can be eliminated. Each derived IV is expressed as a linear function of the basic IV. The elimination reduces register pressure, freeing registers for other uses.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** What is a **dominator** in a control flow graph? Describe the algorithm to compute dominators. Show the dominator sets for the given CFG:

```
        1 (entry)
       / \
      2   3
      |   |
      4 ←-+
      |
      5
     / \
    6   7
     \ /
      8
```

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Loop Unrolling** and **Loop Jamming (Fusion)** as loop optimization techniques. Discuss their advantages and limitations with examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A node $d$ **dominates** node $n$ (written $d \; \text{dom} \; n$) if every path from the entry node $n_0$ to $n$ must pass through $d$. The entry node is dominated only by itself.

**Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Initialize: $\text{Dom}(n_0) = \{n_0\}$ and $\text{Dom}(n) = N$ (set of all nodes) for all other $n$.
2. Iterate: For each node $n \neq n_0$, recompute:

$$
\text{Dom}(n) = \{n\} \cup \bigcap_{p \in \text{pred}(n)} \text{Dom}(p)
$$

3. Repeat until no $\text{Dom}$ set changes.

**Dominator Sets for the Given CFG (4 marks):**

| Node $n$ | $\text{Dom}(n)$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | $\{1\}$ |
| 2 | $\{1, 2\}$ |
| 3 | $\{1, 3\}$ |
| 4 | $\{1, 2, 4\}$ |
| 5 | $\{1, 2, 4, 5\}$ |
| 6 | $\{1, 2, 4, 5, 6\}$ |
| 7 | $\{1, 2, 4, 5, 7\}$ |
| 8 | $\{1, 2, 4, 5, 8\}$ |

> **Verification Logic (sample):**  
> For node 8, predecessors are 6 and 7.  
> $\text{Dom}(6) \cap \text{Dom}(7) = \{1,2,4,5,6\} \cap \{1,2,4,5,7\} = \{1,2,4,5\}$.  
> Adding 8 itself: $\text{Dom}(8) = \{1,2,4,5,8\}$. ✓

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Loop Unrolling (3 marks):** Loop unrolling is a transformation that replicates the loop body $k$ times and reduces the trip count from $n$ to $\lceil n/k \rceil$, thereby reducing loop control overhead.

**Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Original:} \\
& \text{for } (i = 0; i < 4; i++) \;\; a[i] = b[i] + c;\\
\\
& \text{Unrolled (k=2):} \\
& a[0] = b[0] + c;\\
& a[1] = b[1] + c;\\
& \text{for } (i = 2; i < 4; i++) \;\; a[i] = b[i] + c;
\end{aligned}
$$

**Advantages:** Reduced branch overhead, better instruction scheduling, exposes parallelism.
**Limitations:** Increased code size, may exceed I-cache, requires careful handling of remaining iterations.

**Loop Jamming (3 marks):** Also called **loop fusion**, this combines two consecutive loops with identical iteration ranges into a single loop, eliminating redundant loop control.

**Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Original:}\\
& \text{for } (i = 0; i < n; i++) \;\; a[i] = b[i] + 1;\\
& \text{for } (i = 0; i < n; i++) \;\; c[i] = a[i] \cdot 2;\\
\\
& \text{After Jamming:}\\
& \text{for } (i = 0; i < n; i++) \;\{\\
& \quad a[i] = b[i] + 1;\\
& \quad c[i] = a[i] \cdot 2;\\
& \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Advantages:** Halved loop overhead, better cache locality, enables further optimizations.
**Limitations:** May increase register pressure; not always safe (e.g., if aliasing exists between the two loops).

**(1 mark) Summary table of trade-offs.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Loop" with programming syntax:** Students often write code snippets in `C` or `Java` style. The compiler design perspective requires **control flow graph drawings** and **dataflow equations**, not programming.
> 2. **Skipping dominator computation:** Many students attempt to identify loops without computing dominators. KTU examiners will deduct **2–3 marks** for this omission.
> 3. **Wrong back edge condition:** Writing "an edge from a node to itself" is incorrect. A back edge is $n \to h$ where $h$ **dominates** $n$, and $h \neq n$ typically.
> 4. **Ignoring safety checks:** LICM is **not always safe**. Forgetting to verify via available expressions loses marks.
> 5. **Forgetting pre-header creation:** A common KTU pitfall — students move code directly above the loop header instead of creating a dedicated pre-header block.
> 6. **Strength reduction arithmetic error:** When deriving $x_{k+1}$ from $x_k$, students often misapply the substitution. Practice the algebra carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Loop Definition:** Strongly connected region in CFG, dominated by a single **loop header**.
- [ ] **Back Edge:** Edge $n \to h$ where $h$ **dominates** $n$.
- [ ] **Natural Loop:** Header $h$ + all nodes reaching $n$ (the latch) without going through $h$.
- [ ] **Dominator Equation:** $\text{Dom}(n) = \{n\} \cup \bigcap_{p \in \text{pred}(n)} \text{Dom}(p)$.
- [ ] **LICM:** Move invariant statements to a **pre-header** block.
- [ ] **Invariant Condition:** Operands must be constant, defined outside, or defined once invariantly inside.
- [ ] **Safety of LICM:** Must preserve semantics — verified by **available expressions** analysis.
- [ ] **Strength Reduction:** Replace expensive ops (multiply) with cheap ones (add) for **induction variables**.
- [ ] **Induction Variable:** A variable forming an **arithmetic progression** in each iteration.
- [ ] **Basic IV:** The variable updated by `+1` (or similar simple step) each iteration.
- [ ] **IV Elimination:** Express derived IVs as $y = a \cdot x + b$ of the basic IV.
- [ ] **Loop Unrolling:** Replicate body $k$ times, trip count $\to n/k$.
- [ ] **Unroll Gain Formula:** $G = n \cdot T_o \cdot (1 - 1/k)$.
- [ ] **Loop Jamming:** Fuse two adjacent same-range loops → one loop.
- [ ] **Frequency Reduction:** Core justification — a 1-cycle/iter saving over $n$ iters saves $n$ cycles.
- [ ] **90/10 Rule:** 90% runtime in 10% code — usually loops.
- [ ] **Pre-header:** A new block inserted just before the loop header to host hoisted code.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 Weight:** Expect a **14-mark question** combining 2–3 techniques; always draw the **CFG** to score full marks.
- [ ] **Reaching Definitions:** Forward dataflow analysis used to detect invariance.
- [ ] **Available Expressions:** Forward dataflow analysis used to verify LICM safety.
- [ ] **Trip Count:** Number of loop iterations — determines speedup magnitude.
- [ ] **Memory Locality:** Jamming improves cache hit ratio by reusing data within one loop pass.
- [ ] **Side Effects:** LICM is unsafe if the moved statement has side effects (e.g., function call with unknown behavior).
- [ ] **Code Size Trade-off:** Unrolling increases code size, may harm I-cache performance.
- [ ] **Branch Prediction:** Unrolling reduces misprediction penalty by reducing branch frequency.

---

**End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes for "Loops and Iteration" — Module 4, PECIT751.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Case Statement** (also referred to as a **Switch Statement** or **Multi-way Branch**) is a high-level language construct that allows a program to transfer control to one of several possible execution paths based on the value of an *index expression* (also called the *switch selector* or *case index*). 

In the context of **Syntax-Directed Translation (SDT)** of the front-end compiler, a case statement is a non-terminal symbol (typically denoted `switch` or `case_stmt`) whose semantic action is to evaluate an index expression, compare its run-time value against a set of compile-time constant *case labels*, and jump to the matching statement block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A *case statement* is a control-flow abstraction that permits multi-way selection. The compiler's primary job with respect to it is to **translate** the high-level construct into low-level **Three-Address Code (TAC)** while preserving the *fall-through* or *jump-on-match* semantics declared by the source language.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **multi-counter bank** (or a railway enquiry counter). The gatekeeper looks at your **token number** (the index expression). If your token is 5, you are directed to **Counter 5**; if it is 12, you are sent to **Counter 12**; and if the counter number does not exist, you are sent to the **"Default" counter** (where complaints are handled). 

- The **token number** is the *case index*.
- The **counters** are the *case labels / statement bodies*.
- The **default counter** is the *default case*.
- The **decision-making gatekeeper** is the *compiler-generated jump code*.

The compiler is essentially the gatekeeper's rule-book — it has three strategies to send you to the right counter: (i) **linear checking** (one-by-one, slow but simple), (ii) **a pre-printed directory list** (jump table, very fast), or (iii) **asking a binary question** (binary search, balanced).

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Index Expression** | The expression whose value selects the branch (e.g., `E` in `switch(E)`). |
| **Case Label / Constant** | A compile-time integer/character/enum constant that a branch matches. |
| **Default Label** | The optional fall-back branch taken when no case label matches. |
| **Translation Scheme** | The set of SDT productions and semantic actions used by the compiler. |
| **Three-Address Code (TAC)** | The intermediate representation (IR) used as the target of translation. |
| **Fall-through** | The execution model (popular in C) where execution continues into the *next* case if `break` is absent. |
| **Jump Table** | A run-time data structure holding addresses of case bodies, indexed by the case value. |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 scheme places Case Statements under **Module 4: Syntax-Directed Translation Schemes**, immediately after the translation of assignments, boolean expressions, and loops. Students must understand that the *case statement is the most "labelled" control-flow structure* — it relies heavily on generated labels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Case Statement is Compiled

The translation of a `case_stmt` into Three-Address Code is a multi-stage logic puzzle. Below is the structured logic flow used by every KTU-standard compiler (Aho/Sethi/Ullman reference style):

1. **Lexical & Syntactic Recognition:** The parser accepts productions of the form  
   `case_stmt → SWITCH ( E ) { case_list }`  
   where `case_list` is a sequence of `CASE V : stmt` and an optional `DEFAULT : stmt`.

2. **Intermediate Translation:** An *intermediate code generator* emits a *test* and a *jump* for every case label. The index expression `E` is evaluated once and stored in a temporary (e.g., `t1`).

3. **Code Selection Strategy:** Based on the **density** and **range** of the case labels, the compiler chooses one of three strategies:
   - **(A) Linear / Sequential Testing** — for *sparse* or *few* cases.
   - **(B) Jump Table** — for *dense* cases within a *small* range.
   - **(C) Binary Search** — for *many* but *sparse* cases.

4. **Label Allocation:** Two synthetic labels are generated per case body: a **test label** (entry point) and an **end label** (post-switch jump target).

5. **Code Emission:** TAC instructions of the form `if t1 = V goto L_i` and `goto L_next` are emitted.

6. **Scope Management:** The translator maintains a **symbol table stack** to correctly bind the loop labels and the surrounding block.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why store the index in a temporary?** Because the index is evaluated **once** but compared many times — re-evaluating it would cause side effects to be repeated.
- **Why a jump table?** A single **indirect jump** (`goto *table[t1]`) is asymptotically faster than a chain of `n` comparisons (`O(1)` vs `O(n)`).
- **Why binary search?** When labels are sparse but numerous, building a full jump table wastes memory; binary search gives `O(log n)` comparisons with negligible storage overhead.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Jump Table Size** | $S = (V_{max} - V_{min} + 1)$ | Number of entries in the jump table (range of labels). |
| **Memory Wastage** | $W = S - n_{cases}$ | Entries reserved but unused (holes in the range). |
| **Efficiency of Jump Table** | $T_{lookup} = O(1)$ | Constant time, independent of $n_{cases}$. |
| **Efficiency of Linear Search** | $T_{lookup} = O(n_{cases})$ | Worst case compares all labels. |
| **Efficiency of Binary Search** | $T_{lookup} = O(\log n_{cases})$ | Logarithmic comparisons. |
| **Comparison Threshold** | $n \ge \log_2(\text{range}) + 1$ | Switch-over point from jump table to binary search. |
