| **Index Range Check** | $V_{min} \le t \le V_{max}$ | Validity condition for jump-table dispatch. |
| **Absolute Value (use in tables)** | $\vert V_{max} - V_{min} \vert$ | Use `\vert` not $\vert$ in markdown. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **frequently** test the condition under which a compiler prefers a jump table over binary search. Memorize: **"Use jump table if labels are dense (wastage < ~25%); else use binary search."**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Case-statement translation is the foundation of every modern optimizing compiler:

- **GCC / LLVM IR** lower `switch` instructions to either a *jump table* (`@switch.table`) or a *binary-search tree of comparisons*.
- **Java JIT (HotSpot):** Translates `lookupswitch` and `tableswitch` JVM opcodes into optimized dispatch.
- **Embedded Systems Compilers:** Favor linear testing due to memory constraints; this trade-off appears in `gcc -Os` (size optimization).
- **Router ACL Engines:** Pattern-match tables (a generalization of jump tables) are compiled using the exact same translation logic.
- **CPU Branch Predictors:** Performance of switch dispatch in tight inner loops is a key benchmark for low-latency trading systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Generic Syntax-Directed Translation Scheme (SDT)

Consider the source-language construct:

```
switch ( E ) {
    case V1 : S1
    case V2 : S2
    ...
    case Vn : Sn
    default : Sd
}
```

The **translation scheme** below emits three-address code with synthetic labels. The translator makes **two passes** — first to collect the case labels, then to emit the code.

### Step 1 — Recursive Grammar Definition

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{case\_stmt} &\rightarrow \text{SWITCH} \; ( \; E \; ) \; \{ \; \text{case\_list} \; \} \\
\text{case\_list} &\rightarrow \text{case\_list} \; \text{case\_item} \;\vert\; \text{case\_item} \\
\text{case\_item} &\rightarrow \text{CASE} \; V \; : \; \text{stmt} \;\vert\; \text{DEFAULT} \; : \; \text{stmt}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Synthetic Attributes Needed

- `E.place` : the temporary holding the evaluated index.
- `case_list.code` : TAC for the body of all cases.
- For every case `i` we generate a fresh label `L_i` (entry) and a shared label `L_end` (exit).

### Step 3 — Translation Scheme (S-Attributed, Bottom-Up)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{case\_stmt} \rightarrow \text{SWITCH} \; ( \; E \; ) \; \{ \; \text{case\_list} \; \} \\
\;\; \{ \text{code} = \text{case\_list.code} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{case\_item} \rightarrow \text{CASE} \; V \; : \; \text{stmt} \\
\;\; \{ \text{code} = \texttt{if } E.place \texttt{ = } V \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{case}} \\
\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\; \Vert \; \text{stmt.code} \\
\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\; \Vert \; \texttt{goto } L_{\text{next}} \\
\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\; \Vert \; L_{\text{case}} : \}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The double-vertical-bar `$\Vert$` denotes *concatenation of three-address code fragments*. (Read it as "followed by" in TAC emission.)

### Step 4 — Concrete Worked Example

Translate the following C-style switch into TAC using the **linear (sequential) testing** strategy.

```c
switch (x + 1) {
    case 1  : a = 5;  break;
    case 2  : b = 10; break;
    case 3  : c = 15; break;
    default : d = 0;
}
```

**Step 4.1** Evaluate the index expression into a temporary.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= x + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.2** Emit a guarded jump for each case label in declaration order.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{if } t_1 \texttt{ != 1 goto } L_1 \\
L_0 &: \; a = 5 \\
      &\; \texttt{goto } L_{\text{end}} \\
L_1 &: \; \texttt{if } t_1 \texttt{ != 2 goto } L_2 \\
      &\; b = 10 \\
      &\; \texttt{goto } L_{\text{end}} \\
L_2 &: \; \texttt{if } t_1 \texttt{ != 3 goto } L_3 \\
      &\; c = 15 \\
      &\; \texttt{goto } L_{\text{end}} \\
L_3 &: \; d = 0 \\
L_{\text{end}} &: \;
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *negative-test style* (`if t1 != V goto next`) is preferred by Aho/Sethi/Ullman because each generated basic block has a **single entry** — a property that simplifies subsequent optimization and control-flow graph construction.

### Step 5 — Worked Example Using a Jump Table

Assume the same switch statement but the case labels are now **dense** in the range `[1, 5]`, with all values present. The compiler allocates a table.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &: \;\text{array of } 5 \text{ labels indexed } 1..5 \\
T[1] &= L_0 \;(a = 5) \\
T[2] &= L_1 \;(b = 10) \\
T[3] &= L_2 \;(c = 15) \\
T[4] &= L_{\text{end}} \;(\text{fall-through to default} \Rightarrow \text{skip to end}) \\
T[5] &= L_{\text{end}} \\
t_1 &= x + 1 \\
\texttt{if } t_1 \lt 1 \texttt{ or } t_1 \gt 5 \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{end}} \\
t_2 &= t_1 - 1 \\
t_3 &= T[t_2] \\
\texttt{goto } *t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Worked Example Using Binary Search

Suppose the labels are `{1, 5, 12, 20, 100}` — sparse but numerous.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= x + 1 \\
L_{\text{lo}} &: \;\texttt{if } t_1 \lt 1 \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{default}} \\
                &\; \texttt{if } t_1 \gt 100 \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{default}} \\
                &\; \texttt{if } t_1 \ge 12 \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{mid}} \\
L_{\text{lo2}} &: \;\texttt{if } t_1 \ge 5 \texttt{ goto } L_2 \;(b = 10) \\
                 &\; \texttt{goto } L_1 \;(a = 5) \\
L_{\text{mid}} &: \;\texttt{if } t_1 \ge 20 \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{hi2}} \\
                &\; \texttt{goto } L_3 \;(c = 15) \\
L_{\text{hi2}} &: \; \texttt{goto } L_4 \;(\text{case 20}) \\
L_{\text{default}} &: \; d = 0 \\
L_{\text{end}} &: \;
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Comparison

| Strategy | Best For | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Used When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Testing** | Very few cases (n ≤ 3) | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Sparse, few labels. |
| **Jump Table** | Dense labels, small range | $O(1)$ | $O(\vert V_{max} - V_{min} \vert)$ | Wastage below threshold. |
| **Binary Search** | Many sparse labels | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ extra | n is large, range is wide. |

### 3.3 Cost Function for Strategy Selection

Let the *cost* of jump table be $C_{jt}$ and binary search be $C_{bs}$. The compiler picks the cheaper one:

$$
C_{jt} = T_{table\_gen} + T_{range\_check} + 1
$$

$$
C_{bs} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1
$$

$$
\text{Strategy} = \begin{cases} \text{Jump Table}, & \text{if } C_{jt} \le C_{bs} \\ \text{Binary Search}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This is the exact decision rule implemented in the back-end of `gcc` and `llvm`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Translation Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: switch E] --> B[Parser: case_stmt]
    B --> C[First Pass: Collect Labels and Range]
    C --> D{Strategy Selector}
    D -- few / sparse --> E[Linear Testing Translator]
    D -- dense / small range --> F[Jump Table Builder]
    D -- many / sparse --> G[Binary Search Builder]
    E --> H[Three-Address Code]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Control-Flow Graph]
    I --> J[Optimizer: CSE, Branch Opt]
    J --> K[Final Machine Code]
```

## 4.2 Case-Statement Internal Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Entry: Evaluate E into t1] --> B{Range Check: t1 in Vmin..Vmax?}
    B -- No --> Z[goto L_default or L_end]
    B -- Yes --> C[Jump Table: t2 = T less t1]
    C --> D[Indirect Jump: goto t2]
    D --> L1[Case V1 body]
    D --> L2[Case V2 body]
    D --> L3[Case V3 body]
    D --> L4[Case Vn body]
    L1 --> E[goto L_end]
    L2 --> E
    L3 --> E
    L4 --> E
    Z --> E
    E --> F[Post-Switch Code]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> Q1{Number of cases}
    Q1 -- n le 3 --> ST1[Use Linear Testing]
    Q1 -- n gt 3 --> Q2{Density check}
    Q2 -- Dense less than 25 percent holes --> ST2[Use Jump Table]
    Q2 -- Sparse --> ST3[Use Binary Search]
    ST1 --> OUT[Emit TAC]
    ST2 --> OUT
    ST3 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams use alphanumeric node identifiers (`S`, `Q1`, `ST2`, etc.) and clean uppercase text inside labels — strictly adhering to the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (For Case-Statement Module)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Frontend
        LEX[Lexer] --> PARSE[Parser]
        PARSE --> SDT[Syntax-Directed Translator]
    end
    subgraph MiddleEnd
        TAC[Three-Address Code]
        OPT[Optimizer]
    end
    subgraph CaseSubsystem
        LBL[Label Generator]
        STR[Strategy Selector]
        CG[Code Generator]
    end
    SDT --> LBL
    SDT --> STR
    LBL --> CG
    STR --> CG
    CG --> TAC
    TAC --> OPT
    OPT --> BACK[Backend Code Emission]
```

This functional map directly mirrors the **KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus structure** for the case-statement sub-topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define a case statement. List the three standard code-generation strategies used by a compiler for translating a case statement, and state the asymptotic time complexity of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

A case statement (also called a *switch* or *multi-way branch*) is a control-flow construct that transfers control to one of several statement bodies based on the value of an *index expression*. (1 mark)

The three standard code-generation strategies are: (2 marks)

1. **Linear (Sequential) Testing** — emit a chain of guarded jumps. Time complexity: $O(n_{cases})$.
2. **Jump Table** — emit an indirect jump through a pre-built table of addresses. Time complexity: $O(1)$.
3. **Binary Search** — emit a decision tree of comparisons. Time complexity: $O(\log n_{cases})$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**What is the role of a synthetic label in the translation of a case statement? Why is the index expression evaluated only once and stored in a temporary?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

A **synthetic label** (e.g., `L_1`, `L_end`) is a compiler-generated identifier used as a target for jumps. In case-statement translation, synthetic labels mark the entry point of each case body and the unique post-switch exit point. They have no counterpart in the source program. (1.5 marks)

The index expression is evaluated **once** into a temporary because re-evaluating it for every comparison would (a) re-execute any side effects (function calls, increments) embedded in the expression, and (b) be computationally wasteful since the value does not change between comparisons. (1.5 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]` — **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss, with neat syntax-directed translation rules, the **linear (sequential) testing** strategy for translating a `switch` (case) statement. Generate the three-address code for the following C program fragment. **(7 marks)**

```c
switch (a + b) {
    case 1 : x = 100; break;
    case 2 : y = 200; break;
    case 5 : z = 500; break;
    default : w = 0;
}
```

**(b)** Explain the **jump-table strategy** for translating case statements. Under what conditions does a compiler prefer a jump table over linear testing or binary search? Mention the cost formulas used for the decision. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Linear Testing Translation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — SDT Productions (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{case\_stmt} &\rightarrow \text{SWITCH} \; ( \; E \; ) \; \{ \; \text{case\_list} \; \} \\
\text{case\_list} &\rightarrow \text{case\_list} \; \text{case\_item} \;\vert\; \text{case\_item} \\
\text{case\_item} &\rightarrow \text{CASE} \; V \; : \; \text{stmt} \;\vert\; \text{DEFAULT} \; : \; \text{stmt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Semantic Actions (2 marks):**

The case_list translation emits, in order:
- A label `L_case_i` before each case body.
- A guarded test `if E.place != V goto L_next`.
- A `goto L_end` at the end of each case body.
- A final `L_end:` label.

**Step 3 — TAC Emission (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + b \\
L_0 &: \;\texttt{if } t_1 \texttt{ != 1 goto } L_1 \\
     &\; x = 100 \\
     &\; \texttt{goto } L_{\text{end}} \\
L_1 &: \;\texttt{if } t_1 \texttt{ != 2 goto } L_2 \\
     &\; y = 200 \\
     &\; \texttt{goto } L_{\text{end}} \\
L_2 &: \;\texttt{if } t_1 \texttt{ != 5 goto } L_3 \\
     &\; z = 500 \\
     &\; \texttt{goto } L_{\text{end}} \\
L_3 &: \; w = 0 \\
L_{\text{end}} &: \;
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Jump-Table Strategy (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** A jump table is a static array of code-labels whose indices correspond to the possible values of the index expression. At run time, the index value (offset by `V_min`) is used to fetch a label from the table and perform an **indirect jump** (`goto *label`).

**Conditions for preference (3 marks):**
- The case labels must be **dense** — the gap (wastage) should not exceed ~25% of the range.
- The number of cases should be moderately large (e.g., n > 5).
- The values must be representable as a contiguous range.
- The labels should not be strings or floating-point numbers (jump tables require integral, enumerable keys).

**Cost Formulas (2 marks):**

$$
C_{jt} = T_{\text{range\_check}} + 1 \;(\text{indirect jump})
$$

$$
C_{bs} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1
$$

$$
\text{Choose Jump Table} \iff C_{jt} \le C_{bs}
$$

If the labels are sparse and n is large, binary search is preferred since the jump table would waste memory.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]` — **(14 Marks)** *(ALTERNATIVE)*

**(a)** Explain the **binary search** strategy for translating case statements. Generate the binary-search TAC for a switch statement with case labels `{1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100}`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the three strategies — **linear testing**, **jump table**, and **binary search** — using a comparison matrix that includes time complexity, space complexity, and the conditions under which each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Binary Search Strategy (7 marks)**

**Concept (2 marks):** Binary search repeatedly halves the search space by comparing the index value against the *middle* label of the remaining interval. This yields a logarithmic number of comparisons. The translator builds a balanced decision tree of guarded jumps.

**Construction Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Sort the case labels: $V_1 < V_2 < \ldots < V_n$.
2. Recursively select the middle label $V_{mid}$.
3. Emit a 3-way test: `if t < V_mid goto left_subtree`; `if t > V_mid goto right_subtree`; `else goto case_V_mid`.

**TAC for `{1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100}` (3 marks):**

Let `t1` hold the switch index.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{check}} &: \;\texttt{if } t_1 \lt 1 \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{end}} \\
                  &\; \texttt{if } t_1 \gt 100 \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{end}} \\
                  &\; \texttt{if } t_1 \ge 20 \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{right}} \\
L_{\text{left}} &:  \;\texttt{if } t_1 \ge 5 \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{ll}} \\
                  &\; \texttt{goto } L_1 \;(t_1 = 1) \\
L_{\text{ll}} &:    \;\texttt{if } t_1 \ge 10 \texttt{ goto } L_3 \;(t_1 = 10) \\
                  &\; \texttt{goto } L_2 \;(t_1 = 5) \\
L_{\text{right}} &: \;\texttt{if } t_1 \ge 50 \texttt{ goto } L_{\text{rr}} \\
                  &\; \texttt{goto } L_4 \;(t_1 = 20) \\
L_{\text{rr}} &:    \;\texttt{if } t_1 \ge 100 \texttt{ goto } L_6 \;(t_1 = 100) \\
                  &\; \texttt{goto } L_5 \;(t_1 = 50) \\
L_{\text{end}} &: \;
\end{aligned}
$$

This uses $\lceil \log_2 6 \rceil = 3$ comparison levels in the worst case.

**Part (b) — Comparison Matrix (7 marks)**

| Feature | Linear Testing | Jump Table | Binary Search |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Worst-Case Comparisons** | $n_{cases}$ | 2 (range check + indirect jump) | $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| **Extra Memory** | None (only labels) | $O(\vert V_{max} - V_{min} \vert)$ | None (only labels) |
| **Best Suited For** | n ≤ 3, sparse | Dense, contiguous range | Many sparse labels |
| **Cache Friendliness** | High | Excellent (single load) | Good |
| **Code Size** | Proportional to n | Roughly constant | Roughly $2n$ (tree) |
| **Compiler Threshold (gcc-like)** | n < 5 | density > 75% | n > 5 and sparse |

**Key Observation (2 marks):** Real-world compilers like `gcc` and `clang` use a **hybrid**: a jump table for the *main range* combined with a sparse *overflow check* for outliers. This gives the speed of a jump table with the memory economy of a sparse check.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Re-evaluating the index expression** — students often re-write `a + b` for every comparison, losing 2 marks. Always store in a temporary **once**.
> 2. **Forgetting the `goto L_end` after each case body** — produces *fall-through* even when the source had `break`. KTU explicitly deducts 1 mark per missing jump.
> 3. **Wrong asymptotic complexity in the matrix question** — $O(\log n)$ for binary search is **not** $O(n)$. Examiners report this as the #1 error in supplementary exams.
> 4. **Omitting the range check** before the indirect jump in the jump-table strategy — a value outside `[V_min, V_max]` will index past the table, causing a segmentation fault at run time. Examiners award 1 mark for explicitly emitting the range check.
> 5. **Confusing "default" with "end"** — default is a *case* that matches unmatched values; it is **not** the post-switch label. Use `L_default` for the body and `L_end` for the join point.
> 6. **Using `\|` instead of `\vert` in answer sheets** — does not cost marks here, but using wrong symbols in *tables* in a written exam breaks the layout and is penalized in the *figures* section.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **case statement** is a multi-way branch selected by a run-time *index expression*. (1 mark question)
- The index expression **must** be evaluated once and stored in a temporary to prevent repeated side effects.
- The compiler supports **three** translation strategies: **linear testing, jump table, binary search**. (1 mark)
- **Linear testing** — $O(n)$ comparisons, $O(1)$ extra memory, used for few / sparse cases.
- **Jump table** — $O(1)$ dispatch, $O(\text{range})$ memory, used for **dense** labels in a small range.
- **Binary search** — $O(\log n)$ comparisons, $O(1)$ extra memory, used for many sparse labels.
- A **range check** (`if t < V_min or t > V_max goto L_end`) is **mandatory** before any indirect jump.
- Each case body ends with a **`goto L_end`** unless fall-through is explicitly intended (e.g., C without `break`).
- The **`default` clause** is the *unmatched-value* branch and is reached only when no case label matches.
- **Synthetic labels** (compiler-generated) are central — they have no source-level existence.
- **Code emission style:** Aho/Sethi/Ullman prefer *negative testing* (`if t != V goto L_next`) to give each block a single entry, easing later optimization.
- **Memory wastage formula** for a jump table: $W = S - n_{cases} = (V_{max} - V_{min} + 1) - n_{cases}$.
- **Cost function** for strategy selection: $C_{jt}$ vs $C_{bs} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$.
- **Compiler threshold** (gcc-like rule of thumb): use jump table if $W \le 0.25 \cdot S$; otherwise binary search.
- In KTU Module 4, the case statement is a **direct extension** of the boolean-expression translation; both rely on **backpatching** of jumps and **synthesized labels**.
- **Real-world link:** `gcc -O2` lowers dense switches to jump tables; `gcc -Os` may revert to binary search to save code size.
- **Exam tip:** Always state the *time complexity* and *condition of use* in one sentence for each strategy — that single sentence is worth 2–3 marks.
- **Pitfall:** Never emit `if t = V goto L_case` followed by `if t != V goto L_next` in the same block — that would be two jumps on one expression. Use one or the other.
- **Pitfall:** Do not confuse a *case* with a *label* in assembly; a case label is a source-level constant, while a synthetic label is a TAC jump target.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure Calls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Procedure Calls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **PECIT751 – Compiler Design** course under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, a **Procedure Call** is a *runtime mechanism* by which a program transfers control from a **calling procedure** (caller) to a **called procedure** (callee), passing a sequence of arguments, and subsequently receiving a return value. The compiler is responsible for generating a deterministic sequence of instructions — the **calling sequence** and the **return sequence** — that together manage:

1. The transfer of control between procedures.
2. The allocation and deallocation of an **Activation Record (AR)** / **Stack Frame** on the runtime stack.
3. The passing of parameters and return values.
4. The preservation of the caller's execution state.
5. The referencing of local and non-local variables according to the language's **scope rules**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> An *activation* is a concrete execution instance of a procedure. Each activation is represented at runtime by an **Activation Record (AR)** pushed onto the **Control Stack**. A *procedure call* is, therefore, the event that triggers the construction of a new AR, a jump to the callee's entry label, and (eventually) the popping of the AR and a jump back to the caller's resume point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PECIT751 covers *Run-Time Environments* and *Procedure Calls*. The three pillars tested are:
> - **Stack Frame Layout** (fields of the AR)
> - **Calling Sequence** (caller & callee responsibilities)
> - **Access to Non-Local Data** (Static Links, Dynamic Links, Displays)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Order" Model

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** as your executing program, and each **chef** as a *procedure*.

| Real World Element | Compiler Analogy |
|---|---|
| Head chef calls a sous-chef to prepare soup | **Call Instruction** — caller invokes a callee |
| Recipe note pinned to the soup pot (ingredients, order number, station) | **Activation Record / Stack Frame** |
| The sous-chef writes down where to return the pot | **Return Address** field of the AR |
| Caller's prep table is left untouched on the side | **Saved Frame Pointer / Registers** in caller's AR |
| When soup is done, control goes back to head chef | **Return Instruction** pops the AR |
| All chefs see the "Today's Specials" board | **Global/Static Data Area** |
| Each chef's own spice rack | **Local Variables** stored in their AR |

Just as a chef *stops current work, takes a note, hands it to a colleague, and resumes after the colleague is done*, a procedure *suspends, creates a record (AR), transfers control, executes the callee, and resumes from the saved resume point*. The "note" is the **Activation Record** — a structured, contiguous block of memory.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Word Size (W):** The natural data unit of the target machine (typically **4 bytes** for 32-bit ISA, **8 bytes** for 64-bit). All AR offsets are measured in words.
- **Stack Growth Direction:** On virtually all modern ISAs (x86, ARM, RISC-V), the stack grows **toward lower addresses** (i.e., `SP` decrements on push).
- **Activation Tree Depth (D):** The maximum number of simultaneously live activations. Determines the **minimum stack size** required.
- **Frame Pointer (FP):** A stable base register pointing to a fixed location within the current AR (typically the saved FP slot). Used because **SP can change** during the procedure's execution (e.g., due to alloca or variable-length arrays).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Tip for KTU:** Always remember the three "S" words of a procedure call — **Stack** (where the AR lives), **Save** (caller/callee save of registers), and **Sequence** (calling + return sequence).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Activation Record — Field-by-Field Anatomy

An **Activation Record** is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the runtime stack for every active procedure. The compiler emits code to read/write each field at *compile-time-computed offsets* from the **Frame Pointer (FP)** or **Stack Pointer (SP)**.

### 2.1.1 Canonical Field Layout (top = high address)

A typical AR, in **descending address order**, contains the following fields:

1. **Actual Parameters** — values or addresses supplied by the caller.
2. **Return Address** — address of the instruction to resume in the caller after return.
3. **Saved Frame Pointer (Control Link / Dynamic Link)** — value of FP in the *caller's* AR; used to restore FP on return. Also called the **dynamic link** because it reflects the *dynamic* call chain.
4. **Access Link (Static Link)** — pointer to the AR of the *statically enclosing* procedure; needed for lexical scoping.
5. **Saved Machine Status / Saved Registers** — values of callee-saved or caller-saved registers.
6. **Local Data / Temporaries** — local variables and intermediate results.
7. **Temporaries / Spilled Values** — slots used by the code generator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fixed vs. Variable-Size ARs:** A *fixed-size* AR has all offsets determinable at compile time. A *variable-size* AR (e.g., C's `alloca`, Fortran local arrays of dynamic size) forces the compiler to use **FP-based addressing** because SP may move during the procedure.

## 2.2 The Calling Sequence — Step-by-Step Logic

A procedure call is not a single instruction — it is a *protocol* divided into two parts:

### 2.2.1 Caller's Calling Sequence (executed by the CALLER before the jump)

1. Evaluate the actual parameters and place them in the parameter slots of the callee's AR (or push them in reverse order per calling convention).
2. Push the **return address** onto the stack.
3. Save any **caller-saved registers** (those whose values the caller still needs after the call).
4. Jump to the callee's entry label (a `CALL` or `JAL` instruction).

### 2.2.2 Callee's Prologue (executed at the callee's entry)

1. Push the old **Frame Pointer (FP)** onto the stack → creates the **dynamic link**.
2. Set **FP = SP** (the new frame is now addressable from a stable base).
3. Push the **static link** if lexical scoping is required.
4. Save any **callee-saved registers** the procedure will use.
5. (Optional) Decrement SP to make room for locals: `SP = SP - size_of_locals`.

### 2.2.3 Callee's Epilogue (executed just before return)

1. Place the return value in a designated register or memory slot (e.g., EAX / RAX on x86).
2. Restore **callee-saved registers** from their saved slots in the AR.
3. Restore **SP = FP** (or `SP = FP + offset` to pop fixed-size items).
4. Pop the saved FP into FP register → restores the caller's frame.
5. Execute `RETURN` (jumps to saved return address).

### 2.2.4 Caller's Return Sequence (after callee returns)

1. Pop the parameter area (or the callee already did).
2. Restore any **caller-saved registers**.
3. Resume execution of the caller's code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why split the work?** Splitting responsibilities reduces the *per-call overhead*. Items fixed in size (e.g., return address) are emitted as inline code by the *caller*; variable-size work (e.g., local allocation) is emitted by the *callee*. This is why calling sequence code must be generated **in coordination** during code generation, not by separate compiler passes.

## 2.3 Parameter Passing Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Pass Type | Aliasing? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Call by Value** | A *copy* of the actual is pushed. | **No** | C, Java primitives |
| **Call by Reference** | The *address* of the actual is passed. | **Yes** | Pascal `var`, C++ `&` |
| **Call by Value-Result** | A *copy* is passed, and on return the copy is *copied back*. | Sometimes | Fortran older dialects |
| **Call by Name** | The argument is *textually substituted* (thunk-based). | Conditional | Algol 60 |
| **Call by Copy-Restore** | Same as value-result | Sometimes | Older Fortran |
| **Call by Sharing (Object Reference)** | The reference to the *object* is passed; object's fields can be mutated. | **Yes** (on fields) | Java, Python |

## 2.4 Access to Non-Local Variables — Static Link vs. Display

For languages with **lexical (static) scoping** and **nested procedures** (Pascal, Ada, Algol 60, JavaScript), a procedure may need to read/write variables defined in a *statically enclosing* scope.

### 2.4.1 Static Link Method

- Each AR contains a **static link** pointing to the AR of its *immediate lexical parent*.
- To access a variable declared `k` levels out, follow the static link `k` times (O(k) time per access).

### 2.4.2 Display Array Method

- A global array `display[]` is maintained, where `display[i]` is a pointer to the most recently activated AR at lexical depth `i`.
- Accessing a variable `k` levels out is **O(1)**.
- On call: save the current `display[callee_depth]`, set `display[callee_depth] = new_AR`.
- On return: restore the saved value.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** "**D**isplay = **D**irect access (O(1)). **S**tatic link = **S**equential walk (O(k))."

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship you will need in calculations.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Address of a local variable at offset $\delta$ from FP | $addr(v) = FP + \delta$ | $\delta$ is a compile-time integer |
| 2 | Address of a parameter (caller-frame based) | $addr(p) = FP_{caller} + \delta_p$ | $\delta_p > 0$ if above FP |
| 3 | Depth of lexical nesting | $depth(p) = depth(parent) + 1$ | root depth = 0 |
| 4 | Static link walk cost (k levels) | $T_{access} = k \cdot T_{mem}$ | $T_{mem}$ = memory access time |
| 5 | Display access cost | $T_{access} = 1 \cdot T_{mem}$ | constant time |
| 6 | Stack size at any moment | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{D} \vert AR_i \vert$ | $D$ = current call depth |
| 7 | Minimum stack space required | $S_{min} = D_{max} \cdot \vert AR_{max} \vert$ | $D_{max}$ = max recursion depth |
| 8 | Frame pointer update on call | $FP_{new} = SP_{after\_params}$ | after parameter area is set up |
| 9 | Stack pointer growth (push) | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - \vert item \vert$ | grows toward lower address |
| 10 | Return address offset from FP (callee) | $RA = FP + \Delta_{RA}$ | typically $\Delta_{RA} = +word$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Conventions:** All offsets $\delta$ are in **words** (W). Stack grows **down** on x86/ARM/RISC-V. Frame Pointer (FP) conventionally points to the **saved FP slot** (the boundary between caller and callee data).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The procedure-call machinery is the backbone of:

- **Operating Systems:** System calls (`fork`, `execve`, `read`, `write`) are procedure calls across the user-kernel boundary. The calling convention ensures ABI compatibility.
- **Virtual Machines:** JVM, CLR, and WebAssembly maintain a **stack frame** per method, with virtually identical field semantics.
- **Functional Languages:** Tail-call optimization is an aggressive procedure-call transformation that reuses the *current* AR instead of allocating a new one — it is mathematically equivalent to a `goto`.
- **Debuggers & Profilers:** Tools like `gdb`, `perf`, and `VTune` reconstruct *call stacks* by walking dynamic links — the exact same pointers the compiler emits.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through three **KTU-style exhaustive analyses**:
1. **Derivation A:** Stack frame layout and offset calculation for a representative procedure.
2. **Derivation B:** Static-link chain traversal to access a non-local variable.
3. **Derivation C:** Display-array update rules for nested procedure calls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Assumption:** Word size W = 4 bytes; stack grows toward lower addresses; FP points to the saved-FP slot; the return address is pushed by the caller *before* the callee's prologue.

## 3.1 Derivation A — Stack Frame Construction for a Sample Procedure

Consider the following Pascal-like pseudo-procedure (assume all integers are 4-byte words):

```
procedure P(a: integer; b: integer);
var
    x: integer;
    y: integer;
begin
    x := a + b;
    y := a * b;
end;
```

### Step 1: Enumerate the AR fields and assign offsets relative to FP.

The compiler lays out the AR such that **higher offsets are at higher addresses**. We assume the caller's stack at the moment of the CALL has the parameter area already placed. We adopt the convention that **positive offsets from FP point to caller-owned data (parameters, return address)** and **negative offsets point to callee-owned data (saved regs, locals)**.

Field ordering (top to bottom = high to low address):

| Field | Size (words) | Offset from FP |
|---|---|---|
| Parameter `a` | 1 | $+5$ |
| Parameter `b` | 1 | $+4$ |
| Return Address | 1 | $+3$ |
| Saved FP (Dynamic Link) | 1 | $+2$ |
| Static Link (if needed) | 1 | $+1$ |
| Saved Register (if any) | 1 | $-1$ |
| Local `x` | 1 | $-2$ |
| Local `y` | 1 | $-3$ |

*(The exact offsets depend on calling convention; this layout is illustrative.)*

### Step 2: Compute the absolute address of `x` and `y` at runtime.

$$
addr(x) = FP + (-2) = FP - 2W
$$

$$
addr(y) = FP + (-3) = FP - 3W
$$

**Conversion logic:** A *negative* offset means the field is *below* FP in memory (callee's own area), so we subtract. The factor $W$ converts a word count into bytes.

### Step 3: Derive the total frame size.

$$
\vert AR_P \vert = (\text{params}) + (\text{RA}) + (\text{Saved FP}) + (\text{Static Link}) + (\text{Saved Reg}) + (\text{locals})
$$

$$
\vert AR_P \vert = 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 8 \text{ words}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \vert AR_P \vert_{bytes} = 8W = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 4: Derive the caller's stack pointer update.

After the caller pushes the parameters and the return address, but **before** the callee's prologue, the stack looks like:

$$
SP_{precall} = FP_{caller} - 0 \quad \text{(params are above caller's FP at offsets +1, +2)}
$$

After `CALL`, control transfers to callee. The callee's prologue does:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP_{new} &= SP_{old} - 1 \quad &\text{(push saved FP)} \\
FP_{new} &= SP_{new} \quad &\text{(FP now points to saved FP slot)} \\
SP_{new} &= SP_{new} - 1 \quad &\text{(push static link)} \\
SP_{new} &= SP_{new} - 1 \quad &\text{(push saved register)} \\
SP_{new} &= SP_{new} - 2 \quad &\text{(allocate locals } x, y\text{)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Net effect: $SP$ decreases by $5$ words during the prologue (matching the 5 callee-owned fields).

### Step 5: Address translation for `a` and `b`.

The parameters live in the **caller's** frame, *above* the saved FP. They are accessed as:

$$
addr(a) = FP_{new} + 5W
$$

$$
addr(b) = FP_{new} + 4W
$$

**Conversion logic:** The positive offset means the parameter area is at a *higher* address than FP. The compiler emits, e.g., `load r1, 20[FP]` for `a` (since $5W = 20$ bytes for $W=4$).

## 3.2 Derivation B — Static Link Chain Traversal

Consider the following nested procedure (Pascal-style):

```
program Main;
var m: integer;

procedure A;
    var x: integer;
    procedure B;
        var y: integer;
        begin
            y := m + x;
        end;
    begin
        ...
        B;
        ...
    end;

begin
    ...
    A;
    ...
end.
```

### Step 1: Establish lexical depth.

$$
depth(\text{Main}) = 0, \quad depth(A) = 1, \quad depth(B) = 2
$$

### Step 2: Construct the static link of each AR.

When $A$ is called from Main:

$$
\text{StaticLink}(AR_A) = AR_{Main}
$$

When $B$ is called from $A$:

$$
\text{StaticLink}(AR_B) = AR_A
$$

### Step 3: Trace access to `m` from inside B.

`m` is declared in Main, which is **2 lexical levels above** B (B → A → Main). We must follow the static link **2 times**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Temp}_1 &= *(\text{StaticLink}(AR_B)) \quad &\text{// gives } AR_A \\
\text{Temp}_2 &= *(\text{StaticLink}(\text{Temp}_1)) \quad &\text{// gives } AR_{Main} \\
\text{addr}(m) &= \text{Temp}_2 + \delta_m &
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Each dereference of a static link moves us one lexical level outward. After $k$ dereferences, we reach the AR at depth $depth(current) - k$.

### Step 4: Cost analysis.

$$
T_{access}(m) = 2 \cdot T_{deref} + T_{offset} = 2 \cdot T_{mem} + T_{add}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The access time is **linear in lexical distance**, not call depth. This is the key disadvantage of static links.

## 3.3 Derivation C — Display Array Maintenance on a Nested Call

Using the same `Main → A → B` hierarchy with **Display-based access**.

### Step 1: Initialize the display.

On program start:

$$
display[0] = AR_{Main}, \quad display[1..n] = \text{NULL}
$$

### Step 2: When A is called (lexical depth 1).

Save the *current* $display[1]$ (which is NULL) into the AR of A (in the "saved display" slot), then update:

$$
display[1] = AR_A
$$

### Step 3: When B is called (lexical depth 2).

Save the current $display[2]$ (NULL) into B's AR, then:

$$
display[2] = AR_B
$$

### Step 4: Access `m` from B.

`m` is at lexical depth 0, so:

$$
addr(m) = display[0] + \delta_m
$$

This is a **single indirection** — constant time.

### Step 5: When B returns.

Restore the saved $display[2]$ value:

$$
display[2] = \text{savedDisplay}_{2}(AR_B) \quad (\text{which was NULL})
$$

### Step 6: General rule for entry/exit of a procedure at depth $d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{On Entry: } & \text{AR.savedDisplay}[d] = display[d] \\
                    & display[d] = AR_{new} \\
\text{On Exit: }  & display[d] = AR.savedDisplay}[d]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The display is updated *only* for the depth of the called procedure. Lower depths are untouched, preserving their pointers to outer ARs.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Calling Convention Trade-offs

| Strategy | Register-Save Scheme | Code Size | Function Call Overhead | Recursive Friendliness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Caller-Save All | Caller saves every used reg. | Large | High | Poor (lots of saving) |
| Callee-Save All | Callee saves every used reg. | Small | Low (no save if not used) | Better |
| Hybrid (MIPS/x86) | Mixed: some callee-save, some caller-save. | Medium | Optimized | Best |
| Display Method | Extra $O(d)$ display array; O(1) non-local access. | Medium | O(d) on entry/exit | Best |
| Static-Link Method | One extra pointer per AR; O(k) non-local access. | Small | O(1) on entry | Good |
| Tail-Call Optimization | Reuse current AR. | Tiny | None | Excellent |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Activation Record — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **canonical activation record layout**, its field relationships, and how the dynamic link and static link connect ARs in the call chain.

```mermaid
graph TD
    callerFrame["CALLER FRAME top of AR"]
    retAddr["RETURN ADDRESS slot"]
    dynLink["SAVED FP DYNAMIC LINK"]
    statLink["STATIC LINK pointer to enclosing AR"]
    savedReg["SAVED REGISTERS area"]
    locals["LOCAL DATA x y temporaries"]
    params["PARAMETERS area a b c"]

    callerFrame --> params
    params --> retAddr
    retAddr --> dynLink
    dynLink --> statLink
    statLink --> savedReg
    savedReg --> locals
    locals --> nextAR["NEXT AR for called procedure lower address"]

    dynLink -. points to .-> prevFP["CALLER saved FP slot"]
    statLink -. points to .-> enclAR["LEXICAL PARENT AR"]
```

## 4.2 Calling Sequence — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["CALLER executes call statement"] --> A1["Evaluate actual parameters"]
    A1 --> A2["Push parameters onto stack in reverse order"]
    A2 --> A3["Push RETURN ADDRESS"]
    A3 --> A4["Save caller saved registers"]
    A4 --> A5["JUMP to callee entry"]

    A5 --> B1["CALLEE PROLOGUE Push old FP onto stack"]
    B1 --> B2["Set FP equals SP new frame base"]
    B2 --> B3["Push STATIC LINK to enclosing AR"]
    B3 --> B4["Save callee saved registers"]
    B4 --> B5["Allocate local variables SP minus size of locals"]
    B5 --> B6["Execute callee body"]

    B6 --> C1["CALLEE EPILOGUE Place return value in designated register"]
    C1 --> C2["Restore callee saved registers"]
    C2 --> C3["Set SP equals FP"]
    C3 --> C4["Pop saved FP into FP register"]
    C4 --> C5["RETURN jump to saved return address"]

    C5 --> D1["CALLER POST RETURN Restore caller saved registers"]
    D1 --> D2["Pop parameter area if needed"]
    D2 --> D3["Use return value and resume execution"]
```

## 4.3 Static Link vs. Display — Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph STATIC_LINK["STATIC LINK METHOD"]
        SL1["AR B depth 2"] -->|"static link"| SL2["AR A depth 1"]
        SL2 -->|"static link"| SL3["AR Main depth 0"]
        SL1 -. "O(2) walk to reach m" .-> SL3
    end

    subgraph DISPLAY["DISPLAY ARRAY METHOD"]
        D0["display 0 equals AR Main"]
        D1["display 1 equals AR A"]
        D2["display 2 equals AR B"]
        D0 --> D3["AR Main"]
        D1 --> D4["AR A"]
        D2 --> D5["AR B"]
        D2 -. "O(1) lookup via display 0" .-> D3
    end
```

## 4.4 Nested Call Tree with AR Allocation

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0["Program Main depth 0"] -->|"call A"| P1["Procedure A depth 1"]
    P1 -->|"call B"| P2["Procedure B depth 2"]
    P2 -->|"return"| P1
    P1 -->|"call C"| P3["Procedure C depth 2"]
    P3 -->|"return"| P1
    P1 -->|"return"| P0

    P0 -. "static link NULL" .-> P0
    P1 -. "static link to Main" .-> P0
    P2 -. "static link to A" .-> P1
    P3 -. "static link to A" .-> P1
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Call Stack at Runtime (Snapshot)

```mermaid
graph TD
    TOP["Higher addresses"] --> AR1["AR of Main params RA saved FP display 0 display 1 display 2 locals"]
    AR1 --> AR2["AR of A params RA saved FP static link to Main locals"]
    AR2 --> AR3["AR of B params RA saved FP static link to A locals"]
    AR3 --> AR4["AR of D params RA saved FP static link to B locals"]
    AR4 --> BOTTOM["Lower addresses SP points here"]

    AR1 -. "display 1 points here" .-> AR2
    AR2 -. "static link points here" .-> AR1
    AR3 -. "static link points here" .-> AR2
    AR4 -. "static link points here" .-> AR3
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below mirror the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Part A carries **3 marks each**; Part B carries **14 marks each** with an internal choice (a) and (b) sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define an Activation Record. List any four fields typically stored in it and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Activation Record (AR)**, also called a *stack frame*, is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the runtime stack for every active invocation of a procedure. It stores all information necessary to:
- Execute the procedure correctly,
- Restore the caller's state on return, and
- Access both local and non-local data.

**Four typical fields:**

1. **Return Address** — the address of the instruction in the caller where execution must resume after the callee returns. Without it, control would be lost on return.
2. **Saved Frame Pointer (Dynamic Link)** — the value of the FP register in the caller's frame. It is restored into FP during the callee's epilogue, enabling the caller to resume using its own locals.
3. **Static Link (Access Link)** — a pointer to the AR of the *statically enclosing* procedure. Required in languages with lexical scoping and nested procedures (Pascal, Ada) to access non-local variables efficiently.
4. **Local Data Area** — storage for the procedure's own local variables, parameters (in some conventions), and intermediate temporaries generated by the code generator.

> **Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition 1M + 4 fields × 0.5M = 2M + examples 1M = 3M.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between "Call by Value" and "Call by Reference". Which is safer and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Call by Value | Call by Reference |
|---|---|---|
| What is passed? | A *copy* of the actual argument's value. | The *address* (reference) of the actual argument. |
| Effect on actual | Original is **not modified**. | Original **can be modified**. |
| Aliasing | No aliasing. | Possible aliasing of multiple parameters. |
| Cost | Extra copy operation. | Indirection cost (one extra load). |
| Safety | **Safer** (no accidental mutation). | Less safe (mutation visible to caller). |

**Call by value is safer** because the callee cannot inadvertently or maliciously modify the caller's variable. A copy is created, and any mutations affect only the local copy, leaving the original untouched. Languages like C and Java use call-by-value for primitives, while Pascal's `var` parameters and C++ references use call-by-reference.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] — 14 Marks

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the different parameter passing mechanisms used in programming languages. Compare them with suitable examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Call by Value:**
The actual parameter is *evaluated*, and its value is copied into the callee's formal parameter location. The callee operates on the copy.

```
void swap(int a, int b) {  // a, b are COPIES
    int t = a; a = b; b = t;
}
int x = 5, y = 10;
swap(x, y);  // x, y unchanged
```

- **Advantage:** No side effects; secure.
- **Disadvantage:** Expensive for large structs (copy cost); cannot return multiple values.

**2. Call by Reference:**
The *address* of the actual parameter is passed. The callee can read and write the original.

```
void swap(int &a, int &b) {  // a, b are references
    int t = a; a = b; b = t;
}
```

- **Advantage:** Efficient (no copy); enables "out" parameters.
- **Disadvantage:** Aliasing; can cause unexpected side effects.

**3. Call by Value-Result (Copy-Restore):**
A copy is passed (like by value), but on return, the final value of the formal is *copied back* into the actual.

- **Advantage:** Avoids aliasing within the call; can act as an "out" parameter.
- **Disadvantage:** Order of copy-back at multiple parameters is implementation-defined; subtle bugs.

**4. Call by Name:**
The argument is *textually substituted* into the callee's body (Algol 60 thunks). Each use of the formal re-evaluates the actual.

- **Advantage:** Powerful (lazy evaluation, Jensen's device).
- **Disadvantage:** Extremely inefficient; multiple evaluations; hard to reason about.

**5. Call by Sharing (Object Reference):**
A reference to the *object* is passed; the object's fields can be mutated, but rebinding the formal has no effect on the caller. Used in Java, Python, Ruby.

```
def f(lst):
    lst.append(99)   # mutates caller's list
f([1,2,3])  # list is now [1,2,3,99]
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Mechanism | Mutation Visible? | Aliasing? | Re-evaluation? | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value | No | No | No | C, Java primitives |
| Reference | Yes | Yes | No | Pascal `var`, C++ `&` |
| Value-Result | Yes | No | No | Older Fortran |
| Name | Yes | Yes | **Yes** | Algol 60 |
| Sharing | Yes (fields) | Yes (same object) | No | Java, Python |

> **Examiner's Tip (7 marks):** Allocate 1.5M for each of the 4 main mechanisms (definition + example + 1 advantage/disadvantage) and 1M for the summary comparison.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**What is a Display? Explain how it is used to access non-local variables in languages with nested procedures. Why is it more efficient than the static-link method?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
A **Display** is a global array of pointers, indexed by *lexical depth*, where `display[d]` points to the most recently activated AR at lexical nesting level `d`. It is maintained cooperatively by the calling sequence.

**Maintenance on a Call:**
When a procedure of lexical depth $d$ is called:
1. Save the *current* `display[d]` into the new AR (in a "saved display" slot).
2. Set `display[d] = new_AR`.

**Maintenance on Return:**
1. Restore `display[d]` from the saved value in the outgoing AR.

**Accessing a Non-Local Variable:**
A variable $v$ declared at depth $d_v$ is accessed from within a procedure at depth $d_{cur}$ by computing:

$$
addr(v) = display[d_v] + \delta_v
$$

where $\delta_v$ is the compile-time offset of $v$ within its enclosing AR. This is a **single indirection** — O(1) regardless of how deeply nested the access is.

**Example:**
Consider a program with `Main (depth 0) → A (depth 1) → B (depth 2)`. From inside B, accessing a variable `m` declared in Main:

$$
addr(m) = display[0] + \delta_m
$$

Even though there are 2 levels of static nesting between B and the declaration of `m`, access requires only **one memory read** (the `display[0]` fetch) plus one offset addition.

**Efficiency Comparison with Static Links:**

| Aspect | Static Link | Display |
|---|---|---|
| Non-local access time | O(k) — walk k links | **O(1)** — single indirection |
| Per-call overhead | Store 1 pointer (static link) | Store 1 pointer (saved display[d]) + update display |
| Memory per AR | One extra word | One extra word + global array of size D |
| Code complexity | Simple to generate | Requires global array management |

**Why Display is more efficient for repeated access:**
If a procedure at depth 2 accesses 100 non-local variables from depth 0, the static-link method must perform 100 × 2 = 200 pointer dereferences. The display method performs 100 × 1 = 100 dereferences. For deeply nested programs with frequent non-local access, the display's constant-time access dominates the marginal per-call cost.

> **Examiner's Tip (7 marks):** 2M for definition, 2M for maintenance rules, 2M for access mechanism, 1M for the comparison.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4] — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Draw the layout of a typical Activation Record. Describe the function of each field. Explain the role of the dynamic link and the static link with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Layout of a Typical Activation Record (high to low address):**

| Offset from FP | Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| $+(n+1)$ | Parameter $n$ | W | Last parameter |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | W | $\vdots$ |
| $+4$ | Parameter 1 | W | First parameter |
| $+3$ | Return Address | W | Resumption point in caller |
| $+2$ | Saved FP (Dynamic Link) | W | Restores caller's FP on return |
| $+1$ | Static Link | W | Points to lexical parent's AR |
| $-1$ | Saved Registers | k·W | Callee-saved register values |
| $\vdots$ | Local variables | varies | Procedure's own data |
| $-(k+n)$ | Temporaries | varies | Code-generator scratch space |

**Field Functions:**

1. **Parameters:** Hold the actual arguments pushed by the caller.
2. **Return Address:** Where control resumes after the callee returns. Set by the caller's `CALL` instruction.
3. **Saved FP (Dynamic Link):** The chain that lets us walk back through the *dynamic* call stack. On return, the callee pops this into FP.
4. **Static Link:** The chain that lets us access variables in the *statically enclosing* scope. Independent of the dynamic call chain.
5. **Saved Registers:** Preserved register values (callee-save convention).
6. **Local Data:** Local variables and temporaries.

**Dynamic Link Example:**
If `Main` calls `A`, `A` calls `B`, and `B` calls `C`, the dynamic links form:

$$
AR_C \xrightarrow{\text{dyn}} AR_B \xrightarrow{\text{dyn}} AR_A \xrightarrow{\text{dyn}} AR_{Main}
$$

When `C` returns, the dynamic link is followed *upward* to restore `B`'s frame.

**Static Link Example:**
If the lexical structure is `Main ⊃ P ⊃ Q`, and `P` calls `R`, and `R` calls `Q`, then:

- $AR_R$ has static link → $AR_P$ (R is lexically inside P).
- $AR_Q$ has static link → $AR_P$ (Q is lexically inside P, even though it is dynamically called from R).

The static link ignores the *dynamic* call path; it always points to the *lexical* parent.

> **Examiner's Tip (7 marks):** 2M for diagram, 2M for field descriptions, 1.5M for dynamic link example, 1.5M for static link example.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the calling sequence and return sequence for procedure calls. Discuss how the work is divided between the caller and the callee to minimize overhead.**

**Model Solution:**

**Calling Sequence (executed before the jump to the callee):**

1. **Caller evaluates actual parameters** and stores them in the parameter slots of the callee's AR (often by pushing them in reverse order).
2. **Caller pushes the return address** — the location of the instruction immediately after the call.
3. **Caller saves any caller-saved registers** it will need after the call.
4. **Caller executes the CALL/JAL instruction** to transfer control to the callee.

**Callee Prologue (executed at the callee's entry):**

1. **Push the old FP** onto the stack (becomes the dynamic link).
2. **Set FP = SP** (FP now anchors the new frame).
3. **Push the static link** (a copy of the caller's display entry or static link, depending on the language).
4. **Save callee-saved registers** that the procedure will overwrite.
5. **Decrement SP** to allocate the local variable area.

**Callee Epilogue (executed just before return):**

1. **Place the return value** in a designated register (EAX/RAX on x86).
2. **Restore callee-saved registers** from their slots in the AR.
3. **Set SP = FP** (this pops the entire callee frame in one step — efficient).
4. **Pop the saved FP** into the FP register (restoring caller's frame).
5. **Execute RETURN** (jump to the saved return address).

**Caller Post-Return:**

1. **Restore caller-saved registers.**
2. **Pop the parameter area** (if not already consumed by the callee's epilogue).
3. **Use the return value** and resume.

**Work Division Principle:**

The split is designed so that **fixed-size work is done inline by the caller** (e.g., pushing a fixed number of parameters), while **variable-size work is done by the callee** (e.g., allocating locals of unknown size). Specifically:

| Task | Owner | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Parameter push | Caller | Caller knows the number statically |
| Return address push | Caller | Caller knows its own PC |
| Dynamic link push | Callee | Standard at entry of all procs |
| Static link push | Callee | Depends on caller's display |
| Local allocation | Callee | May be variable size |
| Callee-saved register save | Callee | Only callee knows which it needs |
| Frame teardown (SP = FP) | Callee | One bulk operation |
| Caller-saved register restore | Caller | Only caller knows which it needs |

**Optimization — Tail-Call Optimization:**
If the last action of a procedure is a call to another procedure (or to itself recursively), the callee's AR can *reuse* the caller's AR. This eliminates the overhead of pushing/popping a new frame, achieving true tail-recursion in O(1) space per call.

> **Examiner's Tip (7 marks):** 2M for calling sequence steps, 2M for return sequence, 2M for work-division table, 1M for the tail-call optimization note.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Evaluations for Procedure Calls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Dynamic Link and Static Link (–2 marks typical):** Students often write "static link points to the caller" — this is wrong. The static link points to the **lexical parent**, which may not be the dynamic caller. Always clarify the distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to save the display in the AR (–1 mark):** When describing display maintenance, students often omit the "save the old display[d] into the new AR" step. Without this, restoring on return is impossible.
> 3. **Wrong stack growth direction (–1 mark):** On x86/ARM/RISC-V the stack grows **down**. Some students assume upward growth, leading to inverted diagrams.
> 4. **Mixing call-by-value-result and call-by-reference semantics (–1.5 marks):** Value-result does NOT have aliasing during the call (the formal is a copy); reference DOES. This subtle difference is frequently tested.
> 5. **Omitting the FP/SP distinction (–1 mark):** Some answers assume `SP` is constant during a procedure — this is only true if no `alloca` or dynamic locals are used. Otherwise, **FP-based addressing is mandatory**.
> 6. **Forgetting that the static link chain is set up *at call time*, not at declaration time (–1 mark):** The static link is a runtime value, computed by the calling sequence.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (final read-through before KTU exam):**

- **Activation Record (AR) = Stack Frame** — one AR per live procedure call.
- **AR fields (high → low address):** Parameters → Return Address → Saved FP (Dynamic Link) → Static Link → Saved Registers → Local Data → Temporaries.
- **Dynamic Link** = pointer to **caller's** AR (dynamic parent).
- **Static Link** = pointer to **lexical parent's** AR (static parent). Lexical ≠ dynamic.
- **Calling Sequence** = Caller's prelude + Callee's prologue.
- **Return Sequence** = Callee's epilogue + Caller's postlude.
- **Frame Pointer (FP)** = stable base for offsets (since SP can move); FP points to the **saved-FP slot**.
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** = always points to the *top* of the stack (grows downward).
- **Work Division:** Fixed-size tasks → caller; variable-size tasks → callee.
- **Parameter Passing Mechanisms:** Value, Reference, Value-Result, Name, Sharing.
- **Static Link access cost:** O(k) where k = lexical distance.
- **Display access cost:** O(1) — a single array index.
- **Display Update Rule:** On entry, `display[d] = new_AR` after saving the old value; on exit, restore the saved value.
- **Tail-Call Optimization:** Reuse the current AR if the call is the last action → O(1) space recursion.
- **Standard Metrics:** Word size W (4B or 8B); stack grows toward **lower** addresses on x86/ARM/RISC-V.
- **ABI Relevance:** Calling conventions are part of the **Application Binary Interface**; mismatched conventions cause crashes.
- **Real-World Linkage:** System calls (POSIX), JVM/CLR method calls, WebAssembly functions all use stack-frame semantics with caller/callee-save register protocols.

---

**End of Module 4 — Procedure Calls | KTU 2024 Scheme PECIT751**
