topics.title: Code Optimization - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Code Optimization - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Compiler Design (PECIT751)**, **Code Optimization** is formally defined as a phase of the compiler that attempts to improve the intermediate code (or the target machine code) so that the resulting program executes **faster**, occupies **less memory**, and consumes **less power**, without altering the program's observable output behaviour.

Mathematically, for any source program $P$ with intermediate representation $IR$, the optimizer transforms $IR \rightarrow IR'$ such that:

$$\forall\, input\, x : \llbracket IR \rrbracket(x) = \llbracket IR' \rrbracket(x)$$

while minimizing a cost function $C(IR')$, where $C$ typically measures execution time or space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim weightage):** "Code optimization is the technique used to improve the intermediate code so that faster executing target code is generated with less resource consumption, while preserving the meaning of the program."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are writing an essay by hand. After the first draft, you read through it and:
- Remove repeated sentences (**Common Subexpression Elimination**)
- Combine short sentences that say the same thing (**Copy Propagation / Folding**)
- Throw away paragraphs that add no value (**Dead Code Elimination**)
- Replace "very very big" with "enormous" (**Strength Reduction**)

The essay means the **exact same thing**, but it is **shorter, sharper, and faster to read**. This post-writing cleanup is precisely what a compiler does in the **Code Optimization phase**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimization ≠ Best Solution.** A compiler optimizer does not always produce the mathematically optimal code (that is an NP-hard problem in general). It applies safe, locally-reasoned transformations that **provably preserve correctness**.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

The two standard metrics every KTU student must memorize are:

| Metric | Symbol | Goal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Execution Time** | $T_{exec}$ | Minimize |
| **Space (Memory)** | $S_{code}$ | Minimize |
| **Power / Energy** | $E_{cpu}$ | Minimize |

### 1.4 Classification Overview

Optimizations are broadly classified along three axes in the KTU syllabus:

1. **Scope of Application** — Local, Regional (within a basic block), Global (across basic blocks).
2. **Dependency on Machine** — Machine-Independent vs Machine-Dependent.
3. **Timing in Pipeline** — Intermediate (Source-level) vs Target (Assembly-level).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Point:** The KTU 2024 syllabus frequently asks students to distinguish between **Machine-Dependent** and **Machine-Independent** optimizations. Peephole optimization falls in the former category; common subexpression elimination and dead-code elimination fall in the latter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Where Does Optimization Sit in the Compiler Pipeline?

The KTU 2024 scheme positions optimization as a bridge between the **front-end (analysis)** and the **back-end (synthesis)** phases.

**Logical Steps executed during optimization:**

- **Step 1 — Intermediate Representation Selection:** Choose a suitable IR (Three-Address Code, TAC, or Control Flow Graph) that exposes optimization opportunities.
- **Step 2 — Control Flow Analysis:** Partition the IR into **Basic Blocks** — maximal sequences of three-address instructions with a single entry and single exit.
- **Step 3 — Data Flow Analysis:** Gather information about how values are defined and used across the program (reachability, liveness, availability).
- **Step 4 — Transformation:** Apply correctness-preserving rewrite rules to the IR.
- **Step 5 — Validation:** Ensure semantic equivalence with the original program before emission.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Transformation

| Transformation | Core "Why" | Observable Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) | Avoid recomputation of an expression whose value is already available. | Reduces redundant ALU operations. |
| Dead Code Elimination (DCE) | Remove instructions whose results are never consumed. | Lowers code size and execution time. |
| Constant Folding / Propagation | Replace variables bound to constants with the literal value. | Eliminates memory loads and indirection. |
| Strength Reduction | Replace expensive operations (multiplication, exponentiation) with cheaper ones (addition, shift). | Lowers per-instruction CPU cost. |
| Loop Unrolling / Invariant Code Motion | Reduce loop branch overhead; hoist invariant statements outside the loop. | Improves instruction-level parallelism and cache locality. |
| Copy Propagation | If $x = y$ and $x$ is used next, replace $x$ with $y$. | Enables subsequent DCE and CSE. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Use `\vert` for absolute values to keep markdown tables intact.

| Concept / Term | Expression / Rule | Boundary / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Basic Block | $B = (I_{entry},\, I_1,\, I_2,\, \dots,\, I_{exit})$ | Single entry, single exit, no internal jumps except the exit. |
| Leader (start of a BB) | $L_1$ = first instr; $L_i$ = target of jump; $L_j$ = instr immediately after a jump. | Used to partition TAC into BBs. |
| Flow Graph Nodes | $G = (N,\, E)$ where $N$ = set of basic blocks, $E$ = edges representing control transfer. | Directed graph. |
| Execution Cost of a Block | $C(B) = \sum_{i \in B} cost(I_i)$ | Sum of per-instruction cycle costs. |
| Strength Reduction (Mul → Shift) | $x \cdot 2^{k} \;\Longrightarrow\; x \ll k$ | Valid only for unsigned / 2's complement power-of-two multipliers. |
| Strength Reduction (Mul → Add) | $x \cdot c \;\Longrightarrow\; (x \ll \lfloor \log_{2}c \rfloor) + (x \cdot (c - 2^{\lfloor\log_{2}c\rfloor}))$ | For small integer constants $c$. |
| Loop Invariant Code Motion | If $I$ computes a value not modified inside loop $L$, hoist $I$ to preheader. | Requires loop entry dominance. |
| Optimization Correctness | $\llbracket IR \rrbracket = \llbracket IR' \rrbracket$ | Must hold for **all** inputs. |
| Cycle Cost (Peephole) | $T_{opt} = T_{orig} - T_{saved\,instr}$ | Peephole savings are local (window $\le 5$ instructions). |
| Reachability | $Reach(B) = \{ \text{definitions that reach } B \,\}$ | Used in available-expressions analysis. |
| Liveness | $Live_{out}(B) = \bigcup_{S \in succ(B)} (Use(S) \cup (Live_{out}(S) - Def(S)))$ | Standard iterative equation. |
| Absolute Value | $\vert x \vert$ | Use `\vert` to avoid breaking tables. |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Industry Application:** GCC and LLVM use exactly these transformations at `-O2` and `-O3` levels. GCC's flag `-fmove-loop-invariants` implements loop invariant code motion; LLVM's `InstCombine` pass performs peephole-style rewrites at the IR level.
- **Embedded & Systems Engineering:** For ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers, strength reduction and dead-code elimination are critical to fit within tight **Flash** and **RAM** budgets.
- **High-Performance Computing:** Loop transformations such as tiling and interchange directly stem from the basic-block and flow-graph theory covered in this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Three-Address Code to Basic Blocks (Worked Example)

Consider the following TAC snippet — this is the exact style used in KTU 2024 question papers.

```
1)  t1 = a * b
2)  t2 = t1 + c
3)  t3 = a * b
4)  t4 = t3 + c
5)  t5 = t4 + d
6)  if t5 > 100 goto L10
7)  t6 = a * b
8)  t7 = t6 + c
9)  t8 = t7 + d
10) L10:
```

**Step 1 — Identify Leaders.**
A leader is the first instruction of a basic block. Leaders are:
- The very first instruction (instruction 1).
- Any target of a conditional/unconditional jump (instruction 10, target of `goto L10`).
- Any instruction that immediately follows a conditional/unconditional jump (instruction 7, immediately follows the jump in instruction 6).

**Step 2 — Form the Basic Blocks.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \{ 1,\, 2,\, 3,\, 4,\, 5,\, 6 \} \\
B_2 &= \{ 7,\, 8,\, 9 \} \\
B_3 &= \{ 10 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Flow Graph.**
Edges are drawn from a block to:
- The block beginning with the next instruction (fall-through).
- The block whose leader is the target of a jump.

Thus, $B_1 \rightarrow B_2$ (fall-through after `goto L10` evaluates false), and $B_1 \rightarrow B_3$ (jump target).

### 3.2 Derivation: Common Subexpression Elimination on the Above Blocks

**Step 1 — Identify available expressions at the start of $B_2$.**
Reaching definitions from $B_1$:
- $t1 = a * b$ reaches the end of $B_1$ because it is not redefined inside $B_1$.
- $t2 = t1 + c$ also reaches.
- $t3 = a * b$ is a new definition that overrides $t1$.

**Step 2 — Rewrite $B_2$.**
Inside $B_2$, `t6 = a * b` is the same expression as the one computed in $t1$. If $a$ and $b$ are not redefined between the end of $B_1$ and instruction 7, then $t6$ can be replaced by $t1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original: } &\quad t6 = a * b; \;\; t7 = t6 + c; \;\; t8 = t7 + d \\
\text{After CSE: } &\quad t6 = t1; \;\; t7 = t2; \;\; t8 = t5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Copy Propagation.**
Since $t6 = t1$, replace every later use of $t6$ with $t1$. Similarly $t7 = t2 \rightarrow$ use $t2$ directly, and $t8 = t5 \rightarrow$ use $t5$.

**Step 4 — Apply Dead Code Elimination.**
Statements 7, 8, 9 in $B_2$ are now trivial copies. Their definitions $t6, t7, t8$ are overwritten without being used for any visible effect — they are **dead**.

Therefore, the **entire block $B_2$ can be eliminated**, provided its only purpose was to recompute already-available values.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cost-Benefit of Standard Optimizations

For a program $P$ with $n$ basic blocks and $e$ edges, the KTU-expected complexity bounds are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CSE (local)} &: \quad O(n \cdot k^{2}) \quad \text{where } k = \text{max instructions per block}. \\
\text{DCE (iterative)} &: \quad O(n \cdot e \cdot I_{max}) \quad \text{where } I_{max} = \text{max instruction count}. \\
\text{Liveness Analysis} &: \quad O(n \cdot e) \quad \text{(converges in } \le (n \cdot e) \text{ iterations worst case).} \\
\text{Peephole (sliding window)} &: \quad O(n \cdot w) \quad \text{where } w = \text{window size (typically 5).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of each line:** Local CSE runs over a constant-size basic block, so cost is bounded by the square of block length. Iterative DCE must revisit until a fixed-point is reached. Liveness uses the iterative data-flow equations to a fixed point.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Peephole Optimization (Algebraic Simplification)

**Input Peephole Window:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t1 &= x + 0 \\
t2 &= y \cdot 1 \\
t3 &= z \cdot 0 \\
t4 &= a - a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output After Peephole Pass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t1 &= x \\
t2 &= y \\
t3 &= 0 \\
t4 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
- Adding zero to $x$ is the identity — `t1` becomes $x$.
- Multiplying by 1 is the identity — `t2` becomes $y$.
- Multiplying by 0 always yields 0 — `t3` becomes the constant 0.
- Subtracting a value from itself yields 0 — `t4` becomes 0.

These algebraic identities are valid under IEEE-754 for finite values, but peephole optimizers typically insert **NaN-aware guards** before replacing $z \cdot 0$ if the language semantics demand it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Position of the Optimization Phase in a Standard Compiler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Code] --> lex[Lexical Analyzer]
    lex --> syn[Syntax Analyzer]
    syn --> sem[Semantic Analyzer]
    sem --> ir1[Intermediate Representation]
    ir1 --> opt[Machine Independent Optimization]
    opt --> ir2[Optimized IR]
    ir2 --> codeGen[Code Generator]
    ir2 --> peep[Peephole Optimization]
    peep --> tgt[Target Machine Code]
    codeGen --> asm[Assembly]
    asm --> peep
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The flow proceeds left-to-right.
- Optimization operates on the intermediate representation.
- Peephole optimization is shown twice: once at the IR level (machine-independent) and once at the assembly level (machine-dependent), reflecting its dual usage.

### 4.2 Classification of Code Optimizations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    opt[Code Optimization]
    opt --> mi[Machine Independent]
    opt --> md[Machine Dependent]

    mi --> cse[Common Subexpression Elimination]
    mi --> dce[Dead Code Elimination]
    mi --> cf[Constant Folding]
    mi --> cp[Copy Propagation]
    mi --> licm[Loop Invariant Code Motion]
    mi --> sr[Strength Reduction]

    md --> peep[Peephole Optimization]
    md --> reg[Register Allocation]
    md --> sched[Instruction Scheduling]
    md --> cache[Cache / Pipeline Optimization]

    cse --> scope1[Scope Local vs Global]
    dce --> scope1
    cf --> scope1
    licm --> scope1
    peep --> scope2[Window of 2 to 5 instructions]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- All optimizations split into machine-independent (logic-level) and machine-dependent (target-aware).
- Scope (local vs global) applies only to the machine-independent family.
- Peephole has its own characteristic: a small sliding window.

### 4.3 Flow Graph Construction from TAC (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    entry([Entry]) --> B1["Basic Block B1: t1=a*b; t2=t1+c; t3=a*b; t4=t3+c; t5=t4+d; if t5 greater than 100 goto L10"]
    B1 -- true branch --> B3["Basic Block B3: L10 (target)"]
    B1 -- false branch --> B2["Basic Block B2: t6=a*b; t7=t6+c; t8=t7+d"]
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> exit([Exit])
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- $B_1$ has two outgoing edges — the true branch goes to $B_3$ (the jump target), the false branch falls through to $B_2$.
- $B_2$ flows unconditionally to $B_3$.
- $B_3$ is the **merge node** of the two control paths.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define code optimization. List any four machine-independent optimization techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Code optimization is a compiler phase that transforms the intermediate representation to produce semantically equivalent code with reduced execution time, memory usage, or power consumption.

**Four techniques (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
- Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)
- Dead Code Elimination (DCE)
- Constant Folding and Constant Propagation
- Strength Reduction
- Copy Propagation
- Loop Invariant Code Motion

*(Any four listed above earn full credit.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between machine-dependent and machine-independent optimizations with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Machine-Independent | Machine-Dependent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Operates on | Intermediate representation | Target assembly / machine code |
| Goal | Improve algorithm-level redundancy | Exploit target architecture features |
| Example | Common Subexpression Elimination | Peephole Optimization / Register Allocation |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 0.5 each for the example, 0.5 for the differentiating line.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (ESE — 14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 4 questions offer an internal choice. **Attempt either A or B in full.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Adapted]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of **basic blocks** and **flow graphs**. Construct the basic blocks and flow graph for the following three-address code:

```
1)  i = 1
2)  j = 1
3)  t1 = 10 * i
4)  t2 = t1 + j
5)  t3 = t2 * 2
6)  t4 = t3 / 3
7)  t5 = t4 + 5
8)  if t5 > 50 goto L9
9)  j = j + 1
10) if j <= 10 goto L3
11) i = i + 1
12) if i <= 10 goto L2
13) L9:
```

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
- A **basic block** is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions in which flow of control enters at the beginning and leaves at the end without halt or branch except possibly at the exit.
- A **flow graph** is a directed graph whose nodes are basic blocks and whose edges represent control flow.

**Identifying Leaders (2 marks):**
Leaders are:
- Instruction 1 (first instruction).
- Instruction 3 (target of `goto L3` at line 10).
- Instruction 11 (target of `goto L2` at line 12).
- Instruction 13 (target of `goto L9` at line 8).

**Basic Blocks (1.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \{1,\, 2\} \\
B_2 &= \{3,\, 4,\, 5,\, 6,\, 7,\, 8\} \\
B_3 &= \{9,\, 10\} \\
B_4 &= \{11,\, 12\} \\
B_5 &= \{13\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Flow Graph (1.5 marks):** Edges: $B_1 \rightarrow B_2$; $B_2 \rightarrow B_5$ (if true); $B_2 \rightarrow B_3$ (fall-through); $B_3 \rightarrow B_2$ (jump back to L3); $B_3 \rightarrow B_4$ (when loop exits); $B_4 \rightarrow B_2$ (jump back to L2); $B_4 \rightarrow B_5$ (when outer loop exits).

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the following optimization techniques with suitable TAC examples:
- (i) Common Subexpression Elimination
- (ii) Dead Code Elimination
- (iii) Strength Reduction

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Common Subexpression Elimination (2.5 marks):**
If an expression $E$ is computed multiple times with no modification of its operands between the uses, the subsequent computations can be replaced with the variable holding the first result.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before: } &\quad t3 = a + b; \;\; t5 = a + b; \;\; t6 = t3 \cdot c; \;\; t7 = t5 \cdot c \\
\text{After: } &\quad t3 = a + b; \;\; t5 = t3; \;\; t6 = t3 \cdot c; \;\; t7 = t6
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Dead Code Elimination (2.5 marks):**
Instructions whose results are never used (i.e., the defined variable is not live after the definition) can be removed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before: } &\quad x = y + z; \;\; \text{if (false) } \{ p = q \cdot r; \} \\
\text{After: } &\quad x = y + z \quad (\text{the assignment to } p \text{ is unreachable})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Strength Reduction (2 marks):**
Replace an expensive operation by an equivalent cheaper one.
$$
\begin{aligned}
x \cdot 2 &\;\Longrightarrow\; x + x \\
x \cdot 4 &\;\Longrightarrow\; x \ll 2 \\
x \cdot 2^{k} &\;\Longrightarrow\; x \ll k
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Adapted]`

**Q. (a)** What is **peephole optimization**? Explain any **five** peephole transformations with examples.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Peephole optimization is a machine-dependent optimization technique that examines a short sequence of target instructions (the *peephole*, typically 2–5 instructions) and replaces them with an equivalent but more efficient sequence.

**Five Transformations (1 mark each):**

1. **Redundant Load-Store Elimination:**
   `MOV R1, x; MOV x, R1` → removed if `x` is in `R1` already.

2. **Constant Folding:**
   `t1 = 5 + 3` → `t1 = 8`.

3. **Strength Reduction:**
   `MUL R1, R1, 2` → `ADD R1, R1, R1`.

4. **Unreachable Code Elimination:**
   `goto L1; ...; L1:` → unreachable instructions between `goto` and label can be deleted if no other entry exists.

5. **Algebraic Simplification:**
   `x + 0` → `x`; `y * 1` → `y`; `z - z` → `0`.

---

**Q. (b)** Apply **Common Subexpression Elimination**, **Copy Propagation**, and **Dead Code Elimination** on the following three-address code. Show the code after each pass.

```
1)  a = b + c
2)  d = a
3)  e = b + c
4)  f = d + e
5)  b = 10
6)  g = b + c
7)  h = g
8)  i = h
```

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pass 1 — Common Subexpression Elimination (2.5 marks):**
`b + c` in instruction 3 is the same as `b + c` in instruction 1, and operand $b$ has not been redefined between them. So instruction 3 becomes `e = a`.

```
1)  a = b + c
2)  d = a
3)  e = a          (replaced)
4)  f = d + e
5)  b = 10
6)  g = b + c
7)  h = g
8)  i = h
```

**Pass 2 — Copy Propagation (2.5 marks):**
Replace uses of `d` with `a`, uses of `e` with `a`, uses of `h` with `g`, uses of `i` with `h → g`.

```
1)  a = b + c
2)  (d = a)            -- dead after propagation
3)  (e = a)            -- dead
4)  f = a + a
5)  b = 10
6)  g = b + c
7)  (h = g)            -- dead
8)  i = g              (after h → g)
```

**Pass 3 — Dead Code Elimination (2 marks):**
Statements 2, 3, and 7 are now pure copies whose targets are never read; they are dead and removed.

```
1)  a = b + c
4)  f = a + a
5)  b = 10
6)  g = b + c
8)  i = g
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Pitfall 1 (1-mark loss):** Students often forget that CSE is **only safe** when neither operand has been redefined. In instruction 6 above, `b` has been reassigned in instruction 5, so `b + c` is **not** a common subexpression with instruction 1.
> - **Pitfall 2 (1-mark loss):** Copy Propagation must be applied **after** CSE — applying it before gives no benefit because redundant definitions still appear unique.
> - **Pitfall 3 (1-mark loss):** DCE must follow Copy Propagation; otherwise dead copies survive and block further simplification.
> - **Pitfall 4 (2-mark loss):** When drawing flow graphs, students often forget the **fall-through edge** from a conditional branch. Always draw **two** outgoing edges per conditional jump: one for the jump target, one for fall-through.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Pin This:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Code optimization transforms IR into semantically equivalent but faster / smaller / lower-power code.
- [ ] **Goal:** Minimize $T_{exec}$, $S_{code}$, $E_{cpu}$ while preserving $\llbracket IR \rrbracket = \llbracket IR' \rrbracket$.
- [ ] **Two broad families:** Machine-Independent (CSE, DCE, LICM, Strength Reduction) and Machine-Dependent (Peephole, Register Allocation, Instruction Scheduling).
- [ ] **Basic Block:** Single entry, single exit, no internal jumps except possibly at exit.
- [ ] **Leaders:** First instruction, jump targets, instructions immediately after jumps.
- [ ] **Flow Graph:** $G = (N, E)$ — nodes are basic blocks, edges are control transfers.
- [ ] **CSE** replaces recomputed expressions with available copies; requires that operands are unchanged between uses.
- [ ] **Copy Propagation** replaces uses of a copied variable with the original — enables DCE.
- [ ] **DCE** removes instructions whose results are never consumed.
- [ ] **Strength Reduction** rewrites expensive operations as cheaper equivalents: $x \cdot 2 \rightarrow x + x$, $x \cdot 2^{k} \rightarrow x \ll k$.
- [ ] **Loop Invariant Code Motion** hoists computations out of loops if they do not depend on loop variables.
- [ ] **Peephole Optimization** inspects a 2–5 instruction window for redundant loads, algebraic identities, constant folding, unreachable code.
- [ ] **Correctness is mandatory:** every transformation must preserve observable behaviour for **all** inputs.
- [ ] **Optimizer ≠ Optimal:** compilers do not guarantee mathematically optimal code; they apply safe, local-to-global heuristics.
- [ ] **Industry mapping:** GCC `-O2/-O3`, LLVM `InstCombine`, GCC `-fmove-loop-invariants`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Opportunities for Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Opportunities for Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Compiler Design (PECIT751)**, **Opportunities for Optimization** refers to the catalogue of strategic locations, code patterns, and transformation rules within a compiler where the intermediate representation (IR) or target code can be mathematically and structurally improved **without altering the observable program semantics**.

Formally, an optimization opportunity exists at any point in the compilation pipeline where an intermediate construct $C_1$ can be replaced by a semantically equivalent construct $C_2$ such that:

$$\text{Cost}(C_2) \;\leq\; \text{Cost}(C_1)$$

where the **Cost** function typically measures **execution time**, **code size**, **register pressure**, or **energy consumption**, while preserving the program’s input–output behavior $\forall x: f_{C_1}(x) = f_{C_2}(x)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**  
> *"Optimization opportunities are the specific points in a program’s intermediate or target representation where transformations can be applied to produce a semantically equivalent program that is faster, smaller, or more energy-efficient."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Packing Analogy"

Imagine you are a **travel agent packing 10 suitcases** into a cargo truck. Naively, you might:
- Throw items in randomly → wasted space, unbalanced weight.
- Repack efficiently → identical items delivered, but using **fewer suitcases** and **less fuel**.

The items (semantics) are the same, but the **arrangement** is superior. A compiler’s optimizer does the same: it preserves **what the program does**, but rearranges **how the program does it** to make the cargo (machine code) lighter, faster, and more efficient.

### 1.3 The Three Major Opportunity Zones

The KTU 2024 module classifies optimization opportunities into **three primary zones**:

| # | Opportunity Zone | Where it Occurs | Machine Dependency |
|---|------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Source-Level Opportunities** | Within the source code / front-end | Independent |
| 2 | **Intermediate Code Opportunities** | On the 3-Address Code (TAC) / IR | Mostly Independent |
| 3 | **Target Code Opportunities** | On the assembly/machine code | Heavily Dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The bulk of KTU questions on this topic focus on **Intermediate Code Opportunities** — particularly **Basic Block optimizations** and **Global optimizations** (covered in subsequent topics of Module 4).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Optimization Opportunities

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates mastery of the following taxonomy. Each opportunity is described with its **trigger condition** and **transformation rule**.

#### A. Machine-Independent (Source & Intermediate) Opportunities

These are **portable** optimizations that do not require knowledge of the target CPU.

1. **Constant Folding**  
   - **Trigger:** A binary/arithmetic operation with two constant operands.  
   - **Rule:** Evaluate the expression at compile time.  
   - **Example:** `x = 3 * 4 + 5` → `x = 17`.

2. **Constant Propagation**  
   - **Trigger:** A variable is assigned a constant value and used before redefinition.  
   - **Rule:** Substitute the constant directly at the use-site.  
   - **Example:** `x = 5; y = x + 2` → `x = 5; y = 7` (after folding).

3. **Copy Propagation**  
   - **Trigger:** `x = y` followed by uses of `x` before redefinition.  
   - **Rule:** Replace uses of `x` with `y`.  
   - **Example:** `a = b; c = a + d` → `c = b + d`.

4. **Algebraic Simplification / Identity Elimination**  
   - **Rule:** Apply algebraic identities such as $x + 0 = x$, $x \times 1 = x$, $x^2 = x \times x$.

5. **Strength Reduction**  
   - **Trigger:** Use of an expensive operator where a cheaper equivalent exists.  
   - **Rule:** Replace the expensive op.  
   - **Example:** `x * 8` → `x << 3` (multiplication → bit shift).

6. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**  
   - **Trigger:** The same expression is computed more than once.  
   - **Rule:** Compute once, reuse via a temporary.  
   - **Example:** `t1 = a + b; t2 = a + b; x = t1 * t2` → `t1 = a + b; x = t1 * t1`.

7. **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**  
   - **Trigger:** Code whose result is never used (or unreachable branches).  
   - **Rule:** Delete the instruction.  
   - **Example:** `x = 5; y = 10` (if `x` is never read) → remove `x = 5`.

8. **Function Inlining**  
   - **Trigger:** Small, frequently-called functions.  
   - **Rule:** Replace the call with the body of the function.  
   - **Trade-off:** Faster execution vs. larger code size (**code bloat**).

#### B. Local Optimizations (Within a Basic Block)

A **Basic Block** is a maximal sequence of consecutive TAC instructions with:
- Exactly one entry point (the first instruction).
- No branches in the middle (except at the end).
- No branch targets in the middle (except at the start).

Local optimizations operate strictly **within** this linear sequence.

#### C. Global Optimizations (Across Basic Blocks)

These use **Data Flow Analysis** to identify opportunities spanning multiple blocks, such as:
- **Global Common Subexpression Elimination**
- **Global Constant Propagation**
- **Loop Optimizations** (next subsection).

#### D. Loop Optimization Opportunities

The **"90/10 rule"**: ~90% of execution time is spent in ~10% of code, typically loops. Key opportunities:

| Optimization | Mathematical Trigger | Benefit |
|--------------|----------------------|---------|
| **Loop Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** | Computation independent of loop variable | Reduces redundant evaluation |
| **Induction Variable Elimination** | Variable changes by constant per iteration | Removes dependent multiplications |
| **Strength Reduction in Loops** | `i * k` inside loop | Replace with additive induction |
| **Loop Unrolling** | Small, known iteration count | Reduces branch overhead |
| **Loop Fusion / Fission** | Adjacent loops with same bounds | Improves cache locality |

#### E. Machine-Dependent (Target Code) Opportunities

Performed during the **Code Generation** phase:

1. **Peephole Optimization** — sliding a small window over emitted instructions.
2. **Register Allocation** — minimize memory spills.
3. **Instruction Scheduling** — reorder to avoid pipeline stalls.
4. **Use of Addressing Modes** — exploit `auto-increment/decrement` addressing.

### 2.2 The Peephole Optimization — A Detailed Sub-Category

A **peephole** is a small, sliding window (typically 2–5 instructions). The optimizer examines each window and applies pattern-matching transformations:

| # | Pattern | Transformation | Gain |
|---|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | `MOV R1, R2; MOV R2, R1` | Remove redundant moves | 1 instr |
| 2 | `MUL R, 2` | `SHL R, 1` | 3–5× faster |
| 3 | `STR x; STR x` | Keep only one store | 1 instr |
| 4 | `JMP label1; label1: ...` | Remove unreachable `JMP` | 1 instr |
| 5 | `LOAD R, addr; STORE R, addr` | Remove if R unused | 2 instrs |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below are the **mathematically grounded metrics** that examiners expect students to quote when discussing optimization *opportunities*.

| Metric / Formula | Description | Units |
|------------------|-------------|-------|
| $S = \frac{T_{\text{before}}}{T_{\text{after}}}$ | **Speedup Ratio** | Dimensionless (×) |
| $\text{Size Reduction} = 1 - \frac{\vert C_{\text{after}} \vert}{\vert C_{\text{before}} \vert}$ | **Code-Size Reduction Ratio** | Fraction (0–1) |
| $C_{\text{expr}} = f_{\text{depth}}(\text{expr})$ | **Cost of Expression** (number of operators) | Operators |
| $\text{RE} = 1 - \frac{\text{Optimized Instructions}}{\text{Original Instructions}}$ | **Reduction Efficiency** | Fraction (0–1) |
| $\text{Register Pressure} = \text{Live Variables at point } p$ | Drives allocation cost | Variables |

> **Note on Notation:** To avoid markdown table conflicts, we use `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the vertical bar `|` inside any math expression inside table cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems (IoT, Automotive):** Loop optimizations and strength reduction reduce energy consumption — critical for battery-powered devices.
- **HPC (Supercomputers):** LICM, vectorization, and loop unrolling achieve **10×–1000× speedups** in scientific codes (e.g., LINPACK).
- **Compilers in Practice:** GCC uses **~200 optimization passes**, LLVM uses **~150**, Intel ICC has hundreds more — all triggered by opportunities like those above.
- **JIT Compilers (HotSpot JVM, V8):** Constant folding and inlining happen at runtime based on profiling data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Full Optimization Pipeline on a TAC Snippet

Consider the following C-like source:

```c
int a = 5;
int b = a + 2;
int c = a + 2;     // Common subexpression
int d = a * 8;     // Strength reduction candidate
int e = b * 1;     // Identity
int f = c + 0;     // Identity
```

#### Step 1 — Generation of Initial 3-Address Code (TAC)

The compiler first emits the **unoptimized** TAC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 5 \quad \text{(constant)} \\
t_2 &= t_1 + 2 \\
t_3 &= t_1 + 2 \\
t_4 &= t_1 \times 8 \\
t_5 &= t_2 \times 1 \\
t_6 &= t_3 + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Constant Folding (Where Possible)

Since $t_1 = 5$ is a **compile-time constant**, we can directly evaluate:
- $5 + 2 = 7$
- $5 \times 8 = 40$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_2 &= 7 \\
t_3 &= 7 \\
t_4 &= 40
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)

Observe that $t_2$ and $t_3$ are computed from the **same operands** $t_1$ and $2$, with no intervening redefinition of $t_1$. By the CSE rule:

$$
t_3 = t_2
$$

So we **replace** the computation of $t_3$ with a copy of $t_2$. However, since $t_2$ is itself a constant, $t_3$ can be **propagated** to $7$ directly. CSE combined with constant folding yields:

$$
t_3 = 7 \quad \text{(reusing the value already computed for } t_2 \text{)}
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Strength Reduction

The instruction $t_4 = t_1 \times 8$ can be strength-reduced to a bit-shift:

$$
t_4 = t_1 \ll 3
$$

And since $t_1 = 5$, further folding gives $t_4 = 40$.

#### Step 5 — Apply Algebraic Simplification

- $t_5 = t_2 \times 1 \;\Rightarrow\; t_5 = t_2$ (multiplicative identity).
- $t_6 = t_3 + 0 \;\Rightarrow\; t_6 = t_3$ (additive identity).

#### Step 6 — Apply Copy Propagation

Since $t_5$ is just a copy of $t_2$, and $t_6$ is a copy of $t_3$ (which is also $t_2$), all uses of $t_5$ and $t_6$ can be replaced by $t_2$:

$$
t_5 \rightarrow t_2, \quad t_6 \rightarrow t_2
$$

#### Step 7 — Apply Dead Code Elimination (DCE)

After propagation, $t_5$ and $t_6$ have **no uses** (assuming they were temporaries not assigned to live variables). They are removed entirely.

#### Final Optimized TAC

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 5 \\
t_2 &= 7 \\
t_4 &= 40
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 8 — Quantitative Evaluation of Optimization

| Metric | Before | After | Improvement |
|--------|--------|-------|-------------|
| **Instructions** | 6 | 3 | $\text{RE} = 1 - 3/6 = 0.5$ (**50% reduction**) |
| **Multiplications** | 2 | 0 | 100% |
| **Additions** | 2 | 0 | 100% |
| **Shifts** | 0 | 0 | — |

The **speedup ratio** depends on target CPU cycle costs, but on a typical RISC processor:
- $S \approx \frac{6 \cdot t_{\text{avg}}}{3 \cdot t_{\text{avg}}} = 2.0$ × faster (idealized).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When Each Opportunity Applies

To score well in KTU exams, students must understand **trigger conditions** rigorously:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Constant Folding} &: \forall \; \text{op} \in \{+,-,\times,/\}, \; \text{both operands} = \text{const} \\
\text{Constant Propagation} &: x = c \;\wedge\; \text{Use}(x) \;\wedge\; \neg \text{Redef}(x) \\
\text{CSE} &: e_1 = e_2 \;(\text{syntactically equal}) \;\wedge\; \text{Available} \\
\text{Strength Reduction} &: \text{op} \in \text{ExpensiveSet} \;\wedge\; \exists \text{ cheaper equivalent} \\
\text{DCE} &: \neg \text{UsedAfter}(t) \;\vee\; \text{Unreachable}(\text{block}) \\
\text{LICM} &: \text{Computation} \perp \text{LoopVariable}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Loop Optimization Worked Example

Original loop (computing $\sum_{i=1}^{n} a \cdot i + c$ where $a, c$ are loop-invariant):

```
i = 1
sum = 0
L1: if i > n goto L2
    t1 = a * i      // a is loop-invariant
    t2 = t1 + c     // c is loop-invariant
    sum = sum + t2
    i = i + 1
    goto L1
L2: ...
```

**After LICM (hoisting invariants):**

```
i = 1
sum = 0
t1 = a * 1         // Hoisted? No — still depends on i
t2 = a * 1 + c     // PARTIALLY hoistable, but t1 still depends on i
goto L1
L1: if i > n goto L2
    t2_prev = t2   // Use the hoisted value
    sum = sum + t2_prev
    i = i + 1
    t2 = a * i + c // Recomputed (or use induction variable)
    goto L1
```

**After Induction Variable Strength Reduction:**

Replace $t_1 = a \cdot i$ with an additive counter:

```
counter = a * 1
i = 1
sum = 0
L1: if i > n goto L2
    sum = sum + counter
    counter = counter + a    // Strength-reduced from * to +
    i = i + 1
    goto L1
```

This eliminates **one multiplication per iteration**, replaced by a single addition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: Classification of Optimization Opportunities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["OPTIMIZATION OPPORTUNITIES"]:::root --> B["Machine Independent"]
    A --> C["Machine Dependent"]

    B --> D["Source Level"]
    B --> E["Intermediate Code Level"]

    D --> D1["Source Code Refactoring"]
    D --> D2["Algorithm Substitution"]

    E --> E1["Local Intra Block"]
    E --> E2["Global Inter Block"]
    E --> E3["Loop Level"]

    E1 --> E1a["Constant Folding"]
    E1 --> E1b["Constant Propagation"]
    E1 --> E1c["Algebraic Simplification"]
    E1 --> E1d["Strength Reduction"]
    E1 --> E1e["CSE Within Block"]

    E2 --> E2a["Global CSE"]
    E2 --> E2b["Global Copy Prop"]
    E2 --> E2c["Dead Code Elimination"]
    E2 --> E2d["Data Flow Analysis"]

    E3 --> E3a["LICM"]
    E3 --> E3b["Induction Var Reduction"]
    E3 --> E3c["Loop Unrolling"]
    E3 --> E3d["Loop Fusion Fission"]

    C --> F["Target Code Level"]
    F --> F1["Peephole Optimization"]
    F --> F2["Register Allocation"]
    F --> F3["Instruction Scheduling"]
    F --> F4["Addressing Mode Exploitation"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:3px
    classDef default fill:#e0e7ff,color:#0f172a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 The Optimization Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["SOURCE CODE"]:::stage --> S2["FRONT END"]
    S2 --> S3["INTERMEDIATE REPRESENTATION"]
    S3 --> S4["MACHINE INDEPENDENT OPTIMIZER"]
    S4 --> S5["OPTIMIZED IR"]
    S5 --> S6["CODE GENERATOR"]
    S6 --> S7["MACHINE DEPENDENT OPTIMIZER"]
    S7 --> S8["TARGET MACHINE CODE"]:::target

    S4 --> P1["Local Optimizations"]
    S4 --> P2["Global Optimizations"]
    S4 --> P3["Loop Optimizations"]

    S7 --> Q1["Peephole"]
    S7 --> Q2["Register Alloc"]
    S7 --> Q3["Sched"]

    classDef stage fill:#dbeafe,color:#0f172a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef target fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Peephole Optimization: Sliding Window Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1["Window 1: I1 I2 I3 I4 I5"]:::win --> W2["Window 2: I2 I3 I4 I5 I6"]:::win
    W2 --> W3["Window 3: I3 I4 I5 I6 I7"]:::win
    W3 --> W4["..."]:::win
    W4 --> W5["Window N-k: IN-k+1 ... IN"]:::win

    W1 --> R1["Pattern Match?"]:::rule
    R1 -- YES --> R2["Apply Transformation"]:::rule
    R1 -- NO --> N1["Slide Window"]:::rule
    N1 --> W2

    R2 --> N1

    classDef win fill:#fef3c7,color:#78350f,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:1px
    classDef rule fill:#dcfce7,color:#14532d,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow: When to Apply an Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Identify IR Construct"]:::step --> q1{"Is it a constant expression?"}
    q1 -- YES --> a1["Apply CONSTANT FOLDING"]:::act
    q1 -- NO --> q2{"Is a variable assigned a constant?"}
    q2 -- YES --> a2["Apply CONSTANT PROPAGATION"]:::act
    q2 -- NO --> q3{"Is the same expression computed twice?"}
    q3 -- YES --> a3["Apply CSE"]:::act
    q3 -- NO --> q4{"Is an expensive operator used?"}
    q4 -- YES --> a4["Apply STRENGTH REDUCTION"]:::act
    q4 -- NO --> q5{"Is result unused?"}
    q5 -- YES --> a5["Apply DEAD CODE ELIMINATION"]:::act
    q5 -- NO --> endn["No optimization here"]:::step

    classDef step fill:#e0e7ff,color:#0f172a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef act fill:#d1fae5,color:#065f46,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:1px
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*  
**Define optimization opportunities. List any four machine-independent optimization opportunities.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
Optimization opportunities are specific locations or code patterns in the intermediate/target representation where a compiler can apply transformations to produce a semantically equivalent program that is faster, smaller, or more efficient.

**Four Opportunities (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Constant Folding** — Evaluating constant expressions at compile time.
2. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** — Avoiding recomputation of identical expressions.
3. **Strength Reduction** — Replacing expensive operators with cheaper ones (e.g., `×` with `<<`).
4. **Dead Code Elimination** — Removing instructions whose results are never used.

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*  
**What is peephole optimization? Give any two transformations performed by it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
Peephole optimization is a machine-dependent technique where the compiler examines a small, sliding window (typically 2–5 instructions) of the emitted target code and applies local transformations to improve it.

**Two Transformations (2 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Redundant Instruction Elimination:**  
   The pattern `MOV R1, R2` followed by `MOV R2, R1` results in a no-op; both instructions can be removed.

2. **Strength Reduction at Machine Level:**  
   `MUL R, 2` is replaced by `SHL R, 1` (left shift by 1), which is significantly faster on most CPUs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** by **listing** optimizations without showing the **before/after TAC**.
> - Always state the **trigger condition** before applying the rule — partial marks depend on it.
> - Do **not confuse** machine-independent (intermediate) and machine-dependent (target) optimizations; misclassification attracts a **1-mark penalty**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

*[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]*  
**(a)** Explain the major **machine-independent optimization opportunities** in a compiler. Discuss in detail the techniques of **Common Subexpression Elimination** and **Copy Propagation** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** What is **peephole optimization**? Describe any **four characteristic transformations** with examples. Show how it differs from **machine-independent** optimizations. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**  
Machine-independent optimizations operate on the **intermediate representation** (3-address code) and do not require knowledge of the target machine. They are portable across all architectures.

**Classification (2 marks):**
- **Local optimizations** — within a basic block.
- **Global optimizations** — across basic blocks (data flow analysis).
- **Loop optimizations** — focused on iterative constructs.

**Common Subexpression Elimination (2 marks):**  
**Trigger Condition:** If an expression $e$ is computed more than once and the variables in $e$ are **not redefined** between the two computations, $e$ is a common subexpression.  
**Rule:** Compute once; reuse the result.  
**Example:**

```
BEFORE:                          AFTER:
t1 = a + b                       t1 = a + b
t2 = a + b                       t2 = t1
x = t1 * t2                      x = t1 * t1
```

**Copy Propagation (2 marks):**  
**Trigger:** A statement of the form `x = y` (copy) followed by uses of `x` before redefinition.  
**Rule:** Replace each use of `x` by `y`. This may enable further optimizations (e.g., DCE, folding).  
**Example:**

```
BEFORE:                          AFTER:
a = b                            a = b
c = a + d                        c = b + d
```

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Definition of Peephole Optimization (2 marks):**  
Peephole optimization is a **local, machine-dependent** technique where a small window (the "peephole", typically 2–5 instructions) is moved over the target code. For each window position, the optimizer attempts pattern-matched transformations that improve the code.

**Four Transformations (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Redundant Load/Store Elimination:**  
   `STR R; STR R` → single `STR R`.

2. **Strength Reduction:**  
   `MUL R, 2` → `SHL R, 1`.

3. **Null Sequence Elimination:**  
   `MOV R1, R2; MOV R2, R1` → both removed (redundant swap).

4. **Jump Optimization:**  
   `JMP L1; L1: NEXT` → remove unreachable `JMP`.

**Difference from Machine-Independent Optimizations (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Machine-Independent | Peephole |
|--------|---------------------|----------|
| Operates on | Intermediate code | Target assembly |
| Requires hardware info? | No | Yes |
| Scope | Local / Global / Loop | Tiny local window |
| Example | CSE, LICM | Strength reduction, register move |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**  *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

*(a)* What is the significance of **loop optimization** in compiler design? Explain **Loop Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** and **Induction Variable Elimination** with examples. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** A program fragment has the following 3-address code. Apply **constant folding, constant propagation, common subexpression elimination, and dead code elimination** to optimize it. Show each step clearly. **(7 marks)**

```
t1 = 10
t2 = t1 + 20
t3 = t1 + 20
t4 = t2 * 2
t5 = t3 + 0
x = t4
y = t5
z = t1 * 1
```

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Significance (1 mark):**  
The **90/10 rule** states that ~90% of execution time is spent in ~10% of code — almost always loops. Optimizing loops yields the **highest return on investment** in compiler optimization.

**Loop Invariant Code Motion — LICM (3 marks):**  
**Definition:** A computation is **loop-invariant** if its operands are not modified within the loop.  
**Rule:** Hoist such computations to the **pre-header** (before the loop entry).  
**Example:**

```
BEFORE:                          AFTER:
for i = 1 to n:                  t = a * b        // Hoisted
    x = a * b + i                for i = 1 to n:
                                     x = t + i
```

**Induction Variable Elimination (3 marks):**  
**Definition:** An **induction variable** is one whose value changes by a **constant amount** each iteration (e.g., `i = i + 1`).  
**Rule:** Replace expensive multiplications involving induction variables with cheaper additions.  
**Example:**

```
BEFORE:                          AFTER:
i = 1                            i = 1
s = 0                            s = 0
while i <= n:                    k = 0            // k = a*i initially
    t = a * i                    while i <= n:
    s = s + t                        s = s + k
    i = i + 1                        k = k + a
                                       i = i + 1
```

Here, `t = a * i` (multiplication) is replaced by `k = k + a` (addition), saving one expensive `MUL` per iteration.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Initial TAC (Given):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 10 \\
t_2 &= t_1 + 20 \\
t_3 &= t_1 + 20 \\
t_4 &= t_2 \times 2 \\
t_5 &= t_3 + 0 \\
x &= t_4 \\
y &= t_5 \\
z &= t_1 \times 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Constant Folding (1 mark):**  
$t_1 = 10$, so $t_1 + 20 = 30$ and $t_1 \times 1 = 10$.

$$
t_2 = 30, \quad t_3 = 30, \quad z = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Common Subexpression Elimination (1 mark):**  
$t_2$ and $t_3$ are computed from the **same expression** $t_1 + 20$ with **no redefinition** of $t_1$ in between.

$$
t_3 = t_2
$$

**Step 3 — Constant Propagation (1 mark):**  
Substitute $t_3 = t_2 = 30$ in subsequent uses.

$$
y = t_5, \quad t_5 = 30 + 0
$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Simplification (1 mark):**  
- $t_4 = t_2 \times 2 = 30 \times 2 = 60$ (after folding)
- $t_5 = 30 + 0 = 30$ (additive identity)
- $z = 10$ (multiplicative identity)

**Step 5 — Dead Code Elimination (2 marks):**  
After propagation, $t_3$ and $t_5$ are **never used** in any subsequent live computation (since $t_3 = t_2$ and $t_5 = 30$, both subsumed). The `t3 = t1 + 20`, `t5 = t3 + 0`, and `z = t1 × 1` lines are **dead**.

**Final Optimized TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 10 \\
t_2 &= 30 \\
t_4 &= 60 \\
x &= 60 \\
y &= 30 \\
z &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quantitative Result:**  
- Original: **8 instructions** with 1 addition, 1 multiplication, 1 addition, 1 multiplication, 1 addition = **4 arithmetic operations**.
- Optimized: **6 instructions** but only **1 addition** ($30+0$ becomes constant), **0 multiplications** (all folded to constants).
- **Code reduction:** 25%; **Operation reduction:** ~75%.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Exam Pointers — Memorize These:**

- **Three Opportunity Zones:** Source-level, Intermediate (machine-independent), Target (machine-dependent).
- **Local vs Global vs Loop** — always specify the scope of an optimization in your exam answer.
- **Constant Folding vs Constant Propagation** — folding *evaluates*; propagation *substitutes*.
- **CSE requires "Available Expression"** — operands must not be redefined between the two uses.
- **LICM** — only hoist if the computation is **truly invariant** and the loop is entered at least once.
- **Strength Reduction** in loops is a *machine-independent* transformation; in peephole, it is *machine-dependent*.
- **Peephole optimizations** always operate on a small, sliding window — never globally.
- **Dead Code Elimination** comes **last** in the optimization pipeline, after propagation has eliminated most uses.
- **Function inlining** trades **code size for speed** — state this trade-off explicitly in answers.
- **Induction variables** transform `MUL` into `ADD` — a classic KTU long-answer topic.
- **Code Reduction Ratio** and **Speedup Ratio** are the most commonly asked quantitative metrics.
- **The 90/10 rule** is the single most important *justification* for loop optimizations — quote it!
- **DCE ≠ Unreachable Code Elimination** — DCE removes *unused definitions*; UCE removes *unreachable blocks*.
- **Always show the BEFORE and AFTER TAC** in your solution; the transformation is what earns the marks.
- **Trigger Condition → Rule → Example** — this three-part structure is the KTU 2024 valuation key.
- **Anti-pattern to avoid:** Do **not** apply LICM to expressions like `a[i]` if `i` changes inside the loop.
- **Anti-pattern to avoid:** Do **not** eliminate a common subexpression if it has **side effects** (e.g., function calls).
- **Key equations to memorize:** $S = T_{\text{before}}/T_{\text{after}}$; $\text{RE} = 1 - \vert C_{\text{after}} \vert / \vert C_{\text{before}} \vert$.
- **Peephole is local; LICM is loop-scoped; DCE is global** — confused scope = 1–2 mark penalty.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scope of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scope of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scope of Optimization** refers to the **boundaries or regions** of a program (ranging from a single instruction window to the entire program) within which a compiler applies code transformation techniques to improve execution efficiency. The "scope" defines the **visibility horizon** of the optimizer — i.e., the set of statements, basic blocks, loops, procedures, or whole programs it can simultaneously inspect and rewrite.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECIT751 Module 4** syllabus, the principal sources of optimization are classified **by scope** into the following hierarchical regions:

| Scope Level | Region of Inspection | Optimization Type |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Window | A few adjacent instructions | **Peephole Optimization** |
| Basic Block | A straight-line sequence of code | **Local Optimization** |
| Procedure / Function | An entire function body | **Global Optimization** |
| Loop Body | A natural or artificial loop | **Loop Optimization** |
| Across Procedures | Caller + callee | **Interprocedural Optimization** |
| Whole Program | All translation units | **Whole-Program / Link-Time Optimization** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The House Renovation Metaphor**
>
> Imagine you are renovating a **house** for energy efficiency.
> - If you only clean a **single window** → that is **peephole optimization** (tiny scope).
> - If you reorganize **one room** → that is **local optimization** (basic-block scope).
> - If you renovate the **entire floor** → that is **global optimization** (procedure scope).
> - If you install a **central HVAC loop** that circulates through the whole building → that is **loop/interprocedural optimization** (large scope).
>
> The **larger the scope**, the **more redundancy you can spot**, but the **higher the cost** (analysis time, memory, compiler complexity). The "Scope of Optimization" is essentially the **decision of how big a window the optimizer is allowed to look through**.

## 1.3 Why Scope Matters

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Questions on "Scope of Optimization" frequently test whether the student understands the **trade-off curve** between:
> - **Benefit** (more redundant code eliminated as scope grows) **vs.**
> - **Cost** (analysis complexity grows polynomially or worse).
>
> A student who merely *lists* the scopes loses marks. The student who *explains the cost-benefit trade-off* scores full marks.

## 1.4 Scope Hierarchy Diagram (Conceptual)

The scopes form a **nested hierarchy**:

$$\text{Peephole} \subseteq \text{Local (Basic Block)} \subseteq \text{Global (Function)} \subseteq \text{Interprocedural} \subseteq \text{Whole-Program}$$

Each outer scope **encloses** the inner ones and **adds** additional analysis power at the cost of additional compilation effort.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Principal Scopes of Optimization (Detailed)

### 🔹 2.1.1 Peephole Optimization (Window Scope)

- **Scope:** A short sliding window of **typically 2–8 consecutive instructions** (machine code or intermediate code).
- **Technique:** Pattern matching followed by replacement by an equivalent, shorter sequence.
- **Example transformations:**
  - Strength reduction: `MUL r1, 2` → `SHL r1, 1`
  - Redundant load/store elimination
  - Jump-to-jump chaining: `JMP L1; … L1: JMP L2` → `JMP L2`
  - Constant folding: `ADD r1, 0` → eliminated
- **Cost:** $O(n)$ over the instruction stream (linear pass).
- **Benefit:** Limited but cheap; runs at the very end of compilation.

### 🔹 2.1.2 Local Optimization (Basic-Block Scope)

- **Scope:** A **basic block** — a maximal sequence of three-address instructions where **control only enters at the first instruction and leaves at the last** (no jumps in, no jumps out except at the end).
- **Techniques enabled by this scope:**
  - **Common subexpression elimination (CSE)** *within* the block
  - **Constant folding & constant propagation** within the block
  - **Algebraic simplification** (e.g., `x * 1 → x`)
  - **Dead-code elimination** within the block
  - **Statement reordering** for register reuse
- **Why this scope is easy:** No control flow enters from the middle → every definition in the block is the *only* definition reaching a use in the same block → **def-use chains** are linear.

### 🔹 2.1.3 Global Optimization (Function / Intra-Procedural Scope)

- **Scope:** The **entire body of a single procedure/function**.
- **Additional techniques unlocked** (because control flow can now cross basic blocks):
  - **Global CSE** across blocks
  - **Copy propagation**
  - **Induction-variable elimination**
  - **Global dead-code elimination** (unreachable blocks)
  - **Code motion** of loop-invariant code
- **Required machinery:** **Data-flow analysis** with iterative fixed-point computation over the Control Flow Graph (CFG).
- **Cost:** $O(N \cdot K)$ where $N$ is the number of CFG nodes and $K$ is the number of iterations to convergence.

### 🔹 2.1.4 Loop Optimization (Iterative Scope)

- **Scope:** The set of **natural loops** in the CFG (a strongly connected region with a single entry — the loop header).
- **Premium techniques:**
  - **Loop-invariant code motion (LICM)**
  - **Induction-variable strength reduction** (replace multiplication with incremental addition)
  - **Loop unrolling**
  - **Loop fusion / loop fission**
  - **Loop interchange** (for cache locality)
- **Significance:** A program spends ~**90 %** of its execution time in ~**10 %** of the code (the *90-10 rule* / Pareto principle), and that 10 % is almost always inside loops.

### 🔹 2.1.5 Interprocedural Optimization (IPO)

- **Scope:** Spans **multiple procedures** of a translation unit (or across files at link time).
- **Techniques:**
  - **Inlining** (procedure body substitution at call site)
  - **Procedure cloning** for constant arguments
  - **Interprocedural constant propagation**
  - **Alias analysis across calls**
  - **Tail-call optimization**
- **Cost:** Significantly higher — must respect **calling conventions, recursion, aliasing, and visibility rules**.

### 🔹 2.1.6 Whole-Program / Link-Time Optimization (LTO)

- **Scope:** The **entire executable** is analyzed at link time.
- **Techniques:** Whole-program inlining, devirtualization, cross-module dead-code stripping, profile-guided optimization (PGO).
- **Cost:** Highest memory footprint; requires storing intermediate representation across the whole program.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are **required** for solving KTU problems on data-flow-driven global and loop optimization.

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{OUT}[B] = \text{gen}[B] \cup (\text{IN}[B] - \text{kill}[B])$ | Standard data-flow transfer function for *forward* flow problems (e.g., Available Expressions) | Global |
| $\text{IN}[B] = \bigcap_{P \in \text{pred}(B)} \text{OUT}[P]$ | Meet operation for *Available Expressions* (must be available on **all** paths) | Global |
| $\text{IN}[B] = \bigcup_{P \in \text{pred}(B)} \text{OUT}[P]$ | Meet operation for *Reaching Definitions* (need reach on **any** path) | Global |
| $\text{OUT}[B] = \text{IN}[B] - \text{kill}[B] \cup \text{gen}[B]$ | Backward-flow transfer (e.g., Live-Variable Analysis) | Global |
| $D_{\text{loop}} = \{ d \in U \mid d \text{ reaches a use outside the loop} \}$ | Loop-invariant detection: definition $d$ is movable if it dominates all loop exits | Loop |
| $T_{\text{opt}}(n) = O(n \cdot \text{scope-size})$ | Cost of optimization grows monotonically with scope size $n$ | All |
| $\text{Gain}(S) \propto \log(\vert S \vert + 1)$ | Empirical diminishing-returns gain curve as scope $S$ enlarges | All |
| $\text{Cost}_{\text{LTO}} \approx O(V + E)$ over the call graph | Link-time analysis cost ($V$ procedures, $E$ call edges) | Interprocedural |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value notations are written using `\vert` so they do not break the markdown table pipe `|` syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Where Scope-of-Optimization Knowledge is Applied |
|---|---|
| Embedded Systems (e.g., ARM Cortex-M firmware) | **Local + Peephole** optimization to fit code in $\le 256$ KB flash |
| High-Performance Computing (HPC) | **Loop + Interprocedural** for SIMD vectorization and cache blocking |
| Database Query Engines (e.g., PostgreSQL, DuckDB) | **Whole-program** JIT optimization of SQL plans |
| Game Engines (Unreal, Unity IL2CPP) | **Profile-guided IPO** for runtime hot paths |
| Operating System Kernels | **Register allocation + peephole** for context-switch critical paths |

## 2.4 The Cost–Benefit Trade-off Curve (Engineering Decision)

A KTU-favourite conceptual question asks: *Why don't compilers always apply the largest-scope optimization?* The answer is captured by the analytical curve:

$$\text{Net-Benefit}(S) = \text{Gain}(S) - \lambda \cdot \text{Compile-Cost}(S)$$

where $\lambda$ is a compiler-design weighting factor (often user-controlled via flags like `-O0`, `-O1`, `-O2`, `-O3`, `-Ofast` in GCC/Clang).

- For **small scopes**, $\text{Gain}(S)$ rises sharply while cost is low → **high net benefit**.
- For **large scopes**, $\text{Gain}(S)$ saturates (diminishing returns) while cost explodes → **low or negative net benefit**.

This is precisely why GCC exposes optimization *levels* instead of a single "maximum" mode.

# 📙 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **mathematical skeleton** used by an optimizer to *decide* which scope to analyze. We work through three derivations:

1. **Basic-block identification** (for Local scope).
2. **Available-expressions data-flow** (for Global scope).
3. **Loop-invariant code motion condition** (for Loop scope).

## 3.1 Derivation I — Basic Block Identification (Local Scope)

**Goal:** Partition the three-address code into maximal straight-line sequences (basic blocks).

**Step 1 — Identify *Leaders* (entry points of basic blocks):**

A three-address statement $s_i$ is a **leader** if any of the following hold:
- $s_i$ is the **first statement** of the program.
- $s_i$ is the **immediate target** of a conditional or unconditional jump at some $s_j$.
- $s_i$ **immediately follows** a conditional jump at $s_j$.

**Step 2 — Build blocks:** For each leader, its basic block consists of itself and all subsequent statements up to (but not including) the next leader or the end of the program.

### Worked Example (KTU-style)

Given the three-address code:

```
1.  t1 = 10
2.  i = 1
3.  if t1 > i goto 6
4.  t2 = a * 2
5.  t3 = t2 + b
6.  a = t1
7.  i = i + 1
8.  if i <= t1 goto 3
9.  end
```

**Leader identification (show every step):**

| Statement | Condition Met | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | First statement of program | **Leader** |
| 3 | First statement | (already a leader by rule 1 of next derivation? — No) — it is a leader only because… see below |
| 6 | Immediate target of `goto 6` at statement 3 | **Leader** |
| 7 | Immediately follows conditional `goto 3` at statement 8 | **Leader** |
| 9 | Immediate target of `goto 9` ? — there is no such jump; statement 9 is the natural terminator and is **not** automatically a leader unless jumped to. The block containing 9 is created by the previous leader (7). |

**Final block partition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \{1,\ 2,\ 3\} \\
B_2 &= \{4,\ 5\} \\
B_3 &= \{6,\ 7,\ 8,\ 9\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** $B_1$ is the *loop pre-header + entry*, $B_2$ is the *loop body*, $B_3$ is the *loop update + exit*. The local scope of $B_2$ lets the optimizer eliminate redundancy inside the body without worrying about what arrives from outside.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Available Expressions Analysis (Global Scope)

**Goal:** For each block $B$, compute the set $\text{AvailOut}[B]$ of expressions guaranteed to be available (already computed, not subsequently killed) on every path from the entry to the **exit** of $B$.

**Step 1 — Define local predicates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{e\_gen}[B] &= \{\, e \mid e \text{ is computed in } B \text{ and neither operand is redefined later in } B \,\} \\
\text{e\_kill}[B] &= \{\, e \mid \text{some operand of } e \text{ is defined in } B \,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — State the transfer equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AvailOut}[B] &= \text{e\_gen}[B] \cup \bigl( \text{AvailIn}[B] - \text{e\_kill}[B] \bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the meet (intersection) over predecessors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AvailIn}[B] &= \bigcap_{P \,\in\, \text{pred}(B)} \text{AvailOut}[P]
\end{aligned}
$$

The intersection (not union!) is used because an expression is *available* only if it is available on **every** incoming path — a single path that recomputes the expression makes it unavailable.

**Step 4 — Initialize and iterate to fixed point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AvailOut}[\text{Entry}] &= \varnothing \quad \text{(nothing is available at the very start)} \\
\text{AvailIn}[B] &= U \quad \text{for all other } B \quad \text{(universal set — pessimistic init)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Iterate equations (1) and (2) until $\text{AvailOut}[B]$ stabilizes for every block $B$.

### Worked Example — Small CFG with 3 Blocks

Given the CFG with blocks $B_1 \rightarrow B_2$, $B_1 \rightarrow B_3$, and $B_2 \rightarrow B_3$, and:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{e\_gen}[B_1] &= \{a+b\}, \quad \text{e\_kill}[B_1] = \{\,c+d\,\} \\
\text{e\_gen}[B_2] &= \{c+d\}, \quad \text{e\_kill}[B_2] = \{a+b\} \\
\text{e\_gen}[B_3] &= \varnothing, \quad\; \text{e\_kill}[B_3] = \{a+b,\, c+d\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 0 (initialization):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AvailOut}[B_1] &= \varnothing \\
\text{AvailIn}[B_2] &= \text{AvailOut}[B_1] = \varnothing \\
\text{AvailIn}[B_3] &= \text{AvailOut}[B_1] \cap \text{AvailOut}[B_2] = \varnothing \cap \varnothing = \varnothing
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AvailOut}[B_1] &= \{a+b\} \cup (\varnothing - \{c+d\}) = \{a+b\} \\
\text{AvailOut}[B_2] &= \{c+d\} \cup (\varnothing - \{a+b\}) = \{c+d\} \\
\text{AvailOut}[B_3] &= \varnothing \cup (\varnothing - \{a+b,\, c+d\}) = \varnothing
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 (verify fixed point):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AvailIn}[B_2] &= \text{AvailOut}[B_1] = \{a+b\} \\
\text{AvailOut}[B_2] &= \{c+d\} \cup (\{a+b\} - \{a+b\}) = \{c+d\} \\
\text{AvailIn}[B_3] &= \{a+b\} \cap \{c+d\} = \varnothing \\
\text{AvailOut}[B_3] &= \varnothing \quad \text{(stable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The expression $a+b$ is *not* available at the entry of $B_3$ because $B_2$ kills it. Therefore the global CSE pass **cannot** eliminate the recomputation of $a+b$ in $B_3$. This illustrates exactly why **global scope is needed** (the local scope of $B_3$ alone could not determine this).

## 3.3 Derivation III — Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) Condition (Loop Scope)

**Definition.** A definition $d: x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ inside a loop $L$ is **loop-invariant** if, on every iteration, $y$ and $z$ have the **same values** as on the previous iteration. Equivalently, the *only* definitions of $y$ and $z$ that reach $d$ are themselves outside $L$.

**Mathematical condition (using reaching definitions $RD$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d \text{ is loop-invariant} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(\forall\, d' \in \text{RD-into-}d\bigr) \bigl(d' \notin L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(d' \text{ itself is loop-invariant}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Safe motion condition (LICM preconditions):**

A loop-invariant statement $s$ can be moved to the loop pre-header $P$ **iff**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} &\quad s \text{ dominates all loop exits} \\
\text{(ii)} &\quad s \text{ is in every loop iteration's execution path (i.e., no abnormal exit between header and }s\text{)} \\
\text{(iii)} &\quad \text{No definition of } x \text{ (the LHS) inside } L \text{ reaches } s \text{ after motion} \\
\text{(iv)} &\quad \text{Only one definition of } x \text{ reaches the use of } x \text{ that } s \text{ would replace}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example — LICM on a C-style Fragment

Original loop:

```
for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    t = 100 * i;          //  ❌ NOT invariant: depends on i
    a[i] = b[t] + c[t];   //  c[t] uses t; this use of t is invariant? — see below
}
```

**Step-by-step analysis (no truncation):**

1. Examine the definition `t = 100 * i`.
   - The RHS operands are `100` (constant, trivially invariant) and `i`.
   - `i` has definitions inside the loop (`i = i + 1` in the update and `i = 1` in the header).
   - Hence `t = 100 * i` is **NOT** invariant across iterations.

2. However, consider a *modified* loop where `i` is incremented but `t` is set once outside:

```
t = 100 * base;            //  ✅ invariant across iterations of *inner* loop
for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    a[i] = b[t] + c[t];    //  t is invariant within this loop
}
```

3. The condition `t = 100 * base` dominates all exits of the loop.
4. No other definition of `t` exists inside the loop → conditions (iii) and (iv) hold.
5. Hence the compiler is licensed to **hoist** `t = 100 * base` to the pre-header.

> **This illustrates the loop scope in action:** a transformation that is impossible inside a basic block (local scope) becomes possible because the loop provides a *bounded repetitive region* over which invariants can be detected.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Scope for Which Optimization?

| Optimization Technique | Minimum Required Scope | Why This Scope is Necessary |
|---|---|---|
| Constant folding | Peephole | Only 1–2 instructions needed |
| Strength reduction (constant mul by power of 2) | Peephole | Single instruction pattern |
| Local CSE | Basic Block | Def-use chain within block is linear |
| Global CSE | Function | Must track across branches |
| Dead-code elimination (local) | Basic Block | No incoming control flow from middle |
| Dead-code elimination (unreachable blocks) | Function | Needs reachability on CFG |
| Induction-variable elimination | Loop | Needs loop structure |
| Loop unrolling | Loop | Needs known trip count or header dominance |
| Procedure inlining | Interprocedural | Needs caller + callee bodies |
| Devirtualization | Interprocedural + whole-program | Needs class hierarchy info |

## 3.5 Cost-of-Analysis Quantitative Derivation

Let $n$ be the number of three-address statements in the program. The asymptotic compile-time cost $T(n)$ for each scope is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{peephole}}(n) &= O(n) \\
T_{\text{local}}(n) &= O(n) \quad \text{(block partition + per-block linear sweep)} \\
T_{\text{global}}(n) &= O(n \cdot d \cdot k) \quad \text{where } d \text{ = max predecessors, } k \text{ = iterations to fixed point} \\
T_{\text{loop}}(n) &= O(n \cdot L) \quad \text{where } L \text{ = number of natural loops} \\
T_{\text{interproc}}(n) &= O\!\bigl((V + E) \cdot h\bigr) \quad \text{where } h \text{ = call-graph depth}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice $k \le 3$ for bit-vector frameworks and $h \le \log V$, so the dominant cost is the **size of the inspected region** — confirming why *scope size* drives the trade-off.

- The **local scope** of $B_2$ permits eliminating redundancy *inside* $B_2$.
- The **global scope** (function body) permits checking whether expressions computed in $B_1$ are still available when control reaches $B_3$.
- The **loop scope** permits detecting that statements inside $B_2$ are loop-invariant if their operands are defined only outside the loop.

# 📒 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the term "Scope of Optimization" in a compiler. List any FOUR scopes with one example technique for each.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~120 words):**

The *Scope of Optimization* is the **region of the program** (in terms of instructions, basic blocks, loops, procedures, or whole programs) that an optimizer is allowed to inspect and transform. The scope determines the set of optimizations that are mechanically feasible.

| # | Scope | Example Technique |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Peephole** | Redundant load elimination |
| 2 | **Basic Block (Local)** | Local common subexpression elimination |
| 3 | **Global (Function)** | Global dead-code elimination using data-flow analysis |
| 4 | **Loop** | Loop-invariant code motion |
| 5 | **Interprocedural** | Procedure inlining |

*(Writing any 4 with example yields 3 marks: definition = 1, four scopes = 1, four examples = 1.)*

---

### **Q2. Why does increasing the scope of optimization NOT always increase performance gain? Justify with a short example.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~100 words):**

Increasing scope increases the **compile-time cost** of analysis super-linearly (data-flow frameworks require iterative fixed-point computation over larger graphs). Beyond a certain scope size, the **diminishing-returns principle** applies: very few additional redundancies are exposed, while compilation time and memory usage rise sharply.

**Example:** In a program where nearly all redundancy lies within basic blocks, enabling interprocedural optimization adds hours of compile time but eliminates no extra redundant computation. The net benefit curve thus peaks at an intermediate scope. Hence compilers expose *optimization levels* (`-O0` … `-O3`) instead of a single "maximum" mode.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and may have internal choice. We present **Question A** and **Question B** as two completely independent alternatives. Each has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### 🅰️ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Peephole scope** and **Basic-Block scope** of optimization. Compare them in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct the basic blocks and leaders for the following three-address code segment. Identify the **scope** (peephole/local/global) appropriate for each optimization you suggest. **(7 marks)**

```
1.  i = 1
2.  j = 1
3.  t1 = 5 * i
4.  t2 = t1 + j
5.  t3 = 4 * t2
6.  t4 = t3
7.  if t4 > 100 goto 11
8.  i = i + 1
9.  if i <= 10 goto 3
10. j = 0
11. end
```

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Peephole Scope (≈ 3 marks of the 7):**
- A peephole optimizer examines a **small sliding window of 2–8 consecutive instructions**.
- It uses **pattern matching** to replace an instruction sequence by a shorter, equivalent one.
- Examples: redundant `MUL` by 1 → eliminated; `MUL` by 2 → `SHL`; `ADD x, 0` → eliminated; jump-to-jump chaining.
- Cost: linear, very low. Runs typically after code generation.

**Basic-Block Scope (≈ 3 marks):**
- A basic block is a maximal straight-line sequence with **single entry and single exit**.
- Local optimization operates within this scope.
- Examples: local CSE, constant folding, constant propagation, algebraic simplification, local dead-code elimination.
- The def-use chain is *linear* inside the block, so no fixed-point iteration is needed.

**Tabular comparison (≈ 1 mark):**

| Aspect | Peephole | Basic Block |
|---|---|---|
| Region | Few instructions | Whole straight-line block |
| Primary IR | Target machine code | Three-address code |
| Method | Pattern matching | DAG / per-block analysis |
| Cost | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ per block |
| Strength | Easy & fast | Catches intra-block redundancy |
| Limitation | Blind to cross-instruction meaning | Blind to cross-block redundancy |

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify Leaders (3 marks):**

| Statement | Rule that makes it a leader | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | First statement of program | Leader |
| 3 | Immediate target of `goto 3` at statement 9 | Leader |
| 11 | Immediate target of `goto 11` at statement 7 | Leader |
| 8 | Immediately follows conditional `goto 11` at statement 7 | Leader |

**Step 2 — Build Basic Blocks (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \{1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6,\ 7\} \\
B_2 &= \{8,\ 9\} \\
B_3 &= \{10\} \\
B_4 &= \{11\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Scope Identification (1 mark):**

| Block / Instruction | Suggested Optimization | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| `t1 = 5 * i` → `MUL t1, i, 5` followed by `t2 = t1 + j` then `t3 = 4 * t2` | Strength reduce `5*i` if `$i$` is constant in this iteration — but `i` varies, so LICM is the right answer | **Loop scope** |
| `t4 = t3` | Dead store if `t4` unused → dead-code elim | **Local** (within $B_1$) |
| `t3 = 4 * t2` | Local CSE if `4 * t2` recomputed → Local | **Local** |
| `goto 3` two-step via `if i ≤ 10 goto 3` | Pattern: load-immediate replaced by register copy | **Peephole** |

---

### 🅱️ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain **Loop scope** and **Interprocedural scope** of optimization with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following control-flow graph. Identify the **natural loop**, perform **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** analysis by listing which statements are invariant, and state the conditions under which hoisting is legal. **(7 marks)**

```
B1: t1 = 100
B2: i = 1
B3: if i > t1 goto B7
B4: t2 = i * 4
B5: t3 = a[t2]
B6: sum = sum + t3
B7: i = i + 1
B8: if i <= t1 goto B3
B9: end
```

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Loop Scope (≈ 3 marks):**
- A *natural loop* is a strongly connected subgraph of the CFG with a **single entry** (the *header*) and at least one back-edge.
- The optimizer can detect invariants *across iterations* because the loop defines a finite repeated pattern.
- Key techniques: **Loop-invariant code motion (LICM)**, **strength reduction** (replace `i*4` by incremental `+4`), **loop unrolling**, **loop fusion**, **loop interchange**.
- Example: `t = 100 * 2` inside the loop body is invariant and can be hoisted to the pre-header.

**Interprocedural Scope (≈ 3 marks):**
- Optimization across **multiple procedures** within or across translation units.
- Examples: **procedure inlining** (substitute callee body at call site), **interprocedural constant propagation** (constant actual parameter → constant formal parameter), **procedure cloning** for call-site-specific specialization.
- Cost is much higher; must respect recursion, aliasing, calling conventions.

**Tabular contrast (≈ 1 mark):**

| Aspect | Loop | Interprocedural |
|---|---|---|
| Region | One strongly-connected CFG region | Caller + callee / call graph |
| Analysis tool | Dominators, back-edges | Call graph, summary edges |
| Key technique | LICM | Inlining |
| Cost | Moderate | High |
| Risk | Code growth by unrolling | Code growth + loss of debug info |

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the natural loop (2 marks):**

- Header: $B_3$ (the block whose successor set includes a predecessor — the back-edge target).
- Back-edge: $B_8 \rightarrow B_3$ (because $B_8$ jumps to $B_3$ and $B_3$ dominates $B_8$).
- **Loop members:** $\{B_3,\ B_4,\ B_5,\ B_6,\ B_7,\ B_8\}$.
- **Pre-header** is $B_2$ (block immediately preceding the header).

**Step 2 — Iteratively identify loop-invariant statements (3 marks):**
