A student who claims SVN eliminates redundancies inside a `while` loop body across iterations will lose marks unless the loop is fully unrolled first. > 5. **Forgetting to pop the stack**: Failing to discard block-local definitions after `pop()` causes stale VNs to leak into successor blocks — a classic conceptual error. > 6. **Not justifying complexity**: KTU examiners award a full mark only when $O(n)$ complexity is *derived*, not merely stated. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Superlocal Value Numbering** is the regional extension of LVN, operating on Extended Basic Blocks (single entry, multiple exits, no back-edges). - The **scope stack** of hash tables provides lexical scoping of value numbers across blocks. - **Three primitive operations**: `lookup` (search stack), `find` (search current top), `insert` (register new key). - **Three optimization outputs**: (1) redundant computation elimination, (2) constant folding, (3) copy propagation. - **$\phi$-renaming** is essential at merge points where multiple predecessors enter a block. - **Complexity** is $O(n)$ time and $O(n)$ space — strictly linear because no fixed-point iteration is needed. - SVN is **unsound for loops**; use GVN or SCCP instead when back-edges are present. - The technique traces to **Briggs, Cooper, and Torczon (1994)** — the canonical reference in compiler-optimization literature. - Common exam keywords: *"extended basic block"*, *"value number"*, *"scope stack"*, *"$\phi$-function"*, *"congruence"*, *"lexical scoping"*, *"regional optimization"*. - Marks weightage in typical KTU ESE: **3–4 marks on definitions**, **7 marks on worked-example transformations**, and **3–4 marks on comparison with LVN/GVN**.

topics.title: Loop Unrolling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Loop Unrolling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Loop Unrolling** is a well-known compiler optimization technique that falls under the broader family of **loop transformation optimizations** used in the back-end of a modern optimizing compiler. Formally, it is defined as:

> **Loop Unrolling (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** A loop transformation technique in which the body of a loop is replicated multiple times (an **unroll factor**, $u$, number of times) within a single iteration of the unrolled loop, with the aim of reducing the number of iterations (and hence the number of branch instructions executed) and exposing more **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** for the scheduler to exploit.

The transformation is formally characterized by:
- An **Unroll Factor** $u \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, where $u \geq 2$ (a factor of $u=1$ means no unrolling).
- The original loop with $N$ iterations is replaced by a loop with $\lceil N/u \rceil$ iterations.
- A **remainder loop** (or **residual loop**) is generated to handle the leftover $N \bmod u$ iterations.

This technique is studied under **Module 4: Optimizing for Parallelism and Locality** and is intrinsically linked to **software pipelining**, **loop scheduling**, and **register allocation** in the compiler back-end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Loop unrolling is grouped with **loop tiling, loop interchange, loop fusion, and loop fission** as one of the core **affine loop transformations** in the polyhedral compilation framework. Examiners expect you to know the relationship between unrolling, branch overhead reduction, and the increased code size that results.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Tea Stall" Model

Imagine a tea stall owner (the **CPU's branch predictor / pipeline**) who, every 30 seconds, has to:
1. Stop pouring tea (end the current iteration).
2. Decide whether the kettle is still full (the loop test).
3. Refill if needed (the increment step).

This constant **"stop-check-restart"** cycle wastes time. Now suppose the stall owner refills the kettle only **once and serves 4 customers in a row** before rechecking the kettle. The "checking overhead" is reduced to **¼ of its original cost**.

That is exactly what **loop unrolling** does to a CPU pipeline:
- The **customers** = the loop body's actual work.
- The **kettle refill** = the branch and counter-increment instructions (loop overhead).
- **Serving 4 at once** = replicating the loop body 4 times (an unroll factor of 4).

The stall owner still does the same total work but spends **far less time on housekeeping** — exactly the speed-up unrolling provides.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Modern out-of-order processors can already hide small amounts of branch overhead. The real power of loop unrolling emerges because copying the body multiple times **exposes independent instructions** that the hardware scheduler can dispatch in parallel on multiple functional units (e.g., two ALUs, two FPUs in a superscalar CPU).

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unroll Factor** | $u$ | $2 \leq u \leq 8$ | Number of body copies fused per iteration |
| **Speedup (ideal)** | $S$ | $\leq u$ | Ratio of original to unrolled execution time |
| **Code-Size Increase** | $\Delta C$ | $\approx (u-1) \times 100\%$ | Extra bytes introduced by body replication |
| **Register Pressure** | $R$ | Grows with $u$ | Live variables that must be simultaneously held |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Principle (Exam-Ready):** Higher $u$ ⇒ greater ILP, but **larger code** and **higher register pressure**, which can cause **spilling** (storing registers back to memory) and actually *decrease* performance. Choosing $u$ is therefore a **compiler heuristic**, not a fixed rule.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Loop Unrolling Work? — The Underlying Reasons

A standard `for` loop executing $N$ times on a RISC-like processor has **three overhead sources**:

1. **Branch instructions** (`br exit`, `bne`, `jmp`) at the loop's tail.
2. **Counter-increment** (`add` or `addi`) instructions.
3. **Loop-condition test** (`cmp` or `slt`) instructions.

If each iteration's *body* has $B$ useful instructions, and the *overhead* per iteration is $O$ instructions, then the total instruction count of the original loop is:

$$T_{\text{orig}} = N \times (B + O)$$

When we unroll by a factor $u$, the number of iterations becomes $N/u$, and the overhead is amortized over a larger body of $u \cdot B$ useful instructions:

$$T_{\text{unrolled}} = \frac{N}{u} \times (uB + O) = N \cdot B + \frac{N}{u} \cdot O$$

The **overhead portion drops by a factor of $u$**, which is the source of the speedup.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Identify the loop candidate.** The compiler's *Loop-Normalization Pass* (in the polyhedral framework) checks if the loop is countable, affine, and free of abnormal exits.
2. **Decide the unroll factor $u$.** Heuristics: pick the largest $u$ such that $u \cdot \text{registers needed} \leq \text{available registers}$.
3. **Replicate the body.** Duplicate the original body $u$ times. Each copy uses a different induction-variable offset.
4. **Adjust the step.** Change the induction-variable step from $1$ to $u$.
5. **Adjust the trip count.** Replace `for i = 0; i < N; i++` with `for i = 0; i < N - (N mod u); i += u`.
6. **Generate a remainder (epilogue) loop.** The leftover $N \bmod u$ iterations are executed in a smaller, un-unrolled (or partially unrolled) loop to preserve correctness for all $N$.
7. **Reschedule the combined body.** Now that $u$ independent copies exist, the **instruction scheduler** can reorder them to fill pipeline slots that were previously empty (VLIW / EPIC architectures benefit most).
8. **Perform register allocation** on the enlarged live range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Anchor:** Loop unrolling is a **mechanical transformation** — it does not change the program's semantics. It is one of the few optimizations that is provably safe to apply on any loop that has been confirmed side-effect-free by alias analysis.

## 2.3 Types of Loop Unrolling (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Type | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Complete Unrolling** | $u = N$ — the entire loop is replaced by straight-line code. | Only when $N$ is a compile-time constant and small. |
| **Partial / Static Unrolling** | $u$ is a fixed compile-time constant (2, 4, 8). | Most common; targets fixed trip counts. |
| **Dynamic Unrolling** | $u$ is decided at run-time based on hardware feedback (e.g., cache misses). | JIT compilers (e.g., Java HotSpot). |
| **Unroll-and-Jam** | Unroll an outer loop, then fuse the resulting inner loops. | Nested loops; used for cache locality. |
| **Unroll with Interleaving** | Successive iterations are interleaved instruction-by-instruction (A,B,C,A,B,C). | Software pipelining and VLIW scheduling. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Exam-Ready Equations and Their Meaning**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Where It Appears |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{orig}} = N(B + O)$ | Total instructions of original loop | Performance analysis |
| 2 | $T_{\text{unrolled}} = NB + \dfrac{NO}{u}$ | Total instructions after unrolling | Speedup calculation |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{T_{\text{orig}}}{T_{\text{unrolled}}} = \dfrac{N(B+O)}{NB + NO/u}$ | True speedup | Analysis questions |
| 4 | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{B+O}{B}$ | Speedup as $u \to \infty$ (ideal ceiling) | Theoretical limit |
| 5 | $\text{New trip count} = \lceil N/u \rceil$ | Iterations of unrolled loop | Code-transformation questions |
| 6 | $\text{Residual} = N \bmod u$ | Iterations in epilogue loop | Remainder calculation |
| 7 | $\Delta C = (u-1) \times C_{\text{body}}$ | Code-size inflation in bytes | Trade-off questions |
| 8 | $R_{\text{required}} \leq R_{\text{available}}$ | Register-pressure constraint on $u$ | Constraint problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule for KTU Answer Sheets:** In all written exams, write the unroll factor as $u$ or $k$ (both are accepted). Use $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ (floor) when the compiler can prove a multiple, and $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ (ceiling) when it cannot — examiners are strict about this distinction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded DSPs (TI C6000, ARM NEON):** Hand-unrolling is essential because the compiler cannot infer complex SIMD pack/unpack patterns.
- **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** Stencil codes in climate/weather simulation rely on unroll-and-jam to keep the working set in L1 cache.
- **GPU Kernels (CUDA / OpenCL):** `nvcc` and HIP compilers aggressively unroll loops over threads.
- **Cryptography & Signal Processing:** AES, FFT, and FIR filter inner loops are unrolled by hand for deterministic latency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Speedup of an Unrolled Loop

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A loop runs $N = 1000$ times. Each iteration executes $B = 20$ useful body instructions and $O = 5$ overhead instructions. The loop is unrolled by a factor $u = 4$. Calculate the original instruction count, the new instruction count, and the achieved speedup.

### Step 1 — Compute the Original Instruction Count

The total number of instructions executed in the original loop is the product of iterations and instructions per iteration:

$$T_{\text{orig}} = N \times (B + O)$$

Substituting the given numerical values:

$$T_{\text{orig}} = 1000 \times (20 + 5)$$

$$T_{\text{orig}} = 1000 \times 25 = 25{,}000 \text{ instructions}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the New (Unrolled) Trip Count

The unrolled loop executes only $\lceil N/u \rceil$ iterations of the *outer* unrolled loop, plus a small epilogue for the remainder:

$$\text{New iterations} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{u} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1000}{4} \right\rceil = 250$$

The remainder is:

$$\text{Residual} = N \bmod u = 1000 \bmod 4 = 0$$

Because the residual is 0, **no epilogue loop is needed** — this is an *exact* unroll, which always scores bonus marks in KTU valuation.

### Step 3 — Compute the Unrolled Instruction Count

The unrolled body has $u \times B = 4 \times 20 = 80$ useful instructions, and the overhead $O = 5$ is paid only once per outer iteration:

$$T_{\text{unrolled}} = \frac{N}{u} \times (uB + O)$$

Substituting:

$$T_{\text{unrolled}} = \frac{1000}{4} \times (4 \times 20 + 5)$$

$$T_{\text{unrolled}} = 250 \times (80 + 5) = 250 \times 85 = 21{,}250 \text{ instructions}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Achieved Speedup

By definition, the speedup $S$ is the ratio of original to new instruction counts:

$$S = \frac{T_{\text{orig}}}{T_{\text{unrolled}}}$$

$$S = \frac{25{,}000}{21{,}250} = \frac{100}{85} = \frac{20}{17} \approx 1.176$$

> **Interpretation:** A speedup of $\approx 1.18$ corresponds to a **~15.3% reduction in instruction count** — typical for modest unroll factors.

### Step 5 — Compute the Theoretical Maximum Speedup (as $u \to \infty$)

From Formula 4 of the cheat sheet:

$$S_{\max} = \frac{B + O}{B} = \frac{20 + 5}{20} = \frac{25}{20} = 1.25$$

So even if we unroll by an infinite factor (impossible in practice), we cannot exceed a **1.25× speedup** because the 20 useful body instructions are unchanged.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Code-Size Inflation

**Problem Statement:**
A loop body compiles to 200 bytes of machine code. If we unroll by $u = 8$, how much extra code size is generated for the loop body alone (excluding overhead)?

### Step 1 — Apply the Code-Size Formula

$$\Delta C = (u - 1) \times C_{\text{body}}$$

$$\Delta C = (8 - 1) \times 200 = 7 \times 200 = 1{,}400 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 2 — Total Loop Code Size After Unrolling

$$C_{\text{total}} = u \times C_{\text{body}} = 8 \times 200 = 1{,}600 \text{ bytes}$$

So the unrolled body is **8× larger** than the original. The "useful" instruction count is unchanged, but the instruction-cache footprint has grown dramatically.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Full Unrolling When $N$ Is Constant

**Problem Statement:** Unroll the following loop completely (assume $N = 4$):

```c
for (i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
    A[i] = B[i] + C[i];
}
```

### Solution

**Iteration $i = 0$:** `A[0] = B[0] + C[0];`
**Iteration $i = 1$:** `A[1] = B[1] + C[1];`
**Iteration $i = 2$:** `A[2] = B[2] + C[2];`
**Iteration $i = 3$:** `A[3] = B[3] + C[3];`

After unrolling, the code is **straight-line** with no branches. The compiler may now recognize that the four loads are independent and issue them simultaneously on a superscalar machine.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Does Unrolling Help vs. Hurt?

| Condition | Helps Performance? | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Small $u$ (2, 4) with high $B$ | **Yes** | Amortizes branch; low register pressure |
| Large $u$ (16+) with few registers | **No** | Forces spilling to memory stack |
| Tight inner loops in DSP / numerical kernels | **Strongly Yes** | Fits in I-cache; exposes SIMD |
| Loops with function calls inside the body | **No** | Inlining must happen first |
| Loops with data-dependent exits (e.g., `break`) | **No** | Cannot prove trip count statically |
| Loops with very small $B$ (1–2 instructions) | **Marginally** | Overhead reduction is the only gain |
| Loops inside already-pipelined software | **May hurt** | Redundant with software pipelining |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation of the General Speedup Curve

Starting from Formula 3:

$$S(u) = \frac{N(B + O)}{NB + \dfrac{NO}{u}}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $NB$:

$$S(u) = \frac{1 + \dfrac{O}{B}}{1 + \dfrac{O}{uB}}$$

Let $r = O / B$ (the *overhead-to-body ratio*). Then:

$$S(u) = \frac{1 + r}{1 + \dfrac{r}{u}}$$

As $u \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{u \to \infty} S(u) = 1 + r = 1 + \frac{O}{B} = \frac{B + O}{B}$$

This confirms the theoretical ceiling from Formula 4. Notice that **as $r \to 0$** (i.e., when the body is huge and overhead is negligible), the speedup approaches $1$ — meaning unrolling is useless for loops with negligible overhead. **Unrolling pays off only when the overhead is a meaningful fraction of the iteration cost**, typically $r \geq 0.1$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Loop-Unrolling Pass Inside an Optimizing Compiler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AST after Front End] --> B[Loop Normalization Pass]
    B --> C{Is Loop Countable and Side Effect Free?}
    C -- No --> Z[Skip Unrolling]
    C -- Yes --> D[Cost Model: Estimate Gain in ILP]
    D --> E{Are Enough Registers Available?}
    E -- No --> F[Try Smaller Unroll Factor u]
    F --> E
    E -- Yes --> G[Replicate Body u Times]
    G --> H[Adjust Induction Variable Step from 1 to u]
    H --> I[Generate Epilogue Loop for N mod u Iterations]
    I --> J[Schedule the Expanded Body for ILP]
    J --> K[Register Allocation on New Live Ranges]
    K --> L[Emit Optimized Intermediate Code]
    L --> M[Next Pass: Software Pipelining or Back End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Effect of Unrolling on Iteration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIG[Original Loop N=8 Iterations]
        O0[Init i=0] --> O1[Body i=0]
        O1 --> O2[Test i less than 8]
        O2 --> O3[Body i=1]
        O3 --> O4[Test i less than 8]
        O4 --> O5[Body i=2]
        O5 --> O6[Test i less than 8]
        O6 --> O7[Body i=3]
        O7 --> O8[Test i less than 8]
        O8 --> O9[Body i=4]
        O9 --> O10[Test i less than 8]
        O10 --> O11[Body i=5]
        O11 --> O12[Test i less than 8]
        O12 --> O13[Body i=6]
        O13 --> O14[Test i less than 8]
        O14 --> O15[Body i=7]
        O15 --> O16[Test i less than 8]
        O16 --> P[Exit]
    end
    subgraph UNRL[Unrolled Loop with factor u=4]
        U0[Init i=0] --> U1[Body i=0 plus Body i=1 plus Body i=2 plus Body i=3]
        U1 --> U2[Test i less than 8]
        U2 --> U3[Body i=4 plus Body i=5 plus Body i=6 plus Body i=7]
        U3 --> U4[Test i less than 8]
        U4 --> Q[Exit]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Compilation-Pass Interaction with Loop Unrolling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SrcIR[Source IR after Canonicalization] --> AA[Alias Analysis]
    AA --> Dep[Dependence Analysis]
    Dep --> OptChoice{Optimization Selection}
    OptChoice --> LU[Loop Unrolling Pass]
    OptChoice --> LInt[Loop Interchange Pass]
    OptChoice --> LT[Loop Tiling Pass]
    OptChoice --> SP[Software Pipelining Pass]
    LU --> Sched[Instruction Scheduler]
    LInt --> Sched
    LT --> Sched
    SP --> Sched
    Sched --> RA[Register Allocator]
    RA --> Emit[Machine Code Emission]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Unroll Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DECISION[Unroll Decision Unit]
        In[Loop L plus IR Stats] --> Perf[Performance Estimator]
        Perf --> RegCheck[Register Pressure Probe]
        RegCheck --> Profit[Profitability Calculator]
        Profit --> Sel{Profit greater than Threshold?}
        Sel -- Yes --> Ap[Apply Unroll with chosen u]
        Sel -- No --> Skip[Keep Original Loop]
    end
    Ap --> Out[Transformed IR]
    Skip --> Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Note for KTU Answer Sheets:** When the question says "Draw the block diagram of the loop unrolling pass," reproduce the structure of **Diagram 4.1** or **Diagram 4.4** above. Mark each box with the pass name, and use single-headed arrows (→) to show data flow. Examiners reward *labeled* blocks; do not leave any box unnamed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define loop unrolling. What is an unroll factor, and how is the choice of unroll factor related to the available registers in the target machine?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Loop Unrolling (1 mark):** Loop unrolling is a compiler optimization in which the body of a loop is replicated $u$ times (where $u$ is the **unroll factor**, with $u \geq 2$) within each iteration of a transformed loop, in order to reduce loop-branch overhead and expose more **instruction-level parallelism** to the scheduler.
>
> **Unroll Factor (1 mark):** The unroll factor $u$ specifies how many copies of the original loop body are fused into one iteration of the unrolled loop. If the original loop runs $N$ times, the unrolled loop runs $\lceil N/u \rceil$ times, with a remainder of $N \bmod u$ iterations handled by an epilogue loop.
>
> **Relation to Registers (1 mark):** Each unrolled body copy may hold additional live variables (e.g., four independent accumulators for $u = 4$). The choice of $u$ is therefore constrained by $u \cdot R_{\text{per-copy}} \leq R_{\text{available}}$ on the target. If the constraint is violated, the allocator will **spill** registers to memory, erasing the speedup.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*State two benefits and two drawbacks of loop unrolling.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Benefits (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
> 1. **Reduces branch and counter-increment overhead** by amortizing the loop test over $u$ copies of the body.
> 2. **Exposes more instruction-level parallelism** (ILP), enabling the scheduler to dispatch multiple independent operations per cycle.
>
> **Drawbacks (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
> 1. **Increases code size** by a factor of up to $u$, which can pollute the instruction cache (I-cache thrashing).
> 2. **Raises register pressure**; exceeding the number of physical registers causes spilling and can nullify the speedup.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> The KTU 2024 ESE format requires that the student answer **one full question** from a Module-Internal-Choice pair. Both alternatives are given below for completeness.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** A `for` loop is executed $N = 2000$ times. Each iteration contains $B = 40$ useful instructions and $O = 8$ loop-overhead instructions. The compiler decides to unroll the loop with an unroll factor $u = 5$.
   **(i)** Calculate the original and unrolled instruction counts.
   **(ii)** Calculate the speedup obtained and the maximum theoretical speedup as $u \to \infty$.
   **(iii)** How many iterations does the residual (epilogue) loop execute? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the **two main limitations** of loop unrolling. Describe, with a labelled block diagram, how the compiler decides whether to unroll a given loop and at what factor. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) (i) Original and Unrolled Instruction Counts — 3 Marks**

Original total instructions:

$$T_{\text{orig}} = N \times (B + O) = 2000 \times (40 + 8) = 2000 \times 48 = 96{,}000$$

New trip count: $\lceil 2000 / 5 \rceil = 400$ iterations.
Residual: $2000 \bmod 5 = 0$, so **no epilogue is needed** (exact unroll).

$$T_{\text{unrolled}} = \frac{N}{u} \times (uB + O) = 400 \times (200 + 8) = 400 \times 208 = 83{,}200$$

**(a) (ii) Speedup and Theoretical Maximum — 2 Marks**

$$S = \frac{T_{\text{orig}}}{T_{\text{unrolled}}} = \frac{96{,}000}{83{,}200} = \frac{480}{416} = \frac{30}{26} = \frac{15}{13} \approx 1.154$$

Theoretical ceiling as $u \to \infty$:

$$S_{\max} = \frac{B + O}{B} = \frac{48}{40} = 1.20$$

**(a) (iii) Residual Iterations — 2 Marks**

$\text{Residual} = 2000 \bmod 5 = 0$, so the residual loop executes **0 iterations**. The original loop is "exactly divisible" by the unroll factor.

**(b) Limitations and Decision Block Diagram — 7 Marks**

**Limitation 1 — Code-Size Inflation (3.5 marks):**
Unrolling multiplies the body size by $u$. For a 200-byte body with $u = 8$, the loop grows from 200 to 1600 bytes. This may exceed the I-cache, causing **cache thrashing**, where each iteration evicts the previous one's instructions.

**Limitation 2 — Register Pressure (3.5 marks):**
Each unrolled body copy holds $u$ independent live values (e.g., accumulators). If the target has 16 registers and each copy needs 5, then $u > 3$ forces **register spilling** to the stack, which costs more memory traffic than the unrolling saved in branch overhead.

**Block Diagram:** Reproduce Diagram 4.4 from Section 4. Label all five stages: *Loop L + IR Stats → Performance Estimator → Register Pressure Probe → Profitability Calculator → Apply/Skip Decision.*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary, Module 4]**

**(a)** Consider the following C code:
```c
for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
    sum = sum + a[i] * b[i];
}
```
   **(i)** Write the code after applying loop unrolling with a factor of $u = 4$.
   **(ii)** Identify the loop overhead instructions that are *eliminated* by this transformation.
   **(iii)** Briefly explain why this unrolled code is more amenable to vectorization than the original. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Differentiate between **loop unrolling** and **software pipelining**. Can both be applied to the same loop? Justify your answer with an example. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) (i) Unrolled Code — 3 Marks**

Assuming $i$ is incremented by 4 and the residual is handled separately:

```c
for (i = 0; i < 96; i += 4) {
    sum = sum + a[i]   * b[i];
    sum = sum + a[i+1] * b[i+1];
    sum = sum + a[i+2] * b[i+2];
    sum = sum + a[i+3] * b[i+3];
}
for (j = 96; j < 100; j++) {
    sum = sum + a[j] * b[j];
}
```

**(a) (ii) Eliminated Overhead — 2 Marks**

The following instructions are *no longer executed every iteration*:
- The branch (`bne` / `br`) at the loop's tail — now taken only once every 4 iterations.
- The induction-variable increment (`addi i, i, 1`) — replaced by a single `addi i, i, 4`.
- The bounds-comparison test (`slt t0, i, 100`) — now executed once per 4 original iterations.

**(a) (iii) Vectorization Friendliness — 2 Marks**

The four multiplications `a[i]*b[i]`, `a[i+1]*b[i+1]`, `a[i+2]*b[i+2]`, `a[i+3]*b[i+3]` are **independent** of one another. The compiler (or the programmer) can map them to a single SIMD instruction (e.g., SSE `_mm_mul_ps` on x86, NEON `vmla.f32` on ARM), which performs four floating-point multiplications in one cycle.

**(b) Loop Unrolling vs. Software Pipelining — 7 Marks**

| Aspect | Loop Unrolling | Software Pipelining |
|---|---|---|
| **What it does** | Replicates the body $u$ times in space. | Replicates the body in time, overlapping successive iterations. |
| **Effect on code size** | Increases by a factor of up to $u$. | Does **not** increase code size significantly. |
| **What it exploits** | Spatial ILP — independent instructions within one big iteration. | Temporal ILP — independent instructions from *different* iterations. |
| **Register pressure** | High (all $u$ copies live). | Lower (cyclic reuse of registers). |
| **Hardware fit** | Wide-issue superscalar CPUs. | VLIW / EPIC architectures (e.g., Itanium, DSPs). |

**Can they be applied to the same loop? (1 mark):** Yes — they are *complementary*. Unrolling is often applied first, and software pipelining is then applied to the unrolled loop to schedule the iterations in a cyclic pattern. The `gcc -O3` and LLVM `-O3` pipelines routinely perform both transformations in sequence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Loop Unrolling:**
> 1. **Forgetting the residual (epilogue) loop.** If $N$ is not a multiple of $u$, the unrolled loop alone is *wrong*. Always write the residual calculation explicitly: $N \bmod u$.
> 2. **Confusing unroll factor $u$ with new trip count.** A common slip is writing "the loop runs 4 times" when $u = 4$. Correct phrasing: *"Each outer iteration performs 4 units of original work; the new trip count is $N/u$."*
> 3. **Claiming unrolling always speeds up code.** It does not. If $B \ll O$ or registers spill, performance can *degrade*. Examiners deduct a full 2 marks for this misconception.
> 4. **Not drawing a labelled block diagram in part (b).** If a 7-mark question asks for a diagram, leaving it unlabelled loses 2–3 marks even if the explanation is correct.
> 5. **Mixing up unroll-and-jam with simple unrolling.** Unroll-and-jam applies to *nested* loops; ordinary unrolling applies to a *single* loop. Examiners expect this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Loop unrolling replicates the body $u$ times in a single outer iteration to reduce overhead and expose ILP.
- ✅ **Unroll factor $u$:** integer $\geq 2$; chosen by compiler heuristics.
- ✅ **New trip count:** $\lceil N/u \rceil$; **Residual:** $N \bmod u$ (executed by epilogue loop).
- ✅ **Speedup formula:** $S = \dfrac{N(B+O)}{NB + NO/u}$; theoretical ceiling $S_{\max} = (B+O)/B$.
- ✅ **Overhead-to-body ratio** $r = O/B$ — unrolling is most useful when $r$ is high (lots of branch vs. little body).
- ✅ **Code-size inflation:** multiplies by $u$; can cause **I-cache thrashing**.
- ✅ **Register pressure:** $u \cdot R_{\text{per-copy}} \leq R_{\text{available}}$ — else spilling occurs.
- ✅ **Types:** complete, partial/static, dynamic, unroll-and-jam, interleaved.
- ✅ **Use cases:** DSP, HPC, GPU kernels, AES/FFT hand-coded routines.
- ✅ **Complementary to:** software pipelining, loop interchange, loop tiling, vectorization.
- ✅ **Cannot be applied when:** loop has data-dependent exits, function calls inside body without inlining, or aliasing prevents reordering.
- ✅ **KTU keywords to memorize:** affine transformation, polyhedral model, instruction-level parallelism, trip count, epilogue, register spilling, I-cache thrashing.

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Loop Unrolling (PECIT751, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Optimization: Finding Uninitialized Variables with Live Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Global Optimization: Finding Uninitialized Variables with Live Sets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Global Data Flow Analysis**, a variable is said to be **uninitialized at a program point p** if there exists at least one execution path from the **ENTRY node of the program** to point `p` along which **no definition (assignment) of that variable** occurs. A *use* of such a variable is a **potentially dangerous uninitialized use** and must be flagged by the compiler as a logical / safety bug.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liveness** and **Uninitialized-ness** are dual concepts:
> - **Liveness** (backward analysis) tells us *where a value might be consumed in the future*.
> - **Uninitialized Detection** (forward analysis) tells us *where a value might not have been produced in the past*.
> When a variable is **both uninitialized at point p AND live at point p**, it constitutes a **verified uninitialized use**.

A **Live Variable** `v` at point `p` means: the value held by `v` at `p` may be read along **some path** before being overwritten. Formally, `v ∈ LIVE(p)` ⇔ ∃ a path `p → q` such that `v` is **used at q** and `v` is **not redefined** on the path segment `[p, q)`.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a row of water taps in a laboratory, each labelled with a variable name (`x`, `y`, `z`).

- **Liveness = "Will someone drink from this tap later?"** You look *forward* in time. If a scientist is going to collect water from tap `y` before the experiment ends, tap `y` is *live*.
- **Uninitialized = "Has anyone turned on this tap yet?"** You look *backward* in time. If the main pipeline (entry of program) was opened but tap `y` was never turned on along the path the experiment took, then `y` is *uninitialized*.
- **The Bug** = *Collecting water from a tap that was never turned on.* That's exactly what a C/Java program does when it reads an uninitialized variable — the value is **garbage** (stack/register residue).

The compiler must answer two questions, one flowing backward, one flowing forward, and **intersect** the answers to locate the real bugs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** This topic is part of **Module 4 – Global Optimisation**. It is tested as either a 3-mark direct question (definition of Live-In / Live-Out) or as a 14-mark full analysis problem requiring iterative data flow evaluation.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `USE[B]` | Variables used in block `B` **before** any definition in `B` |
| `DEF[B]` | Variables **definitely defined** in `B` before any use in `B` |
| `LIVE_IN[B]` | Variables live at the entry of block `B` |
| `LIVE_OUT[B]` | Variables live at the exit of block `B` |
| `UNDEF_IN[B]` | Variables potentially uninitialized at entry of `B` |
| `UNDEF_OUT[B]` | Variables potentially uninitialized at exit of `B` |
| `GEN_undef[B]` | Variables used in `B` before being defined in `B` (contribute new uninit candidates) |
| `KILL_undef[B]` | Variables **definitely defined** in `B` (remove from uninit set) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Live Variable Analysis (Backward Flow)

Liveness is a **backward, may** data flow problem. We start at program EXIT and work back to ENTRY.

**Per-block data flow equations:**

$$LIVE\_IN[B] = USE[B] \cup \left( LIVE\_OUT[B] - DEF[B] \right)$$

$$LIVE\_OUT[B] = \bigcup_{S \in \text{succ}(B)} LIVE\_IN[S]$$

**Iteration rule (worklist / iterative algorithm):**
1. Initialise `LIVE_OUT[B] = ∅` and `LIVE_IN[B] = ∅` for all blocks `B`.
2. Repeat until no change:
   - Recompute `LIVE_IN[B]` and `LIVE_OUT[B]` for every block using the equations above.
3. The algorithm always converges in at most **`N × (V + 1)`** iterations for `V` variables, because each iteration adds at least one new variable to a set (monotonic lattice over the power-set lattice).

### 2.1.2 Uninitialized Variable Analysis (Forward Flow)

This is a **forward, may** data flow problem. We start at ENTRY and work toward EXIT.

**Per-block data flow equations:**

$$UNDEF\_OUT[B] = \left( UNDEF\_IN[B] - KILL\_undef[B] \right) \cup GEN\_undef[B]$$

$$UNDEF\_IN[B] = \bigcup_{P \in \text{pred}(B)} UNDEF\_OUT[P]$$

where:

$$GEN\_undef[B] = \bigcup_{s \in B} \left( USE(s) - \bigcup_{t \text{ precedes } s \text{ in } B} DEF(t) \right)$$

$$KILL\_undef[B] = \text{set of variables that receive a \textbf{definite} definition in } B$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "may" and not "must"?** If we use a **must** analysis (intersection), we would only flag variables that are uninitialized on **every** path. That misses real bugs that occur on **some** path. KTU answers should always use the **may (union)** version for uninitialized detection.

### 2.1.3 The Combined Bug Condition

At any program point `p` inside a statement `s`:

$$\text{UNINIT\_USE}(p) = \left\{ v \;\big|\; v \in UNDEF(p) \;\land\; v \in LIVE(p) \;\land\; v \text{ is used at } s \right\}$$

A compiler **warning** is emitted for every `v ∈ UNINIT_USE(p)`.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Direction | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $LIVE\_IN[B] = USE[B] \cup (LIVE\_OUT[B] - DEF[B])$ | Backward | May | Computes live variables at entry of B |
| 2 | $LIVE\_OUT[B] = \bigcup_{S \in succ(B)} LIVE\_IN[S]$ | Backward | May | Joins from successors |
| 3 | $UNDEF\_IN[B] = \bigcup_{P \in pred(B)} UNDEF\_OUT[P]$ | Forward | May | Joins from predecessors |
| 4 | $UNDEF\_OUT[B] = (UNDEF\_IN[B] - KILL\_undef[B]) \cup GEN\_undef[B]$ | Forward | May | Propagates uninit set |
| 5 | $v \in UNINIT\_USE(p) \iff v \in UNDEF(p) \land v \in LIVE(p)$ | Both | Predicate | Final bug condition |
| 6 | Iterations ≤ $N \cdot (V+1)$ | — | Bound | Convergence bound (N blocks, V variables) |
| 7 | Transfer function: $f_B(x) = (x - KILL\_undef[B]) \cup GEN\_undef[B]$ | — | Monotone | For uninit analysis |
| 8 | Transfer function: $f_B(x) = USE[B] \cup (x - DEF[B])$ | — | Monotone | For liveness analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Modern production compilers (GCC `-Wuninitialized`, Clang static analyzer, LLVM `mem2reg` pass) all implement exactly this pair of analyses. The forward "uninit" pass and the backward "liveness" pass are linked into a single warning engine. The technique is also used in **formal verification tools** (CBMC, SeaHorn) and **embedded safety-critical code checkers** (MISRA-C compliance).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — A Complete Analysis

### 3.1.1 Source Program (C-style)

```c
int x, y, z;
x = 1;          // B1
if (x > 0)       // B2
    y = 2;       // B3
else
    z = 3;       // B4
print(y + z);   // B5
```

### 3.1.2 Control Flow Graph (CFG)

```
ENTRY
  |
  v
[B1] x = 1
  |
  v
[B2] if (x > 0) ----> [B3] y = 2 ---+
   |                                 |
   +---------------> [B4] z = 3 ----+
                                  |
                                  v
                                [B5] print(y + z)
                                  |
                                  v
                                EXIT
```

### 3.1.3 Per-Block USE / DEF / GEN / KILL Computation

| Block | Statement | `USE` | `DEF` | `GEN_undef` | `KILL_undef` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| B1 | `x = 1` | ∅ | {x} | ∅ | {x} |
| B2 | `if (x>0)` | {x} | ∅ | {x} | ∅ |
| B3 | `y = 2` | ∅ | {y} | ∅ | {y} |
| B4 | `z = 3` | ∅ | {z} | ∅ | {z} |
| B5 | `print(y+z)` | {y, z} | ∅ | {y, z} | ∅ |

> *Note: `GEN_undef` for a block = variables that are used before any definition inside the block. `KILL_undef` = variables that get a definite definition in the block.*

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step Liveness Iteration (Backward)

**Initial:** `LIVE_IN[B] = ∅`, `LIVE_OUT[B] = ∅` for all B.

**Iteration 1:**

For B5: succ(B5) = {EXIT}
- `LIVE_OUT[B5] = ∅` (assuming nothing live after EXIT)
- `LIVE_IN[B5] = USE[B5] ∪ (LIVE_OUT[B5] - DEF[B5]) = {y,z} ∪ (∅ - ∅) = {y, z}`

For B3: succ(B3) = {B5}
- `LIVE_OUT[B3] = LIVE_IN[B5] = {y, z}`
- `LIVE_IN[B3] = USE[B3] ∪ (LIVE_OUT[B3] - DEF[B3]) = ∅ ∪ ({y,z} - {y}) = {z}`

For B4: succ(B4) = {B5}
- `LIVE_OUT[B4] = LIVE_IN[B5] = {y, z}`
- `LIVE_IN[B4] = USE[B4] ∪ (LIVE_OUT[B4] - DEF[B4]) = ∅ ∪ ({y,z} - {z}) = {y}`

For B2: succ(B2) = {B3, B4}
- `LIVE_OUT[B2] = LIVE_IN[B3] ∪ LIVE_IN[B4] = {z} ∪ {y} = {y, z}`
- `LIVE_IN[B2] = USE[B2] ∪ (LIVE_OUT[B2] - DEF[B2]) = {x} ∪ ({y,z} - ∅) = {x, y, z}`

For B1: succ(B1) = {B2}
- `LIVE_OUT[B1] = LIVE_IN[B2] = {x, y, z}`
- `LIVE_IN[B1] = USE[B1] ∪ (LIVE_OUT[B1] - DEF[B1]) = ∅ ∪ ({x,y,z} - {x}) = {y, z}`

**Iteration 2:** Re-evaluate; nothing changes ⇒ **Converged**.

**Final Liveness Result:**

| Block | LIVE_OUT | LIVE_IN |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | {x, y, z} | {y, z} |
| B2 | {y, z} | {x, y, z} |
| B3 | {y, z} | {z} |
| B4 | {y, z} | {y} |
| B5 | ∅ | {y, z} |

### 3.1.5 Step-by-Step Uninitialized Variable Iteration (Forward)

**Initial:** `UNDEF_IN[ENTRY] = {x, y, z}` (all locals begin uninitialized at program start).

**Iteration 1:**

For B1: pred(B1) = {ENTRY}
- `UNDEF_IN[B1] = {x, y, z}`
- `UNDEF_OUT[B1] = (UNDEF_IN[B1] - KILL_undef[B1]) ∪ GEN_undef[B1] = ({x,y,z} - {x}) ∪ ∅ = {y, z}`

For B2: pred(B2) = {B1}
- `UNDEF_IN[B2] = UNDEF_OUT[B1] = {y, z}`
- `UNDEF_OUT[B2] = ({y,z} - ∅) ∪ {x} = {x, y, z}`

For B3: pred(B3) = {B2}
- `UNDEF_IN[B3] = UNDEF_OUT[B2] = {x, y, z}`
- `UNDEF_OUT[B3] = ({x,y,z} - {y}) ∪ ∅ = {x, z}`

For B4: pred(B4) = {B2}
- `UNDEF_IN[B4] = UNDEF_OUT[B2] = {x, y, z}`
- `UNDEF_OUT[B4] = ({x,y,z} - {z}) ∪ ∅ = {x, y}`

For B5: pred(B5) = {B3, B4}
- `UNDEF_IN[B5] = UNDEF_OUT[B3] ∪ UNDEF_OUT[B4] = {x, z} ∪ {x, y} = {x, y, z}`
- `UNDEF_OUT[B5] = ({x,y,z} - ∅) ∪ {y, z} = {x, y, z}`

**Iteration 2:** No change ⇒ **Converged**.

**Final Uninit Result:**

| Block | UNDEF_IN | UNDEF_OUT |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | {x, y, z} | {y, z} |
| B2 | {y, z} | {x, y, z} |
| B3 | {x, y, z} | {x, z} |
| B4 | {x, y, z} | {x, y} |
| B5 | {x, y, z} | {x, y, z} |

### 3.1.6 Detecting the Uninitialized Use Bug

At the use point `print(y + z)` in **B5**, we apply the combined predicate:

$$v \in UNINIT\_USE(p) \iff v \in UNDEF(p) \land v \in LIVE(p)$$

At the entry of B5: `UNDEF_IN[B5] = {x, y, z}` and `LIVE_IN[B5] = {y, z}`.

- **`y`**: `y ∈ {x,y,z}` ✓ AND `y ∈ {y,z}` ✓ AND `y` is used in B5 ✓ → **BUG FLAGGED** (z is initialized on the path through B4, but **y is uninitialized** when control flows B2 → B3 → B5 happens. Actually y is defined in B3 along that path. Wait — let me re-check.)

**Re-check:** Path B2 → B3 → B5: y is defined in B3, so y is initialized along that path. Path B2 → B4 → B5: y is NOT defined, so y is uninitialized along that path. Since this is **may analysis**, y is flagged.

- **`y`**: y ∈ {x,y,z} ✓ AND y ∈ {y,z} ✓ AND y is used in B5 ✓ → **WARNING EMITTED** for `y`.
- **`z`**: z ∈ {x,y,z} ✓ AND z ∈ {y,z} ✓ AND z is used in B5 ✓ → **WARNING EMITTED** for `z`.
- **`x`**: x ∈ {x,y,z} ✓ AND x ∈ {y,z} ✗ → **No warning** (x is not live here, so its uninit-ness is harmless).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The intersection of `LIVE` and `UNDEF` at the use point is the **precise set of bugs**. This is why **liveness is essential** — without it, we would also flag `x` as a "bug", producing noisy false positives.

## 3.2 Algorithmic Summary (Iterative Worklist Form)

```
ALGORITHM: Detect_Uninitialized_With_LiveSets(CFG)
INPUT  : Control Flow Graph G = (N, E, ENTRY, EXIT), variables V
OUTPUT : Warning set W of (variable, program_point) pairs

1.  // -------- PHASE 1: Compute Liveness (Backward) --------
2.  for each block B in N do
3.      LIVE_IN[B]  := ∅
4.      LIVE_OUT[B] := ∅
5.  end for
6.  changed := true
7.  while changed do
8.      changed := false
9.      for each block B in N do                  // reverse postorder preferred
10.         new_in  := USE[B] ∪ (LIVE_OUT[B] - DEF[B])
11.         new_out := ∪ { LIVE_IN[S] : S ∈ succ(B) }
12.         if new_in ≠ LIVE_IN[B] or new_out ≠ LIVE_OUT[B] then
13.             LIVE_IN[B]  := new_in
14.             LIVE_OUT[B] := new_out
15.             changed := true
16.         end if
17.     end for
18. end while
19.
20. // -------- PHASE 2: Compute Uninit Set (Forward) --------
21. for each variable v in V do UNDEF[ENTRY] := V
22. UNDEF_IN[ENTRY]  := V          // All variables initially uninitialized
23. UNDEF_OUT[ENTRY] := ∅
24. for each block B in N \ {ENTRY} do
25.     UNDEF_IN[B]  := ∅
26.     UNDEF_OUT[B] := ∅
27. end for
28. changed := true
29. while changed do
30.     changed := false
31.     for each block B in N \ {ENTRY} do
32.         new_in  := ∪ { UNDEF_OUT[P] : P ∈ pred(B) }
33.         new_out := (new_in - KILL_undef[B]) ∪ GEN_undef[B]
34.         if new_in ≠ UNDEF_IN[B] or new_out ≠ UNDEF_OUT[B] then
35.             UNDEF_IN[B]  := new_in
36.             UNDEF_OUT[B] := new_out
37.             changed := true
38.         end if
39.     end for
40. end while
41.
42. // -------- PHASE 3: Emit Warnings --------
43. W := ∅
44. for each program point p in program do
45.     for each variable v used at p do
46.         if v ∈ UNDEF(p) ∧ v ∈ LIVE(p) then
47.             W := W ∪ {(v, p)}
48.         end if
49.     end for
50. end for
51. return W
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Compiler Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code] --> B[Lexical Analysis]
    B --> C[Syntax Analysis]
    C --> D[Semantic Analysis]
    D --> E[CFG Construction]
    E --> F[Phase 1: Liveness Analysis]
    F --> G[Phase 2: Uninitialized Analysis]
    G --> H[Phase 3: Intersection LIVE AND UNDEF]
    H --> I[Warning Generation]
    I --> J[Code Generation]

    subgraph "Global Optimisation Module 4"
        F
        G
        H
        I
    end
```

## 4.2 Liveness vs Uninit — Two Phases Visualised

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Program Entry]) --> P1[Phase 1: Backward Pass - Liveness]
    P1 --> P2[Compute LIVE_IN and LIVE_OUT per block]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 2: Forward Pass - Uninitialized]
    P3 --> P4[Compute UNDEF_IN and UNDEF_OUT per block]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 3: Combine - Intersection]
    P5 --> P6{For each use point p and variable v}
    P6 -->|v in UNDEF and v in LIVE| P7[Emit Warning: v uninitialized at p]
    P6 -->|Otherwise| P8[No Warning]
    P7 --> End([Output Warnings])
    P8 --> End
```

## 4.3 Iterative Data Flow Convergence Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Init[Initialise all sets to empty] --> Loop{Changed in last iteration?}
    Loop -->|Yes| Iterate[Visit each basic block]
    Iterate --> ApplyEq[Apply data flow equations]
    ApplyEq --> Compare{Any set changed?}
    Compare -->|Yes| MarkChanged[Set changed flag true]
    Compare -->|No| NoChange[Set changed flag false]
    MarkChanged --> Loop
    NoChange --> Loop
    Loop -->|No| Done[Converged: All sets final]
    Done --> Output[Output LIVE and UNDEF tables]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic for Bug Detection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[At program point p, variable v is USED] --> B{v in LIVE at p?}
    B -->|No| C1[No warning: value will be overwritten]
    B -->|Yes| D{v in UNDEF at p?}
    D -->|No| C2[No warning: v was definitely assigned]
    D -->|Yes| E1[WARNING: Uninitialized use of v at p]
    C1 --> End([End])
    C2 --> End
    E1 --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Explain the concept of "Live Variable" in global data flow analysis. Define `LIVE_IN` and `LIVE_OUT` of a basic block.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A variable `v` is **live** at a program point `p` if the value of `v` at `p` may be used in the future before being redefined on some execution path starting from `p`.

- `LIVE_IN[B]` = set of variables that are live **at the entry** of basic block `B`.
- `LIVE_OUT[B]` = set of variables that are live **at the exit** of basic block `B`.

Formally:
$$LIVE\_IN[B] = USE[B] \cup (LIVE\_OUT[B] - DEF[B])$$
$$LIVE\_OUT[B] = \bigcup_{S \in succ(B)} LIVE\_IN[S]$$

Liveness is a **backward, may** data flow analysis because the information flows from EXIT to ENTRY, and a variable is live if it is *possibly* used later. (3 marks — full distribution)

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*How is the "uninitialized variable detection" problem related to live variable analysis? Why is the intersection of both analyses required?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **uninitialized detection problem** finds variables that may be used without ever being defined on some path from program entry. It is a **forward, may** analysis using sets `UNDEF_IN` and `UNDEF_OUT`.

The **liveness analysis** finds variables whose current value may be used in the future (backward analysis). A variable that is uninitialized is *not necessarily a bug* — it is only a bug **if its value is actually consumed** later. Liveness provides this consumption information.

The **intersection** of `UNDEF` and `LIVE` at a use point is required because:
1. `UNDEF` alone produces false positives (variables that are uninitialized but never read).
2. `LIVE` alone cannot identify missing initializations.
3. Their intersection yields the **precise set of dangerous uses** of uninitialized variables, eliminating noise. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] For the following program, construct the Control Flow Graph (CFG), identify `USE[B]`, `DEF[B]`, `GEN_undef[B]`, and `KILL_undef[B]` for each block.

```c
1.  a = 5
2.  b = a + 1
3.  if (a < b) goto 6
4.  c = 10
5.  goto 7
6.  c = 20
7.  d = c + a
8.  print(d, b, c)
```

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the `LIVE_IN` and `LIVE_OUT` sets for every block using the iterative algorithm. List the iterations clearly. Then, using the uninitialized variable analysis, identify any **uninitialized use bugs** in the program.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – June 2025, Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with proper data flow equations how the combination of *liveness analysis* and *uninitialized variable analysis* detects uninitialized variable bugs. State the final predicate for emitting a warning and justify why both analyses are needed.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider the following CFG of 5 basic blocks. Compute the `UNDEF_IN` and `UNDEF_OUT` sets iteratively. Then verify which variables are uninitialized at the use points and whether they are live there.

| Block | Statement | `USE` | `DEF` |
|---|---|---|---|
| B1 | `a = 1; b = 2` | ∅ | {a, b} |
| B2 | `if (a > b)` | {a, b} | ∅ |
| B3 | `c = a + b` | {a, b} | {c} |
| B4 | `d = c` | {c} | {d} |
| B5 | `print(d, c, b)` | {d, c, b} | ∅ |

Edges: `ENTRY → B1 → B2 → B3 → B4 → B5 → EXIT` and `B2 → B4` (true branch), `B2 → B3` (false branch).

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — CFG and Sets [7 Marks]**

CFG with 7 blocks:
```
ENTRY → B1: a = 5        → B2: b = a + 1
        → B3: if (a<b) ──(true)──→ B5: c = 20
                          ──(false)─→ B4: c = 10
B4: c = 10 → B6: goto 7 → B7: d = c + a
B5: c = 20 ──────────────────────→ B7: d = c + a
B7 → B8: print(d, b, c) → EXIT
```

| Block | Statement | `USE[B]` | `DEF[B]` | `GEN_undef[B]` | `KILL_undef[B]` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| B1 | `a = 5` | ∅ | {a} | ∅ | {a} |
| B2 | `b = a+1` | {a} | {b} | {a} | {b} |
| B3 | `if (a<b)` | {a, b} | ∅ | {a, b} | ∅ |
| B4 | `c = 10` | ∅ | {c} | ∅ | {c} |
| B5 | `c = 20` | ∅ | {c} | ∅ | {c} |
| B6 | `goto 7` | ∅ | ∅ | ∅ | ∅ |
| B7 | `d = c+a` | {c, a} | {d} | {c, a} | {d} |
| B8 | `print(d,b,c)` | {d, b, c} | ∅ | {d, b, c} | ∅ |

*(3 marks for CFG drawing, 4 marks for tables)*

**Part (b) — Liveness and Uninit Iteration [7 Marks]**

**Liveness Iteration 1 (Backward):**
- `LIVE_IN[B8] = {d, b, c}`, `LIVE_OUT[B8] = ∅`
- `LIVE_IN[B7] = {c, a} ∪ ({d,b,c} - {d}) = {a, c}`, `LIVE_OUT[B7] = {d,b,c}`
- `LIVE_IN[B6] = ∅`, `LIVE_OUT[B6] = {a, c}`
- `LIVE_IN[B5] = ∅`, `LIVE_OUT[B5] = {a, c}`
- `LIVE_IN[B4] = ∅`, `LIVE_OUT[B4] = {a, c}`
- `LIVE_IN[B3] = {a, b}`, `LIVE_OUT[B3] = LIVE_IN[B5] ∪ LIVE_IN[B4] = {a, c}`
- `LIVE_IN[B2] = {a} ∪ ({a,c} - {b}) = {a, c}`, `LIVE_OUT[B2] = {a, b}`
- `LIVE_IN[B1] = ∅ ∪ ({a,c} - {a}) = {c}`, `LIVE_OUT[B1] = {a, c}`

**Liveness Iteration 2:** Recompute; values stabilise.

**Final Liveness:**

| Block | LIVE_IN | LIVE_OUT |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | {c} | {a, c} |
| B2 | {a, c} | {a, b} |
| B3 | {a, b} | {a, c} |
| B4 | ∅ | {a, c} |
| B5 | ∅ | {a, c} |
| B6 | ∅ | {a, c} |
| B7 | {a, c} | {d, b, c} |
| B8 | {d, b, c} | ∅ |

**Uninit Iteration (Forward):** Starting with `UNDEF_IN[ENTRY] = {a, b, c, d}`.
- After B1: kills `a` ⇒ `UNDEF_OUT[B1] = {b, c, d}`
- After B2: GEN adds `a` (used) ⇒ `UNDEF_OUT[B2] = {a, b, c, d}`
- B3 path: `UNDEF_IN[B3] = {a,b,c,d}`, kills `c` ⇒ `UNDEF_OUT[B3] = {a, b, d}`
- B4 path: `UNDEF_IN[B4] = {a,b,c,d}`, kills `c` ⇒ `UNDEF_OUT[B4] = {a, b, d}`
- B5 path: `UNDEF_IN[B5] = {a,b,c,d}`, kills `c` ⇒ `UNDEF_OUT[B5] = {a, b, d}`
- B6: pass-through
- B7: `UNDEF_IN[B7] = {a,b,d} ∪ {a,b,d} = {a,b,d}`, GEN adds `c, a` (used) ⇒ `UNDEF_OUT[B7] = {a, b, c, d}` (since c is used and not defined in B7)
- B8: `UNDEF_IN[B8] = {a, b, c, d}`

**Bug Detection at B8:** Uses `d, b, c`. `LIVE_IN[B8] = {d, b, c}` and `UNDEF_IN[B8] = {a, b, c, d}`.
- **`d`**: in both → **WARNING** (d is used in B8 but never assigned along path ENTRY→B1→B2→B4→B6→B7→B8? Wait — d IS defined in B7, but only along paths that reach B7. All paths reach B7, so d should be defined.)

*Correction:* `KILL_undef[B7] = {d}` because `d = c + a` defines d. So `UNDEF_OUT[B7] = ({a,b,d} - {d}) ∪ {c, a} = {a, b, c}`. Then `UNDEF_IN[B8] = {a, b, c}`. Re-check: at B8, uses `{d, b, c}`. `d` is NOT in `UNDEF_IN[B8] = {a,b,c}`, so d is fine. `b` is in both → WARNING. `c` is in both → WARNING.

**Final Answer (Part b):** Uninitialized uses detected: `b` and `c` at `print` in B8. Variable `a` is uninitialized at B8 entry but not live there ⇒ not flagged. Variable `d` is initialized in B7 along all paths ⇒ not flagged. *(4 marks)*

---

### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation [7 Marks]**

*(Full text answering the question with both data flow equation systems and the combined predicate. Refer to Sections 2.1 and 2.2 of these notes. 7 marks distributed: 2 for liveness equations, 2 for uninit equations, 2 for combined predicate, 1 for justification.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]**

| Block | USE | DEF |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | ∅ | {a, b} |
| B2 | {a, b} | ∅ |
| B3 | {a, b} | {c} |
| B4 | {c} | {d} |
| B5 | {d, c, b} | ∅ |

**Uninit Iteration (Forward):**

| Iter | Block | UNDEF_IN | UNDEF_OUT |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | B1 | {a, b, c, d} | {c, d} (kills a, b) |
| 1 | B2 | {c, d} | {a, b, c, d} (adds a, b from use) |
| 1 | B3 | {a, b, c, d} | {a, b, d} (kills c) |
| 1 | B4 | {a, b, d} | {a, b, c} (kills d, adds c from use) |
| 1 | B5 | {a, b, c} | {a, b, c, d} (adds d, c, b from use) |

Iteration 2 produces no changes. Converged.

**Liveness Iteration (Backward):**

| Block | LIVE_OUT | LIVE_IN |
|---|---|---|
| B5 | ∅ | {d, c, b} |
| B4 | {d, c, b} | {c, b} (kills d) |
| B3 | {c, b} | {a, b} (kills c) |
| B2 | {a, b} ∪ {c, b} = {a, b, c} | {a, b, c} |
| B1 | {a, b, c} | {a, b, c} |

**Bug Detection at B5:** Uses `d, c, b`. `UNDEF_IN[B5] = {a, b, c}` and `LIVE_IN[B5] = {d, c, b}`.
- `b` ∈ UNDEF ✓ and `b` ∈ LIVE ✓ → **WARNING: uninitialized use of b**
- `c` ∈ UNDEF ✓ and `c` ∈ LIVE ✓ → **WARNING: uninitialized use of c**
- `d` ∉ UNDEF (d is defined in B4) → **No warning for d**
- `a` ∈ UNDEF but `a` ∉ LIVE → **No warning (dead code, not actually used)**

**Result: Two uninitialized-use bugs detected — `b` and `c` at the `print` statement in B5.** *(7 marks total)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing `USE` and `DEF`:** USE means "used **before** any definition in the block". A block `a = a + 1` has USE = ∅ and DEF = {a}, not the other way around. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Wrong flow direction:** Liveness is **backward**; uninit is **forward**. Reversing them is a fundamental error. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Using INTERSECTION (must) instead of UNION (may) for uninit:** Misses real bugs. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting to compute both LIVE and UNDEF at the use point:** The final intersection must be done at the program point of the USE statement, not just at block boundaries. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Not showing iterations clearly:** KTU examiners require explicit iteration-by-iteration working. Skipping directly to the final answer loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorise before exam):**

- ☐ **Liveness is BACKWARD**, **Uninit is FORWARD** — opposite directions.
- ☐ Both are **"may" analyses** using **UNION** (∪) at merge points.
- ☐ `USE[B]` = uses before defines in B; `DEF[B]` = defines before uses in B.
- ☐ Liveness equations:
  - $LIVE\_IN[B] = USE[B] \cup (LIVE\_OUT[B] - DEF[B])$
  - $LIVE\_OUT[B] = \bigcup_{S \in succ(B)} LIVE\_IN[S]$
- ☐ Uninit equations:
  - $UNDEF\_IN[B] = \bigcup_{P \in pred(B)} UNDEF\_OUT[P]$
  - $UNDEF\_OUT[B] = (UNDEF\_IN[B] - KILL\_undef[B]) \cup GEN\_undef[B]$
- ☐ Final bug condition: $v \in UNDEF(p) \;\land\; v \in LIVE(p) \;\land\; v$ used at `p`.
- ☐ Convergence bound: **at most N·(V+1)** iterations for N blocks and V variables.
- ☐ All transfer functions are **monotone** (always grow or stay same) over the powerset lattice ⇒ algorithm always terminates.
- ☐ **Engineering use:** Powers `-Wuninitialized` in GCC, Clang static analyzer, LLVM `mem2reg`, MISRA-C compliance checkers, formal verification (CBMC, SeaHorn).
- ☐ **Liveness eliminates false positives**: without it, every uninitialized variable would be flagged, even dead ones. Liveness filters to only the actually-consumed values.
- ☐ Always show iteration tables explicitly in KTU answers — direct answers without iteration steps lose marks.
- ☐ Order blocks in **reverse postorder** for liveness and **postorder** for uninit to speed up convergence (a small KTU bonus point).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Code Placement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Global Code Placement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Global Code Placement** is a *machine-independent (or peephole-level) global optimization* technique that repositions instructions across basic block boundaries within a **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** such that the resulting code executes fewer operations on frequently traversed paths. The goal is to **minimize dynamic instruction count** by aligning the static layout of code with the runtime execution profile.

In simple terms, the compiler acts like a *traffic engineer*. It studies which "roads" (code paths) in your program are used most often (the **hot paths**) and which are rarely used (the **cold paths**), and then re-routes the traffic — moving useful code onto the fast, frequently-traveled routes and off the slow, rarely-traveled ones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters in KTU exams:** Code Placement is the *parent strategy* under which **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)**, **Straightening**, **Branch Alignment**, and **Tail Merging** are discussed. A clear understanding here unlocks the entire Module 4 global optimization chapter.

### Real-World Analogy
Imagine a hospital emergency ward. The most critical equipment (defibrillators, oxygen) is placed *exactly at the entry point* of the most-used corridor. The rarely-used orthopedic tools are placed in a far corner. **Global Code Placement** does the same thing — it places frequently-executed code near the top of the `if-true` branch (the hot path) and pushes rare error-handling code to the cold branches, so the CPU spends fewer cycles fetching, decoding, and executing.

### Key Standard Metrics
- **Dynamic Instruction Count**: The number of instructions actually executed at runtime.
- **Static Instruction Count**: The number of instructions in the compiled binary.
- **Branch Probability $p$**: The estimated likelihood that a given conditional branch is taken. Usually $0 \leq p \leq 1$, with **biased branches** having $p > 0.5$ (likely-taken) or $p < 0.5$ (likely-not-taken).
- **Hot Path**: The CFG path with the highest accumulated execution frequency.
- **Cold Path**: The CFG path with the lowest accumulated execution frequency.

> [!TIP]
> A **basic block** is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address code instructions where flow enters at the beginning and leaves only at the end (no jumps in the middle, no jumps out except at the end).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Philosophy: "Move Code to Where It Runs Less"
The unifying principle behind every code-placement strategy is:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{optimized}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot c_i < \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot c_i \Big\vert_{\text{original}}
$$

where:
- $f_i$ = execution frequency of instruction $i$
- $c_i$ = execution cost (cycles) of instruction $i$
- $n$ = total number of instructions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The compiler does **not** change program *semantics*. It only changes the **position** of code.

### 2.2 The Four Sub-Strategies of Global Code Placement

#### (A) Straightening (Unification of Successive Basic Blocks)
When two basic blocks are connected by a **unconditional jump** and the predecessor has only one successor, the jump can be **eliminated** by merging the two blocks into one.

- **Condition for safety**: $B_2$ is the *only* successor of $B_1$, and $B_1$ is the *only* predecessor of $B_2$ (i.e., $B_2$ does not appear as the target of any other branch).
- **Benefit**: Eliminates a `JUMP` instruction, saving one fetch + one decode cycle on every traversal of this edge.

#### (B) Branch Target Alignment (Likely/Unless Hints)
When a biased conditional branch is detected (e.g., $p > 0.9$ for the fall-through case), the compiler **aligns the hot target** to be the fall-through block. This exploits the CPU's **static branch prediction** — most modern CPUs predict forward branches as "not-taken" by default.

- **KTU favourite example**: GCC's `__builtin_expect((x), 1)` and C++20's `[[likely]]` / `[[unlikely]]` attributes are direct applications of this principle.

#### (C) Tail Merging (Cross Jumping)
When two basic blocks end with the **same sequence of instructions**, and they are followed by the **same successor**, the duplicate trailing instructions are removed and replaced with a forward jump from each block to a single shared copy.

- **Reduction formula**: If two blocks each end with $k$ identical instructions, the savings is $2k - k - 1 = k - 1$ instructions.

#### (D) Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) — Discussed as a Special Case
A piece of computation inside a loop is **invariant** if its operands are not modified within the loop. Such code can be moved to a **preheader** (a block that sits just before the loop entry) and executed only once per loop iteration of the outer region, rather than once per loop iteration of the inner loop.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Variable Meaning | Constraint / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Cost | $D = \sum f_i \cdot c_i$ | $f_i$ freq, $c_i$ cost | $f_i \geq 0$, $c_i > 0$ |
| Branch Bias Detection | $\vert p - 0.5 \vert > \tau$ | $\tau$ bias threshold (typically **0.1**) | $0 \leq p \leq 1$ |
| Tail Merging Saving | $S = k - 1$ | $k$ duplicate trailing instructions | $k \geq 2$ |
| LICM Frequency Reduction | $F_{\text{new}} = F_{\text{outer}}, \quad F_{\text{old}} = F_{\text{loop}} \cdot N$ | $N$ loop trip count | $F_{\text{new}} \ll F_{\text{old}}$ |
| Hot Path Selection | $B_{\text{hot}} = \arg\max_{B} \sum_{e \in \text{edges}(B)} f(e)$ | edge frequencies from profiling | sum over CFG |
| Straightening Block Merging | $B_{\text{merged}} = B_1 \cup B_2$ | concatenated instruction list | $B_1$ has single successor $= B_2$ |
| Reduction Ratio | $R = 1 - \dfrac{D_{\text{opt}}}{D_{\text{orig}}}$ | $0 \leq R \leq 1$ | Higher is better |
| Threshold for Hot Block | $f(B) > \theta_{\text{hot}}$ | $\theta_{\text{hot}}$ is the **hot block frequency threshold** | compiler-tuned |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical LaTeX safety note for tables:** Notice the use of `\vert` and `\dfrac` above. The vertical bar `|` character is **forbidden** inside KTU table cells because it conflicts with the markdown column separator. Always use `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\dfrac` in tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Compilers**: GCC `-fprofile-generate` / `-fprofile-use`, LLVM `PGO` (Profile-Guided Optimization), Intel ICC `-prof-use`.
- **Embedded Systems**: In microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M, AVR), global code placement directly reduces interrupt latency and energy consumption.
- **Just-In-Time Compilers**: HotSpot JVM, V8 (Chrome/Node.js) reorder hot traces inline within megamorphic call sites.
- **DSP Programming**: On TI C6000 and SHARC processors, code placement controls VLIW packet packing and fetch packet efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cost Minimization Objective

We begin with a CFG containing $n$ basic blocks $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ with associated execution frequencies $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n$ and per-instruction costs $c_{i,1}, c_{i,2}, \ldots$.

**Step 1:** Write the *unoptimized dynamic cost* of executing the entire program:

$$
D_{\text{orig}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot \sum_{j \in B_i} c_{i,j}
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $f_i$ can be obtained from a **profile run** (instrumentation) or estimated by static heuristics (loop nesting depth):

$$
f_i \approx \prod_{L \in \text{loops}(B_i)} N_L
$$

where $N_L$ is the estimated trip count of loop $L$ enclosing block $B_i$.

**Step 3:** After code placement, the same program executes with a *new* frequency vector $f_i'$ and (possibly) a *new* instruction set per block $B_i'$. The cost reduction is:

$$
\Delta D = D_{\text{orig}} - D_{\text{new}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( f_i \cdot C_i - f_i' \cdot C_i' \right), \quad \text{where } C_i = \sum_{j \in B_i} c_{i,j}
$$

**Step 4:** A placement is **profitable** if and only if $\Delta D > 0$. Equivalently, the **reduction ratio** is:

$$
R = 1 - \frac{D_{\text{new}}}{D_{\text{orig}}}
$$

**Step 5 (Lagrangian interpretation):** For straightening, $D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{orig}} - 1 \cdot f_{\text{edge}}$, where $f_{\text{edge}}$ is the traversal frequency of the eliminated jump edge. Thus:

$$
R_{\text{straighten}} = \frac{f_{\text{edge}}}{D_{\text{orig}}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Tail Merging on Two CFG Paths

Consider two basic blocks $A$ and $B$ with the following three-address code:

$$
A: \quad t_1 := a + b \quad ; \quad t_2 := t_1 \cdot c \quad ; \quad \text{GOTO } M
$$

$$
B: \quad t_3 := x - y \quad ; \quad t_2 := t_1 \cdot c \quad ; \quad \text{GOTO } M
$$

Both end with the identical two-instruction sequence `t_2 := t1 * c; GOTO M`. The compiler applies **tail merging**:

**Step 1 (Identify):** The trailing two instructions of $A$ and $B$ are identical.
**Step 2 (Extract):** Create a new shared block $S$ containing the common tail.
**Step 3 (Rewrite):**

$$
A': \quad t_1 := a + b \quad ; \quad \text{GOTO } S
$$

$$
B': \quad t_3 := x - y \quad ; \quad \text{GOTO } S
$$

$$
S: \quad t_2 := t_1 \cdot c \quad ; \quad \text{GOTO } M
$$

**Step 4 (Count savings):** Original had $2 + 2 = 4$ instructions in the tails. New has $2$ instructions in $S$ plus $2$ jumps $= 4$ static instructions but the **duplicated computation** of $t_2$ is removed. If $f_A$ and $f_B$ are both large, the **dynamic** saving is $f_A + f_B$ executions of the multiplication.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Apply Each Strategy

| Strategy | Pre-condition | Effect on Static Code | Effect on Dynamic Cost | Risk if Misapplied |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Straightening | Single succ, single pred | $-1$ jump per merge | $-f_{\text{edge}}$ cycles | Merging a shared block (would break other predecessors) |
| Branch Alignment | $\vert p - 0.5 \vert > 0.1$ | Same | Improves branch prediction accuracy by $\Delta A$ | Wrong guess reverses prediction benefit |
| Tail Merging | Identical suffix, shared successor | May add 1 jump | $-k \cdot (f_A + f_B)$ cycles | Breaks if the suffixes use *different* temporaries with same names |
| LICM | Operand not modified in loop | Adds 1 preheader jump | $\div N$ executions of moved block | Breaks if moved code has side effects (e.g., `printf`, memory aliasing) |

### 3.4 Profile-Guidance Heuristic: Hot/Cold Splitting

Given a CFG, the **profile-guided placer** performs the following:

$$
\text{For each block } B_i: \quad \text{label}(B_i) = \begin{cases} \text{HOT} & \text{if } f_i > \theta_{\text{hot}} \\ \text{COLD} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Then physically lay out the binary such that all HOT blocks are **contiguous** (in `.text.hot` section in ELF) and all COLD blocks are grouped (in `.text.unlikely`). This maximizes **I-cache locality** and **TLB reach**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Graph — Before Code Placement

```mermaid
graph TD
    entryNode["ENTRY"] --> checkNode["if x > 0"]
    checkNode -- "TRUE p=0.95" --> hotNode["BLOCK A: t1 = a + b, t2 = t1 * c, JUMP M"]
    checkNode -- "FALSE p=0.05" --> coldNode["BLOCK B: error_handler_log, JUMP M"]
    hotNode --> mergeNode["M: print t2, EXIT"]
    coldNode --> mergeNode
```

**Visual reading of the diagram above:** The hot path (`p = 0.95`) goes through `BLOCK A` which contains a `JUMP` to `M`, while the rare error path also jumps to `M`. Notice the **redundant jump** in BLOCK A.

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Graph — After Straightening + Branch Alignment

```mermaid
graph TD
    entryNode2["ENTRY"] --> checkNode2["if x > 0"]
    checkNode2 -- "TRUE fall-through HOT" --> hotNode2["BLOCK A: t1 = a + b, t2 = t1 * c, print t2, EXIT"]
    checkNode2 -- "FALSE cold p=0.05" --> coldNode2["BLOCK B: error_handler_log, JUMP A_exit_tail"]
    hotNode2 --> exitNode2["EXIT"]
    coldNode2 --> exitNode2
```

**Visual reading:** The hot path is now the **fall-through**, eliminating one jump. The cold path now jumps *forward* to the exit — a backward-or-forward jump that's perfectly predicted as not-taken by static prediction hardware.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Profile-Guided Placer

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcInput["IR / 3AC Code"] --> cfgBuilder["CFG Builder"]
    cfgBuilder --> profiler["Profiler: Static or Dynamic"]
    profiler --> freqMap["Block Frequency Map"]
    freqMap --> strategySelector["Placement Strategy Selector"]
    strategySelector --> str8["Straightening Pass"]
    strategySelector --> align["Branch Alignment Pass"]
    strategySelector --> tail["Tail Merging Pass"]
    strategySelector --> licmPass["LICM Pass"]
    str8 --> mergedIR["Optimized IR"]
    align --> mergedIR
    tail --> mergedIR
    licmPass --> mergedIR
    mergedIR --> emitAsm["Emit Hot then Cold Sections"]
    emitAsm --> finalBin["Hot Text and Cold Text Segments"]
```

**Reading:** This shows the pipeline of a modern optimizer. The `Placement Strategy Selector` chooses which pass to apply based on the frequency map, and the final emitter lays out hot code contiguously in memory.

### 4.4 I-Cache Locality Before vs After Hot/Cold Splitting

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph beforeOpt["BEFORE: Mixed Layout"]
        a1["B1 HOT"] --> a2["B2 COLD"] --> a3["B3 HOT"] --> a4["B4 COLD"] --> a5["B5 HOT"]
    end
    subgraph afterOpt["AFTER: Hot Cold Splitting"]
        b1["B1 HOT"] --> b2["B3 HOT"] --> b3["B5 HOT"]
        b4["B2 COLD"] --> b5["B4 COLD"]
    end
```

**Reading:** The "before" layout alternates hot and cold blocks, causing I-cache **thrashing**. The "after" layout groups hot blocks contiguously, so the cache line stays warm during inner-loop execution.
