## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Global Code Placement. State any two situations where it is most effective.*

> **Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
> **Global Code Placement** is a machine-independent optimization that repositions instructions across basic block boundaries in a CFG to reduce dynamic instruction count by placing frequently executed code in execution-efficient locations.
> **Two situations where it is most effective:**
> 1. **Biased conditional branches** — when the probability of one branch is $> 0.9$, alignment eliminates costly mispredictions.
> 2. **Identical instruction tails** in two or more blocks merging at a common successor — enables **tail merging** with large dynamic savings on hot paths.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between **Straightening** and **Tail Merging** as global code-placement transformations.*

> **Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
> | Aspect | Straightening | Tail Merging |
> |---|---|---|
> | Purpose | Remove an unconditional jump between two consecutive blocks | Remove duplicate code at the **end** of two blocks |
> | Condition | $B_2$ is the sole successor of $B_1$ and vice versa | Both blocks end with the **same instruction sequence** and share a common successor |
> | Effect | Block count decreases by 1 | Block count may increase by 1 (shared tail), but duplicate code decreases |
> | Savings | One jump per merge | Multiple instructions per duplicated tail |

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **ESE Module 4 — Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Coverage

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]
*With a neat CFG diagram, explain the **global code placement** strategies: (i) Straightening, (ii) Branch Alignment, and (iii) Tail Merging. State the pre-condition for each.*

> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
> 
> **Introduction (1 mark):** Global code placement repositions instructions across basic blocks to reduce dynamic execution cost while preserving semantics.
> 
> **(i) Straightening [2 marks]:**
> Draw two blocks $B_1$ and $B_2$ where $B_1$ ends with an unconditional `GOTO B2` and $B_2$ is the *only* successor of $B_1$, and $B_1$ is the *only* predecessor of $B_2$. Merge them into a single block $B_{12}$. The unconditional jump is eliminated. Cost reduction: one fetch-decode-execute per traversal of the eliminated edge.
> 
> **(ii) Branch Alignment [2 marks]:**
> Draw a conditional `if x > 0` block with two successors: hot block (fall-through) and cold block. Show the original layout where the cold block is the fall-through, then the optimized layout where the hot block is the fall-through. State that CPUs with static prediction predict forward branches as not-taken; hence aligning the likely-taken branch as the jump-target (and the not-taken as fall-through) **inverts** the default. Mention compiler hints like GCC `__builtin_expect`.
> 
> **(iii) Tail Merging [2 marks]:**
> Draw two blocks $A$ and $B$ with identical instruction tails of length $k$ and a shared successor $M$. Show the new shared block $S$ containing the common tail. State that the savings in dynamic instructions is $(f_A + f_B) \cdot k$.
> 
> **Pre-conditions summary [extra credit, 1 mark bonus often given]:**
> - Straightening: unique successor + unique predecessor.
> - Branch Alignment: $\vert p - 0.5 \vert > 0.1$.
> - Tail Merging: identical suffix + shared successor.

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*Consider the following three-address code. Construct the basic blocks, draw the CFG, and apply **tail merging** and **straightening** to optimize it. Show the optimized code.*

> **Given Three-Address Code:**
> ```
> 1.  t1 := a + b
> 2.  if t1 > 0 goto L1
> 3.  t2 := 0
> 4.  goto L2
> L1: t3 := a - b
> 5.  t2 := t3
> 6.  goto L2
> L2: t4 := t2 * 2
> 7.  print t4
> 8.  halt
> ```
> 
> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
> 
> **Step 1: Identify Leaders and Basic Blocks [1 mark]**
> - Leader at instruction 1 (entry).
> - Leader at instruction 2 (target of conditional).
> - Leader at instruction 3 (target of unconditional goto? No — fall-through from 2).
> Actually, leaders: 1, 2, 3 (target of goto L1 is 5... wait, L1 = instruction 5, not 3). Let us re-index: `L1: t3 := a - b` is instruction 5. Leaders are 1, 2, 3, 5, 6.
> 
> **Step 2: Basic Blocks [2 marks]**
> - **B1**: `1. t1 := a + b; 2. if t1 > 0 goto L1`
> - **B2**: `3. t2 := 0; 4. goto L2`
> - **B3** (L1): `5. t3 := a - b; 6. goto L2`
> - **B4** (L2): `6. t2 := t3 [this is wrong, instruction 6 is goto L2 already]`
> Re-parse: B4 is the merge target `L2`: instructions 6, 7, 8: `6. t2 := t3; 7. t4 := t2 * 2; 8. print t4; 9. halt`. Wait — the original listing has `t2 := t3` at line 5. Let me re-correct.
> 
> Final blocks:
> - **B1**: `t1 := a + b; if t1 > 0 goto B3`
> - **B2**: `t2 := 0; goto B4`
> - **B3**: `t3 := a - b; t2 := t3; goto B4`
> - **B4**: `t4 := t2 * 2; print t4; halt`
> 
> **Step 3: Apply Tail Merging [2 marks]**
> B2 and B3 both end with `t2 := <value>; goto B4`. Extract a shared tail block $S$: `goto B4`. Rewrite B2 as `t2 := 0; goto S` and B3 as `t3 := a - b; t2 := t3; goto S`. (Note: a stricter tail-merge would only merge if the *value-assignment* is the same; here they differ, so we may not fully merge. For exam credit, state this constraint.)
> 
> **Step 4: Apply Straightening [1 mark]**
> The shared tail `S` is a single-instruction block `goto B4` with single predecessor (after rewrite). Merge $S$ into $B_4$, eliminating the jump.
> 
> **Step 5: Final Optimized Code [1 mark]**
> ```
> B1: t1 := a + b; if t1 > 0 goto B3
> B2: t2 := 0
> B3: t3 := a - b; t2 := t3
> B4: t4 := t2 * 2; print t4; halt
> ```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*Explain how **profile-guided global code placement** reduces I-cache misses. Mention the role of block frequency estimation and hot/cold splitting.*

> **Model Solution:**
> 1. **I-cache basics [1 mark]**: A cache line is typically 64 bytes. If hot and cold blocks are interleaved, the working set of hot blocks exceeds cache capacity, causing evictions.
> 2. **Block frequency estimation [2 marks]**: Either via *static heuristics* (loop nesting depth, branch bias) or *dynamic profiling* (instrumented binary run on representative input). The result is a frequency $f_i$ for each block.
> 3. **Hot/cold splitting [2 marks]**: Sort blocks by $f_i$, partition at threshold $\theta_{\text{hot}}$, lay out hot blocks contiguously in the `.text.hot` section, cold blocks in `.text.unlikely`. This maximizes spatial locality.
> 4. **Quantification [2 marks]**: Cache miss rate is approximated by $\text{Miss Rate} \approx 1 - \text{Reuse}$. By clustering hot blocks, reuse increases from $\sim 0.3$ to $\sim 0.9$ in typical workloads, yielding $2\text{x}$ to $3\text{x}$ speedup on cache-bound programs.

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]
*Write short notes on: (i) Loop-Invariant Code Motion as a special case of global placement, (ii) Hazards of incorrect code placement.*

> **Model Solution:**
> **(i) LICM [4 marks]:**
> A computation inside a loop whose operands are *not* modified in the loop is invariant. LICM moves it to a *preheader* (a synthetic block just before the loop). It runs once per loop entry rather than once per iteration. Conditions:
> 1. The block dominates all loop exits.
> 2. The block is the only one that assigns to the operands.
> 3. The moved code is *pure* (no side effects, no memory aliasing).
> Mention that LICM reduces frequency from $N \cdot f_{\text{loop}}$ to $f_{\text{outer}}$, where $N$ is the trip count.
> 
> **(ii) Hazards [3 marks]:**
> 1. **Aliasing**: Moving `*p = *p + 1` out of a loop is invalid if `p` aliases a loop-modified variable.
> 2. **Exception semantics**: Moving `x/y` out of a loop changes when a divide-by-zero exception is raised.
> 3. **Debugger confusion**: Aggressive placement makes single-stepping unintuitive.
> 4. **Branch misprediction reversal**: Over-aligning a branch whose actual profile is *opposite* to the assumed bias inverts the benefit into a penalty.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "static" with "dynamic" savings.** Static instructions removed is *not* the same as cycles saved. Always multiply by frequency. Many students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting the pre-conditions.** Just stating "merge two blocks" without checking for *unique predecessor* will cost full marks for straightening.
> 3. **Mixing LICM with global placement loosely.** LICM requires dominance analysis and loop detection — do not hand-wave it.
> 4. **Incorrect CFG arrows.** Examiners in KTU penalize *undirected* or *bidirectional* CFG edges. Always use directed edges with arrowheads.
> 5. **Missing semantics preservation.** Every transformation in Module 4 *must* state "semantics preserved" or "program behaviour unchanged."

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Global Code Placement** repositions instructions across basic block boundaries to reduce **dynamic instruction count**.
- The four key sub-strategies are: **Straightening**, **Branch Alignment**, **Tail Merging**, and **LICM**.
- **Straightening** requires a *unique predecessor–successor* relationship between two blocks.
- **Branch Alignment** exploits the CPU's static prediction of forward branches as not-taken.
- **Tail Merging** extracts identical instruction tails into a shared block; dynamic savings $= (f_A + f_B) \cdot k$.
- **LICM** moves loop-invariant computations to a preheader; frequency reduction from $N \cdot f_{\text{loop}}$ to $f_{\text{outer}}$.
- The objective function is $\Delta D = D_{\text{orig}} - D_{\text{new}} > 0$.
- The **reduction ratio** $R = 1 - \dfrac{D_{\text{new}}}{D_{\text{orig}}}$ must be positive for a profitable optimization.
- **Hot/cold splitting** reduces I-cache misses by clustering frequently executed blocks in `.text.hot`.
- **Profiling** can be *static* (heuristic, loop-depth based) or *dynamic* (instrumentation-based, e.g., GCC PGO).
- The **hazards** are: aliasing, exception movement, debugger opacity, and reversed branch bias.
- For 7-mark questions, always draw the CFG **before** and **after**; for 14-mark questions, include the cost calculation.
- **Semantics preservation** is the universal pre-condition — never write an optimization that changes program output.
- The bias threshold for "likely" branches is typically $\tau = 0.1$ (so $\vert p - 0.5 \vert > 0.1$).
- Profile-guided optimization can yield **2x–3x speedups** on cache-bound programs but requires a representative input set.
